docs.title: Welcome to the Corepedia
docs.text:
"Knowledge is all that survives." — StarCom Archival Directive 01

Welcome citizen to the official StarCom archival network.

The Corepedia is the largest surviving publicly accessible historical, technical, and political archive within civilized human space. Distributed through approved CoreNet terminals, relay hubs, educational institutions, and licensed vendor systems, this archive preserves the collective record of the Core and surrounding frontier regions.

docs.title: About the Archive
docs.text:
StarCom historians, navigators, military analysts, archivists, and intelligence specialists continuously maintain and expand this archive using recovered Empire-era records, government transmissions, navigational logs, battlefield reports, diplomatic archives, and verified civilian documentation.

The Corepedia contains records concerning:

  • The Great Houses and major political powers
  • Mercenary Organizations and the Mercenary Guild
  • Mechs of the Core and the war machines of modern civilization
  • The Core and the structure of interstellar society
  • Major Worlds and notable locations
  • Military Forces including fleets and Battlestars
  • Technology and industrial development
  • Historical Figures and notable leaders
  • Core History from the Empire to the present day

docs.title: Content Disclaimer
docs.text:
Due to the fragmented nature of surviving historical records, certain entries may contain disputed information, contradictory accounts, or incomplete archival reconstruction. Many records originate from wartime transmissions, damaged Imperial databanks, conflicting state narratives, or restricted intelligence sources later cleared for limited public release.

StarCom does not guarantee complete historical accuracy for all archived material.

docs.title: Usage Warnings
docs.text:
Certain restricted entries remain unavailable to civilian users under current Conclave information security protocols.

Citizens are reminded that unauthorized tampering with archival records, restricted data caches, navigational libraries, or CoreNet relay infrastructure constitutes a violation of StarCom interstellar communications law and may result in suspension of network access, financial penalties, or criminal prosecution under applicable jurisdictional authority.

This archive endures so that civilization may remember.

StarCom — To Know the Future

docs.title: The Core
docs.text: “Civilization survives only so long as the Core endures.” — StarCom archival proverb

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Central Authority The Star Regency Current Star Regent Jacob Caledon Ruling House House Caledon Capital World Citadel Major Powers The Five Great Houses Current Era 5016 AE Words of House Caledon “Rule Above All.”

The Core refers to the central region of known human civilization and the political, military, industrial, and cultural heart of interstellar society.

The region is composed of several dozen densely populated and highly developed worlds governed collectively through the authority of the Star Regency and the alliance known as the Stellar Conclave.

Although commonly spoken of as a unified civilization, the Core is in practice a complex balance of competing powers, ancient traditions, military alliances, industrial interests, and political rivalries held together through diplomacy, mutual dependence, and the stabilizing authority of House Caledon.

While the Core remains the most prosperous and technologically advanced region of known space, competition between the Great Houses continues to shape nearly every aspect of interstellar life. Trade disputes, territorial disagreements, industrial rivalries, and political ambitions frequently spill into open conflict. As a result, Mercenaries and Mechs have become central institutions of Core society. The Great Houses rely upon vast military forces, independent mercenary companies, and powerful war mechs not only to defend against pirates, bandits, and frontier raiders, but also to wage proxy conflicts, conduct raids, and pursue their interests without risking full-scale interstellar war.

docs.title: Why the Core Matters
docs.text:
For most of human space, the Core is more than a region. It is the center of political authority, economic power, military strength, and cultural influence.

The Great Houses, Star Regency, StarCom, Mercenary Guild, and many of the most important institutions in modern civilization all trace their authority to the Core. Decisions made on its worlds often shape events thousands of light-years away.

To supporters, the Core represents humanity's greatest achievement since the Great Restoration.

To critics, it represents a wealthy collection of successor states living among the ruins of a greater civilization.

docs.title: The Great Houses
docs.text:
Five dominant powers comprise the political foundation of the modern Core:

Great House Ruling House General Character Confederate Vanguard Union Hawkins Militarized industrial state emphasizing strength, discipline, and collective sacrifice Helios Sovereignty Aerin & Payne Wealthy centralized power focused on stability, order, and economic influence Omnisphere Imperium Lytherius Ancient aristocratic realm preserving many old traditions of the Core Starcrest Protectorate Konnen Harsh frontier power built around resilience, honor, and martial duty Orion Corporate Noddack Technological and scientific authority focused on innovation and internal development

Together these powers form the Stellar Conclave, the governing alliance of the Core Worlds.

Each maintains independent territory, militaries, economies, and political interests while recognizing the authority of the Star Regent and the necessity of preserving stability throughout the Core.

docs.title: The Star Regency
docs.text:
→ See: The Star Regency

The Core is formally governed by the Star Regent from the capital world of Citadel.

The current Star Regent is Jacob Caledon of House Caledon, the ancient ruling line responsible for re-establishing civilization during the Great Restoration.

Though House Caledon controls comparatively little territory directly, its legitimacy and symbolic authority remain recognized throughout the Core Worlds.

The words of House Caledon are:

Rule Above All.

Among supporters of the Regency, these words are understood to mean that civilization, order, and stability must supersede the ambitions of any individual faction or ruler.

Critics occasionally argue that the Regency has become increasingly ceremonial in modern centuries, though few openly question the importance of preserving unity among the Great Houses.

docs.title: StarCom
docs.text:
→ See: StarCom

No institution exerts greater infrastructural influence across the Core than StarCom.

Officially neutral and operating independently of the Great Houses, StarCom maintains the communications relays, navigational archives, hyperspace routing systems, merchant traffic networks, and deep-space observation infrastructure upon which modern civilization depends.

The organization emerged during the early years of the Great Restoration and has since become indispensable to interstellar society. Few governments trust one another completely, but all of them trust StarCom's networks because there is no practical alternative.

The organization emerged during the early years following the Great Restoration and has since become indispensable to interstellar civilization. Without StarCom, large-scale communication and coordinated governance across the Core would be impossible.

docs.title: Civilization and Society
docs.text:
The Core contains the largest concentration of population, industry, military infrastructure, and scientific development in known space. Compared to the frontier regions beyond the Core, living standards are generally higher and political institutions more stable. However, increasing tensions between the Great Houses, expanding frontier instability, piracy, economic rivalry, and political extremism continue to threaten the long-term stability of the region.

Despite frequent political tensions, most citizens experience the Core as a period of relative peace and prosperity. Interstellar trade is common, communications are reliable, and travel between major worlds is routine. While individual House cultures vary considerably, shared institutions such as the Star Regency, StarCom, the Mercenary Guild, and centuries of economic integration have produced a broadly recognizable Core identity.

docs.title: Warfare
docs.text:
Modern warfare throughout the Core is dominated by mechanized combat, mercenary operations, limited territorial conflicts, covert actions, proxy wars, and strategic competition between the Great Houses.

Mechs form the backbone of nearly all modern ground combat forces. Meanwhile, mercenary companies — commonly referred to throughout civilized space as “Metal Mercs” — play a major role in frontier security, deniable military operations, and inter-house conflicts. Large-scale space warfare remains comparatively rare.

The technological and industrial capabilities required to sustain massive fleet warfare were partially lost during the Empire's collapse and the long dark age that followed. As a result, modern powers generally avoid direct fleet attrition whenever possible.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
→ See: The Empire and The Dark Age

Much of the Core's earliest history has been lost to time.

According to surviving records, the Core was once the heartland of the Golden Empire, a vast interstellar civilization that united thousands of worlds through trade, military power, administration, and shared institutions. Ruled by the Star Emperor and supported by hundreds of noble houses, the Empire governed a territory far larger than the modern Core and achieved levels of technological and industrial development that have never been fully recovered.

At its height, the Empire represented the largest and most powerful civilization in recorded human history.

Sometime around 4000 AE, the Empire collapsed during a catastrophic conflict associated with the mysterious Ophidian Supremacy. The exact nature of the Supremacy remains one of the greatest unanswered questions in human history. Traditional accounts describe the Ophidians as the force responsible for the destruction of the Empire and the subsequent occupation of much of the Core. However, surviving records from the era are fragmentary, contradictory, and often separated from the events they describe by centuries.

Very little direct evidence of the Supremacy has ever been discovered. Some historians believe the Ophidians were a rival interstellar civilization that invaded from beyond known space. Others argue they were merely one factor among many in a broader collapse caused by internal conflict, economic decline, or civil war. Some historians argue the Ophidian Supremacy became a historical metaphor for a broader collapse rather than a single identifiable enemy.

Whatever the truth, the Fall was followed by several centuries of chaos known as The Dark Age.

During this period, interstellar communications collapsed, trade networks disappeared, and much of the Core fragmented into isolated worlds and competing regional powers. Famine, piracy, warfare, and political instability became widespread. Entire worlds vanished from recorded history, while countless technologies, institutions, and historical records were lost. A handful of states endured the turmoil, most notably the Imperium, the Starcrest Protectorate, and the Orion Corporate, but much of the Core was consumed by centuries of decline and conflict.

The Dark Age eventually came to an end with the Great Restoration. According to accepted history, exiled remnants of the Empire returned from the Deep Fringe under the leadership of House Caledon and gradually re-established stability throughout the Core. In the centuries that followed, the modern Great Houses emerged, the Stellar Conclave was established, and interstellar civilization began to recover.

Most surviving historical records originate from the post-Restoration era. As a result, many details concerning the Empire, the Ophidian Supremacy, and the Dark Age remain subjects of ongoing scholarly debate.

docs.title: Geography and Regions
docs.text: Although commonly depicted as a single political entity, the Core is geographically divided into several broad regions.

docs.title: The Prime Meridian
docs.text:
Most inhabited worlds of the Core lie within a dense galactic plane known as the Prime Meridian. This region contains the oldest trade routes, the greatest concentration of population, and the majority of the Core's industrial and military infrastructure.

The Prime Meridian is traditionally divided into two subregions:

docs.title: Central Worlds
docs.text:
The Central Worlds occupy the heart of the Core and are generally considered the most prosperous, stable, and heavily defended systems in known space.

Notable Central Worlds include:

  • Citadel, seat of the Star Regency
  • Drake's Landing, headquarters of the Mercenary Guild
  • Lumina, capital of the Helios Sovereignty
  • Celestia Prime, capital of the Omnisphere Imperium

The Central Worlds benefit from dense trade networks, strong StarCom infrastructure, rapid military response capabilities, and some of the highest standards of living in human space.

docs.title: Outer Worlds
docs.text:
Beyond the Central Worlds lie the Outer Worlds.

These systems remain firmly within Core territory but are generally less developed, less densely populated, and more vulnerable to piracy, smuggling, and frontier unrest.

Notable Outer Worlds include:

  • Khorhall, capital of the Starcrest Protectorate
  • Horizon's Reach, capital of Orion Corporate
  • Starhold, capital of the Confederate Vanguard Union

The distinction between Central and Outer Worlds is not political but practical. While life in the Outer Worlds remains far safer than in the Frontier, citizens often experience weaker governmental oversight, longer supply routes, and greater exposure to raids originating from the Fringe.

Worlds situated along the outermost edge of the Core are commonly referred to as Border Worlds. These systems serve as the first line of defense against piracy, bandit activity, and external threats emerging from the Fringe.

docs.title: The Upper Meridian
docs.text:
Above the Prime Meridian lies a secondary concentration of inhabited systems known as the Upper Meridian.

Smaller than the Prime Meridian and roughly comparable in radius to the Central Worlds, the Upper Meridian contains a mixture of industrial, agricultural, and commercial systems. While generally stable and prosperous, these worlds tend to resemble the Outer Worlds in both population density and security.

Though less politically influential than the Prime Meridian, the Upper Meridian remains an important contributor to the Core's economy and military manpower.

docs.title: The Lower Meridian
docs.text:
Below the Prime Meridian lies the Lower Meridian, a broad collection of systems somewhat larger than the Upper Meridian.

The Lower Meridian contains a mixture of industrial worlds, mining systems, military outposts, and independent commercial hubs. Conditions vary considerably between systems, though most are comparable to the Outer Worlds in development and security.

Among its most famous worlds is Titan Prime, the renowned gladiator world whose arenas attract competitors and spectators from across human space.

docs.title: Neutral Worlds
docs.text:
A small number of worlds maintain special statuses recognized by all Great Houses regardless of their location.

Among the most prominent are Drake's Landing and Titan Prime. Their neutrality, economic importance, and strategic value have made them important gathering places for merchants, mercenaries, diplomats, and travelers throughout the Core.

Although the Great Houses periodically attempt to increase their influence over these worlds, centuries of custom and mutual interest have generally preserved their independence.

docs.title: Beyond the Core
docs.text:
→ See: The Fringe and The Frontier

Beyond the borders of the Core lie vast frontier regions containing isolated colonies, mining worlds, pirate territories, mercenary enclaves, independent settlements, and abandoned systems.

While formally connected to the Core through trade and StarCom infrastructure, many frontier regions operate with limited oversight from the Great Houses. Some maintain close economic ties with the Core, while others are effectively independent in all but name.

The Frontier remains a source of valuable resources, mercenary recruits, exploration opportunities, and occasional threats. Pirates, warlords, and independent powers are far more common beyond the Core than within it.

docs.title: The Myth of Civilization
docs.text:
Most citizens of the Core believe they live within the restored heart of human civilization.

This belief is not entirely unfounded. The Core is wealthy, technologically advanced, and militarily powerful. Trade routes are secure, governments are stable, and the Great Houses maintain the strongest known military forces in human space.

Yet many historians argue that the Core has not restored the Empire so much as inherited its ruins.

The Core governs only a fraction of the territory once controlled by Imperial authorities. Vast regions of human space remain poorly mapped, weakly governed, or entirely outside House influence.

To its citizens, the Core represents civilization.

To many historians, it represents civilization's most successful remnant.

docs.title: Demographics and Settlement
docs.text:
→ See also: Demographics and Irregular Forces

Although the Core contains more than sixty billion inhabitants, most worlds remain surprisingly undeveloped outside major population centers.

A typical Core world may contain one or two enormous cities and a handful of smaller settlements, surrounded by vast regions of wilderness, agricultural land, mining territories, abandoned Empire-era infrastructure, and isolated communities.

This pattern dates back to both the Empire and the Dark Age. Many worlds possess infrastructure designed to support populations far larger than those that currently inhabit them, while others contain enormous abandoned districts whose original purpose has long been forgotten.

As a result, even heavily populated worlds often contain enormous regions that remain lightly settled, poorly surveyed, or rarely visited.

These vast and often sparsely monitored regions have also contributed to the persistence of bandits, pirates, smugglers, separatists, and other irregular forces throughout the Core. While the Great Houses maintain powerful militaries and extensive security networks, the sheer scale of inhabited space makes complete oversight impossible. Many remote regions, abandoned facilities, and forgotten settlements remain beyond the routine reach of government authorities, providing opportunities for both legitimate communities and those operating outside the law.

docs.title: "The Core controls most of human space."
docs.text:
False.

The Core controls only a portion of known human territory.

docs.title: "The Frontier is largely inhabited by pirates."
docs.text:
Partially true.

Pirates exist, but many Frontier worlds possess long histories, stable governments, and unique cultures.

docs.title: "The Great Houses are the strongest powers in existence."
docs.text:
Unknown.

No reliable census exists for the regions beyond the Frontier.

docs.title: "The Empire and the Core are comparable in size."
docs.text:
False.

The Empire was vastly larger.

docs.title: "Humanity is fully mapped."
docs.text:
False.

StarCom continuously discovers and rediscovers settlements, ruins, and navigation routes.

docs.title: Demographics of the Core
docs.text:
"Most inhabited worlds are known by their cities. Everything beyond them is often a matter of rumor, survey records, and old maps."

— StarCom Survey Office

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The modern Core contains approximately 72 billion inhabitants spread across the territories of the Five Great Houses, the Star Regency, and several independent worlds.

Although it represents the political, economic, and cultural center of human civilization, the Core occupies only a fraction of the territory once governed by the ancient Empire. Much of humanity's former domain was lost during the Fall, the Dark Age, and the centuries of fragmentation that followed.

Population is distributed unevenly throughout the region. Some worlds contain billions of inhabitants and vast urban centers, while others support only a few million settlers scattered across isolated colonies and industrial outposts. Even among the most developed systems, settlement patterns are often concentrated around major population centers rather than spread evenly across planetary surfaces.

This combination of high population, uneven development, and enormous territorial scale creates a Core that often feels both crowded and empty at the same time. Bustling cities, orbital habitats, and industrial megacomplexes may exist only a few hundred kilometers from wilderness regions that remain effectively untouched.

docs.title: Population by Major Power
docs.text:
Nation Population Military Mechs Battlestars Starcrest Protectorate 9.2 Billion 288,000 3 Helios Sovereignty 11.7 Billion 273,000 2 Orion Corporate 13.4 Billion 189,000 2 Confederate Vanguard Union 16.8 Billion 346,000 3 Omnisphere Imperium 12.3 Billion 220,000 2 Citadel 4.2 Billion N/A N/A Drake's Landing 1.8 Billion N/A N/A Titan Prime 2.6 Billion N/A N/A

Total Known Population: ~72 Billion

docs.title: Settlement Patterns
docs.text:
→ See also: Irregular Forces

Despite containing tens of billions of inhabitants, most Core worlds are far less densely populated than outsiders often imagine.

Population tends to concentrate within major cities, industrial corridors, arcology complexes, orbital habitats, and transportation hubs. Beyond these regions, settlement density frequently declines rapidly. Vast wilderness areas, mountain ranges, forests, deserts, oceans, and uninhabited territories remain common even on heavily populated worlds.

Most worlds are better understood as networks of settlements rather than uniformly developed planets. A handful of major cities often contain the majority of a world's population, connected by transportation corridors, mining operations, industrial facilities, agricultural regions, military installations, and smaller communities scattered across the globe.

Many planets also contain large quantities of abandoned infrastructure dating back to the Empire, the Dark Age, or earlier colonial eras. Forgotten mining camps, depopulated settlements, abandoned military bases, obsolete transit networks, and partially reclaimed urban districts can be found throughout the Core. Some have been documented and preserved. Others have simply been forgotten.

As worlds age, layers of development accumulate. New cities are built atop old foundations. Industrial regions expand and contract. Colonies are founded, abandoned, and resettled. Entire communities may disappear without significantly affecting a planet's overall population statistics.

For this reason, few worlds are comprehensively mapped beyond their inhabited regions. Governments maintain detailed records of major population centers and critical infrastructure, but many remote regions remain poorly surveyed. Old records are often incomplete, contradictory, or centuries out of date.

As a result, a planet with a population of one billion inhabitants may still contain vast stretches of wilderness, abandoned facilities, forgotten settlements, and regions that no living resident has personally explored.

The Core may be the center of human civilization, but much of it remains surprisingly vast, layered with history, and only partially occupied.

docs.title: Urbanization
docs.text:
Most Core citizens live in cities.

The largest metropolitan regions contain hundreds of millions of inhabitants spread across planetary capitals, arcology networks, orbital habitats, and industrial districts. These population centers serve as the economic and political engines of the Great Houses.

Outside the major cities, however, population density often falls dramatically. Many citizens spend their entire lives in a single metropolitan region and may never travel beyond their home world.

docs.title: Irregular Forces
docs.text:
"No government can be everywhere."

— Mercenary Guild Handbook

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Irregular Forces is a broad term used to describe armed organizations that operate outside the authority of recognized governments, military organizations, or licensed security institutions.

These groups range from small bands of raiders to large separatist movements capable of controlling significant territory. Some are criminal enterprises motivated by profit, while others pursue political, ideological, or religious objectives.

Although the Great Houses maintain powerful militaries and extensive security networks, the sheer scale of inhabited space ensures that irregular forces remain a persistent feature of life throughout the Core.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
The continued existence of irregular forces is largely a consequence of geography, history, and scale.

Most inhabited worlds are not uniformly developed. Population tends to cluster around major cities, transportation corridors, industrial centers, and orbital facilities, leaving vast regions sparsely populated or rarely visited.

Many planets also contain large quantities of abandoned infrastructure dating back to the Empire, the Dark Age, or earlier colonial eras. Forgotten mining camps, depopulated settlements, abandoned military bases, obsolete transit networks, and partially reclaimed urban districts can be found throughout the Core. Some have been documented and preserved. Others have simply been forgotten.

As worlds age, layers of development accumulate. New cities are built atop old foundations. Industrial regions expand and contract. Colonies are founded, abandoned, and resettled. Entire communities may disappear without significantly affecting a planet's overall population statistics.

For this reason, few worlds are comprehensively mapped beyond their inhabited regions. Governments maintain detailed records of major population centers and critical infrastructure, but many remote regions remain poorly surveyed. Old records are often incomplete, contradictory, or centuries out of date.

Most worlds are also too large to police comprehensively. Governments concentrate their resources around major population centers, transportation corridors, industrial sites, and strategic infrastructure. Beyond these regions, law enforcement and military presence often becomes increasingly sparse.

These conditions create ideal environments for irregular forces. Remote wilderness areas, abandoned settlements, forgotten military installations, and isolated mountain ranges frequently serve as refuges for groups seeking to avoid government oversight. Many organizations establish hidden bases within regions that may be visited only rarely by authorities.

docs.title: Strongholds and Hideouts
docs.text:
Contrary to popular perception, most successful irregular forces do not operate from temporary camps.

Many occupy abandoned settlements, forgotten military installations, decommissioned industrial complexes, mining colonies, or other relics of earlier eras. Some strongholds originated as legitimate communities that gradually fell beyond effective government control, while others were deliberately established in remote regions chosen for their isolation.

Large organizations may control entire valleys, island chains, mountain ranges, or sparsely populated regions. The most successful groups often develop workshops, landing facilities, supply depots, training grounds, and defensive networks capable of supporting long-term operations.

Some strongholds have existed for generations and become communities in their own right. Children are born there, economies emerge, and local identities develop independent of the governments that nominally claim the territory.

docs.title: Bandits and Raiders
docs.text:
Bandits are among the most common irregular threats throughout the Core.

These groups survive through robbery, extortion, smuggling, protection rackets, and attacks against isolated settlements or transportation routes. Most begin as small criminal organizations, though successful groups can grow into regional powers controlling substantial territory.

Bandit organizations emerge from many sources. Some are founded by criminals and opportunists seeking profit, while others originate from failed separatist movements, collapsing frontier communities, abandoned military formations, or mercenary companies that have fallen upon hard times. Throughout history, poorly disciplined companies, abandoned garrisons, and unpaid soldiers have occasionally drifted into piracy, banditry, or outright rebellion.

While smaller organizations may rely primarily upon conventional vehicles and infantry, larger groups frequently field mechs acquired through theft, salvage, black market purchases, battlefield recovery, or defections from legitimate military forces. Veteran mech pilots are highly valued and often command considerable authority within bandit hierarchies.

Many prominent bandit leaders are former mercenaries, discharged soldiers, failed revolutionaries, or local strongmen. As a result, some organizations possess surprisingly sophisticated military capabilities despite their criminal origins.

docs.title: Pirates
docs.text:
Pirates operate primarily in space, targeting civilian shipping, isolated colonies, frontier traffic, and lightly defended commercial routes.

Most pirate organizations rely upon speed, surprise, and superior local knowledge rather than overwhelming military strength. Successful groups carefully select targets, avoid major fleet engagements, and retreat before authorities can organize a response.

Many pirate crews operate from hidden bases located within remote systems, abandoned stations, asteroid belts, or poorly monitored regions of space. The largest organizations often maintain support networks involving smugglers, corrupt officials, black market traders, and informants.

Although heavily armed pirate fleets are uncommon, some pirate lords have accumulated sufficient wealth and military power to dominate entire sectors for years before being removed.

docs.title: Smugglers and Criminal Syndicates
docs.text:
Not all irregular forces rely upon open violence.

Smuggling networks transport contraband, restricted technologies, weapons, stolen goods, and other illicit cargo between worlds. Criminal syndicates often operate extensive commercial networks that may span multiple star systems.

Unlike bandit groups, syndicates frequently attempt to infiltrate legitimate institutions rather than oppose them directly. Bribery, corruption, money laundering, and political influence are often more valuable than military strength.

Nevertheless, major criminal organizations commonly maintain private security forces and mech units capable of defending their operations. Some possess resources rivaling those of minor governments.

docs.title: Separatists and Insurgents
docs.text:
Some irregular forces are politically motivated rather than criminal.

Separatist movements seek independence from existing governments, while insurgent organizations attempt to reform, weaken, or overthrow established authorities. Such groups often emerge in regions where local populations feel neglected, exploited, culturally distinct, or politically marginalized.

Many separatist movements view themselves as legitimate governments-in-waiting rather than rebels. They frequently establish administrative structures, taxation systems, local militias, and territorial claims within areas under their control.

While some movements remain peaceful, others develop into major military organizations capable of fielding conventional armies, mech formations, aerospace assets, and long-term resistance campaigns.

docs.title: Mercenary Operations
docs.text: Mercenaries are employed extensively throughout the Core, both in the suppression of irregular forces and in proxy conflicts between the Great Houses.

docs.title: Security and Suppression
docs.text:
Even the Great Houses cannot continuously patrol every trade route, investigate every report of suspicious activity, or maintain garrisons in every remote settlement. Mercenary units therefore serve as a flexible reserve force, allowing clients to respond rapidly to developing situations without sustaining the enormous expense of expanding their standing militaries. Governments, corporations, settlements, and private citizens regularly hire mercenaries to combat pirates, bandits, smugglers, separatists, and other irregular forces.

Common contracts include:

  • anti-piracy operations
  • bandit suppression
  • convoy escort missions
  • settlement defense
  • anti-smuggling raids
  • counterinsurgency operations
  • recovery of stolen property
  • reconnaissance of hostile territories

Many assignments involve lengthy search operations, anti-piracy patrols, and the systematic elimination of hostile strongholds hidden within remote regions. Others involve border disputes, covert raids, security operations, military advising, or deniable actions carried out on behalf of larger powers.

As a result, irregular forces remain one of the largest sources of employment for mercenary organizations throughout human space, though they are far from the only source.

docs.title: Proxy Warfare
docs.text:
The Great Houses also frequently employ mercenary units to supplement military operations, wage proxy conflicts, provide plausible deniability, secure remote territories, and respond to emerging threats.

The boundary between mercenary and irregular force is not always clear. Not every raid or ambush is necessarily the work of irregular forces. The Great Houses and other powerful interests frequently employ mercenary units as proxy forces. Although rival governments often recognize the true sponsors behind such operations, the use of mercenaries helps contain disputes that might otherwise escalate into open war. Raids, sabotage, border actions, and industrial strikes carried out by mercenary forces have become an accepted, if unofficial, part of interstate competition throughout much of the Core.

As a result, organizations publicly or officially identified as bandits, raiders, separatists, or local militias may in reality be acting on behalf of larger political powers.

As one popular Outer Worlds saying puts it:

"The difference between a bandit, a rebel, and a merc is who signs the paycheck."

docs.title: Government Response
docs.text:
The Great Houses devote considerable resources to combating irregular threats.

Military patrols, intelligence operations, security forces, local defense organizations, and licensed mercenary units continuously work to suppress criminal and insurgent activity. Strategic transportation corridors, major cities, industrial centers, and critical infrastructure receive particularly heavy protection.

The distribution of irregular forces is not uniform throughout the Core. Activity tends to be lowest within the Central Worlds of the Prime Meridian, where population density, military presence, law enforcement, and surveillance infrastructure are at their greatest. Worlds such as Citadel and the capital worlds of the Great Houses maintain extensive security networks that make it exceedingly difficult for bandits, pirates, or irregular mech formations to operate openly.

This does not mean irregular activity is entirely absent. Smuggling, organized crime, espionage, and other covert operations still occur even within the most secure systems. However, heavily armed groups rarely survive for long once detected. Any significant bandit force, pirate base, or unauthorized mech formation discovered near a major population center would likely face an immediate and overwhelming response from local authorities.

Beyond the Central Worlds, irregular forces become increasingly common. The Outer Worlds, Upper Meridian, and Lower Meridian generally possess lower population densities, fewer security assets, and larger expanses of lightly monitored territory. These conditions provide greater opportunities for bandits, pirates, smugglers, separatists, and other irregular organizations to establish strongholds and evade authorities.

docs.title: A Permanent Reality
docs.text:
Despite centuries of anti-piracy campaigns, military operations, and law enforcement efforts, complete eradication of irregular forces in the Core has proven impossible. Every successful operation removes one threat, but new groups inevitably emerge elsewhere. A destroyed pirate fleet may scatter into several smaller organizations. A defeated bandit leader may simply be replaced by another. Suppressed insurgencies often reappear years later under new leadership.

For most governments, irregular forces are not a problem to be solved permanently but a reality to be managed continuously. Success is measured not by elimination, but by containment.

As long as worlds remain vast, borders remain porous, and authority remains imperfect, irregular forces will continue to emerge wherever opportunities exist. Some are motivated by greed, others by desperation, ideology, ambition, or simple survival. Their causes vary, but their presence remains remarkably consistent across cultures and eras.

For many citizens, irregular forces are a distant concern encountered only through news reports and security briefings. For others, particularly those living in remote settlements and isolated regions, they are an everyday reality.

The Core may be the center of human civilization, but beyond the reach of its institutions there will always be places where other powers take root.

docs.title: The Empire That Was
docs.text:
The Old Empire (also sometimes refered to as the Golden Empire or just The Empire) was the largest human civilization ever known.

At its height, it ruled tens of thousands of worlds and maintained military forces beyond anything possessed by the modern Great Houses.

Many technologies used throughout the Core originate from Imperial infrastructure that can no longer be reproduced.

docs.title: Legacy
docs.text:
Modern civilization exists within the shadow of the Empire.

Nearly every government claims some degree of cultural or historical connection to it.

The Great Houses frequently describe themselves as the heirs of Imperial civilization.

docs.title: The Great Illusion
docs.text:
Many citizens believe the Core has restored the prosperity and unity of the Empire.

Historians generally disagree.

The Core remains prosperous and powerful, but it governs only a fraction of the territory once administered by Imperial authorities.

docs.title: Battlestars
docs.text:
The great Battlestars serving the Houses today are relics of Imperial industry.

No modern power possesses the capability to construct new vessels of comparable scale.

docs.title: History of the Core
docs.text: “The Houses did not restore the Empire. They built a wall around its ruins.” — Unknown Mercenary Commander

docs.title: Historical Overview
docs.text:
Era Common Description Estimated Start Estimated End Early History The early history of civilizations in the Core - ??? AE The Empire The united civilization of humanity ??? AE ~4000 AE The Dark Age Occupation, fragmentation, and civilizational decline ~4000 AE ~4500 AE The Great Restoration Return of the Regent and reclamation of the Core ~4500 AE - The Modern Era Rise of the Great Houses and the Stellar Conclave ~4500 AE -

The history of the Core is fragmented, contradictory, and often mythologized. Dates presented are in AE (After expansion). Dates are estimated based on historical records though not all dates ranges are agreed upon.

Most surviving records originate only from the period following the Great Restoration. Earlier eras are known primarily through damaged archives, oral traditions, military legends, religious stories, and reconstructed StarCom historical records.

As a result, much of early Core history remains heavily disputed among historians.

Some scholars argue that many commonly accepted events may have been exaggerated, altered, or entirely fabricated over centuries of instability and record loss.

Nevertheless, a broad historical framework is generally accepted throughout civilized space.

docs.title: The Empire
docs.text:
→ See also: The Empire

Long before the modern Great Houses existed, the Core is believed to have been united beneath a vast interstellar civilization commonly referred to simply as the Empire.

No universally accepted name survives. Depending on the source, historians may refer to it as the Old Empire, the First Empire, the Golden Empire, or simply the Empire. All of these labels describe what is believed to have been the largest and most powerful human civilization in recorded history.

According to surviving records, the Empire controlled territory far beyond the borders of the modern Core. Its authority was enforced through immense military forces, vast industrial infrastructure, interstellar trade networks, and a complex administrative system that connected thousands of worlds.

Much of what is claimed about the Empire remains difficult to verify. Surviving descriptions speak of technological capabilities that modern civilization cannot fully reproduce, stable governance across enormous distances, and levels of industrial output that seem almost implausible by contemporary standards.

Whether these accounts represent historical reality or centuries of exaggeration remains a matter of debate. What few historians dispute is that the Empire existed, that it dominated human space for centuries, and that its collapse permanently reshaped the course of human history.

docs.title: The Fall
docs.text:
→ See also: The Fall

At some point around 4000 AE, the Empire collapsed.

The precise sequence of events remains unclear, but surviving records agree that the final centuries of Imperial history were marked by widespread warfare, political instability, and the gradual breakdown of institutions that had governed human space for generations. Within a relatively short period, the Empire ceased to function as a unified civilization.

Most traditional accounts associate this catastrophe with the mysterious Ophidian Supremacy.

According to the orthodox interpretation, the Supremacy was a rival interstellar power whose conflict with the Empire triggered the destruction of the old order. This remains the dominant view among governments, universities, and official historical institutions throughout the Core.

Not all historians agree.

Some scholars argue that the Ophidians were not a single civilization but a coalition of hostile powers. Others contend that the Empire was already collapsing due to internal conflict, economic decline, political fragmentation, or civil war long before any external threat emerged.

A smaller but influential school of thought proposes that the Ophidian Supremacy may have become a historical metaphor for the forces that destroyed the Empire. According to this interpretation, later generations transformed a complex and poorly understood collapse into the simpler narrative of a great external enemy.

More speculative theories suggest the Supremacy originated beyond known space, was non-human in nature, or never existed at all.

No explanation has successfully accounted for all surviving evidence.

What is generally accepted is that the Fall shattered the political and technological foundations of human civilization and ushered in the centuries of instability that followed.

docs.title: The Dark Age
docs.text:
→ See also: The Dark Age

Following the Fall, the Core entered the period now known as the Dark Age.

The collapse of centralized authority shattered the political, economic, and technological systems that had once connected human space. Interstellar trade declined, communication networks fragmented, and countless worlds found themselves isolated from their neighbors. In many regions, knowledge that had once been commonplace became increasingly difficult to preserve.

The severity of the collapse varied dramatically across the Core. Some worlds retained functioning governments and industrial bases. Others descended into generations of warfare, famine, piracy, or political fragmentation. Entire systems vanished from surviving records, while once-important trade routes became dangerous or impossible to navigate.

It was during this era that many of the institutions and cultures that define the modern Core first emerged. Mercenary companies expanded rapidly, frontier identities developed, and countless local powers rose and fell in the absence of any overarching authority.

Although often remembered as an age of decline, the Dark Age was also a period of adaptation and survival. Many of the worlds, organizations, and traditions that would later shape the Great Restoration can trace their origins to these centuries of uncertainty.

Even today, the Dark Age remains one of the least understood periods in human history. Much of what occurred during those centuries has been lost to time, leaving historians to reconstruct events from scattered records, archaeological evidence, and surviving oral traditions.

docs.title: Survivors of the Dark Age
docs.text: While much of civilization collapsed, several powers survived in diminished forms.

docs.title: The Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
→ See also: The Omnisphere Imperium

The Omnisphere Imperium is the only modern Great House widely acknowledged to have survived continuously from the time of the Empire itself.

Though the Imperium lost significant territory during the Collapse, its core worlds successfully endured through a combination of defensive isolation, fortified infrastructure, and strict internal control.

Imperium records claim their ancestors resisted the Ophidian Supremacy directly during the final years of the Empire.

Because of this continuity, the Imperium often presents itself as the oldest legitimate civilization remaining within the Core.

docs.title: The Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
→ See also: The Starcrest Protectorate

The origins of the modern Starcrest Protectorate trace back to a larger pre-Collapse polity that did not survive the dark age intact.

The Protectorate itself emerged from the final defensive frontier regions of this fallen power.

Though greatly diminished, these surviving frontier defense forces managed to preserve their territory throughout the collapse and gradually expanded in strength over subsequent centuries.

This legacy continues to shape Protectorate culture, which strongly emphasizes resilience, sacrifice, and defensive duty.

docs.title: The Orion Cooperative
docs.text:
→ See also: The Orion Corporate

The organization that would eventually become Orion Corporate first emerged during the dark age as a defensive alliance between three isolated worlds attempting to survive the widespread instability of the era.

By pooling:

  • scientific expertise
  • industrial production
  • technological research
  • defensive resources

the Cooperative succeeded in preserving relative stability while much of the surrounding Core deteriorated.

Their intense focus on technological advancement developed largely as a reaction against the collapse they witnessed around them.

Over time, their superior technology and organizational efficiency allowed them to expand their influence substantially.

docs.title: The Long Exile
docs.text:
As the Empire collapsed, not all of its people remained within the Core.

According to Restoration-era records, elements of the Imperial military, administrative leadership, and civilian population fled beyond known space during the final years of the Fall. Among these refugees was the bloodline that would eventually become House Caledon.

The exact destination of the exiles remains unknown.

Traditional accounts describe a long migration into the deep fringe, where the survivors endured generations of hardship while preserving fragments of Imperial knowledge, military organization, and political authority. These stories form a central part of Restoration-era history and the legitimacy of the modern Star Regency.

Not all historians accept these accounts at face value. Some argue that the exiles may have settled in remote frontier regions rather than truly leaving known space. Others suggest the centuries spent in exile have been heavily mythologized by later generations seeking to strengthen the authority of House Caledon and the Restoration governments.

Whatever the truth, few dispute the historical consequences of their return.

Several centuries after the Fall, forces claiming descent from the exiles reappeared at the head of powerful military formations and began the campaign that would become known as the Great Restoration. Whether viewed as refugees, conquerors, liberators, or heirs to a lost civilization, their arrival permanently altered the course of human history.

docs.title: The Great Restoration
docs.text:
→ See also: The Great Restoration

Several centuries after the Fall, forces claiming descent from the Imperial exiles returned to the Core under the leadership of House Caledon.

What followed was a series of military campaigns, political alliances, and reconstruction efforts collectively known as the Great Restoration.

The Core they encountered was not an empty ruin. Numerous civilizations, successor states, mercenary powers, and regional governments had emerged during the Dark Age. Some welcomed the returning forces as liberators. Others viewed them as foreign conquerors seeking to impose authority over worlds that had survived without them for generations.

The Restoration therefore involved more than simple reunification. It was a struggle to create a new political order from the fractured remnants of the old one.

Over the course of the campaign, House Caledon and its allies reclaimed major trade corridors, restored interstellar communications, rebuilt jump networks, and re-established many of the institutions necessary for large-scale civilization. At the same time, alliances forged during the Restoration laid the foundations for the governments that would eventually become the modern Great Houses.

By the end of the conflict, much of the Core had once again been connected beneath a common political framework. The Star Regency was established, trade resumed across vast distances, and the long recovery from the Dark Age began in earnest.

Most modern institutions trace their origins to this period. The Great Restoration is therefore remembered not simply as a military victory, but as the event that created the modern Core itself.

docs.title: The Rise of the Great Houses
docs.text:
The decades following the Great Restoration saw the emergence of the modern Great Houses.

Many of these governments originated from organizations, military commands, regional powers, and political alliances that had played important roles during the Restoration itself. As House Caledon consolidated authority through the Star Regency, trusted allies were granted territory, responsibilities, and varying degrees of political autonomy.

Over time, these arrangements evolved into the five Great Houses recognized today: the Confederate Vanguard Union, Helios Sovereignty, Starcrest Protectorate, Omnisphere Imperium, and Orion Corporate.

Although each House developed its own culture, institutions, and political identity, all trace part of their modern legitimacy to the Restoration era. The relationships forged during those years continue to shape Core politics centuries later.

→ See also:

  • Confederate Vanguard Union
  • Helios Sovereignty
  • Starcrest Protectorate
  • Omnisphere Imperium
  • Orion Corporate

docs.title: The Modern Era
docs.text:
→ See also: The Modern Era

The current era began with the establishment of the Star Regency, the Stellar Conclave, the Great Houses, StarCom, and the network of institutions that continue to govern the Core today.

For several centuries, this system has provided a level of stability unmatched since the Fall of the Empire. Trade flows between thousands of worlds, communication networks span vast distances, and the Great Houses have largely avoided the kind of total wars that once devastated human civilization.

To many citizens, the modern Core represents the most prosperous and secure age in living memory.

Yet beneath this stability, tensions continue to grow.

Competition between the Great Houses has intensified as populations expand, frontier territories are settled, and strategic resources become increasingly valuable. Mercenary activity has risen, piracy remains a persistent threat, and political rivalries frequently spill into proxy conflicts beyond the direct authority of the Regency.

At the same time, historians, explorers, and StarCom analysts continue to uncover fragments of the past. Lost worlds, forgotten technologies, and unanswered questions surrounding the Fall, the Ophidian Supremacy, and the Great Restoration remind many that humanity's understanding of its own history remains incomplete.

As a result, the modern Core occupies a curious position. It is simultaneously the most successful civilization to emerge since the collapse of the Empire and a society still living among the ruins of a greater age.

Whether the centuries ahead bring a new golden age or another period of fragmentation remains one of the defining questions of modern history.

docs.title: The Dark Age
docs.text:
The Dark Age was the turbulent period that followed the Fall of the Empire. Lasting for several centuries, it was marked by political fragmentation, economic collapse, isolation, and near-constant conflict. With the destruction of the Empire's government, military, and communications infrastructure, no authority remained capable of maintaining order across the Core.

For many historians, the Dark Age represents the lowest point in recorded human history.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
The Dark Age began with the Fall of the Empire and the invasion of human space by the Ophidian Supremacy. Though details remain uncertain, historical records indicate that the Empire's military was shattered during the conflict and that much of the Empire's political and administrative structure ceased functioning within a relatively short period of time.

The exact sequence of events remains one of the greatest mysteries in human history. Surviving records from the era are fragmented, contradictory, and often separated by centuries of copying and interpretation. As a result, historians disagree on the precise causes and timeline of the Empire's collapse.

Despite these uncertainties, most scholars agree that the destruction of the Empire created a power vacuum on a scale never before seen. Within a few generations, the institutions that had governed humanity for centuries had either disappeared entirely or been reduced to isolated remnants.

docs.title: The Ophidian Occupation
docs.text:
The Dark Age is sometimes characterized as the period of Ophidian occupation that followed the Fall of the Empire.

According to traditional accounts, the Ophidian Supremacy occupied large portions of the Core for several generations following its victory over the Empire. However, determining the true extent of this occupation has proven nearly impossible.

By the time surviving records become available, communications between worlds had largely collapsed, interstellar travel had become increasingly dangerous, and countless systems were already fighting for their own survival. Nearly every region of the Core experienced political fragmentation, warfare, piracy, famine, or economic collapse. Because of this, historians struggle to distinguish between worlds that were directly occupied by Ophidian forces and worlds that simply collapsed as a consequence of the Empire's destruction.

Some records describe entire sectors being abandoned due to fears of Ophidian activity. Others suggest that vast regions of space were deliberately avoided, with travelers choosing longer routes rather than risk entering territory rumored to be under Ophidian control. Yet despite these accounts, very little direct evidence of the Supremacy has ever been discovered.

As a result, many questions remain unanswered. Which worlds were occupied? How many Ophidian forces remained in the Core following the Fall? How were they eventually defeated? Historians continue to debate these questions, and no consensus has emerged.

docs.title: The Collapse of Civilization
docs.text:
Entire fleets vanished during the Fall. Some were destroyed outright, while others fled into deep space and were never seen again. The communications networks that had once linked thousands of worlds fell silent. Trade routes collapsed. Administrative institutions ceased to function. In the span of only a few generations, much of human civilization lost contact with itself.

For many worlds, the Fall was not a single event but the beginning of a slow and relentless decline.

Without reliable communication between systems, rumors and paranoia flourished. Few knew which worlds had survived, which had fallen, or whether the Ophidians still occupied parts of the Core. Entire sectors became avoided regions as travelers feared drawing attention to themselves or unknowingly entering hostile territory.

Many populations abandoned major cities and population centers, believing that large concentrations of people would attract invaders. Communities retreated into remote settlements, underground facilities, isolated valleys, and hidden colonies where they hoped to avoid notice.

docs.title: Famine and Economic Breakdown
docs.text:
The collapse of interstellar trade proved devastating.

Many worlds had spent centuries specializing within the Empire's economy. Agricultural worlds produced food for entire sectors, while industrial worlds manufactured machinery, starship components, and advanced technology. When shipping ceased, these systems broke down almost overnight.

Food shortages became widespread. Entire populations were forced to ration supplies, abandon settlements, or compete violently for dwindling resources. Some colonies simply disappeared from the historical record. Whether they were destroyed, abandoned, or merely lost remains unknown.

Technologically sophisticated worlds often suffered particularly severe collapses. Their populations possessed highly specialized knowledge but frequently lacked the practical skills necessary to survive without the vast support networks that had sustained them. Power grids failed. Manufacturing facilities shut down. Medical systems collapsed. On many worlds, descendants of advanced civilizations found themselves struggling merely to survive.

docs.title: The Age of Warlords
docs.text:
The disappearance of galactic authority created a power vacuum that was quickly filled by local strongmen, military commanders, pirate kings, and warlords.

Across the Core, thousands of conflicts erupted as factions fought over surviving infrastructure, military stockpiles, agricultural land, and population centers. Alliances were often temporary. Borders shifted constantly. Few governments survived for more than a generation.

Some warlords ruled only a single city or moon. Others commanded fleets capable of threatening entire sectors. Many claimed to be the legitimate heirs of the Empire, while others openly embraced piracy and conquest.

For ordinary citizens, survival often depended less upon law and more upon whichever armed force happened to control their region at the time.

docs.title: Bastions of Survival
docs.text: Despite the chaos, a handful of civilizations endured.

docs.title: The Imperium
docs.text:
The Imperium survived by retreating inward. Closing its borders and abandoning much of its former territory, it consolidated its population around roughly a dozen heavily defended worlds.

Protected by ancient fortifications, disciplined institutions, and a deeply rooted cultural identity, the Imperium weathered the chaos largely intact. Though diminished, it preserved more continuity with the pre-Fall era than almost any other surviving power.

docs.title: The Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
The Starcrest Protectorate traces its origins to this period as well.

Originally established as a defensive frontier command tasked with resisting the Ophidians, it became increasingly isolated as communications with the wider galaxy disappeared. Over time, the military expedition evolved into a self-governing state dedicated to protecting the handful of worlds that remained under its control.

Through discipline, sacrifice, and constant vigilance, it survived where many others did not.

docs.title: The Orion Corporate
docs.text:
The Orion Corporate also emerged during the Dark Age. The term Corporate in Core terminology refers to a mutual defense pact.

Faced with escalating raids, piracy, and regional warfare, three neighboring worlds formed a defensive alliance dedicated to collective security and scientific cooperation. Through technological innovation and careful resource management, the alliance successfully defended itself against external threats and gradually expanded to include additional worlds.

By the end of the Dark Age, the Orion Corporate had become one of the most stable and prosperous regions in the recovering Core.

docs.title: Legacy
docs.text:
Despite isolated successes, the Dark Age remained a period defined by instability and survival. Most worlds were concerned not with rebuilding civilization, but with enduring another year.

Entire cultures vanished. Countless worlds were abandoned. Technologies were lost. Historical records were destroyed. Even centuries later, modern scholars continue to uncover forgotten colonies, abandoned cities, and remnants of civilizations that disappeared during this era.

The modern Core was shaped as much by what was lost during the Dark Age as by what survived it.

Only with the eventual rise of the Great Houses and the formation of the Stellar Conclave would humanity begin its long recovery from the devastation of the Fall.

docs.title: Early History
docs.text:
The period preceding the rise of the Empire is among the least understood eras in human history.

Unlike the Age of Empire, which left behind vast ruins, archives, and surviving institutions, the civilizations that existed before it are known primarily through fragmented legends, scattered archaeological discoveries, and traditions preserved by a handful of surviving cultures. Most records from the period have been lost, destroyed, or rendered impossible to verify.

As a result, historians often disagree on where history ends and mythology begins.

docs.title: The First Kingdoms
docs.text:
The oldest surviving traditions originate within the Starcrest Protectorate.

According to Protectorate histories, the earliest known civilizations were a collection of rival kingdoms scattered across the region of space that would one day become Protectorate territory. These kingdoms fought wars, forged alliances, and established dynasties that endured for centuries.

Little is known about the societies themselves. Surviving accounts describe hereditary monarchies, warrior aristocracies, and noble bloodlines, but provide few concrete details beyond this. Archaeological evidence suggests that humanity had already achieved interstellar travel by this period, though the technology appears to have been far more limited than that used in later eras.

Most notably, there is no evidence that War Mechs existed during this time.

The origins of faster-than-light travel are generally believed to lie somewhere within this era, though exactly how humanity first achieved interstellar travel remains unknown. The engineers, scientists, and civilizations responsible have vanished from history.

docs.title: The Blood of the Titans
docs.text:
Many traditions from the First Kingdoms revolve around a concept known as the Blood of the Titans.

According to Protectorate mythology, the ancient kings claimed descent from colossal beings known as Titans. These figures are variously described as giants, demigods, ancestors, or creators depending on the source. Some legends portray them as physical beings who once walked among humanity, while others treat them as symbolic representations of power, destiny, or divine favor.

The exact meaning of these stories has been debated for centuries.

Modern historians generally regard the Titans as mythological figures, noting the complete absence of reliable evidence for their existence. Nevertheless, belief in the Blood of the Titans remains an important part of Protectorate culture.

House Konnen, rulers of the Starcrest Protectorate, continue to trace their lineage to the ancient kings and are traditionally regarded as bearers of the Blood of the Titans. While few outside the Protectorate take such claims literally, the symbolism remains politically and culturally significant.

docs.title: The Rise of the Imperium
docs.text:
The next major period of recorded history begins with the rise of House Lytherius and the formation of the Imperium.

According to surviving accounts, the invention of the first Mech fundamentally transformed warfare. The technology gave House Lytherius an overwhelming military advantage over its rivals and allowed the fledgling Imperium to expand at an unprecedented rate.

Over the following centuries, the Imperium conquered much of known human space, absorbing rival kingdoms and establishing what was, at the time, the largest political entity in recorded history.

For many generations, the Imperium dominated human civilization.

Though greatly diminished compared to its ancient height, the modern Imperium remains the only major power that can trace its existence directly back to this era.

docs.title: Before the Empire
docs.text:
At some point after the rise of the Imperium, a new power emerged.

The details are unclear. Surviving records become sparse, contradictory, and increasingly difficult to verify. What is generally accepted is that a civilization larger and more powerful than the Imperium eventually conquered the known worlds and established what would later become the Golden Empire.

Who these conquerors were remains unknown.

No reliable accounts identify their place of origin, their leaders, or the circumstances under which they unified human space. By the time detailed historical records begin to appear, the Empire was already firmly established as the dominant power in the galaxy.

As a result, the transition from the age of kingdoms and early empires to the Age of Empire remains one of the largest gaps in recorded history.

docs.title: Humanity's Lost Origins
docs.text:
→ See also: Humanity's Lost Origins

Among all the mysteries of early history, none is greater than the question of humanity's origin.

No world can conclusively claim to be humanity's birthplace.

No surviving archaeological record identifies a first colony, first civilization, or homeworld. Ancient migration records have long since disappeared, and competing traditions place humanity's origins on dozens of different worlds.

Some scholars argue that the evidence was simply lost during the Fall and the Dark Age. Others believe humanity had already spread across so many worlds before reliable record-keeping emerged that identifying a true point of origin may be impossible.

Whatever the answer, it lies beyond the reach of modern history.

Humanity's homeworld, if such a world can still be identified, remains unknown.

docs.title: The Modern Era
docs.text:
The Modern Era refers to the period following the Great Restoration and the establishment of the political institutions that continue to govern the Core today.

It is an age of relative prosperity, expanding trade, and renewed civilization. For centuries, the Core has enjoyed a degree of stability unimaginable during the Dark Age. Interstellar commerce flourishes, major trade routes remain secure, and most citizens live their entire lives without experiencing major warfare.

Yet beneath this stability lies a growing sense of uncertainty.

The institutions that rebuilt civilization following the Fall remain powerful, but many observers question whether they remain strong enough to withstand the challenges of the modern age.

docs.title: The Restoration Order
docs.text:
The Modern Era began with the creation of the Restoration Order, a collection of institutions designed to prevent humanity from ever descending into another Dark Age.

These institutions include:

  • The Star Regency
  • The Stellar Conclave
  • The Great Houses
  • StarCom
  • The Mercenary Guild
  • The restored interstellar trade network

Together, these organizations form the framework through which the Core governs itself.

Though individual Houses maintain considerable autonomy, most major disputes are resolved through diplomacy, economic competition, limited military actions, or the mediation of the Conclave rather than open warfare.

For centuries, this system has succeeded in maintaining peace.

docs.title: The Great Houses
docs.text:
The modern Core is dominated by five Great Houses.

Unlike the countless smaller houses that existed during the Age of Empire, the Great Houses control enormous territories spanning hundreds of worlds and command military forces rivaling those of entire sectors from earlier eras.

Though officially united under the authority of the Stellar Conclave, each House pursues its own interests and ambitions.

The balance between these powers forms the foundation of modern Core politics.

Supporters view the Great Houses as guardians of civilization. Critics argue they are little more than rival kingdoms restrained only by mutual fear and economic necessity.

docs.title: The Long Peace
docs.text:
The centuries following the Restoration are often referred to as the Long Peace.

While conflicts have certainly occurred, no war has approached the scale or devastation of the Fall or the Dark Age. Trade expanded steadily. Population centers grew. Scientific knowledge gradually recovered. New colonies were established. Once-abandoned worlds were resettled.

Many citizens regard this period as the most prosperous era in recorded history since the Empire itself.

Yet historians caution against such comparisons.

The modern Core remains vastly smaller than the Empire, possesses only fragments of its technological knowledge, and lacks many of the institutions that once maintained galactic unity. In many ways, the Core resembles a successful successor state built atop the ruins of a far greater civilization.

docs.title: Technology and Recovery
docs.text:
Much of modern technology traces its origins to discoveries, maintenance traditions, and recovered knowledge inherited from the Age of Empire.

While innovation certainly occurs, many technologies remain poorly understood. Engineers often know how to build, repair, and improve systems without fully understanding the scientific principles behind them.

Ancient facilities, forgotten data archives, and lost technologies remain among the most valuable discoveries in known space.

As a result, exploration and salvage continue to play important roles throughout the Core economy.

Entire fortunes have been built upon the recovery of forgotten knowledge.

docs.title: Mercenaries and Limited War
docs.text:
One of the defining features of the Modern Era is the widespread use of mercenary forces.

The Great Houses frequently compete with one another, but rarely seek direct confrontation. Instead, disputes are often fought through proxy wars, frontier conflicts, private military contracts, and deniable operations.

Mercenary companies fill this role.

Ranging from small independent commands to vast military organizations controlling entire fleets, mercenaries have become an accepted part of the political landscape. In some regions, they are viewed as neutral professionals. In others, they are little more than legalized warlords.

Their influence is so significant that many observers consider the Mercenary Guild to be one of the most important institutions in the Core.

docs.title: The Frontier
docs.text:
Despite centuries of recovery, much of human space remains unsettled.

Beyond the secure trade routes and prosperous Core worlds lie the Frontier, the Fringe, the Badlands, and countless isolated systems.

Some are home to thriving colonies. Others are ruled by pirates, warlords, criminal syndicates, or independent powers. Still others contain forgotten relics of earlier ages.

These regions provide opportunity and danger in equal measure.

Many fortunes are made beyond the borders of civilized space. Many more are lost.

docs.title: Rising Tensions
docs.text:
Although the Modern Era is often viewed as an age of peace, few believe the current balance will last forever.

Competition between the Great Houses continues to intensify. Political rivalries grow sharper. Economic disputes become more frequent. Frontier conflicts have increased in both scale and frequency. New technologies continue to alter the military balance between competing powers.

At the same time, confidence in the institutions of the Restoration Order has begun to weaken.

Some believe the Stellar Conclave has become ineffective. Others argue that StarCom exerts too much influence over interstellar affairs. Still others question whether the Great Houses remain committed to cooperation at all.

Many observers see troubling similarities between the present era and the final centuries of the Empire.

docs.title: A Turning Point
docs.text:
Whether these concerns are justified remains uncertain.

To some, the Core stands on the brink of a new golden age. Trade reaches farther than ever before. Exploration pushes into previously inaccessible regions. Scientific discoveries continue to reshape society.

To others, the warning signs are unmistakable.

The Core has grown wealthy. It has grown powerful. It has grown complacent.

The Empire once stood in the same position.

What followed is remembered as the Fall.

Whether the Modern Era represents the beginning of humanity's next great age—or the final years before another collapse—remains one of the defining questions of the age.

docs.title: The Great Restoration
docs.text:
The Great Restoration was the centuries-long military, political, and economic campaign through which the descendants of the Empire's exiled survivors returned to the Core and reestablished interstellar civilization.

The Restoration ended the Dark Age, established the modern Great Houses, restored major trade networks, and created many of the institutions that continue to govern the Core today.

To most citizens, it represents the rebirth of civilization.

To critics, it represents the moment one ruling elite replaced another.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
According to accepted history, the descendants of the Empire's exiles returned from deep space under the leadership of House Caledon several generations after the Fall. Led by the Star Regent, heir to the bloodline of the Last Emperor, they launched a campaign to reclaim the Core and destroy the remaining forces of the Ophidian Supremacy.

Traditional accounts describe the Restoration as a heroic war of liberation in which the returning exiles freed humanity from generations of Ophidian occupation.

Modern historians are often more skeptical.

While some evidence suggests that Ophidian forces remained active in parts of the Core during this period, surviving records indicate that much of the region was already dominated by independent warlords, isolated states, pirate kingdoms, and competing successor governments. After centuries of warfare, famine, and political fragmentation, few regions possessed the strength necessary to resist a coordinated interstellar campaign.

As a result, many scholars view the Restoration less as a war of liberation and more as a large-scale reconquest of a fractured and exhausted Core.

Regardless of interpretation, the result was the same: for the first time since the Fall, a single political movement successfully reunited much of human space.

docs.title: The Return of the Exiles
docs.text:
The origins of the Restoration lie in the evacuation fleet that escaped the Core during the Fall.

For several generations, the descendants of the refugees lived beyond known space, preserving what remained of the Empire's institutions, military traditions, and historical records. During this period, the office of Star Regent emerged as the governing authority of the exiled population.

By the time the exiles returned, they possessed a unified leadership structure, experienced military forces, and a clear political objective: reclaim the Core and restore civilization.

The exact location of their exile remains unknown.

Many theories exist regarding where the exiles settled during the Dark Age, but no definitive evidence has ever been discovered.

docs.title: The Restoration Wars
docs.text:
The first decades of the Restoration were marked by rapid military expansion.

The returning fleets secured key jump routes, reclaimed strategic worlds, and gradually expanded their influence throughout the Core. In many regions they encountered little resistance. Local populations often welcomed the return of stable government, secure trade routes, and protection from raiders and warlords.

Elsewhere, resistance was fierce.

Warlords, pirate coalitions, rival successor states, and independent rulers fought bitterly to preserve their power. Hundreds of campaigns were fought across the recovering Core as the Restoration forces expanded outward from their initial footholds.

Though later histories often describe the conflict as a united war against the Ophidians, surviving records suggest the reality was far more complex.

docs.title: The Rise of the Great Houses
docs.text:
The Restoration was not accomplished by House Caledon alone.

Several major powers that had survived the Dark Age joined the campaign and became indispensable allies in the rebuilding of civilization.

The Imperium cooperated with the returning forces while simultaneously expanding its own territory and influence. Drawing upon institutions that predated both the Empire and the Dark Age, it emerged from the Restoration as one of the dominant powers of the new order.

The Starcrest Protectorate likewise joined the campaign. Originally founded as a defensive frontier command during the Dark Age, it expanded significantly during the Restoration and incorporated numerous systems into its growing sphere of influence.

The Orion Corporate also participated extensively. Its scientific expertise, industrial capacity, and secure trade networks made it a critical partner in rebuilding interstellar civilization. During the Restoration, it expanded far beyond the handful of worlds that had formed the original Corporate.

In addition to these established powers, Houses Aerin and Payne rose to prominence as loyal collaborators of the Restoration effort. Both houses distinguished themselves through military service and political support for the returning Regent.
