As a reward, they were granted sovereign territories and eventually recognized as Great Houses in their own right.

By the conclusion of the Restoration, the Core had become dominated by a handful of exceptionally large political entities unlike anything seen during either the Empire or Dark Age eras.

docs.title: The Founding of the Union
docs.text:
One of the most consequential decisions of the Restoration involved the vast territories reclaimed by the Regent's vanguard forces.

As the campaign expanded, enormous regions fell under the direct control of military formations loyal to House Caledon. Yet the Regent had little interest in personally ruling such extensive territory. His primary objective remained the restoration of order and the recapture of Citadel, the ancient throne world of the Empire.

Rather than annex these territories directly, the Regent entrusted them to House Hawkins, whose commanders had served as some of the most trusted military leaders of the Restoration.

These worlds were organized into a new political entity known as the Confederate Vanguard Union.

Originally intended as a frontier protectorate and buffer state, the Union gradually evolved into one of the most powerful nations in the Core.

docs.title: The Capture of Citadel
docs.text:
For House Caledon, the symbolic center of the Restoration was always Citadel.

As the ancient throne world of the Empire, its recapture carried immense political significance. Possession of Citadel provided legitimacy to the Restoration and reinforced House Caledon's claim as heir to the Empire's legacy.

Following the capture of the world, the Star Regent formally established a new government dedicated not to restoring the Empire itself, but to preventing another collapse.

The title of Star Emperor was not revived.

Instead, the Regent retained the title of Star Regent, preserving the ancient tradition that the throne of the Last Emperor remained vacant.

docs.title: Mercenaries and the Rebuilding of the Core
docs.text:
Mercenary companies played a crucial role throughout the Restoration.

Many independent military organizations fought alongside Restoration forces, protected trade routes, eliminated pirate strongholds, and assisted in stabilizing newly reclaimed territories.

In many regions, mercenary commands provided security long before permanent governments could be established.

The importance of these forces helped elevate the status of the Mercenary Guild and contributed to the unique role mercenary organizations continue to play throughout the modern Core.

docs.title: The Rise of StarCom
docs.text:
The exact origins of StarCom remain a subject of debate.

Most historians agree that the organization emerged during the later centuries of the Dark Age, though surviving records provide little clarity regarding its founding, leadership, or early development. References to independent navigators, communication networks, exploration groups, and relay stations appear throughout the historical record, but determining which of these organizations were direct predecessors of StarCom is difficult.

By the beginning of the Great Restoration, StarCom already existed in a recognizable form.

Its personnel operated communications relays, maintained navigation data, coordinated long-distance transmissions, and facilitated contact between worlds that had otherwise become isolated from one another. How these capabilities were developed during the chaos of the Dark Age remains unclear.

Several theories have been proposed. Some historians argue that StarCom emerged from cooperative efforts between scientific institutions, merchant organizations, and surviving governments. Others suggest it evolved gradually from a loose collection of independent communications networks that became increasingly interconnected over time. A minority of scholars have proposed that StarCom may preserve organizational traditions that predate even the Restoration itself.

No consensus exists.

What is known is that StarCom played a significant role during the Restoration. As House Caledon and its allies expanded throughout the Core, StarCom assisted in restoring communication infrastructure, mapping navigation routes, and reconnecting isolated systems. These efforts greatly improved coordination between distant regions and accelerated the reestablishment of trade and governance.

Unlike the Great Houses, StarCom did not emerge as a territorial power. Instead, its influence grew through the infrastructure it maintained and the services it provided. As the Restoration progressed, its importance became increasingly difficult to ignore.

By the end of the Restoration, StarCom had been formally recognized as an independent institution responsible for communications, navigation, exploration, and the maintenance of critical interstellar infrastructure throughout the Core.

Today, StarCom remains one of the most influential organizations in human space. Though formally accountable to the broader framework of the Stellar Conclave, its neutrality and technical expertise have allowed it to maintain a unique position within the modern political order.

Many historians argue that while the Great Houses rebuilt governments, StarCom rebuilt the connections between them.

docs.title: The Restoration Settlement
docs.text:
As military conquest gave way to political reconstruction, the leaders of the victorious powers faced a critical challenge: preventing another Dark Age.

The solution was the creation of a new political order based upon cooperation rather than direct imperial rule.

The Stellar Conclave was established to provide a forum for diplomacy and conflict resolution. StarCom was entrusted with maintaining interstellar communications and navigation infrastructure. Trade routes were restored. Borders were formalized. The Great Houses were recognized as sovereign powers within a broader political framework.

Together, these institutions formed the foundation of the modern Core.

docs.title: In Modern Memory
docs.text:
The Great Restoration occupies a unique place in modern history.

House Caledon portrays it as the fulfillment of a sacred duty and the restoration of civilization after centuries of darkness.

The Great Houses emphasize their own contributions to the campaign and often celebrate the Restoration as the moment their nations achieved greatness.

Historians remain divided.

Some view the Restoration as humanity's greatest achievement since the Age of Empire. Others argue that it merely replaced the chaos of the Dark Age with a more stable balance of competing powers.

Few dispute its importance.

Without the Great Restoration, the modern Core would not exist.

docs.title: The Empire
docs.text:
The Age of Empire represents the high-water mark of human civilization.

For nearly two millennia, the Golden Empire united thousands of worlds through trade, administration, military power, and shared institutions. Its influence stretched across known space, connecting distant systems into a single political and economic order. Even centuries after its destruction, the Empire remains the foundation upon which nearly every modern government, culture, and institution in the Core is built.

To many, the Empire is remembered as a golden age of stability, prosperity, and technological achievement. Whether that memory is entirely deserved remains a matter of debate.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Nearly every government in the modern Core claims some degree of legitimacy through a connection to the Empire. Dynasties trace their bloodlines to Empire-era nobility, governments preserve fragments of Empire law, and military traditions often claim descent from Empire-era doctrine. Yet few truly understand the systems that allowed the Empire to function.

The Empire remains the longest-lasting and most influential political entity in recorded history. Built gradually over centuries of expansion and consolidation, it governed a population vastly larger than that of the modern Core and possessed technological and industrial capabilities that modern powers struggle to comprehend.

Reliable records from the era are scarce. What survives consists largely of fragmented archives, damaged data stores, oral traditions, and archaeological discoveries. Even so, surviving evidence suggests that the Empire's military dwarfed all modern forces combined.

Fleet records indicate that Empire naval forces may have operated as many as one hundred BattleStars at their height. By comparison, the modern Core maintains only twelve. More importantly, the industrial infrastructure and scientific expertise required to construct vessels of that scale no longer exist.

Many technologies commonly used today are understood only in part, maintained through inherited procedures and generations of accumulated experience rather than complete scientific understanding. The Empire created wonders that its descendants can repair, replicate, and occasionally improve—but rarely fully explain.

docs.title: Governance
docs.text:
The Golden Empire was ruled by the Star Emperor, who served as the supreme political, military, and administrative authority of the known galaxy. While the Emperor possessed ultimate authority, the Empire itself was not governed as a single centralized state. Instead, it functioned through a vast hierarchy of noble houses, regional governments, and local institutions that administered Empire territory on behalf of the throne.

The Empire encompassed all of what is now known as the Core and is believed to have extended far beyond it. Many worlds currently considered Fringe, or Frontier, or otherwise peripheral territories were likely fully integrated Empire's provinces during its height. The true extent of Empire territory remains unknown, as surviving records from the era are fragmentary and often contradictory.

Beneath the Star Emperor existed a complex network of noble houses that governed regions of Empire space as vassals. These houses controlled planets, systems, and entire sectors while owing loyalty, taxes, military service, and political obedience to the Empires government. Unlike the Great Houses of the modern Core, Empire-era houses were generally smaller in territorial scope and political influence. Power was distributed among a far greater number of noble families, creating a highly layered political structure that balanced local autonomy against the Empire's authority.

Many of these houses disappeared during the Fall and the centuries that followed. Some were destroyed outright, while others were absorbed into larger powers or faded into obscurity. A handful survived and continue to exist in altered forms to this day, though few retain more than fragments of their former influence.

Much of what is known about Empire-era governance originates from records preserved by the Imperium. Of the major powers that exist today, the Imperium is unique in having existed before, during, and after the Age of Empire. Long before the rise of the Star Emperor, the Imperium was itself an independent interstellar empire. At some point during the Empire's early expansion, it conquered and absorbed the Imperium.

Though proud of its ancient heritage, the Imperium ultimately became one vassal among many within the larger Empire. During the Age of Empire, the territory controlled by the Imperium was significantly smaller than the territory it governs today. Nevertheless, its institutions, traditions, and historical archives survived where those of many other houses did not.

This historical continuity has given the Imperium considerable influence over modern understanding of the Empire. As a result, many accepted accounts of Empire-era government, law, and society originate from Imperial records preserved by the Imperium itself, leading some historians to question whether modern interpretations of the Empire may reflect an Imperium-centered perspective.

In many ways, the modern Core represents the opposite of the Empire's political structure. The Empire governed a vast territory through hundreds of noble houses of varying size and influence. The modern Core, by contrast, is dominated by only a handful of Great Houses, each controlling territories and resources that would have rivaled entire sectors of the old Empire. While the modern houses are fewer in number, they are individually far larger and more powerful than most of their Empire-era predecessors.

Indeed, had the Great Houses exited in the Empires-era, they would each be considered super powers. The political fragmentation that followed the Fall did not create smaller states—it created fewer, larger ones.

docs.title: The Fall
docs.text:
For all its apparent permanence, the Empire ultimately fell.

According to accepted history, the Empire was invaded and destroyed by a mysterious power known as the Ophidian Supremacy sometime around 4000 AE. The details of this conflict remain among the greatest mysteries in human history.

What little evidence survives presents a troubling contradiction. Historical accounts describe the Supremacy as a force capable of defeating the largest military civilization humanity had ever produced. Yet virtually no evidence exists of a civilization large enough to accomplish such a feat. No vast chain of conquered worlds marks their advance. No major archaeological sites can be definitively linked to them. No surviving fleet wrecks, cities, or industrial centers have been identified with certainty.

Most surviving accounts agree on one point: the Empire's collapse was shockingly rapid.

The Empire had endured for centuries and possessed military forces that were widely regarded as invincible. Its famed knights, BattleStar fleets, and vast administrative institutions had maintained order across thousands of worlds for generations. Yet in the face of the Ophidian invasion, these seemingly eternal structures collapsed with alarming speed. Entire sectors disappeared from communication. Fleets vanished. Worlds were abandoned. Within a relatively short period of time, the Empire ceased to function as a unified state.

Exactly how the Supremacy achieved this remains unknown.

The official historical narrative holds that the Supremacy occupied much of the Core for several generations before eventually being defeated by Empire forces operating from exile. These returning exiles would later restore order, establish the Stellar Conclave, and lay the foundations of the modern political order.

Yet even this account raises uncomfortable questions.

If the Supremacy truly ruled the Core for generations, why have so few traces of their civilization been discovered? Why do surviving records from the period contain so many contradictions? And how could a civilization capable of conquering humanity's greatest empire vanish so completely?

docs.title: The Last True Emperor
docs.text:
One of the few events from the Fall that remains broadly consistent across surviving historical traditions is the death of the Last True Emperor.

As the Empire collapsed, a massive evacuation fleet is said to have assembled and fled beyond the Core. Carrying refugees, soldiers, government officials, and surviving noble houses, the fleet represented the last hope of preserving what remained of the Empire.

Beyond these broad details, the historical record becomes uncertain.

The most popular tradition holds that the Emperor personally led the Empire's remaining military forces in a final battle against the pursuing Ophidian Supremacy. According to these accounts, he sacrificed himself to buy time for the evacuation fleet to escape into deep space. This event is remembered in countless stories, paintings, poems, and historical accounts as the Empire's final act of defiance.

Other traditions tell a darker story.

Some accounts suggest the Emperor was betrayed by rival nobles during the chaos of the Fall. Others claim he was assassinated by political enemies seeking to secure their own survival as the Empire collapsed around them. A handful of records even suggest he may have died before the evacuation fleet departed, with later generations inventing the story of a heroic last stand to provide a more inspiring ending to the Age of Empire.

Whatever the truth, all surviving accounts agree on one point: the Emperor died during the Fall.

His death marked more than the end of a ruler. It marked the end of the Empire itself.

With the death of the Last Emperor, the title of Star Emperor also passed from history. No successor was ever crowned. No claimant was ever recognized. The throne was left vacant.

The evacuation fleet survived and disappeared beyond the Core into deep space. For several generations, the descendants of the surviving houses lived in exile, preserving what remained of the Empire's institutions while humanity endured the Dark Age.

It was during this exile that the title of Star Regent first emerged.

Rather than proclaiming a new Emperor, House Caledon's heirs chose to rule as regents, governing in his absence rather than claiming his authority for themselves. Officially, the Star Regent was never intended to replace the Emperor, but merely to safeguard the throne until it could be restored.

When the exiles eventually returned to the Core during the Great Restoration, they retained this tradition. The restored government would be led by a Star Regent rather than an Emperor, a practice that continues to the present day.

Few seriously believe the Last Emperor will ever return. Yet the symbolism remains important. The Star Regent does not sit upon the throne as Emperor, but beside it as its guardian.

In this way, the title preserves one of the oldest traditions in the Core: the belief that the Age of Empire ended with the death of the Last Emperor, and that no ruler since has possessed the right to claim his crown.

docs.title: Historical Debate
docs.text:
Scholars continue to debate the true nature of the Ophidian Supremacy.

The orthodox position maintains that the Supremacy was a rival interstellar civilization that invaded human space during the Fall of the Empire. According to this view, the subsequent defeat and collapse of the Supremacy erased much of its legacy, leaving only fragmentary records, archaeological evidence, and conflicting historical accounts. Most universities, governments, and official historical institutions support some variation of this interpretation.

Others remain unconvinced.

Alternative theories range from the plausible to the fantastical. Some suggest the Supremacy was not a single civilization but a coalition of hostile powers operating under a common banner. Others argue that the Empire was already suffering from severe internal problems, including political fragmentation, economic decline, regional separatism, and civil conflict. In this interpretation, the Ophidian Supremacy may represent only one factor among many that contributed to the Empire's collapse.

A smaller group of historians has proposed that the Supremacy was never a unified enemy at all. They argue that later generations transformed a complex period of internal collapse into the simpler narrative of a great external invasion. According to this theory, the "Ophidian Supremacy" may have become a historical metaphor for the forces that destroyed the Empire rather than a single identifiable civilization.

More fringe theories claim the Supremacy was non-human in origin, arrived from beyond known space, or never existed in any form whatsoever.

No theory has succeeded in explaining all available evidence, and the true nature of the Ophidian Supremacy remains one of the greatest unresolved questions in human history.

docs.title: Legacy
docs.text:
Though centuries have passed since its fall, the Empire continues to shape every aspect of life in the modern Core.

Ancient Empire infrastructure still forms the backbone of interstellar commerce. Lost Empire-era technologies remain among the most valuable treasures in known space. Great Houses derive prestige from claimed Empire ancestry. Entire industries are dedicated to recovering and studying Empire-era relics.

For many citizens, the Empire represents humanity's greatest achievement. For others, it is a warning.

The Empire was larger, wealthier, more technologically advanced, and militarily stronger than anything that exists today. Yet it still fell.

Whatever destroyed it remains unknown.

docs.title: Major Powers in the Core
docs.text:
“The Core does not survive through unity. The Core survives because the Houses fear open war more than they desire victory.” — StarCom political analysis archive

The modern Core Worlds are dominated by five Great Houses operating beneath the authority of the Star Regency. Though united under the Stellar Conclave, the Houses remain divided by ideology, culture, history, and competing visions for humanity’s future.

Alongside these powers stand major interstellar institutions whose influence rivals that of the Great Houses themselves.

docs.title: The Star Regency
docs.text:
"Rule Above All."

The central governing authority of the Core Worlds under House Caledon. The Regency exists to preserve stability and prevent open war between the Great Houses.

Beneath the Regency operates the Stellar Conclave, a permanent political assembly composed of representatives from the Great Houses and major interstellar institutions. The Conclave serves as the primary forum through which the powers of the Core negotiate disputes, coordinate policy, establish common law, and manage matters affecting civilization as a whole.

Though disagreements within the Conclave are common, it remains one of the most important institutions in human space and serves as the foundation of the modern Core political order.

docs.title: StarCom
docs.text:
"To Know the Future."

The neutral communications and navigation authority that maintains the infrastructure of interstellar civilization itself. Without StarCom, the modern Core could not function.

docs.title: The Great Houses
docs.text:
The five Great Houses are the dominant political powers of the modern Core. Each governs vast territories, maintains its own military forces, controls its own economy, and holds significant influence within the Stellar Conclave. Together they form the backbone of interstellar civilization.

While all five Houses formally recognize the authority of the Star Regent and participate in the Stellar Conclave, each largely governs its own affairs. Their competing interests, rivalries, and ambitions shape nearly every major political, economic, and military development throughout the Core.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
"Forged From Victory."

A militarized industrial superpower built upon discipline, sacrifice, and overwhelming mechanized warfare. The Union believes civilization survives only through strength and unity.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
"Wealth is Power."

A wealthy aristocratic state dominated by diplomacy, administration, finance, and political influence. The Sovereignty views itself as the civilized center of the modern Core.

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
"Fierce Beauty."

The oldest surviving civilization in known space. The Imperium combines martial tradition, artistic excellence, and ancient cultural identity beneath the rule of House Lytherius.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
"Smoke and Steel."

A harsh frontier military state forged through survival during the Collapse. The Protectorate values endurance, sacrifice, discipline, and defensive warfare above conquest or luxury.

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text:
"For Futures Yet To Come."

The technological powerhouse of the Core. Orion Corporate believes civilization survives only through scientific advancement, rational governance, and continued progress.

docs.title: The Core Political Order
docs.text:
The modern Core exists as a delicate balance of competing powers rather than a single unified state. While the Star Regent and the Stellar Conclave provide a common framework for governance, no single institution possesses the authority to completely dominate the Great Houses.

The Great Houses maintain their own governments, economies, militaries, territories, and foreign policies. While they acknowledge the authority of the Regency and participate in the Conclave, they frequently disagree with one another and compete for influence throughout both the Core and frontier regions.

This balance is reinforced by several powerful institutions. StarCom maintains the communications, navigation, and logistical infrastructure that allows interstellar civilization to function. The Mercenary Guild provides a neutral framework through which independent military companies operate. Together, these organizations help prevent any single House from acquiring overwhelming influence over the others.

Military power ultimately remains the foundation upon which this system rests. Every Great House fields vast armies of mechs supported by fleets of warships and ancient Battlestars. These forces are powerful enough that a major war between the Houses could devastate the Core itself. As a result, open conflict is rare, not because the Houses trust one another, but because the cost of total war is considered unacceptable by nearly every major power.

The result is a political order built upon deterrence, negotiation, and carefully managed rivalry. Alliances shift, tensions rise and fall, and proxy conflicts erupt across the frontier, yet the institutions of the Core continue to endure because no faction can easily afford to destroy the system upon which they all depend.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
“We are not soldiers. We are conquerors.” — Ryder Hawkins

State propaganda illustration depicting Victor Hawkins (center) and his children, Maximus (left) and Ryder (right) Hawkins, standing before the military strength of the Confederate Vanguard Union. Images of this kind are commonly displayed throughout Confederate space to symbolize unity, strength, and reverence for House Hawkins.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Government Type Military State Capital Starhold Leadership Confederate Military Council Population 16.8 billion Primary Strength Industry and Mechanized Warfare Military Specialty Heavy Mech Assault National Motto “Forged From Victory.”

The Confederate Vanguard Union, commonly referred to simply as the Union, is one of the five dominant powers of the Core Worlds.

The Union is widely regarded as the most militarized state in the Core and possesses the largest standing mech force among the Great Houses. Its society is heavily shaped by military service, industrial labor, and state-directed nationalism.

Union culture emphasizes strength, sacrifice, discipline, and collective identity above individual ambition. Service to the state is regarded not merely as duty, but as the foundation of civilization itself.

While critics often describe the Union as authoritarian and expansionist, supporters argue that the Confederates possess the resolve and industrial capacity necessary to preserve humanity against the growing instability of the frontier.

More than any other Great House, the Union derives its legitimacy from military victory. Unlike powers founded upon bloodlines, commerce, technology, or tradition, the Union was born from conquest and restoration. To Confederates, the state itself stands as proof that victory shapes history.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of the Confederate Vanguard Union trace back to the collapse of the Old Empire and the dark age that followed. During the occupation of the Core by the Ophidian Supremacy, remnants of the Empire's Vanguard military forces escaped into exile, carrying with them the traditions, discipline, and organizational structure of the old regime.

For generations these exiled forces survived beyond the Core, preserving their military heritage while waiting for an opportunity to reclaim their homeland. That opportunity finally arrived during the Great Restoration, when the Vanguard returned and helped lead the campaigns that shattered the Ophidian occupation and restored human rule to the Core.

In recognition of their service, the newly established Star Regent granted vast territories to the victorious Vanguard armies. From these lands emerged the Confederate Vanguard Union.

This origin continues to shape Confederate identity. Unlike other powers that trace their legitimacy to ancient bloodlines, economic success, or political institutions, the Union traces its existence to a military victory. The state quite literally owes its existence to those who fought and won a war.

This belief lies at the heart of the Union's national philosophy. The Confederate motto, "Forged From Victory," is not merely a slogan. It is a statement of historical fact. To the Union, victory is not simply desirable—it is the force through which nations, civilizations, and destinies are created.

docs.title: Starhold
docs.text:
Starhold is the capital world of the Confederate Vanguard Union and the beating heart of its military-industrial machine. Vast foundries, fortress-cities, mech assembly complexes, and orbital shipyards dominate much of the planet's landscape, producing the weapons, vehicles, and war machines that sustain the Union's military power.

The world itself is one of extremes. Its northern hemisphere is covered in frozen tundra and glacial plains, while much of the southern hemisphere consists of harsh deserts and rocky badlands. Despite these conditions, Starhold supports a massive population through extensive industrial infrastructure and carefully managed urban arcologies.

More than a capital, Starhold serves as a symbol of Confederate identity. It is home to the Confederate Military Council, the Honor Guard, numerous military academies, and some of the largest training facilities in the Core. To many Union citizens, Starhold represents the ideal toward which all Confederates should strive: strong, disciplined, productive, and prepared.

docs.title: House Hawkins
docs.text:
The Confederate Vanguard Union is ruled by House Hawkins, a family whose history is inseparable from the military traditions of the Union itself. For generations, members of the house have served as officers, generals, commanders, and political leaders, helping shape the state into the most militarized power in the Core.

The current ruler, High Minister Victor Hawkins, is widely regarded as one of the most capable strategists in the Core. Quiet, calculating, and intensely disciplined, Victor is known for approaching both warfare and politics with the same deliberate mindset. Though a formidable warrior in his own right, his true strength lies in long-term planning and strategic maneuvering. Victor learned from an early age that battles are won not only on the battlefield but also through diplomacy, intelligence, economics, and political influence. Supporters view him as a harsh but fair leader whose foresight has strengthened the Union. Critics often regard him as manipulative and difficult to read. His involvement in supporting elements of the Free Suns Rebellion within the Imperium remains one of the most debated episodes of his career, with many believing he quietly sought to weaken a rival power without drawing the Union directly into conflict.

Despite his reputation for pragmatism, Victor possesses a deeply personal side rarely seen in public. His wife was widely regarded as the great love of his life, and following her death he never remarried nor took another lover. To many within the Union, this quiet loyalty stands in contrast to the ruthless image often projected by his political opponents.

Victor's designated heir is his daughter, Ryder Hawkins. A gifted mech pilot, accomplished battlefield commander, and natural warrior, Ryder embodies many of the qualities Confederate society admires most. She is fearless, charismatic, and possesses an instinctive understanding of combat that has earned her numerous victories. Yet Ryder's greatest strengths are also often viewed as her greatest weaknesses. Impulsive, hedonistic, and frequently dismissive of convention, she is notorious for drinking with enlisted soldiers, fraternizing with troops, and disregarding the social expectations normally associated with high-ranking nobility. Her aggressive tendencies extend to the battlefield, where she has occasionally taken risks that more cautious commanders consider reckless. While her skill has thus far allowed her to survive such decisions, many within the Union quietly question whether she possesses the discipline necessary to someday lead the state. Ryder herself is aware of her reputation and is known to joke that she has only ever been good at two things: fighting and fraternizing.

Her brother, Maximus Hawkins, presents a stark contrast. While not considered an exceptional mech pilot, Maximus is widely respected as a capable commander and dependable leader. Careful, measured, and deeply professional, he follows both military regulations and chains of command with a consistency that has earned the trust of his soldiers. Unlike his sister, Maximus has little personal ambition for higher office. He serves because he believes it is his duty, not because he seeks power. Many officers privately view him as the steadier of the two siblings, though even they acknowledge that he lacks Ryder's natural charisma and battlefield brilliance.

Together, Victor, Ryder, and Maximus represent three different expressions of the values that define the Union: strategic vision, martial excellence, and dutiful service. Throughout Confederate space, House Hawkins is often portrayed as the embodiment of Union ideals—strength, sacrifice, discipline, and an unwavering commitment to victory.

docs.title: Government
docs.text:
The Union is governed through a centralized military-administrative system dominated by the Confederate Military Council. While House Hawkins occupies the highest position within the state, governance is distributed among senior military officers, industrial directors, regional governors, and state administrators.

Unlike many powers of the Core, the Union does not view military service solely as a means of national defense. It views service as one of the most effective ways to develop leadership, discipline, responsibility, and practical decision-making. As a result, veterans occupy many of the highest positions throughout Confederate society, and military experience is widely regarded as one of the strongest indicators of competence.

This belief extends beyond the armed forces themselves. Industrial managers, planetary governors, infrastructure planners, and political leaders often possess military backgrounds, creating a government culture that emphasizes organization, accountability, and results.

Supporters argue that this system produces leaders capable of making difficult decisions under pressure and prioritizing the needs of society above personal interests. Critics contend that it concentrates excessive power within military institutions and encourages a narrow view of governance.

The Union rejects such criticism. Confederate leaders maintain that civilization survives only because capable individuals are willing to accept responsibility for defending and managing it. To the Union, leadership is not a privilege—it is a duty earned through service.

docs.title: Society and Culture
docs.text:
Confederate society is built upon the belief that civilization survives through strength, sacrifice, and collective effort. Military service is regarded as one of the highest callings available to a citizen, but soldiers are not the only figures celebrated within Union culture. Industrial workers, engineers, miners, logistics specialists, and manufacturers are all viewed as essential contributors to the state's success.

The Union places enormous emphasis on discipline, duty, and personal responsibility. Citizens are taught that rights are inseparable from obligations and that every individual owes a debt to the society that protects them. This philosophy has produced a population that is highly patriotic, intensely resilient, and often willing to accept sacrifices that citizens of other powers would consider unreasonable.

Public life is saturated with military symbolism and historical remembrance. Monuments, museums, military academies, and memorials can be found throughout Confederate territory. State media frequently celebrates military victories, industrial achievements, and examples of personal sacrifice in service to the Union.

Critics often describe Confederate society as authoritarian and excessively nationalistic. Confederate citizens generally respond that freedom without security is meaningless, and that the prosperity enjoyed throughout the Core was built by those willing to fight for it.

docs.title: Military
docs.text:
The Confederate Vanguard Union fields the largest military force in the Core and is widely regarded as one of its most dangerous. Descended from the Vanguard armies that helped reclaim the Core during the Great Restoration, the Union continues to view military excellence as both a practical necessity and a defining element of national identity.

Unlike many rival powers that specialize in particular forms of warfare, the Union favors balanced and versatile forces capable of operating effectively under almost any battlefield conditions. Union mechs are rarely the fastest, the heaviest, or the most technologically advanced machines in the Core. Instead, they are designed to be dependable, durable, and effective across a wide range of combat scenarios.

Union engineers place particular emphasis on battlefield endurance. Advanced cooling systems allow Confederate mechs to sustain combat operations longer than many rival designs, while standardized components simplify maintenance and repair. Combined with the Union's immense industrial capacity and logistical expertise, this allows Confederate forces to maintain relentless battlefield pressure during extended campaigns.

The Union's true strength, however, lies in large-scale military coordination. Confederate commanders are renowned throughout the Core for their ability to manage complex operations involving thousands of mechs, vehicles, aircraft, and support personnel. Throughout the modern era, the Union has frequently been called upon to coordinate large-scale military operations for the Conclave, earning a reputation as the de facto generals of the Core.

Confederate doctrine favors decisive engagements, overwhelming force, and sustained offensive momentum. When the Union commits to war, it seeks victory through superior organization, logistics, and battlefield endurance rather than technological gimmicks or elaborate maneuvering.

docs.title: The Battlestars
docs.text:
Battlestars are immense vessels are among the oldest and most valuable military assets still operating in human space, relics of earlier ages whose construction techniques have long since been lost.

Far more than simple warships, Battlestars function as self-contained military cities. Each carries vast populations of soldiers, technicians, industrial workers, support personnel, and civilians. They possess their own manufacturing facilities, food production systems, hospitals, command centers, and logistical infrastructure, allowing them to sustain military operations for extended periods far from friendly territory.

While most Great Houses possess only two operational Battlestars, the Union controls three: The Indomitable, The Wrath Bringer, and The Justice. Together, these colossal vessels can transport tens of thousands of personnel and thousands of mechs, giving the Union unmatched strategic mobility and force projection capabilities.

The Battlestars are deeply woven into Confederate identity. They are viewed not merely as military assets but as symbols of the Union's heritage as the descendants of the Vanguard armies that helped reclaim the Core. Their silhouettes appear throughout Confederate propaganda, military ceremonies, and public monuments as reminders of the sacrifices that forged the Union.

Many military historians argue that the Union's possession of three operational Battlestars remains one of the greatest strategic advantages enjoyed by any Great House in the modern era.

docs.title: Frontier Policy
docs.text:
The Union maintains one of the most active military presences beyond the borders of the Core. Confederate leaders believe that threats ignored on the frontier inevitably become threats within civilized space, and therefore advocate confronting instability before it can spread.

This philosophy places Union forces at the forefront of anti-piracy campaigns, frontier security operations, territorial disputes, and military interventions throughout the outer regions of known space. Confederate commanders often argue that many of the dangers facing the Core are addressed only because the Union is willing to commit forces where others hesitate.

Critics frequently accuse the Union of using security concerns as a justification for expanding its influence. Confederate leaders counter that history repeatedly demonstrates the dangers of complacency. In their view, civilizations do not collapse because they confront threats too early. They collapse because they wait too long.

As a result, the Union often favors proactive action over cautious observation, a philosophy that has earned it both loyal allies and wary rivals throughout the Core.

docs.title: Politics and Foreign Relations
docs.text:
The Union occupies a unique position within the Core. As the largest military power among the Great Houses and the direct successor to the Vanguard forces that reclaimed the Core during the Great Restoration, it views itself as both a guardian of civilization and the ultimate guarantor of regional stability.

Relations with the Helios Sovereignty are often tense. Union leaders frequently criticize the Sovereignty's aristocratic culture, political maneuvering, and concentration of wealth, viewing many of its elites as disconnected from the realities faced by ordinary citizens. The Sovereignty, in turn, often regards the Union as overly militaristic and prone to solving political problems through force. Despite these differences, the two powers remain important economic and political partners within the Conclave.

Relations with the Starcrest Protectorate fluctuate between mutual respect and rivalry. Both states value discipline, military service, and personal sacrifice, and their soldiers generally respect one another's capabilities. At the same time, competition for influence along shared frontier regions and differing approaches to governance occasionally place the two powers at odds.

The Union's relationship with Orion Corporate is defined by both cooperation and suspicion. Union leaders admire the Corporate's industrial output and technological achievements, yet remain wary of its growing influence over the Core's scientific, economic, and technological infrastructure. Many Confederates believe the Corporate places too much faith in technology and not enough in the people who ultimately wield it.

Relations with the Omnisphere Imperium are similarly complicated. Both powers possess proud military traditions and view themselves as heirs to humanity's greatest achievements. This shared self-confidence has historically produced both cooperation and rivalry, with neither state particularly eager to concede military prestige to the other.

Despite its fiercely independent character, the Union remains loyal to the Star Regent and the institutions of the Stellar Conclave. However, Confederate leaders have never been shy about voicing their disagreements with Regency policy, and many regard the Union as one of the few powers willing to openly challenge the Crown when they believe the security of the Core is at stake.

docs.title: Mercenary Relations
docs.text:
The Union is one of the largest employers of mercenary companies in the Core and generally maintains positive relations with independent military contractors.

Mercenaries often find Confederate military culture highly competitive, but they are usually treated as valued auxiliaries rather than expendable outsiders. Union commanders respect competence and battlefield performance above nearly all other qualities, and successful mercenary units can earn considerable prestige through Confederate service.

While Union contracts rarely offer exceptional pay or salvage rights, they frequently provide outstanding logistical support. Mercenary companies operating alongside Confederate forces often receive access to transportation assets, repair facilities, supply networks, and deployment allowances unavailable elsewhere in the Core. As a result, contractors are frequently able to field larger and heavier forces than they could under similar contracts from rival powers.

Many mercenaries describe Union contracts as practical rather than lucrative. A company is unlikely to become wealthy serving the Confederates, but it will rarely find itself lacking ammunition, spare parts, or support.

As one common mercenary saying goes:

"The Union won't make you rich. It'll make sure you win."

docs.title: The Vanguard Legacy
docs.text:
More than any other Great House, the Union defines itself through its history.

Confederates trace their origins to the Vanguard armies that survived exile, reclaimed the Core, and helped restore civilization following the Ophidian occupation. While centuries have passed since those events, their memory remains central to Confederate identity.

Throughout Union territory, monuments, museums, military academies, and public ceremonies commemorate the Vanguard and their sacrifices. Children are taught their history from an early age, and many citizens view themselves as inheritors of a legacy earned through blood and hardship.

To the Union, the Vanguard are not simply historical figures. They are proof that civilization survives only when ordinary men and women are willing to fight for it.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
The Union believes that peace is preserved through strength, not goodwill. While many powers of the Core continue to enjoy centuries of relative stability, Confederate leaders increasingly warn that such stability should not be mistaken for permanence.

From the Union's perspective, the history of humanity is defined by cycles of conflict, collapse, and recovery. The lessons of the Collapse, the Ophidian occupation, and countless frontier wars all point toward the same conclusion: threats rarely announce themselves until it is too late to prepare for them.

As a result, the Union continues to expand its industrial capacity, modernize its armed forces, and strengthen its frontier defenses. Critics view these efforts as evidence of militarism and expansionist ambition. Confederate leaders counter that preparedness is not aggression, and that the cost of readiness is far lower than the cost of being caught unprepared.

Many within the Union believe another great crisis will eventually confront the Core. Whether that challenge emerges from the frontier, from within the Conclave, or from beyond known space matters little. In the Confederate view, civilization survives only when strong men and women are willing to defend it.

The Union's answer to an uncertain future is simple: when the next war comes, be ready to win.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
“Strength wins battles. Wealth wins wars.” — Arthur Payne

Archon Alissa Aerin and Lord Arthur Payne standing united at the center of the Helios Sovereignty, surrounded by the heirs and bloodlines of Houses Aerin and Payne. From left to right stand Archer Payne, Torrin Payne, Alexander Payne, Alissa Aerin, Arthur Payne, Elara Aerin, Lex Aerin, Ravin Aerin, and Aurora Aerin, with the towering war machines of the Sovereignty looming behind them.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Government Type Aristocratic Sovereignty Ruling Houses House Aerin and House Payne Capital World Lumina Population 11.7 billion Primary Strength Commerce, administration, and diplomacy :material-lion: Common Nickname The Lions House Words “Wealth is Power.”

The Helios Sovereignty is the wealthiest state in the Core. Ruled from the radiant world of Lumina by the cousin houses Aerin and Payne, the Sovereignty exerts influence across nearly every major institution of civilized space. Its merchants finance wars, its administrators govern vast territories, and its diplomats shape policy throughout the Conclave.

Supporters view the Sovereignty as the foundation upon which modern civilization rests. Critics see a decadent aristocracy insulated from the hardships faced by frontier worlds. Both agree on one fact: few powers wield greater influence than the Sovereignty.

With nearly nineteen billion citizens, immense industrial wealth, and one of the most advanced militaries in known space, the Sovereignty remains a central pillar of the modern Core.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Unlike the Union, Protectorate, or Imperium, the Helios Sovereignty was not forged primarily through conquest. Its power emerged from administration, commerce, and reconstruction.

In the aftermath of the Great Restoration, House Caledon and the Vanguard armies reclaimed vast regions of the Core. Military victories alone, however, could not rebuild civilization. Trade routes had to be restored. Communications networks repaired. Governments established. Economic systems stabilized. The countless worlds reconnected by the Restoration needed administrators as desperately as they needed soldiers.

These responsibilities increasingly fell upon the merchants, financiers, navigators, and bureaucratic institutions that would eventually form the Helios Sovereignty.

House Caledon relied heavily upon these organizations to coordinate reconstruction throughout the recovering Core. They restored commerce between isolated worlds, re-established safe travel along major jump routes, rebuilt economic systems shattered during the Dark Age, and helped construct many of the administrative institutions that continue to govern interstellar civilization today.

Through this process, the ruling Houses Aerin and Payne accumulated enormous wealth, influence, and political authority. What began as a practical coalition of merchants, navigators, and administrators gradually evolved into one of the most powerful governments in human space.

Over generations, many of the families responsible for rebuilding civilization transformed into a new aristocracy. Administrators became nobles. Merchant dynasties became ruling houses. Wealth, influence, education, and political sophistication became the defining virtues of Sovereignty culture.

Supporters argue that the Sovereignty earned its position through centuries of competent governance and economic stewardship. Critics contend that it has drifted from its original reconstruction ideals, becoming increasingly elitist, aristocratic, and detached from the realities faced by ordinary citizens.

Nevertheless, few dispute the Sovereignty's historical importance. Many of the institutions that underpin modern civilization—from trade networks and financial systems to interstellar administration itself—owe their existence in part to the efforts of the organizations that would become the Helios Sovereignty.

docs.title: Houses Aerin and Payne
docs.text:
The Helios Sovereignty is governed through a unique partnership between House Aerin and House Payne, two ancient cousin houses whose relationship has shaped the Sovereignty for centuries.

House Aerin traditionally oversees the civilian government and holds the title of Archon, making it the formal head of state. House Payne commands the military and bears the title of Archduke, serving as protector of the Sovereignty and commander of its armed forces. Together, the two families form the foundation upon which Sovereignty power rests.

docs.title: House Aerin
docs.text:
House Aerin serves as the political and administrative heart of the Sovereignty. Throughout its history, the house has produced diplomats, financiers, governors, and statesmen who helped transform the Sovereignty into one of the wealthiest and most influential powers in the Core.

The current Archon is Alissa Aerin, one of the most respected political leaders of her generation. Beneath her gracious demeanor lies a formidable strategist whose skill in diplomacy and governance has preserved the Sovereignty influence throughout the Core. Many who initially mistake her kindness for weakness eventually discover otherwise.

House Aerin is mildly matriarchal, and female rulers have historically been more common than male ones. Archons are often women, though men have occasionally held the title. Members of the family are frequently recognizable by their fair hair and refined appearance.

Alissa's eldest daughter, Elara Aerin, is heir to the Archonate and is widely viewed as possessing the intelligence and capability required to one day lead the Sovereignty. Her brother Ravin has established himself as a talented diplomat and administrator, while Lex Aerin continues to search for her place within the family, often frustrating her relatives through impulsive decisions despite her sincere desire to contribute. The youngest sibling, Aurora, shows little interest in politics and instead prefers the social life of the Sovereignty high society.

docs.title: House Payne
docs.text:
Where House Aerin governs, House Payne commands.

For centuries the Paynes have served as military leaders, generals, admirals, and defenders of the Sovereignty. Their influence permeates every level of military culture, from the highest strategic councils to the officer academies that train future commanders.

The current Archduke, Arthur Payne, is a stern and uncompromising reformer. When he inherited leadership of the house, the military was suffering through a prolonged period of decline. Generations of nepotism, favoritism, and aristocratic privilege had left many commands in the hands of officers whose wealth, family name, or political connections mattered more than their competence. While the armed forces retained their advanced equipment and prestigious reputation, many observers believed their effectiveness had steadily eroded.

Upon assuming power, Arthur launched an aggressive campaign of reform. Noble officers who had long treated commands as hereditary privileges found themselves removed, promotion standards were tightened, and military professionalism was elevated above social standing. The reforms earned Arthur numerous enemies among the aristocracy, many of whom viewed his actions as an attack on longstanding traditions. Others regarded them as long overdue.

Though favoritism and political influence have not been entirely eliminated, Arthur's reforms are widely credited with reversing decades of institutional decay. Supporters view him as the architect of the military's resurgence, while critics see him as a ruthless authoritarian. Both sides generally agree on one point: the Sovereignty's armed forces are considerably stronger today than they were when he inherited them.

House Payne traditionally favors male rulers, though several notable women have led the house throughout its history. Members of the family are commonly dark-haired and are often associated with military service, discipline, and duty.

Arthur's eldest son, Alexander Payne, is widely viewed as his father's political and military heir. Severe, disciplined, and uncompromising, Alexander commands fierce loyalty from those who serve beneath him. His brother Torrin Payne possesses little interest in warfare and instead excels as an intellectual and political thinker. The youngest son, Archer Payne, is known for his unusual willingness to associate with common soldiers and frontline troops, a trait that frequently frustrates both Arthur and Alexander.

docs.title: The Partnership
docs.text:
Although House Aerin technically outranks House Payne, the relationship between the two families is best understood as a partnership rather than a hierarchy.

Archons frequently defer to the judgment of the Archduke in military matters, while the Archdukes generally respect the authority and legitimacy of the civilian government. Disagreements occur, but open conflict between the two houses is exceedingly rare. Both understand that the prosperity of the Sovereignty depends upon cooperation between Sovereignty wealth and military power.

Throughout the Core, Houses Aerin and Payne are often viewed as embodying the two halves of the Sovereignty itself: prosperity and strength, diplomacy and war, the pen and the sword.

docs.title: Government
docs.text:
The Sovereignty is governed through a layered network of noble houses, economic authorities, ministerial offices, and administrative institutions. While local elections and representative bodies exist in limited forms, real power remains concentrated among the aristocracy and senior bureaucratic families.

Unlike many states of the Core, the Helios Sovereignty views governance as a profession. Civil servants, diplomats, economists, and administrators enjoy enormous prestige, and political influence is often measured as much by one's ability to navigate institutions as by military accomplishments.

This emphasis on administration has produced one of the most stable governments in the Core, though critics argue it has also created an increasingly detached and self-serving elite.

docs.title: The Voice of the Regent
docs.text:
Few institutions better illustrate the Helios Sovereignty influence than the office of Voice of the Regent.

For generations, members of Houses Aerin and Payne have frequently occupied this position, acting as chief advisors, administrators, and representatives of the Star Regent. While the title grants no formal authority over the Great Houses, it provides unparalleled access to the political machinery of the Core.

Critics often claim that Sovereignty influence within the Regency borders on excessive. Sovereignty politicians respond that the stability of the Core depends upon competent administration, and few houses can match the Sovereignty's expertise in that regard.

docs.title: Lumina
docs.text:
Lumina is the capital of the Helios Sovereignty and among the most prosperous worlds in the Core. Home to sprawling arcologies, towering financial districts, prestigious universities, and estates belonging to some of the wealthiest families in human space, Lumina embodies both the achievements and excesses of Sovereignty civilization.

The world serves as the economic heart of the Sovereignty. Trade agreements affecting billions are negotiated within its halls, and entire sectors of the economy can rise or fall based upon decisions made in its ministries and boardrooms.

To its admirers, Lumina is the pinnacle of civilization. To its critics, it is a monument to aristocratic excess.

docs.title: Society and Culture
docs.text:
Sovereignty culture prizes refinement, education, wealth, and social standing. Success is expected to be displayed, not hidden. Noble galas, formal ceremonies, political salons, and economic summits dominate the social calendar of the upper classes.

Status within Sovereignty society is measured not merely through military victories or political titles, but through influence, education, connections, and personal achievement. Families invest heavily in elite schooling, etiquette, and public reputation.

Among the upper classes, displays of wealth are not merely tolerated but expected. Noble families compete through architectural projects, charitable foundations, artistic patronage, lavish celebrations, and public works intended to demonstrate both prosperity and prestige. Critics frequently describe this culture as extravagant and self-indulgent, while supporters argue that such competition has helped make the Sovereignty the cultural center of the Core.

This emphasis on prestige has produced some of the most accomplished diplomats, economists, and administrators in the Core. It has also fostered accusations of elitism, vanity, and growing separation between the ruling classes and ordinary citizens.

docs.title: Economy
docs.text:
The Helios Sovereignty possesses the largest economy in the Core. Its banks finance governments, its corporations control major trade routes, and its merchant houses operate on nearly every civilized world.

Much of the Sovereignty's influence stems not from military force but from economic leverage. Entire planetary economies depend upon Sovereignty investment, shipping, insurance, and credit systems. Critics argue this allows the Sovereignty to exert influence far beyond its borders without firing a shot.

As a result, many observers jokingly claim that while other Great Houses conquer worlds, the Sovereignty simply buys them.

docs.title: Military
docs.text:
Although not as openly militarized as the Confederate Vanguard Union, the Sovereignty maintains one of the most technologically sophisticated armed forces in the Core.

Sovereignty doctrine emphasizes precision strikes, battlefield coordination, mobility, and command superiority. Rather than overwhelming opponents through brute force, Sovereignty commanders prefer to exploit superior intelligence, advanced targeting systems, and long-range firepower to destroy enemy formations before they can effectively respond.

The Sovereignty is famous for its extensive use of missile systems, Long-Range High Energy Cannons, and Magnetically Accelerated Cannons (MACs), expensive and technically demanding weapons rarely fielded in large numbers outside Sovereignty space. Sovereignty mechs are also known for their compact construction and widespread use of jump jets, giving them excellent mobility and making them particularly effective in difficult terrain.

Sovereignty forces have earned both admiration and resentment for their preference for long-range engagements. Sovereignty commanders routinely attempt to inflict devastating losses upon an enemy before the opposing force can close the distance. The Starcrest Protectorate in particular regards many of these tactics as cowardly and dishonorable, contributing to a longstanding rivalry between the two powers.

Mercenary companies serving the Sovereignty are often offered highly lucrative contracts, though Sovereignty negotiators are infamous for their strict interpretation of contractual obligations. Deployment costs are typically the responsibility of the mercenary company, allowing skilled commanders to maximize profits through efficient operations while exposing less capable companies to substantial financial risk.

Among mercenaries, a common warning regarding Sovereignty contracts is:

"Beware the Golden Hand."

The Sovereignty may reward success generously, but it has little sympathy for contractors who underestimate the true cost of war.

docs.title: Politics and Foreign Relations
docs.text:
The Sovereignty views itself as the steward of civilization. Sovereignty leaders frequently position themselves as mediators, negotiators, and defenders of stability within the Core.

Relations with the Star Regency are exceptionally close, reflecting centuries of cooperation between House Caledon and the ruling houses of Sovereignty. Ties with Orion Corporate are generally positive due to shared economic interests and technological cooperation.

Relations with the Confederate Vanguard Union are considerably more strained. Union leaders often view Sovereignty as decadent and politically manipulative, while Sovereignty commentators frequently characterize the Union as militaristic and authoritarian.

The deepest rivalry exists with the Starcrest Protectorate. The Protectorate's warrior culture regards many Sovereignty military doctrines as cowardly, while Sovereignty elites often view the Protectorate as backward and unnecessarily aggressive. Though outright war remains unlikely, tensions between the two powers remain among the most persistent in the Core.

Throughout human space, opinions of Sovereignty are sharply divided. Admirers see the Sovereignty as the architect of modern civilization. Critics see an increasingly arrogant aristocracy convinced of its own superiority.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
The Helios Sovereignty continues to position itself as the administrative and economic center of the modern Core.

As instability spreads throughout frontier space and tensions between the Great Houses intensify, Sovereignty leadership increasingly argues that only strong centralized coordination and enlightened governance can preserve civilization from fragmentation.

Whether the Sovereignty ultimately represents humanity’s greatest achievement in stability and prosperity — or the slow decadence of an overconfident elite — remains one of the defining political questions of the modern era.

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
“When the stars went dark, the Imperium carried the flame of civilization.” — Imperial remembrance inscription

State propaganda illustration depicting Imperator Jada Lytherius (center) flanked by her children: Takarion Lytherius (left), Sylara Lytherius (lower left), Valtorian Lytherius (right), and Rythor Lytherius (lower right), standing before the military and cultural power of the Omnisphere Imperium. Towering war machines, dragon banners, and the spires of the Imperial Core rise behind the ruling bloodline of House Lytherius.

docs.title: :material-dragon: Overview
docs.text:
Government Type Imperial aristocracy Ruling House House Lytherius Current Ruler Imperator Jada Lytherius Capital World Celestia Prime Population 12.3 billion Military Strength 220,000 mechs Friendly Ties Starcrest Protectorate Hostilities Orion Corporate; Confederate Vanguard Union Words “Fierce Beauty.”

The Omnisphere Imperium, often called the Imperium or the Dragon Riders, is one of the five Great Houses of the Core and the oldest surviving state in known human civilization.

The Imperium is widely recognized as the only surviving power from the age of the ancient Empire. Its roots extend even further back, to the centuries before the Empire united the Core, when the region that would become the Imperium was divided among smaller states known for metallurgy, engineering, and fierce competition over rare minerals and energy sources.

In the modern Core, the Imperium is known for honor, duty, artistic excellence, military discipline, and a cultural philosophy that joins beauty with strength. Its words, “Fierce Beauty,” reflect this union of refinement and martial power.

The Imperium also maintains a deep symbolic association with fire. Fire represents civilization, continuity, memory, life, and the will to endure. Imperial tradition often describes House Lytherius and the Imperium as the keepers of the flame that survived the Collapse and carried civilization through the dark age.

docs.title: History and Origins
docs.text:
The history of the Imperium begins centuries before the rise of the ancient Empire.

Before its formation, the region that would become the Imperial heartland was fragmented among multiple smaller powers. These states possessed distinct cultural practices, but many shared a focus on metallurgy, engineering, and resource extraction. Frequent conflicts over rare minerals and energy sources shaped the early military and industrial traditions of the region.

The rise of House Lytherius is traditionally tied to Vyrexia Lyther, a brilliant and ambitious leader remembered as both a skilled warrior and a tactical visionary.

The defining moment in early Imperial history was the development of the first war mech. This breakthrough granted House Lytherius a sudden and overwhelming battlefield advantage, allowing rapid expansion across surrounding territories and laying the foundation for the Omnisphere Imperium.

The Imperium’s early conquests funded both military and artistic growth. Wealth from conquered territories supported the industrial base necessary to maintain mech armies, while also patronizing artists, poets, engineers, philosophers, and scholars.

This fusion of martial supremacy and artistic achievement became one of the defining features of House Lytherius culture.

docs.title: Matriarchal Tradition
docs.text:
The Imperium is one of the few major powers of the Core where women traditionally occupy the highest positions of authority. This custom traces its origins to its first Imperator, Vyrexia, whose descendants established many of the noble traditions that continue to shape Imperial society.

To the Lytherians, lineage is sacred. Noble houses maintain genealogical records stretching back centuries, and legitimacy is often derived from bloodline as much as law. Throughout Imperial history, succession disputes have toppled dynasties, fractured provinces, and ignited wars. As a result, the Imperium developed an intense cultural aversion to uncertainty in matters of inheritance.

A common Lytherian saying holds that "A father is claimed; a mother is known." While modern science is fully capable of establishing ancestry, the tradition predates such technologies by centuries and remains deeply embedded in Imperial culture. Because maternal lineage is viewed as unquestionable, dynastic authority is often traced through women, and daughters are frequently favored as heirs. To lose track of a legitimate heir—or worse, to place the wrong heir upon a throne—is considered one of the gravest failures imaginable in Lytherian society. This tradition extends even to the Imperator herself. Throughout history, Imperators have commonly taken male consorts for the purpose of producing heirs, but those consorts are rarely considered part of the ruling dynasty. To the Lytherians, the children of an Imperator belong not to a family, but to the Imperium itself. From an early age they are educated, groomed, and prepared for service to the state, with their loyalty directed first toward the reigning Imperator and the Imperium rather than toward any paternal lineage. Fathers are generally expected to play little role in the upbringing of Imperial heirs, a custom that reinforces the belief that dynastic continuity flows through the maternal line alone.

This does not prevent men from inheriting titles or exercising power. Male nobles regularly serve as rulers, generals, governors, and statesmen. However, among the oldest Lytherian houses, women are often regarded as the safest custodians of dynastic continuity. The result is a society where female rulers are common, expected, and widely respected, though not universally required.

docs.title: The Old Empire and the Collapse
docs.text:
At its height, the early Imperium commanded vast territories and possessed unmatched technological superiority through its mech armies.

Over time, however, the scale of its territory became a vulnerability. Internal rebellions, political betrayal, economic strain, and external pressures weakened Imperial dominance.

Eventually, the broader ancient Empire emerged, and the reduced but still powerful Omnisphere Imperium became a member state within that larger order.

During the Collapse and the dark age that followed, the Imperium endured where nearly every other major power fell.

Imperial records claim the Imperium resisted the forces associated with the Ophidian Supremacy and preserved its core territories through fortified defense, discipline, and strict internal cohesion.

Although the Imperium lost worlds during the era, its government, ruling house, and central culture survived intact.

This continuity remains central to Imperial identity.

docs.title: Darker History
docs.text:
The modern Imperium often emphasizes honor, art, beauty, and stability, but StarCom historical records also preserve accounts of a harsher early Imperial past.

Early Imperial expansion was frequently brutal.

Defeated forces were often given grim ultimatums: swear loyalty or face execution. In several accounts, defiant captives were annihilated immediately using mech weaponry as a demonstration of Imperial power.

The early Imperium also relied upon slave labor to accelerate industrialization across conquered worlds. Dissenters, rebels, and military prisoners were often publicly executed to suppress resistance.

Whole populations were relocated into strategic settlements, local cultures were disrupted, and Imperial dominance was displayed through grand military parades featuring war trophies and captives.

Blood sports involving prisoners also formed part of early Imperial spectacle, serving both as entertainment and as a reminder of state power.

The modern Imperium has formally abandoned the most brutal of these practices. However, this darker history is often softened, romanticized, or minimized within later Imperial accounts.

docs.title: Celestia Prime
docs.text:
Many of the Imperium's greatest monuments, museums, military academies, temples, and historical archives are located on Celestia Prime. To Imperial citizens, the world is more than a capital—it is a living symbol of continuity stretching back thousands of years.

The world escaped much of the devastation that consumed the Core during the Collapse, and many structures standing today predate the formation of several modern Great Houses.

docs.title: House Lytherius
docs.text: The Omnisphere Imperium is ruled by House Lytherius, the oldest continuously governing dynasty in the Core and one of the oldest surviving political institutions in known human history. For more than two millennia, the house has guided the Imperium through periods of expansion, decline, invasion, and renewal, becoming inseparable from the state it governs.

docs.title: Imperator Jada Lytherius
docs.text:
The current ruler of the Imperium is Imperator Jada Lytherius, a leader widely respected for her wisdom, patience, and long-term vision. Stoic and composed by nature, Jada rarely acts impulsively, preferring careful deliberation and strategic planning over dramatic displays of authority. While some rivals mistake her measured demeanor for indecision, few who know her well make that mistake twice.

Under Jada's leadership, the Imperium has pursued a cautious and deliberate foreign policy, avoiding reckless conflicts while maintaining a reputation for responding to threats with overwhelming force when necessary. Her reign has emphasized stability, continuity, and the preservation of Imperial institutions.

Like many Imperators before her, Jada never married. Instead, she took consorts in accordance with Lytherian tradition and bore four children with prominent regional leaders from within the Imperium. As Imperial custom dictates, her children were raised primarily within the institutions of House Lytherius and the state itself. To the Lytherians, heirs of the Imperator belong first to the Imperium and only secondarily to any individual family.

docs.title: The Heirs of the Imperium
docs.text:
Jada's eldest sons, Takarion Lytherius and Valtorian Lytherius, rank among the Imperium's most respected military commanders. Both embody many of the traditional Lytherian virtues: discipline, patience, strategic thinking, and unwavering devotion to duty. Though similar in temperament, they possess distinct command styles and have earned reputations as formidable battlefield leaders.

The designated heir to the Imperium is Sylara Lytherius, whose talents lie not in warfare but diplomacy. Intelligent, thoughtful, and deeply idealistic, Sylara has become known throughout the Core for her efforts to resolve conflicts through negotiation rather than force. Her views often place her at odds with more traditional elements of the Imperial establishment, including members of her own family, who worry that her faith in diplomacy may prove inadequate in an increasingly dangerous age. Supporters, however, see her as the future of a changing Imperium.

The youngest child, Rythor Lytherius, stands apart from his siblings. A gifted warrior and talented mech pilot, Rythor possesses little interest in governance or court politics. He thrives on competition, tournaments, and the thrill of battle, often treating combat as a challenge to be mastered rather than a solemn duty. Though frequently frustrated by his responsibilities, he remains one of the most naturally gifted members of his generation and is widely admired by younger warriors throughout the Imperium.

Together, the children of Jada Lytherius represent the competing visions that may one day shape the future of the Imperium: military strength, diplomatic reform, tradition, ambition, and the enduring legacy of House Lytherius itself.

docs.title: Society and Culture
docs.text:
Imperial culture is built around the pursuit of excellence. Honor, duty, discipline, artistry, scholarship, and martial skill are not viewed as separate virtues, but as different expressions of the same ideal: the continual refinement of oneself in service to a greater purpose.

Unlike many societies of the Core, the Imperium does not draw a sharp distinction between warriors, artists, scholars, or statesmen. The ideal Imperial citizen strives to cultivate multiple disciplines throughout their life. A military commander may also be a poet. An engineer may study philosophy. An artist may be expected to understand strategy and history. To the Imperium, true mastery requires both strength and refinement.

This philosophy is reflected throughout Imperial society. Public spaces are designed not only to be functional, but beautiful. Military academies teach history and literature alongside tactics. Noble families sponsor artists, musicians, engineers, and scholars with the same enthusiasm they support military officers. Across the Imperium, there exists a widespread belief that anything worth doing is worth doing exceptionally well.

Honor and duty remain central pillars of Imperial identity. Citizens are taught from an early age that individual ambition must ultimately serve the stability and prosperity of the Imperium. Service to family, community, and state is regarded as a sacred obligation, and those who sacrifice personal gain for the greater good are often celebrated as role models.

To outsiders, Imperial culture can appear proud, rigid, and even elitist. Imperial citizens generally regard such criticism as the inevitable consequence of maintaining high standards. In the Imperial view, civilization is not preserved through comfort or convenience, but through discipline, excellence, and the continual pursuit of perfection.

docs.title: The Eternal Flame
docs.text:
Fire occupies a central place within Imperial culture, philosophy, and symbolism.

To the Lytherians, flame represents far more than heat or light. It symbolizes civilization itself: knowledge preserved across generations, memory carried through history, sacrifice made for future descendants, and the enduring will of humanity to survive.

This symbolism is rooted in the Imperium's understanding of its own history. Imperial tradition teaches that while much of civilization collapsed during the Dark Age, the Imperium preserved a fragment of the old world and carried that flame forward until order could be restored. Whether interpreted literally or metaphorically, the image remains one of the most powerful symbols in Imperial identity.
