Eternal flames burn within military academies, government buildings, memorials, and temples throughout the Imperium. State ceremonies frequently involve torch processions, ceremonial braziers, and fire-lit vigils honoring both historical heroes and fallen soldiers.

The dragon imagery associated with House Lytherius is closely connected to this philosophy. In Imperial thought, dragons are not merely creatures of destruction. They are guardians of civilization's flame, protectors of knowledge, and symbols of the immense responsibility that accompanies power.

To outsiders, these traditions can appear theatrical or romanticized. To the Lytherians, they serve as a constant reminder that civilization survives only so long as someone is willing to tend the fire.

docs.title: Politics and Diplomacy
docs.text:
The Imperium employs a deliberate and cautious political approach.

Its leaders prefer measured responses to threats and challenges rather than hasty escalation. When provoked, however, the Imperium responds with calculated resolve and formidable force.

The Imperium maintains friendly ties with the Starcrest Protectorate, as both powers share cultural foundations rooted in honor and duty.

Relations with the Orion Corporate and the Confederate Vanguard Union are strained.

Orion Corporate often views the Imperium as traditional and outdated, too bound to ritual and inherited customs.

The Union views the Imperium as a serious military contender, particularly because Imperial military strength challenges the Union’s claim to military supremacy.

Relations with the Helios Sovereignty are generally reasonable, largely because geography limits direct conflict between them rather than because of deep cultural similarity.

docs.title: Economy
docs.text:
The Imperium possesses one of the oldest industrial economies in human space. Many of its economic foundations can be traced back centuries before the rise of the ancient Empire itself.

Mining, metallurgy, advanced manufacturing, military production, and artistic craftsmanship remain central pillars of Imperial industry. While the Sovereignty dominates commerce and finance, and Orion Corporate leads in technological innovation, the Imperium is renowned for the quality of its production rather than the scale of its output.

Imperial artisans, armorers, engineers, and manufacturers often spend years refining designs that other states would mass-produce. This pursuit of excellence increases costs but has earned Imperial goods a reputation for exceptional quality throughout the Core.

To the Imperium, beauty and utility are not opposing ideals. A machine should function flawlessly. It should also be worthy of admiration.

docs.title: Weapons and Doctrine
docs.text:
The Imperium's military philosophy reflects the same pursuit of excellence that shapes its culture.

Rather than emphasizing overwhelming numbers or industrial efficiency, Imperial doctrine focuses on precision, mobility, initiative, and mastery. Commanders are encouraged to view warfare as both a science and an art, rewarding creativity, discipline, and adaptability over rigid adherence to doctrine.

This philosophy is reflected in the distinctive appearance and performance of Imperial war machines. Lytherian mechs favor sleek, serpentine designs that sacrifice some armor protection in exchange for exceptional speed and agility. Their machines are often among the fastest in their weight class, allowing Imperial forces to dictate the terms of engagement and exploit weaknesses before an opponent can respond.

The Imperium's signature technological achievement is the advanced arcanite engine, a propulsion system that provides superior mobility without sacrificing battlefield endurance. Combined with generations of refinement in neural interfaces, metallurgy, and weapons engineering, these systems have produced some of the most feared combat machines in the Core.

Imperial forces rarely seek prolonged battles of attrition. Instead, they prefer rapid strikes, precision ambushes, concentrated opening volleys, and carefully timed withdrawals. Victory is achieved not by overpowering an opponent directly, but by controlling the flow of battle itself.

This doctrine is reinforced by the Imperium's distinctive weapon systems. The most famous of these is the Blaster, a burst-fire energy weapon that stores multiple charges before releasing them in a devastating opening volley. While blasters are less efficient than conventional lasers during extended engagements, they excel at delivering overwhelming damage during the first moments of combat. Imperial commanders frequently use this advantage to strike hard, disengage, and reposition before an enemy can effectively respond.

Imperial forces also make extensive use of heat warfare. Scorchers, High Energy Cannons, and other thermal weapons are employed not merely to damage opponents, but to overwhelm cooling systems, restrict mobility, and gradually reduce battlefield effectiveness. Once an enemy begins struggling with heat management, Imperial units exploit the resulting weaknesses through superior speed and maneuverability.

Throughout the Core, Imperial mech pilots are often known as the Dragon Riders. Originally coined by outsiders, the nickname refers both to the serpentine appearance of Imperial mechs and to their style of warfare: swift, elegant, disciplined, and devastating when allowed to strike on their own terms.

docs.title: The Dragon Riders
docs.text:
Imperial mech pilots are often referred to throughout the Core as the Dragon Riders. The name originates from the distinctive serpentine designs favored by Imperial engineers, many of which resemble the dragons that occupy a central place within Imperial mythology and symbolism.

Over time, the nickname became synonymous with the Imperium's style of warfare itself: swift, elegant, devastating, and difficult to pin down. Though originally coined by outsiders, many Imperial warriors have proudly embraced the title.

docs.title: Mercenary Relations
docs.text:
Mercenaries often find the Imperium difficult to work for at first.

The Imperium does not believe in rewards without trust. New mercenary companies may struggle financially while attempting to prove their reliability and earn Imperial confidence.

However, those who gain the trust and respect of the Imperium often find it to be one of the most generous employers in the Core.

As a result, mercenaries who successfully establish themselves with the Imperium are often fiercely loyal to its cause.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
The Omnisphere Imperium inspires stronger opinions than almost any other power in the Core.

To its supporters, the Imperium represents the highest ideals of civilization. Its citizens point to centuries of cultural achievement, military excellence, scientific innovation, and political stability as evidence that the Lytherian model has endured where countless others have failed. Many view the Imperium as the closest surviving connection to the greatness of the ancient Empire.

Critics see something different.

Outside observers often accuse the Imperium of arrogance, elitism, and excessive reverence for its own traditions. Imperial nobles are frequently portrayed as believing themselves intellectually, culturally, and morally superior to the rest of the Core. While Imperials generally reject such accusations, their confidence rarely does much to discourage them.

Relations with other powers are similarly complex. Union citizens often view the Imperium as proud and detached. Protectorate citizens tend to respect its military accomplishments while questioning its aristocratic culture. Corporate analysts admire Imperial engineering but criticize what they see as a reluctance to embrace disruptive innovation. Within the Sovereignty, opinions range from admiration for Imperial refinement to frustration with its traditionalism.

Even among its critics, however, there remains a certain degree of respect.

The Imperium's military academies are among the most prestigious in human space. Its engineers continue to produce some of the finest war machines in the Core. Its artists, scholars, and philosophers exert influence far beyond its borders.

As a result, many citizens hold two contradictory views simultaneously: they may dislike the Imperium, but they rarely dismiss it.

As one popular saying puts it:

"Everyone complains about the Imperium. Nobody ignores it."

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
The Omnisphere Imperium remains one of the most stable and respected powers in the Core.

It is no longer the dominant civilization it once was, yet it has endured longer than any other known state.

As tensions rise between the Great Houses, the Imperium continues to present itself as the keeper of civilization’s flame: ancient, disciplined, beautiful, and prepared to defend what remains of the Core.

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text:
“For Futures Yet to Come.” — Words of the Corporate

Corporate propaganda illustration depicting Prodirector Deckard Noddack standing at the center of the Orion Corporate Directorate alongside his wife, Dr. Alise Noddack (right), Zyce Noddack-Voss (left) head of the Corporate Internal Security Forces, and Celeste Noddack (far right) the daughter and heir of the Corporate. Behind them loom the sleek cyclopean mechs of the Orion Corporate, their glowing mono-eye sensors watching over the technological heart of the Core.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Government Type Corporate-Technocratic Alliance Capital World Horizon's Reach Founding Structure The Three Pillars Population 13.4 billion Primary Strength Technology and Scientific Advancement Military Specialty Advanced Electronics and Precision Warfare National Motto “For Futures Yet To Come.”

Orion Corporate is one of the five Great Houses of the Core and the foremost technological power in known space. Ruled by House Noddack from the world of Horizon's Reach, the Corporate traces its origins not to ancient dynasties or military conquest, but to a coalition of survivors who endured the dark age through science, cooperation, and long-term planning.

Where other powers often look to the past for guidance, the Corporate focuses relentlessly upon the future. Its universities educate the brightest minds in the Core, its laboratories produce a constant stream of innovation, and its industrial infrastructure powers much of modern civilization. Across countless worlds, Corporate engineers, scientists, and administrators work toward a singular goal: ensuring that humanity never again suffers the collapse that nearly destroyed it.

Supporters regard the Corporate as humanity's greatest engine of progress and innovation. Critics accuse it of technocratic arrogance and an excessive faith in planning, efficiency, and expertise. Yet even its rivals acknowledge that few institutions have contributed more to the advancement of modern civilization.

The Corporate's words, "For Futures Yet To Come," reflect a philosophy that permeates nearly every aspect of Orion society: that each generation bears a responsibility to leave the future better than it found it.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of Orion Corporate lie in the chaos that followed the Collapse. As interstellar civilization fragmented and countless worlds descended into isolation, piracy, and economic ruin, three surviving colonies chose a different path. Rather than retreating behind their own borders, they formed a defensive and economic alliance dedicated to preserving knowledge, industry, and social stability.

This alliance eventually became known as the Three Pillars.

The experience profoundly shaped the Corporate's worldview. While many surviving powers concluded that strength, tradition, or military authority were the keys to survival, the founders of the Corporate reached a different conclusion. They believed civilization had nearly perished because humanity had failed to cooperate, failed to preserve knowledge, and failed to plan for the future.

As the dark age continued, the Three Pillars gradually expanded their influence through technological expertise, industrial production, and coordinated development. Their worlds became islands of stability in an increasingly dangerous galaxy. New colonies joined the alliance, institutions grew more sophisticated, and the Corporate steadily evolved into one of the most advanced powers in human space.

By the time the Great Restoration began, the Corporate was already among the few multi-world civilizations to have survived the dark age largely intact. The lessons learned during that era continue to shape Corporate policy today. To Corporate leaders, technological progress is not merely desirable—it is civilization's best defense against repeating the mistakes of the past.

docs.title: The Three Pillars
docs.text:
The foundation of modern Orion Corporate can be traced to an alliance known as the Three Pillars.

During the dark age, as much of interstellar civilization fragmented into isolation, piracy, and warfare, three surviving colonies formed a defensive pact to ensure their mutual survival. In the language of the Core, such an alliance was known as a corporate—a formal agreement through which multiple worlds pooled resources, coordinated defense, and shared responsibility for their collective future.

Rather than relying solely upon military strength, the Three Pillars invested heavily in scientific research, engineering, industrial production, and technological development. Their leaders believed that knowledge and cooperation offered a more sustainable path to survival than conquest or isolation. Through shared resources, coordinated planning, and advanced technology, the alliance gradually transformed three vulnerable colonies into one of the most stable regions of human space.

As surrounding worlds descended into decline, the Three Pillars became a beacon of security and prosperity amid the chaos of the dark age. Over time, the alliance expanded, incorporating new territories and institutions until it eventually evolved into the modern Orion Corporate.

Even today, the Three Pillars remain central to Corporate identity. The Corporate continues to view cooperation, scientific advancement, and long-term planning as the foundations upon which civilization itself rests.

docs.title: The Great Restoration
docs.text:
When House Caledon and the Vanguard forces returned during the Great Restoration, Orion Corporate was already one of the few functioning multi-world powers remaining in the Core.

The returning Regency relied heavily upon Orion technological infrastructure and engineering expertise during the reconstruction of civilization.

Through close cooperation with the Regency, Orion gained major political legitimacy and increasing territorial responsibility throughout the recovering Core.

Many historians believe the Restoration could not have succeeded as rapidly without Orion support.

docs.title: Government
docs.text:
The Orion Corporate is governed through a hybrid system that combines local autonomy with centralized coordination. Unlike most Great Houses, which concentrate authority within a ruling dynasty or military hierarchy, the Corporate traces its origins to an alliance of independent worlds that voluntarily united for mutual survival during the dark age.

This legacy remains visible in its modern government. Each major member world maintains representation within the Corporate Council, a legislative body responsible for setting policy, coordinating large-scale initiatives, and resolving disputes between member systems. The council functions similarly to a parliamentary assembly, with representatives balancing local interests against the broader needs of the Corporate as a whole.

As the Corporate expanded and governing a growing interstellar civilization became increasingly complex, a centralized executive office emerged to coordinate policy and provide long-term strategic leadership. This office eventually became associated with House Noddack, whose members have guided the Corporate for generations. While the Prodirector possesses significant authority, the position functions more as a chief executive and coordinator than as an absolute ruler.

The result is one of the most decentralized governments among the Great Houses. Individual worlds retain substantial authority over local administration, economic policy, infrastructure, and internal affairs. Supporters argue that this system encourages innovation, flexibility, and local accountability. Critics contend that it can slow decision-making and make the Corporate less responsive during major crises.

To Corporate citizens, however, this balance between local autonomy and centralized coordination reflects one of the state's founding principles: that cooperation produces stronger outcomes than domination.

docs.title: House Noddack
docs.text:
The Orion Corporate is ruled by House Noddack, a family whose influence spans science, governance, military planning, and industrial administration. Unlike many noble houses of the Core, House Noddack derives its prestige less from military conquest or ancient bloodlines and more from generations of scientific achievement and public service.

The current ruler, Prodirector Deckard Noddack, is both a statesman and an accomplished scientist. Reserved, analytical, and intensely forward-thinking, Deckard is known for treating governance as a problem to be solved rather than a stage upon which to perform. He remains actively involved in many of the Corporate's largest scientific and industrial initiatives, believing that leaders should understand the technologies shaping the future rather than merely oversee them.

His wife, Dr. Elise Noddack, is one of the most respected mathematicians in the Core. Equally comfortable advising government committees or lecturing at Central Academy, Elise embodies the Corporate ideal that scholarship and public service should exist side by side.

Their daughter and heir, Celeste Noddack, has already earned a reputation as a prodigy within Corporate academic circles. Brilliant, ambitious, and intensely driven, she possesses an almost relentless desire to prove herself worthy of both her family name and the future leadership of the Corporate.

The administrative machinery of Orion is overseen in large part by Zysce Noddack-Voss, Deckard's cousin and one of the most influential political figures within the state. In addition to managing much of the Corporate bureaucracy, Zysce directs internal security and counter-espionage operations. Their efforts help protect Orion's research institutions, technological secrets, and scientific infrastructure from rivals throughout the Core.

Together, House Noddack represents the values upon which Orion was founded: knowledge, competence, innovation, and responsibility to future generations.

docs.title: The Future Doctrine
docs.text:
More than any other Great House, Orion Corporate thinks in decades rather than years.

Corporate planners frequently pursue projects whose benefits may not be realized for generations. Infrastructure, research programs, colonization initiatives, educational systems, and technological development are often evaluated according to their long-term contribution to civilization rather than their immediate political value.

This philosophy is embodied by the Corporate's words:

"For Futures Yet To Come."

To Corporate citizens, the phrase is more than a slogan. It reflects the belief that every generation inherits a responsibility to improve the world for those who follow. Decisions are therefore judged not only by their present consequences, but by the future they help create.

This perspective frequently places the Corporate at odds with other powers. Critics accuse the Corporate of arrogance and excessive faith in planning. Supporters argue that civilization survives only when someone is willing to think beyond the next election, the next crisis, or the next war.

docs.title: Horizon's Reach
docs.text:
Horizon's Reach is the capital world of the Orion Corporate and among the most technologically sophisticated planets in human space. Vast research arcologies, automated manufacturing districts, orbital shipyards, and sprawling university complexes dominate the landscape, creating a world that often feels more like a living laboratory than a traditional capital.

Unlike the grand palaces of the Imperium or the luxurious estates of the Sovereignty, Horizon's Reach was designed around function, efficiency, and innovation. Much of the planet's surface is dedicated to research institutions, industrial infrastructure, educational centers, and scientific development. Entire districts are devoted to experimental technologies that may not become commercially viable for decades.

The world attracts scientists, engineers, entrepreneurs, and scholars from across the Core. To many, Horizon's Reach represents humanity's future. To others, it serves as a warning about what civilization becomes when efficiency begins to replace tradition.

docs.title: Society and Culture
docs.text:
Orion society prizes knowledge, innovation, competence, and achievement. From an early age, citizens are encouraged to pursue education and develop expertise in whatever fields best suit their talents. Academic success, scientific accomplishment, and technical skill carry enormous social prestige throughout the Corporate.

Unlike many of the aristocratic powers of the Core, Orion places relatively little emphasis on bloodline, heritage, or social standing. Advancement is expected to be earned through performance rather than inherited through family connections. While reality does not always perfectly match this ideal, the belief remains central to Corporate identity.

This meritocratic culture has produced some of the finest engineers, scientists, mathematicians, physicians, and researchers in known space. It has also fostered an intensely competitive environment in which excellence is expected and mediocrity is rarely tolerated.

Many outsiders perceive Corporate citizens as highly intelligent but emotionally reserved. Corporate citizens often counter that they simply prefer solutions to rhetoric, evidence to tradition, and results to appearances.

docs.title: Technology and Research
docs.text:
Orion Corporate is widely regarded as the technological heart of the Core. Its universities, laboratories, industrial complexes, and research institutes produce a constant stream of innovations that influence nearly every aspect of modern civilization.

Many technologies now considered commonplace throughout the Core were originally developed or refined by Corporate scientists, including advances in computing, automation, reactor engineering, battlefield networking, sensors, communications, and industrial manufacturing.

The Corporate's commitment to scientific advancement stems directly from its experiences during the Collapse. Witnessing the near-loss of humanity's accumulated knowledge convinced Orion's founders that technological stagnation represented one of the greatest threats to civilization itself. As a result, research and education remain among the highest priorities of the modern Corporate.

Unlike many powers that focus primarily on immediate concerns, Orion frequently invests in projects whose benefits may not be realized for decades. To Corporate planners, technological progress is not simply a tool for gaining power—it is a responsibility owed to future generations.

docs.title: The Lost Research Centers
docs.text:
Among Orion's most prized possessions are several surviving research complexes dating back to the Golden Empire.

Though the facilities themselves are no longer operational and much of their original technology has been lost to history, they remain among the most important historical sites within the Corporate. Scientists, historians, and engineers from across the Core travel to study these ancient monuments in hopes of uncovering fragments of forgotten knowledge.

Beyond their practical value, the centers possess immense symbolic significance. To Corporate citizens, they serve as reminders of both humanity's greatest achievements and the consequences of allowing civilization to decline. Many regard them as sacred institutions dedicated to preserving humanity's intellectual heritage.

The existence of these facilities has further strengthened Orion's reputation as the foremost center of science, education, and technological scholarship within the Core.

docs.title: Exploration Beyond the Core
docs.text:
While most Great Houses remain focused upon the politics and rivalries of the Core, Orion Corporate maintains a growing interest in regions beyond known space.

Corporate leaders view exploration as both a scientific endeavor and a long-term strategic necessity. To them, the future of humanity cannot remain permanently confined within the borders of the Core. New resources, technologies, discoveries, and opportunities may await beyond the frontier.

As a result, the Corporate maintains the largest exploration and survey programs of any Great House. Corporate expeditions regularly venture into the Fringe, mapping unknown systems, investigating ancient ruins, and establishing isolated outposts far beyond traditional borders.

Critics frequently dismiss these efforts as expensive distractions. Corporate researchers counter that every great civilization eventually stagnates if it ceases to explore.

In many ways, Orion's frontier initiatives represent the purest expression of its guiding philosophy: preparing humanity for futures yet to come.

docs.title: Military
docs.text:
The Orion Corporate fields one of the most technologically advanced militaries in the Core. While other Great Houses often emphasize brute force, overwhelming numbers, or individual heroics, the Corporate approaches warfare as an engineering problem to be solved through information, coordination, and precision.

Corporate commanders seek to understand the battlefield before committing to it. Reconnaissance networks, sensor arrays, battlefield simulations, and predictive analytics are employed extensively to identify weaknesses and exploit them with minimal losses. Corporate officers are trained to view unnecessary casualties as failures of planning rather than unavoidable costs of war.

This philosophy has produced a military that excels at coordinated operations, long-range engagements, electronic warfare, and battlefield control. Corporate formations are often capable of sharing targeting data, sensor information, and tactical updates across an entire combat theater, allowing units to react with remarkable speed and efficiency.

The Corporate's decentralized political structure is also reflected in its military organization. Unlike most Great Houses, whose armed forces answer directly to a centralized authority, much of the Corporate's military strength remains distributed among planetary governments, regional magistrates, and local administrative authorities. While these forces are formally part of the larger Corporate military command, local leaders retain significant influence over their deployment and operations. As a result, assembling large expeditionary armies often requires the cooperation and approval of numerous political stakeholders. Supporters argue that this system prevents the concentration of military power in any single set of hands and encourages local accountability. Critics counter that it can slow mobilization efforts and complicate strategic decision-making during rapidly evolving crises.

The Corporate's military strength is not without weaknesses. Corporate officers are frequently selected for their intelligence, education, and technical expertise, sometimes at the expense of intuition or battlefield aggression. Critics argue that Corporate commanders can become overly reliant upon planning and simulation, occasionally struggling when confronted with unpredictable opponents who refuse to behave as expected.

Nevertheless, few powers can match the Orion Corporate's ability to integrate technology, information, and firepower into a single coordinated fighting force.

docs.title: Shield Matrix Systems
docs.text:
The Orion Corporate is the only power in the Core to field Shield Matrix technology on a large scale.

Shield Matrix systems generate powerful electromagnetic energy barriers through specialized coil assemblies integrated directly into a mech's frame. These barriers are capable of disrupting missiles, rockets, laser fire, and High Energy Cannon attacks before they can damage the mech itself.

Perhaps most remarkably, Shield Matrix systems gradually regenerate over time, allowing Corporate mechs to recover from damage during extended engagements. This gives Orion forces an unusual degree of battlefield endurance despite often fielding lighter armored designs than many of their rivals.

The technology is not without weaknesses. Sustained fire can overwhelm a shield faster than it can regenerate, and ballistic weapons such as Autoguns, Cannons, MAC Guns, and certain heat-based weapons largely bypass its protective effects. As a result, Orion commanders must carefully manage their shields and avoid situations where enemies can exploit these vulnerabilities.

Despite these limitations, the Shield Matrix remains one of the most significant defensive innovations in modern warfare.

docs.title: Prismatic Lasers
docs.text:
Prismatic Lasers are the signature weapon system of Orion Corporate and among the most sophisticated energy weapons ever developed.

Unlike conventional laser systems, Prismatic Lasers utilize advanced focusing arrays and battlefield simulation computers to deliberately ricochet beams between multiple targets. Through precise calculations performed in real time, a single shot can strike two, three, or even more nearby enemy units in rapid succession.

The system is especially effective against tightly grouped formations, where reflected beams can inflict devastating damage across an entire battle line. Opposing commanders frequently attempt to counter Prismatic Lasers by dispersing their forces, though battlefield conditions do not always permit such spacing.

Corporate targeting computers continuously model the battlefield, calculating reflection angles and target priorities faster than any human operator could manage alone. The result is a weapon system capable of producing battlefield effects that many observers initially mistake for advanced automation or artificial intelligence.

Throughout the Core, Prismatic Lasers have become synonymous with Orion's approach to warfare: technologically elegant, mathematically precise, and devastatingly effective when employed under the proper conditions.

docs.title: Politics and Foreign Relations
docs.text:
Orion Corporate maintains relationships with every major power in the Core, though its emphasis on technology, meritocracy, and scientific progress often places it at odds with more traditional societies.

Its most strained relationship is with the Omnisphere Imperium. While the Imperium views history and tradition as the foundation of civilization, the Corporate places its faith in innovation and adaptation. Imperial traditionalists frequently criticize the Corporate as culturally sterile and excessively dependent upon technology. Corporate citizens often respond that progress is impossible without a willingness to challenge old assumptions.

Relations with the Sovereignty are generally positive. Both powers share interests in administration, economics, education, and long-term planning, though competition between the two remains substantial. Many of the Core's largest technological and economic initiatives involve cooperation between Orion and Helios institutions.

The Confederate Vanguard Union views Orion with a mixture of respect and suspicion. Union leaders admire the Corporate's industrial capabilities but remain wary of its technological advantages and growing political influence. The Corporate, in turn, often regards the Union as overly focused on military solutions to problems better solved through diplomacy, economics, or technological development.

Relations with the Starcrest Protectorate are generally cordial. While the two powers possess very different cultures, both share a practical approach to governance and a strong sense of responsibility toward the long-term stability of civilization.

Throughout the Core, the Corporate is often regarded as both a partner and a competitor. Many governments depend upon Corporate technology, expertise, and infrastructure even while remaining wary of its growing influence.

docs.title: Mercenary Relations
docs.text:
Orion Corporate employs a contract structure unlike any other Great House.

Where most powers offer modest advances and substantial completion bonuses, Orion frequently reverses the arrangement. Mercenary companies are often provided with generous upfront payments, while mission completion rewards tend to be comparatively modest.

Corporate contracts also place unusual emphasis on measurable battlefield results. Rather than rewarding success solely through mission completion, Orion commonly supplements payment with bounties for confirmed battlefield kills and destroyed enemy assets. During large-scale conflicts this system can prove extraordinarily profitable, while smaller operations often generate more modest returns.

The Corporate is generally regarded as fair, predictable, and professional in its dealings. However, it possesses little tolerance for companies that abandon contracts or fail to fulfill their obligations. Mercenary units that betray Corporate trust frequently find themselves blacklisted from future employment and shut out of valuable business opportunities throughout Orion space.

As a result, veteran mercenaries often describe Orion contracts as some of the safest and most predictable in the Core—provided one intends to honor the agreement.

"Don't piss off the Corporate." — Common mercenary warning

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As instability spreads throughout the Core and tensions between the Great Houses continue to rise, Orion Corporate increasingly argues that humanity stands at a crossroads. To Corporate leaders, the greatest threats facing civilization are not merely military rivals or political disputes, but stagnation, complacency, and the loss of knowledge.

Many within the Corporate believe the lessons of the Collapse have been forgotten. They warn that civilizations do not fall suddenly, but gradually, through the accumulation of neglected problems, declining institutions, and a failure to prepare for future challenges. For this reason, the Corporate continues investing heavily in scientific research, industrial development, education, exploration, and military innovation.

Whether these efforts ultimately secure humanity's future remains an open question. Supporters see the Corporate as the architect of a brighter age of discovery and progress. Critics fear that its growing influence may produce a society increasingly governed by experts, algorithms, and institutions rather than people.

To Orion, however, the answer remains simple. The future is not something to be awaited. It is something to be built.

docs.title: The Star Regency
docs.text:
“Rule above all.” — Words of House Caledon

House Caledon, the ruling lineage of the Stellar Conclave upon Citadel. Seated upon the Throne of the Core is the Star Regent Jacob Caledon, flanked by his heir apparent and eldest son to the left alongside the heir’s wife and children. To the Regent’s right stands his younger brother and his brother's wife, beside his own son. Honor Guard troops and ceremonial war mechs stand watch within the Hall of Ascension beneath the banners of the Conclave.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Government Type Supranational Regency Current Star Regent Jacob Caledon Ruling Dynasty House Caledon Seat of Power Citadel Governing Alliance The Stellar Conclave Words of House Caledon “Rule Above All.”

The Star Regency is the central governing authority of the Core Worlds and the political institution responsible for maintaining unity among the Great Houses.

Ruled by House Caledon from the capital world of Citadel, the Regency serves as both the symbolic continuation of the ancient Empire and the stabilizing force holding modern interstellar civilization together.

Though the Great Houses maintain independent governments, militaries, territories, and political ambitions, all formally recognize the authority of the Star Regent and the legitimacy of the Regency.

In practice, the Regency functions less as a traditional empire and more as a system of managed balance between rival powers.

docs.title: House Caledon
docs.text:
House Caledon traces its lineage to the final years of the Empire.

According to Restoration-era historical records, the last Emperor fell defending the evacuation of the Empire’s surviving military forces during the Fall. The precise details remain uncertain, though most surviving accounts describe a final defensive action carried out against the forces associated with the Ophidian Supremacy.

During the evacuation and subsequent exile beyond known Core space, leadership of the surviving fleet is believed to have passed to the ancestors of House Caledon under the title of Star Regent.

How long the exiled fleet remained beyond the Core remains unclear.

Most historians believe the exile lasted at least one generation, though some traditions claim it endured far longer.

Centuries later, House Caledon returned to the Core alongside the surviving Vanguard forces during the Great Restoration.

Following the reclamation of the Core Worlds, House Caledon established the modern Regency from Citadel and has ruled continuously ever since.

docs.title: Citadel
docs.text:
Citadel serves as the capital world of the Core and the seat of House Caledon.

The planet is regarded as politically neutral territory under direct Regency authority and functions as: - the center of Conclave governance - the residence of the Star Regent - the location of major diplomatic assemblies - the administrative heart of the Core

Citadel is among the most heavily defended worlds in known space.

Much of its infrastructure dates back to the earliest years following the Great Restoration, though portions of the world are believed to contain structures significantly older than the modern era.

docs.title: The Star Regent
docs.text:
The Star Regent serves as the supreme political authority of the Core Worlds.

Although the office does not directly govern the territories of the Great Houses, the Regent possesses extensive powers involving: - inter-house diplomacy - treaty enforcement - Conclave arbitration - emergency coordination - military oversight - preservation of interstellar law

The current Star Regent is Jacob Caledon.

Among both allies and rivals, Jacob is generally regarded as cautious, disciplined, and deeply committed to preserving stability throughout the Core.

Advanced age and declining health have increasingly shifted political attention toward the question of succession within House Caledon.

docs.title: The Stellar Conclave
docs.text:
The Stellar Conclave is the alliance of the five Great Houses under Regency authority.

The member powers are: - the Confederate Vanguard Union - the Helios Sovereignty - the Omnisphere Imperium - the Starcrest Protectorate - Orion Corporate

Each Great House maintains independent: - militaries - territorial governments - industrial economies - political systems

The Conclave exists primarily to: - prevent open war between the Houses - preserve trade and stability - coordinate large-scale policy - maintain the unity established during the Great Restoration

Political tensions between the Houses remain constant, however.

Competition over: - territory - influence - trade - technology - military prestige - frontier operations

continues to shape much of modern Core politics.

docs.title: The Voice of the Regent
docs.text:
Among the most prestigious political offices in the Core is the position known as the Voice of the Regent.

The Voice functions as the chief political administrator and primary executive representative of the Regency, overseeing much of the day-to-day governance of the Core Worlds.

Historically, members of Houses Aerin and Payne of the Helios Sovereignty have frequently served in this role due to their administrative and economic influence during the reconstruction of the Core following the Great Restoration.

In practice, the Voice often functions as: - chief diplomat - political coordinator - strategic advisor - administrator of Conclave affairs

while the Star Regent serves as the symbolic and stabilizing center of the Core itself.

docs.title: The Peace of the Core
docs.text:
The Regency officially forbids open warfare between the Great Houses.

In practice, maintaining peace across the Core remains extraordinarily difficult.

Proxy conflicts, covert operations, economic warfare, political sabotage, and mercenary engagements occur regularly throughout frontier regions and contested territories.

It is widely understood throughout the Core that the Great Houses frequently employ mercenary forces to advance political interests while avoiding direct confrontation.

Although the Regency officially condemns such practices, enforcement remains inconsistent.

Many political observers believe House Caledon tolerates limited indirect conflict because the alternative — open war between the Great Houses — would threaten the stability of civilization itself.

As a result, the Regency’s true role is often described not as preventing conflict entirely, but containing it within survivable limits.

docs.title: Relationship with StarCom
docs.text:
The Regency maintains extremely close ties with StarCom.

StarCom infrastructure enables: - interstellar governance - communications - navigational coordination - diplomatic operations - strategic intelligence gathering

Without StarCom support, the Regency could not effectively maintain authority across the Core Worlds.

Despite this relationship, StarCom officially remains politically neutral and institutionally independent from the Regency itself.

docs.title: Public Perception
docs.text:
Throughout the Core, perceptions of the Regency vary significantly.

Supporters view House Caledon as the final stabilizing force preventing humanity from descending once again into collapse and fragmentation.

Critics occasionally argue that the Regency has become overly bureaucratic, politically cautious, or incapable of adapting to growing instability across the frontier.

Even among critics, however, few openly advocate for the dissolution of the Regency itself.

The memory of the Collapse and the dark age that followed remains deeply embedded within Core political culture.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions between the Great Houses continue to intensify, the authority of the Regency faces increasing strain.

Frontier instability, growing militarization, economic rivalry, and political fragmentation have all placed new pressure upon the institutions established during the Great Restoration.

Whether House Caledon can continue preserving unity among the Great Houses may ultimately determine the future of civilization throughout the Core.

docs.title: StarCom
docs.text: “To Know the Future.” — StarCom Foundational Doctrine

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Interstellar Communications and Navigation Authority Operational Reach Core Worlds and Frontier Regions Primary Headquarters Classified Primary Responsibilities Communications, Navigation, Infrastructure Coordination Political Alignment Neutral under the Star Regency Official Motto “To Know the Future.”

StarCom is the primary communications, navigation, and interstellar coordination authority of the Core Worlds.

Operating independently of the Great Houses under the protection of the Star Regency, StarCom maintains the infrastructure necessary for large-scale civilization throughout known space.

Its responsibilities include: - interstellar communications - navigational routing - hyperspace telemetry - merchant traffic coordination - relay maintenance - archival preservation - strategic observation - deep-space monitoring

No major power within the Core can function effectively without StarCom infrastructure.

As a result, StarCom is widely regarded as one of the most essential institutions in modern civilization.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
StarCom emerged during the early decades following the Great Restoration.

In the aftermath of the Dark Age, the Core suffered from: - fragmented communications - unstable jump routes - isolated systems - collapsed navigation infrastructure - unreliable interstellar travel

As House Caledon and the returning Vanguard forces reclaimed the Core, it became increasingly clear that no lasting civilization could survive without a unified communications and navigation network.

StarCom was therefore established to restore: - interstellar coordination - trade routes - navigational safety - long-range communications - centralized archival systems

Over the centuries, the organization expanded from a reconstruction authority into the vast interstellar institution that exists today.

docs.title: Neutrality
docs.text:
StarCom officially maintains strict political neutrality between the Great Houses.

Its infrastructure and services are made available equally throughout the Core under Regency charter.

This neutrality is considered essential to preserving trust in the organization’s communications and navigational systems.

Although political accusations occasionally emerge during periods of heightened tension between the Great Houses, no verified evidence has ever demonstrated systemic StarCom interference in Conclave affairs.

Most mainstream political analysts regard claims of secret StarCom political manipulation as speculative or heavily exaggerated.

docs.title: Communications Network
docs.text:
The StarCom Relay Network is among the largest surviving technological systems in known space.

The network consists of: - hyperspace relay stations - long-range communications arrays - navigation buoys - telemetry platforms - orbital traffic control systems - deep-space sensor installations

These systems coordinate civilian, commercial, and military traffic throughout the Core.

In many frontier regions, StarCom relay stations serve as the only stable connection to wider civilization.

The destruction of a major relay corridor is considered a severe strategic emergency capable of destabilizing entire sectors.

docs.title: Navigational Authority
docs.text:
StarCom maintains the largest known archive of jump-route data in existence.

Its archives contain: - navigational calculations - stellar drift analysis - gravity mapping - jump telemetry - route stabilization records - frontier exploration surveys

Due to the dangers associated with long-range interstellar travel, accurate navigation data is considered one of the most valuable strategic resources in civilized space.

Unauthorized tampering with StarCom navigational systems is punishable under Conclave law.

docs.title: The Historical Directorate
docs.text:
Among StarCom’s most respected divisions is the Historical Directorate.

The Directorate is responsible for: - archival preservation - historical reconstruction - record authentication - cultural preservation - restoration-era documentation

Because much of humanity’s earlier history was lost during the Collapse and the Dark Age, the Directorate frequently works with incomplete or contradictory records.

As a result, many historical subjects remain disputed among scholars.

StarCom officially maintains that preserving surviving records — even fragmentary ones — is essential to preventing civilization from repeating the mistakes of the past.

The Corepedia itself is maintained by the Historical Directorate.

docs.title: Frontier Operations
docs.text:
StarCom maintains installations throughout both the Core and the frontier regions beyond it.

These outposts are responsible for: - maintaining relay systems - coordinating navigation - monitoring trade routes - assisting distressed vessels - observing frontier instability - supporting long-range exploration

Because many frontier systems lack stable local governance, StarCom outposts often serve as important neutral infrastructure hubs in otherwise unstable regions.

docs.title: Security Forces
docs.text:
Although StarCom is not formally considered a military power, the organization maintains extensive security assets to protect critical infrastructure.

These include: - relay defense garrisons - escort fleets - security mechs - patrol craft - rapid response units

StarCom security forces are generally regarded as technologically sophisticated and highly disciplined.

Their stated purpose is defensive rather than territorial.

docs.title: Relationship with the Regency
docs.text:
StarCom maintains close institutional ties with House Caledon and the Star Regency.

The Regency relies heavily upon StarCom infrastructure for: - communications - diplomacy - navigational coordination - intelligence analysis - interstellar administration

Despite this relationship, StarCom remains legally independent from direct Regency control.

This balance between neutrality and cooperation has allowed the organization to maintain trust across otherwise rival political factions.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Within the Core, StarCom is generally viewed as: - reliable - stable - professional - politically neutral - technologically essential

In frontier regions, StarCom often represents the most visible surviving institution of wider civilization.

Conspiracy theories concerning hidden StarCom influence occasionally emerge in fringe political circles, particularly during periods of political unrest.

Such claims are generally regarded by mainstream observers as speculative and unsupported by credible evidence.

Even among critics, however, few seriously dispute the organization’s importance to the continued stability of the Core.

docs.title: Beyond the Maps
docs.text:
Unlike the Great Houses, StarCom maintains extensive exploration and survey programs beyond established Core territories.

As a result, StarCom personnel are often among the first to recognize discrepancies between popular assumptions and reality.

Many within StarCom privately acknowledge that humanity's understanding of its own territory remains incomplete.

New settlements, forgotten colonies, abandoned Imperial installations, and previously unknown navigation routes continue to be discovered with surprising frequency.

This perspective has occasionally placed StarCom at odds with political leaders who prefer to portray the boundaries of civilization as settled and secure.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions between the Great Houses continue to rise and instability spreads throughout frontier space, StarCom’s role has only grown more important.

Today, the organization occupies a position unlike any other institution in known space: - politically neutral yet strategically indispensable - civilian yet deeply influential - decentralized yet foundational to civilization itself

Many historians believe the survival of the modern Core would be impossible without StarCom.

For this reason, the organization’s guiding principle remains unchanged since the earliest years of the Restoration:

“StarCom, To Know the Future.”

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
“We stand where others would flee.” — Riker Konnen

Herzog Riker Konnen and Lady Konnen (center) standing at the center of House Konnen, rulers of the Starcrest Protectorate, surrounded by their bloodline and heirs. Riker's son and heir Korvan Konnen (left), and daughters Lyra and Selene Konnen (far right), stand with the armored ranks of the Storm Guards and the colossal war machines of the Protectorate looming behind them beneath the frozen peaks and fortress-palaces of Khorhall.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Government Type Military Protectorate Capital World Khorhall Population 9.1 billion Environmental Character Harsh frontier worlds Military Specialty Defensive Warfare and Heavy Combat Strongest Alliance Omnisphere Imperium Words “Smoke and Steel.”

The Starcrest Protectorate is one of the five Great Houses of the Core and one of its most respected military powers. Ruled by House Konnen from the fortress world of Khorhall, the Protectorate occupies nearly half of all Core space despite possessing one of the smallest populations among the Great Houses.

Born from the surviving frontier defense forces of a civilization destroyed during the Collapse, the Protectorate built its identity around endurance, sacrifice, and the belief that civilization survives only because someone is willing to defend it. While other powers measure success through wealth, influence, or conquest, the Protectorate places its faith in discipline, duty, and hard work.

The Protectorate is known throughout the Core for its harsh frontier worlds, oversized war machines, industrious population, and unwavering military culture. Supporters view it as civilization's shield. Critics see a stubborn and increasingly old-fashioned state unwilling to adapt to a changing galaxy. Few dispute its resilience.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of the Starcrest Protectorate trace back to the final years of the Collapse. Before the dark age, the worlds that now form the Protectorate belonged to a larger frontier civilization that ultimately failed during the disintegration of the old order. As the surrounding territories collapsed, the surviving frontier defense commands consolidated around heavily fortified systems and defensive strongholds.

Though greatly diminished, these isolated defensive forces endured where many larger powers vanished entirely. Over generations, the survivors gradually expanded outward from these protected regions, rebuilding trade routes, settlements, and military infrastructure across nearby frontier space. This long struggle for survival profoundly shaped Protectorate identity.

The Collapse left a permanent mark upon the Protectorate's collective memory. Where many Core powers see civilization as the natural state of humanity, the Protectorate sees it as something fragile and temporary. To its people, order survives only because ordinary men and women are willing to defend it. Nearly every aspect of Protectorate culture—from its military traditions to its political institutions—can be traced back to this belief.

Unlike powers that emerged through conquest, wealth, or political influence, the Protectorate believes its civilization was earned through sacrifice and endurance.

docs.title: Khorhall
docs.text:
Khorhall, the capital world of the Protectorate, is infamous throughout the Core for its brutal climate, high gravity, and unforgiving environment. Frozen wastelands, towering mountain ranges, violent storms, and sparse habitable regions dominate much of the planet's surface.

Unlike most Core worlds, which fall close to standard gravity, Khorhall possesses a significantly stronger gravitational field and dense atmosphere. Visitors often find even simple physical activity exhausting. For native-born citizens, however, these harsh conditions produce stronger muscles, denser bones, and remarkable endurance. Throughout the Core, Khorhall natives have earned a reputation as some of the toughest soldiers and laborers in human space.

The planet's gravity also provides strategic advantages. Any invading force must contend not only with the Protectorate military, but with the environment itself. The same conditions that challenge visitors also contribute to the production of the dense armors and oversized components used in many Protectorate mech designs.

To outsiders, Khorhall appears harsh and unforgiving. To its citizens, it is a constant reminder that strength is earned, not given.

docs.title: Government
docs.text:
The Protectorate is governed through a highly structured military-civil administration built around the principles of duty, discipline, and public service. While House Konnen sits at the apex of the state, much of the day-to-day governance is handled through regional protector councils, frontier governors, military administrators, and civil service institutions.

Unlike the Union, the Protectorate does not glorify conquest. Military service carries immense prestige, but the armed forces are viewed primarily as guardians rather than conquerors. Protectorate leaders frequently describe their role as preserving civilization rather than expanding it.

This philosophy shapes much of Protectorate governance. Public officials are expected to place service above personal ambition, and competence is often valued more highly than wealth or political influence. While corruption certainly exists, displays of excessive privilege are generally viewed with suspicion by the wider population.

docs.title: House Konnen
docs.text:
The Starcrest Protectorate is ruled by House Konnen, whose sigil depicts a sparking warhammer upon a blue field. Throughout the Core, the house is widely regarded as one of the most honorable noble lineages in existence.

Unlike many noble houses, the Konnens cultivate an image of accessibility and service. Members of the family are frequently seen training alongside soldiers, visiting frontier settlements, and participating directly in military operations. Whether this reputation is entirely deserved is a matter of debate, but it has made House Konnen unusually popular among ordinary citizens.

The current ruler, Herzog Riker Konnen, is respected as both a warrior and a statesman. Known for his forthright nature and unwavering sense of honor, Riker has earned the loyalty of his people and the respect of many rivals. Yet the same qualities that make him admired often leave him vulnerable to political maneuvering by less scrupulous opponents. Riker has little interest in intrigue and prefers direct action to political games.

His son and heir, Korvan Konnen, is among the most popular figures in the Protectorate. Approachable, humble, and fiercely self-critical, Korvan possesses considerable talent as a battlefield commander but lacks the exceptional mech piloting abilities often associated with noble heirs. Rather than hide his shortcomings, he openly acknowledges them and instead focuses upon leadership, physical training, and personal improvement. He can often be found sparring with soldiers or training in the frozen courtyards of Khorhall's great fortress.

Korvan's sisters, Lyra and Selene Konnen, serve within the prestigious Stormguard and are widely regarded as exceptional mech pilots. Together, House Konnen embodies many of the values the Protectorate most admires: honor, humility, duty, resilience, and service before self.

docs.title: Society and Culture
docs.text:
Protectorate culture prizes resilience, discipline, self-reliance, and personal honor. Life on many Protectorate worlds is difficult, and generations of hardship have produced a society that tends to value practical skills over social status and results over appearances.

Compared to the aristocratic worlds of the Sovereignty or the elaborate ceremonies of the Imperium, Protectorate society is notably direct and austere. Public respect is earned through service, competence, sacrifice, and reliability rather than wealth, lineage, or political influence. Military veterans, industrial workers, miners, engineers, and frontier settlers all enjoy considerable social prestige.

The Protectorate is also one of the most industrialized powers in the Core. The phrase "Smoke and Steel" reflects both its military tradition and its industrial identity. Across countless factory worlds and mining colonies, generations of workers have forged the weapons, armor, machines, and infrastructure upon which the state depends.

Many outsiders view Protectorate culture as grim and overly serious. Protectorate citizens often respond that comfort and luxury are poor substitutes for strength and character.

docs.title: Economy
docs.text:
The Protectorate possesses one of the smallest economies among the Great Houses, yet it remains one of the most productive industrial powers in the Core.

Mining, refining, heavy manufacturing, and military production form the backbone of the Protectorate economy. While the Sovereignty dominates finance and commerce, and Orion Corporate leads in advanced technology, the Protectorate specializes in producing the steel, armor, machinery, and industrial goods that keep civilization functioning.

Many Protectorate worlds are resource-rich but environmentally hostile, requiring enormous effort to settle and exploit. This has fostered a culture that values hard work and self-sufficiency while contributing to the state's reputation for resilience.

Much of the Protectorate's economy is effectively organized around supporting its vast military establishment. Mines produce ore for armor and weapons, factories manufacture military equipment, and transportation networks exist primarily to sustain distant frontier garrisons. Critics argue this has limited economic diversification, while supporters view it as a necessary consequence of defending the largest territory in the Core.

Though comparatively poor by Core standards, the Protectorate compensates through industrial output, military efficiency, and a population accustomed to accomplishing much with limited resources.

docs.title: Military
docs.text:
The Protectorate maintains the second largest military force in the Core and is widely regarded as one of its most formidable. Unlike the wealthy Sovereignty or technologically advanced Orion Corporate, the Protectorate relies upon durability, simplicity, and overwhelming battlefield resilience.

Protectorate doctrine emphasizes fortified defense, attritional warfare, battlefield endurance, and the ability to continue fighting under conditions that would break most armies. Their commanders are taught that wars are rarely won by brilliance alone. They are won by the side that remains standing when the smoke clears.

Protectorate mechs are among the largest and heaviest machines fielded by any Great House. While most Core powers regularly deploy machines between 200 and 1000 tons, Protectorate designs rarely fall below 400 tons, and some exceed even the largest machines fielded elsewhere. The legendary Ragnarok, at 1200 tons, remains the largest operational mech ever constructed in the Core.

This philosophy extends to the Protectorate's technology. Rather than pursuing elegant or expensive solutions, Protectorate engineers favor rugged systems that can be maintained under battlefield conditions. Their forces make extensive use of ballistic weaponry, oversized High Energy Cannons, and the famous SmokeShot system. These launchers deploy dense clouds of radar-disrupting smoke across the battlefield, neutralizing many long-range advantages and forcing enemies into close-range engagements.

Once the distance closes, Protectorate mechs employ another uniquely brutal innovation: rocket-assisted battle fists. These oversized melee weapons use short-duration booster systems to propel devastating strikes capable of rivaling the destructive force of far larger weapons. The result is a style of warfare that many outsiders consider primitive, but few survive long enough to mock.

The Protectorate's military strength is especially remarkable given the state's limited economic resources. While it remains one of the poorest Great Houses in the Core, it compensates through discipline, industrial output, and an unwavering willingness to endure hardship. As many Protectorate officers like to say:

"Hit 'em hard, and put 'em down."

docs.title: The Stormguard
docs.text:
The Stormguard are the elite warriors of the Starcrest Protectorate and among the most respected military formations in the Core. Serving as both the personal guard of House Konnen and the Protectorate's premier combat force, the Stormguard are expected to embody the highest ideals of Protectorate society: honor, discipline, courage, and self-sacrifice.

Admission into the Stormguard is extraordinarily competitive. Candidates are drawn from the most accomplished soldiers, officers, and mech pilots throughout the Protectorate, often after years of distinguished service. Technical skill alone is not enough. Prospective Stormguard must also demonstrate exceptional character, leadership, and commitment to duty. Those selected undergo years of additional training before earning the right to wear the hammer insignia of the order.

On the battlefield, the Stormguard are frequently deployed to the most critical sectors of a conflict. They serve as shock troops, battlefield commanders, honor guards, and strategic reserves, often arriving where the fighting is fiercest and the situation most desperate. Their presence is widely regarded as a sign that the Protectorate intends to hold a position at any cost.

Members of House Konnen traditionally serve within the Stormguard, a custom intended to ensure that the ruling family shares the same dangers and hardships as the soldiers they command. As a result, it is not uncommon to find members of the Protectorate's nobility fighting alongside common soldiers on the front lines.

Throughout the Core, the Stormguard possess a near-legendary reputation for steadfastness under fire. Stories of Stormguard units holding impossible defensive positions, fighting while surrounded, or sacrificing themselves to protect civilians have become part of Protectorate folklore. Whether every tale is true matters little to the people of the Protectorate. The Stormguard represent the ideal to which every Protectorate soldier aspires.

"When the storm comes, we will stand. When the line breaks, we will hold. When all else falls, we will become the wall." — Stormguard oath

docs.title: Mercenary Relations
docs.text:
The Protectorate maintains a reputation for honesty in its dealings with mercenary companies. While few contractors become wealthy working Protectorate contracts, many appreciate the straightforward nature of Protectorate negotiations.

The state's limited financial resources mean that advance payments and completion bonuses are often modest compared to those offered by the Sovereignty or Orion Corporate. To compensate, Protectorate contracts frequently provide generous salvage rights and unusually high deployment allowances. Mercenary commanders are often permitted to field heavier forces than would be authorized elsewhere in the Core.

As a result, many veteran mercenaries view Protectorate contracts as dependable rather than lucrative. The pay may be limited, but the terms are generally fair, the objectives clearly defined, and the Protectorate is known for honoring its agreements.

As one common mercenary saying goes:

"The Protectorate won't make you rich, but they'll never cheat you."

docs.title: Politics and Foreign Relations
docs.text:
The Protectorate maintains close ties with the Omnisphere Imperium, with whom it shares many cultural values including honor, duty, military service, and respect for historical tradition. Though the two powers differ in important ways, they are generally regarded as reliable allies.

Relations with the Confederate Vanguard Union are more complicated. Both powers respect military strength and discipline, yet the Protectorate frequently views the Union's expansionist tendencies with suspicion. Union leaders, in turn, often regard the Protectorate as overly cautious and resistant to change.

The Protectorate's most strained relationship is with the Sovereignty. Many Protectorate citizens view the wealthy aristocrats of Lumina as detached from the realities faced by frontier populations and deeply distrust what they perceive as Helios political manipulation and intrigue. Sovereignty leaders often respond by viewing the Protectorate as stubborn, provincial, and overly reliant upon outdated traditions.

Relations with Orion Corporate remain generally stable, though some Protectorate officials express concern regarding Orion's growing dependence on automation, advanced algorithms, and technological systems.

Throughout the Core, the Protectorate is widely respected for its reliability, resilience, and military professionalism. Even its rivals often acknowledge that few powers have done more to preserve stability along the dangerous frontiers of civilized space.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions throughout the Core continue to intensify, the Protectorate increasingly warns against complacency, political arrogance, and internal division.

Protectorate leadership maintains that the Core has grown dangerously comfortable during centuries of relative stability.

For many within the Protectorate, the lessons of the Collapse remain painfully simple:

civilization survives only so long as someone is willing to stand the line.

docs.title: Other Beliefs
docs.text:
Beyond the major religious traditions of the Core exist countless smaller sects, philosophical movements, mystery cults, and esoteric belief systems. While most possess relatively few followers compared to the Pantheon of Stars, the Eternal Flame, or the Blood of Titans, many have exerted significant influence upon Core philosophy, art, science, and politics throughout history.

Some are regarded as harmless intellectual traditions, while others are viewed with suspicion due to their association with fringe cults or radical explorers.

docs.title: The Veil of Eternity
docs.text:
The Veil of Eternity teaches that reality is separated from the realm of higher beings by a metaphysical barrier known as the Veil.

Followers believe certain individuals possess the ability to glimpse beyond this boundary through visions, meditation, mathematics, or altered states of consciousness. Some sects associate Hypercomplex Numbers with humanity’s limited ability to perceive truths hidden behind the Veil.

The religion places heavy emphasis on intuition, prophecy, symbolism, and spiritual perception.

docs.title: The Great Dreamer
docs.text:
One of the oldest surviving philosophical traditions in the Core, the Great Dreamer teaches that the universe exists within the dream of a vast sleeping consciousness.

Followers disagree on whether the Dreamer is divine, cosmic, or purely symbolic. Many interpret the belief metaphorically, viewing reality itself as interconnected layers of perception and consciousness.

The faith remains especially influential among artists, philosophers, mathematicians, and scientists interested in the deeper implications of Hypercomplex theory and temporal physics.

docs.title: The Eternal Cycle
docs.text:
The Eternal Cycle teaches that the universe endlessly repeats through alternating periods of creation and destruction.

Two cosmic principles govern this cycle: - the Weaver, associated with creation and order - the Unraveler, associated with decay and collapse

Followers believe civilizations rise and fall endlessly across cosmic history. Many interpret the decline of the Empire and the instability of the modern Core as evidence that humanity currently exists in the later stages of a cycle.

The Beyond is often viewed within this tradition as a region where reality itself begins to unravel.

docs.title: The Hollow Crown
docs.text:
The Hollow Crown is a highly philosophical belief system centered on themes of emptiness, uncertainty, and unrealized potential.

Followers teach that absence itself contains infinite possibility and that true understanding emerges only when individuals confront the void within themselves and the universe.

The religion has heavily influenced minimalist architecture, abstract art, and existential philosophy throughout portions of the Core.

Some sects believe the Beyond itself represents the domain of the Hollow Crown.

docs.title: The Fractured God
docs.text:
The Fractured God tradition teaches that the universe was created when a divine entity shattered itself into countless fragments.

Every living being, world, and star is believed to contain a piece of this original consciousness. Followers seek meaning through self-discovery, artistic expression, and the pursuit of unity amid fragmentation.

This belief system remains especially popular among intellectuals, scientists, and artists seeking philosophical reconciliation between spirituality and modern physics.

docs.title: The Silent Choir
docs.text:
The Silent Choir teaches that reality is sustained by inaudible cosmic harmonies produced by unseen entities dwelling between the stars.

Followers believe these harmonies shape gravity, time, orbital motion, and the structure of existence itself. Rituals often involve music, resonance, synchronized movement, and attempts to detect hidden cosmic frequencies through advanced technology.

The tradition has inspired major schools of music and performance art throughout the Core.

docs.title: Older Myths
docs.text:
Beyond organized religions exist numerous fragmented myths and more ancient traditions. These stories speak of incomprehensible entities, forgotten civilizations, impossible geometries, and regions of space where reality itself breaks down. Among the most famous legends are:

  • the Yellow King
  • the Queen at the End of Time
  • the Whispering Void
  • the Traveler and the Shadow

Most citizens dismiss these tales as superstition or fringe mysticism. Nevertheless they maintain a devoted following among artists, explorers, philosophers, and isolated frontier cults.

docs.title: The Lost History of Humanity
docs.text:
Very little is known with certainty about the earliest origins of humanity. Though the Core spans hundreds of star systems and contains billions of inhabitants, no surviving historical record definitively explains where humanity first originated or how it first spread among the stars.

No verified archaeological or fossil evidence concerning humanity’s true point of origin has ever been discovered within the Core. As a result, the earliest history of civilization remains one of the greatest unresolved mysteries in human history.

The destruction of historical records during centuries of warfare, political collapse, and interstellar fragmentation has left only scattered fragments of humanity’s distant past. Ancient archives recovered from Imperial ruins are often incomplete, contradictory, or heavily corrupted. Entire historical eras remain known only through myth, fragmented data caches, oral traditions, and competing political narratives.

Central to many of these mysteries are the so-called Ancient Engineers, a shadowy civilization or group credited with developing many of the technologies that continue to sustain modern human civilization. Among their greatest legacies are Hypercomplex Numbers, the mathematical framework underlying faster-than-light travel, jump navigation, and numerous advanced technologies still used throughout the Core.

Modern scientists and mathematicians can utilize Hypercomplex systems but do not fully understand why they function. Even the most advanced researchers struggle to comprehend the deeper mathematics involved. Many technologies derived from Hypercomplex principles continue to operate reliably despite incomplete theoretical understanding.

Little is known about the Ancient Engineers themselves. No universally accepted historical record survives describing who they were, where they came from, or how their civilization disappeared. Numerous competing theories exist throughout the Core.

Different factions throughout the Core interpret these mysteries according to their own political and cultural interests. Certain governments and organizations actively search for lost historical archives and Ancient Engineer facilities, believing such discoveries could grant immense technological or political advantages. Others deliberately suppress archaeological discoveries or conflicting historical evidence to preserve ideological stability and public order.

The mystery of humanity’s origins carries significant cultural importance throughout civilized space. For many citizens the uncertainty reinforces the belief that humanity exists within a long cycle of rise and decline stretching far beyond recorded history. Others view the search for humanity’s true origins as one of civilization’s highest callings.

Despite centuries of research and exploration, humanity remains fundamentally uncertain of where it came from, who first unlocked the stars, or whether the civilization of the modern Core is truly humanity’s first great age—or merely the latest.

docs.title: Religions and Beliefs of the Core
docs.text:
Religion within the Core is highly diverse and fragmented, shaped by thousands of years of political upheaval, lost history, scientific advancement, and interstellar expansion. While no single faith dominates human civilization, countless religious movements, philosophical traditions, mystery cults, and spiritual schools exist throughout the Core and Fringe alike.

Most modern belief systems attempt to answer the same unresolved questions: where humanity came from, who the Ancient Engineers were, and what lies beyond the known boundaries of space and time.

Unlike many ancient civilizations, the Core does not sharply separate science from spirituality. Concepts such as Hypercomplex Numbers, faster-than-light travel, artificial gravity, and the temporal distortions associated with the Beyond are so poorly understood that they frequently blur the line between scientific theory and metaphysical belief.

Though the Core is generally tolerant of differing faiths, religious and philosophical disagreements occasionally influence political rivalries, cultural movements, and factional propaganda.

docs.title: The Pantheon of Stars
docs.text:
The Pantheon of Stars is the most widespread religious tradition within the Core. It teaches that the universe is governed by a collection of godlike entities associated with celestial forces and cosmic phenomena.

Among the most widely recognized figures are:

  • Solara, goddess of stars, fusion, and life
  • Voidus, lord of black holes, entropy, and the Beyond
  • Naviga, patron of exploration, mathematics, and jump travel
  • Chronos, keeper of time and causality

Most modern followers interpret these gods symbolically rather than literally, viewing them as metaphors for natural forces beyond human comprehension. Traditional sects however still conduct prayers, rituals, and offerings before dangerous journeys, major military operations, or difficult jump calculations.

The Pantheon remains especially popular among navigators, pilots, explorers, and frontier populations.

docs.title: The Eternal Flame
docs.text:
The Eternal Flame is the dominant spiritual tradition of the Omnisphere Imperium. Followers worship a primordial cosmic fire believed to burn at the center of existence.

Imperial mythology teaches that the founders of the Imperium stole a fragment of this divine flame and used it to forge their civilization. The flame symbolizes endurance, authority, sacrifice, and dynastic continuity.

Imperial ceremonies frequently involve sacred fires, torch processions, and rites of endurance. The faith heavily influences Imperial art, architecture, military symbolism, and rulership traditions.

docs.title: The Blood of Titans
docs.text:
