Popular within the Protectorate, the Blood of Titans teaches that humanity descends from ancient godlike beings known as the Titans.

According to tradition, the Titans once ruled the universe before being overthrown by younger gods. Their scattered essence survives within chosen bloodlines, granting strength, resilience, and legitimacy to those worthy of wielding their inheritance.

The belief system strongly emphasizes personal strength, endurance, leadership, and martial honor. Ceremonial reforging rituals and physical trials remain central components of Protectorate culture.

docs.title: Lesser Faiths and Esoteric Beliefs
docs.text: Beyond the major religious traditions of the Core exist countless smaller sects, philosophical movements, mystery cults, and esoteric belief systems. See Lesser Religions.

docs.title: Religion in Modern Core Society
docs.text:
Most citizens of the Core do not follow religion in a rigid or institutional sense. Belief systems are often highly personal, philosophical, or symbolic rather than dogmatic. It is common for individuals to combine elements from multiple traditions simultaneously.

Scientific advancement has not diminished spirituality within the Core so much as transformed it. The mysteries surrounding humanity’s origins, the Ancient Engineers, and Hypercomplex mathematics ensure that questions concerning existence, fate, and the nature of reality remain deeply important throughout civilized space.

For many citizens, religion serves less as a strict explanation of the universe and more as a means of confronting humanity’s smallness within it.

docs.title: Mechs of the Core
docs.text:
"Fleets threaten worlds. Mechs take them." — Military proverb of the Core

The Core is dominated by ancient mech traditions, house arsenals, mercenary salvage, and the constant pressure of war. The designs below are grouped by origin rather than by current owner: common designs remain in their own category, while faction-specific machines are listed under the Great House or state most associated with their development and doctrine.

docs.title: Why Mechs Dominate the Battlefield
docs.text:
For over a thousand years, military planners have repeatedly predicted the end of the mech. Every time, reality has proven otherwise. While tanks, aircraft, and conventional vehicles remain important parts of modern armies, the mech occupies a unique role that no other platform has successfully replaced. A single trained pilot can command hundreds or even thousands of tons of armored firepower while retaining the flexibility, initiative, and adaptability of an individual soldier.

Mechs thrive because they solve many of the problems that limit conventional armies. They can operate in cities, mountains, forests, swamps, industrial zones, and other terrain that hinders tracked or wheeled vehicles. Their compact fusion cores allow them to operate for months or even years without refueling, dramatically reducing logistical demands and enabling operations deep behind enemy lines. Most designs can be deployed directly from dropships, or even orbital drops, and require far less supporting infrastructure than traditional armored formations.

Perhaps most importantly, mechs concentrate an extraordinary amount of military capability into a single platform. A mech can engage enemy armor, infantry, fortifications, aircraft, and other mechs without requiring specialized variants or extensive support. While conventional forces often rely upon separate formations of tanks, infantry, artillery, reconnaissance units, and air support working together, a single mech formation can perform many of those battlefield roles on its own. This flexibility allows military commanders to deploy decisive combat power rapidly and with far fewer personnel than a comparable conventional force.

Equally important, mechs are resilient, self-sufficient, and highly adaptable. Damaged machines can often be repaired in the field, rebuilt from salvaged components, or kept operational long after conventional vehicles would be abandoned. While a mech is vastly more expensive than a conventional vehicle, military planners generally regard the investment as worthwhile due to the extraordinary flexibility and battlefield dominance a single mech can provide. Their combination of mobility, endurance, firepower, and battlefield presence has ensured their dominance across the worlds of the Core for centuries.

docs.title: Air Power and the Mech Revolution
docs.text:
Military historians often compare the modern mech to the combat aircraft of earlier eras.

Before the rise of mech warfare, air superiority was widely regarded as the decisive factor in major conflicts. Aircraft possessed unmatched speed, reconnaissance capability, and striking power. Armored formations, logistics hubs, and frontline positions could be devastated by concentrated air attacks long before ground forces arrived.

Despite their advantages, aircraft suffered from fundamental limitations. They required extensive support infrastructure, including airfields, maintenance facilities, fuel depots, spare parts, and highly trained ground crews. Aircraft could remain over a battlefield only briefly before returning to base to refuel or rearm, and even heavily armed strike craft were vulnerable to anti-air systems, interceptor aircraft, and adverse environmental conditions.

The emergence of modern war mechs fundamentally altered this equation.

A mech possesses many of the battlefield advantages traditionally associated with aircraft while eliminating many of their weaknesses. Mechs deliver extraordinary firepower, advanced sensors, rapid tactical response, and overwhelming battlefield presence without requiring permanent access to airfields or extensive logistical networks. Capable of remaining operational in the field for weeks, months, or even years with periodic resupply and maintenance, they can operate in adverse weather, occupy strategic terrain, secure objectives, and maintain a continuous military presence.

As a result, military doctrine throughout the Core increasingly shifted away from reliance upon air superiority as the primary determinant of victory. Aircraft remain valuable for reconnaissance, rapid transport, interception, and specialized strike missions, but modern commanders generally regard mechs as the decisive instrument of battlefield control. No other military platform can destroy defenses, defend objectives, and occupy territory simultaneously.

docs.title: Pilot Survival and Long-Term Operations
docs.text:
Modern mechs are designed not only as combat platforms but also as long-duration operational shelters capable of sustaining their pilots during extended deployments.

Because mech units frequently operate far from established supply lines, most military and mercenary designs include comprehensive survival equipment intended to support pilots for weeks after becoming isolated from friendly forces. Standard equipment typically includes emergency rations, water reserves, purification systems, medical supplies, shelter components, environmental protection gear, navigation tools, communications equipment, and personal defense weapons.

Ejection systems are generally designed to carry the most critical survival equipment with the pilot. Most modern escape pods contain emergency food supplies, medical kits, distress beacons, survival tools, and environmental protection systems sufficient to sustain a pilot until rescue arrives.

Even when damaged or immobilized, mechs often serve as highly effective survival shelters. Functional reactors can continue supplying power for life-support systems, communications equipment, environmental controls, and sensor arrays long after a machine is no longer combat-capable. In many circumstances, remaining inside a disabled mech is significantly safer than attempting to reach civilization on foot.

This self-sufficiency has shaped military doctrine throughout the Core. A disabled mech is not necessarily considered a lost asset. Recovery teams routinely retrieve damaged machines weeks or even months after combat operations conclude, provided the pilot survives and the chassis remains recoverable. Salvage, reconstruction, and battlefield recovery have therefore become integral parts of mech warfare and contribute significantly to the remarkable longevity of many famous machines.

docs.title: History and Origins
docs.text:
The origins of the modern mech predate both the Great Restoration and the rise of the Empire itself.

The earliest predecessors were crude machines: powered industrial exoskeletons used for heavy labor, mining, construction, and hazardous environments. During periods of conflict, some of these machines were armored and armed, producing a variety of primitive combat walkers that historians often describe as little more than mobile pillboxes. Though impressive for their time, these early designs were slow, cumbersome, and of limited military value.

The true revolution came centuries later with the rise of the civilization that would eventually become the Omnisphere Imperium.

Imperial engineers pioneered several technologies that transformed the concept of the mech from an engineering curiosity into the dominant battlefield platform of human civilization. Advances in lightweight high-strength alloys dramatically reduced structural mass while increasing durability. Neural interface systems allowed pilots to control machines with unprecedented precision and responsiveness. Most importantly, the development of compact fusion reactors provided enough energy to power massive armored machines while simultaneously supporting advanced mobility systems and energy weapons.

Combined together, these innovations produced the first true war mechs.

By modern standards these early machines would likely be classified as medium mechs, yet their impact on warfare was extraordinary. They possessed mobility that conventional armored vehicles could not match, firepower capable of reducing fortifications, and operational endurance measured in months rather than days. Defenses that had dominated warfare for generations suddenly became vulnerable.

The strategic consequences were immediate. The Imperium expanded rapidly, leveraging its technological superiority to dominate neighboring powers and establish itself as the largest and most influential state of its age. As Imperial influence spread, so too did mech technology. Rival nations began developing their own designs, and within a few centuries war mechs had become a common feature of military forces throughout known space.

Today, mechs are so deeply integrated into military doctrine that many citizens struggle to imagine warfare without them. Entire civilizations have risen and fallen beneath their shadow.

docs.title: The Lytherian Debate
docs.text:
Not all historians agree that the Imperium truly invented the first war mech.

The traditional Imperial account credits Lytherian engineers with the creation of the first practical combat walker and the technologies that made modern mech warfare possible. This remains the dominant historical interpretation.

Some scholars, however, question whether the early Imperium possessed the scientific and industrial base necessary to achieve such a dramatic technological breakthrough independently. Alternative theories suggest that the Lytherians may have acquired, adapted, or reverse-engineered earlier technologies developed elsewhere.

A few historians have even proposed that primitive mech technology may predate the Imperium entirely, originating from civilizations whose names have long since been lost.

No conclusive evidence has emerged to settle the debate. Whatever their ultimate origin, the appearance of the first true war mechs permanently transformed human history and altered the balance of power throughout the Core.

docs.title: Core Mech Catalog
docs.text: Category Designs Notes Common Core Mechs 12 These widely circulated designs are not exclusive to any one Great House. They appear across mercenary rosters, house garrisons, security contracts, and older battlefield formations throughout the Core. Confederate Vanguard Union Mechs 18 Union designs emphasize durability, direct firepower, disciplined battlefield roles, and rugged logistics. Their mechs tend to favor practical weapons, thick armor, and formations built around coordinated pressure. Helios Sovereignty Mechs 18 Sovereignty machines are prestige weapons as much as battlefield assets: imposing, polished, and expensive, with elite formations built around decisive strikes and symbols of noble power. Omnisphere Imperium Mechs 18 Imperium mechs reflect ancient traditions of martial spectacle and aristocratic warfare. Their machines often combine elegant mobility with vicious close-range killing power. Orion Corporate Mechs 18 Corporate mechs showcase Orion’s technical edge: prismatic weapons, advanced shielding, predictive systems, and carefully engineered battlefield efficiency. Starcrest Protectorate Mechs 18 Protectorate designs are brutal and workmanlike, favoring armor, intimidation, and close-range violence. Many are built to survive ugly fights on industrial worlds and contested borders.

docs.title: Battlefield Roles
docs.text:
Military organizations throughout the Core classify mechs according to their intended battlefield function. Although terminology varies somewhat between factions, the following role designations are widely recognized by military planners, procurement agencies, mercenary companies, and StarCom analysts.

Role Description Scout Fast, lightly armed machines optimized for reconnaissance, screening operations, target acquisition, and pursuit. Scouts are often expected to avoid prolonged engagements whenever possible. Striker Mobile attack mechs designed to rapidly engage vulnerable targets before repositioning. Strikers typically emphasize speed and concentrated firepower over durability. Cavalry Striker Highly mobile strike platforms equipped with jump capability. These machines specialize in flanking maneuvers, rapid redeployment, and exploiting weaknesses in enemy formations. Line Mech General-purpose combat machines intended to form the backbone of military formations. Line mechs balance mobility, protection, and firepower while remaining adaptable to a wide range of battlefield conditions. Brawler Heavily armored close- to medium-range combatants designed to absorb punishment while maintaining offensive pressure. Brawlers are common among forces that favor aggressive engagements and sustained combat. Fire Support Long-range combat platforms armed with missiles, rockets, artillery systems, or other stand-off weapons. Fire support units provide battlefield coverage from positions behind the main line of engagement. Missile Skirmisher Mobile missile or rocket carriers that combine indirect fire capability with high mobility. These machines rely on maneuver, harassment, and positional advantage rather than direct confrontation. Assault Heavy frontline machines intended to break enemy formations, anchor defensive positions, or spearhead major offensives. Assault mechs often serve as the centerpiece of battlefield operations. Shielded Assault A specialized assault classification most commonly associated with Orion Corporate designs. These machines combine heavy firepower with advanced shield systems, allowing them to absorb extraordinary levels of punishment. Superheavy Assault The largest combat machines regularly fielded by modern militaries. Rare, expensive, and strategically significant, superheavy assault mechs are often treated as operational assets rather than ordinary battlefield units.

Although these classifications are widely used, battlefield roles frequently overlap. Experienced commanders often employ machines outside their intended doctrine, and many of the most famous mechs in history achieved distinction precisely because they excelled in roles for which they were never originally designed.

docs.title: Mech Pilots
docs.text:
Mech pilots occupy a unique place within Core society. Though vastly outnumbered by conventional soldiers, pilots command the most powerful battlefield assets available to modern militaries and are widely regarded as elite personnel.

Most Great Houses maintain dedicated military academies that train pilots over periods ranging from one to two years. These programs combine simulator instruction, live-fire exercises, battlefield doctrine, logistics, leadership, and conventional military education. Advanced simulators and training mechs allow cadets to participate in large-scale war games before entering active service.

While all major powers produce capable pilots, training philosophies vary. The Confederate Vanguard Union emphasizes discipline, logistics, and combined-arms doctrine. The Omnisphere Imperium prioritizes individual excellence and martial achievement. The Starcrest Protectorate is known for harsh training regimes designed to produce resilient pilots capable of enduring extreme battlefield conditions. The Helios Sovereignty maintains prestigious academies, though critics occasionally point to the influence of wealth and family connections. Orion Corporate programs are technologically sophisticated but lack the centuries of combat experience available to some rival powers.

Outside the Great Houses, standards vary dramatically. Mercenary companies may spend months training recruits, while bandit groups often place barely qualified pilots into combat-ready machines.

The public image of mech pilots is overwhelmingly positive. Popular media frequently portrays them as heroes, adventurers, or modern-day knights. Many pilots adopt callsigns, cultivate rivalries, and participate in a highly competitive culture reminiscent of fighter aces from earlier eras.

Despite the dangers of combat, piloting is considered a relatively survivable profession compared to infantry service or aerospace combat. Modern ejection systems, battlefield recovery operations, and longstanding customs surrounding escape pods have contributed to career lengths that often span decades.

docs.title: Salvage and Recovery
docs.text:
"A destroyed mech is a tragedy. An unrecovered mech is a crime."

— Mercenary Guild saying

Throughout the Core, battlefield recovery and salvage operations are considered essential components of warfare. The immense cost of mech construction, combined with centuries of technological decline and resource scarcity, has ensured that few machines are ever considered truly disposable.

Most military contracts include detailed salvage clauses specifying ownership rights for disabled enemy equipment, battlefield debris, spare parts, and intact chassis. Among mercenary companies, salvage rights are often considered nearly as important as financial compensation.

Dedicated recovery crews accompany most major military formations. These specialists retrieve damaged mechs, recover ejected pilots, secure escape pods, and transport salvageable equipment back to repair facilities. In many campaigns, recovery operations continue for weeks after active combat has ended.

The value of salvage extends beyond economics. Many older machines incorporate components manufactured during the Restoration or even earlier periods of history. Such equipment is often impossible to reproduce using modern industrial techniques and is therefore highly prized by militaries, corporations, collectors, and mercenary companies alike.

As a result, the destruction of a mech rarely marks the end of its service life. Chassis are rebuilt, components are transplanted, and battle-damaged machines are restored repeatedly across decades or even centuries of warfare. Some famous mechs have been destroyed and reconstructed so many times that little of the original machine remains beyond its name and legacy.

docs.text: These widely circulated designs are not exclusive to any one Great House. They appear across mercenary rosters, house garrisons, security contracts, and older battlefield formations throughout the Core.

docs.title: Assault
docs.text:
• Bulldog (BD-770, 700 tons): The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.
  • Marksman (MK-350, 700 tons): The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.
  • Gladiator (GL-882, 750 tons): A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.
  • Kingsguard (KG-950, 800 tons): The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Brawler
docs.text: • Lineman (LM-30C, 500 tons): Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Fire Support
docs.text: • Longstrike (LG-210, 600 tons): An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Line Mech
docs.text:
• Yeoman (YM-10F, 450 tons): The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.
  • Lancer (LNC-220, 550 tons): The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Missile Skirmisher
docs.text: • Champion (CH-70K, 600 tons): Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Scout
docs.text: • Hornet (HN-120, 200 tons): One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Striker
docs.text:
• Lynx (LNX-40E, 300 tons): The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.
  • Outrider (OR-640, 400 tons): Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.text: One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty Mechs
docs.text:
→ See also: The Helios Sovereignty

Sovereignty machines are prestige weapons as much as battlefield assets: imposing, polished, and expensive, with elite formations built around decisive strikes and symbols of noble power.

Sovereignty mechs are slightly easier to transport and deploy than comparable mechs from other factions. Some Sovereignty mechs also have jump capabilities.

docs.title: Assault
docs.text:
• Dark Knight (DK-2R, 700 tons): The Dark Knight carries an aura of dread deliberately cultivated by Sovereignty engineers. Long-range energy weapons paired with a heavy laser give it punishing reach, while its sinister name and black-lacquered armor turn it into an icon of fear. Where the Dark Knight walks, rebellion tends to wither.
  • Excalibur (EX-3R, 750 tons): Few names carry more weight than Excalibur, and the Sovereignty bestowed it upon this duelist’s chassis. Armed with MACs, lasers, and rockets, it was designed for decisive, theatrical victories. Excaliburs often lead tournament fields, their pilots draped in noble colors, striking as much for honor as for conquest.
  • Lionheart (LHR-5T, 800 tons): The Lionheart epitomizes Sovereignty nobility: powerful, courageous, and unflinching. With heavy lasers, rockets, and long-range cannons, it dominates battlefields while bearing crests of golden lions. More than a weapon, it is a living coat of arms, proof that Sovereignty bloodlines rule by strength and valor.

docs.title: Brawler
docs.text:
• Legionnaire (LGN-A, 500 tons): The Legionnaire was mass-produced during Sovereignty campaigns to enforce order in the Core. Fitted with a medium MAC, missiles, and lasers, it is a versatile soldier’s machine. The name recalls the legions of ancient empires—disciplined, unstoppable, and loyal to the Sovereignty above all else.
  • Herald (HRL-6D, 550 tons): Where nobles feared assassination or insurrection, Heralds were deployed. Heavy armor and powerful missile barrages made them excellent protectors of VIPs and sacred sites. To this day, Sovereignty rulers keep Heralds in their personal retinues, their name a constant reminder of paternal care—and veiled threat.
  • Paladin (PLDN-00C, 550 tons): A mech wrapped in religious and chivalric symbolism, the Paladin was designed to embody the Sovereignty’s holy mandate. Rockets for righteous fury, lasers for guiding light, and a MAC to deliver judgment. In battle, it is as much a sermon as a weapon, reminding allies and enemies alike of the Sovereignty’s divine right to rule.

docs.title: Cavalry Striker
docs.text:
• Icarus (ICS-3G, 250 tons): The Icarus embodies Sovereignty daring and hubris—fast, jump-capable, and armed with rockets for dramatic strikes. Though lightly armored, its theatrics earned it glory in countless tournaments. Pilots know the cautionary tale of its name, but many still fly it recklessly, chasing the sun of renown.
  • Messenger (MSG-1R, 250 tons): A swift harasser and scout, the Messenger relies on jump jets and missile bursts to unsettle lighter foes. Its role is to deliver a clear message on the battlefield—that Sovereignty forces are advancing, and their arrival is inevitable.
  • Pegasus (PGS-H, 350 tons): Inspired by myth, the Pegasus was designed as an aerial duelist, its jump jets giving it a graceful leap few others could match. Bristling with light and medium lasers, it dances around heavier foes, striking with elegance. In parades as in battle, the Pegasus is prized as a symbol of Sovereignty’s radiant majesty.
  • Warden (WRD-N1, 450 tons): Where the Sentry watches, the Warden intervenes. Agile for its size, it was built to guard noble households and convoy routes. Its heavy laser and missiles strike with authority, while its name reinforces Sovereignty propaganda: every subject is safe under noble protection—so long as they remain loyal.

docs.title: Fire Support
docs.text:
• Sentry (SN-21, 400 tons): The Sentry was built for watchful dominance, its long-range high energy cannon and missile banks intended to hold borders in clear view. It lacks mobility, but its very presence is a deterrent, projecting Sovereignty vigilance. Many noble estates still keep a Sentry posted at their gates, a statue of steel as much as a guardian.
  • Protector (PRO-T1, 600 tons): The Protector was created as a battlefield shield for Sovereignty banners. Mixing heavy lasers with rockets and missiles, it can blunt enemy advances while rallying allies. Noble houses often decorate Protectors with gilded heraldry, ensuring that their role as both bulwark and symbol is unmistakable.

docs.title: Line Mech
docs.text: • Lamplighter (LLT-6Y, 450 tons): A mech named for illumination, the Lamplighter was created to pierce darkness both literal and symbolic. Its long-range energy cannon and array of lasers allow it to light up a battlefield from afar. To the Sovereignty, deploying Lamplighters is an act of enlightenment, burning away shadows of rebellion with radiant fire.

docs.title: Superheavy Assault
docs.text:
• Lancelot (LNC-10T, 850 tons): The Lancelot was built to combine elegance with battlefield dominance. Its MAC and missile systems give it both reach and crushing power, while its lasers keep foes at bay. No Sovereignty parade is complete without a Lancelot striding at the fore, the paragon of chivalric warfare.
  • Colossus (COL-U5, 900 tons): Massive and imposing, the Colossus is a walking fortress designed to awe as much as destroy. Mounting a heavy MAC alongside multiple lasers, it crushes resistance under overwhelming firepower. Wherever a Colossus is deployed, it is as much a proclamation as an army—Sovereignty power manifest in steel.
  • Great Sword (SWRD-001, 900 tons): The Great Sword is the Sovereignty’s answer to the Union’s brute heavies: less subtle, more theatrical. Quad MACs and paired heavy lasers strike with the force of a descending blade. Its very silhouette evokes a weapon raised for the killing blow, a message that Sovereignty justice is swift and merciless.
  • Monarch (MON-A7, 950 tons): The Monarch was never just a war machine—it was designed as a throne of iron for Sovereignty lords. With missile arrays and heavy lasers, it wields devastating power while carrying the regalia of its house. To see a Monarch on the field is to witness Sovereignty authority embodied, its very name leaving no doubt of who rules.
  • Olympian (OLYM-D, 1000 tons): The Olympian is the pinnacle of Sovereignty design philosophy: mythic in name, majestic in presence, and terrible in power. With MACs, long-range cannons, missiles, and lasers, it commands every battlefield range. Sovereignty propaganda hails it as proof of their divine heritage—an Olympian god made metal, standing above all mortal challengers.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Where the Sentry watches, the Warden intervenes. Agile for its size, it was built to guard noble households and convoy routes. Its heavy laser and missiles strike with authority, while its name reinforces Sovereignty propaganda: every subject is safe under noble protection—so long as they remain loyal.

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium Mechs
docs.text:
→ See also: The Omnisphere Imperium

Imperium mechs reflect ancient traditions of martial spectacle and aristocratic warfare. Their machines often combine elegant mobility with vicious close-range killing power.

Imperium mechs utilize advance arcanite engines making their mechs faster than comparable mechs from other factions.

docs.title: Assault
docs.text:
• Nightwing (NGT-W4, 700 tons): The Nightwing prowls battlefields like a shadow, unleashing rockets, flamethrowers, and blaster fire before fading back into darkness. Heavily armed for its size, it embodies the Imperium’s preference for intimidation as well as annihilation. Its name, like its silhouette, evokes wings spread across the night sky.
  • Onagor (ONA-37, 800 tons): The Onagor was built as a shock unit, carrying heavy vulkans and blasters to overwhelm enemies with sheer volume of fire. Though slower than some peers, it compensates with raw, thunderous destruction. Onagor is an ancient Imperium name, drawn from pre-unification myths describing a colossal entity whose advance was said to end wars by erasing the ground they were fought on.
  • Praetorian (PRA-97, 800 tons): The Praetorian is one of the Imperium’s most feared and recognizable war machines. With paired heavy energy cannons, blasters, and scorchers, it combines versatile firepower with an imposing silhouette that dominates the field. More than a frontline brawler, the Praetorian is a commander’s mech, deployed to anchor formations and inspire dread in equal measure.

docs.title: Line Mech
docs.text:
• Drakkon (DRK-82, 600 tons): A monstrous war machine, the Drakkon is drenched in flamethrowers and blasters, spewing destruction in every direction. It earned its name not from myth, but from its battlefield presence—unyielding, terrifying, and impossible to ignore. To the Imperium, the Drakkon is both dragon and executioner.
  • Warclaw (WCL-61, 650 tons): The Warclaw was commissioned during the Imperium’s early expansion campaigns, when rapid assaults proved insufficient against fortified resistance. Designed for prolonged engagements, it combines blasters, Vulkan cannons, and scorcher systems to apply constant pressure while advancing methodically. The Warclaw is deployed when territory must be taken and held, not merely breached.

docs.title: Missile Skirmisher
docs.text: • Ordosmech (ORD-07, 650 tons): The Ordosmech embodies the Imperium’s martial faith, built as a crusader’s machine. Armed with Vulkan cannons, rockets, and blasters, it balances mass firepower with religious symbolism. Pilots clad their Ordosmechs in heraldry and ritual markings, treating each battle as both ritual war and ancestral duty.

docs.title: Scout
docs.text:
• Zephyr (ZPH-01, 200 tons): A relic of the Imperium’s dueling traditions, the Zephyr was designed for blinding speed, able to outpace heavier mechs with ease. Armed with a blaster and light lasers, it darts in, strikes, and vanishes, leaving foes bewildered. Its name speaks to its nature: swift as wind, impossible to grasp.
  • Drake (DRK-06, 250 tons): The Drake was crafted in homage to the Imperium’s dragon symbology, its blaster and twin scorchers breathing fire across enemy lines. Lightly armored but terrifying up close, it thrives in ambushes and raids. Pilots often paint their machines with scaled patterns, reinforcing its image as a dragon reborn in steel.
  • Ronin (RON-12, 250 tons): A mech for warriors without masters, the Ronin was adopted by Imperium mercenary bands during the Old Imperium. With rockets for sudden fury and a blaster for dueling, it embodies the lone fighter’s creed: self-reliance, defiance, and a readiness to sell its blade to any cause.
  • Pyrefox (PYX-53, 300 tons): True to its name, the Pyrefox engulfs battlefields with blasters and fire weapons, making it a terror in close combat. Developed to cleanse rebellious enclaves, it carries both symbolic and practical weight—reminding enemies that resisting the Imperium will always end in flames.

docs.title: Striker
docs.text:
• Incursor (ICR-99, 350 tons): A reckless raider’s machine, the Incursor is fast, lightly protected, and dangerously over-armed for its frame. Rockets, blasters, and lasers make it unpredictable and deadly in the hands of bold pilots. Many Imperium aces built their reputations in Incursors, treating each battle as both gamble and performance.
  • Jaguar (JGR-72, 400 tons): Named for the fierce predator, the Jaguar embodies speed and ferocity. Its vulkan cannon and blaster combination allows it to tear through lighter foes, while its design prioritizes the swift, lethal strike over defense. In Imperium culture, Jaguars are seen as hunters born for war, and this mech carries that tradition.
  • Ocelot (OCE-45, 400 tons): The Ocelot was built to haunt enemies at range, its heavy energy cannon and blasters striking before foes can retaliate. Though lightly armored, its eerie precision and sudden appearances earned it a reputation as a phantom of the battlefield. Among Imperium warriors, piloting a Ocelot is considered an honor tied to ancestral rites of death and remembrance.
  • Umbrantarch (UMB-39, 450 tons): The Umbrantarch is a fast, fear-inducing strike mech armed with a heavy blaster and scorcher systems, optimized for repeated hit-and-run assaults. More than a combat platform, the Umbrantarch is regarded as a terror weapon—its silhouette emerging through fire and smoke like an omen. Umbrantarch pilots are said to walk with spirits, feared as much by allies as by enemies.
  • Redeemer (RDM-10, 500 tons): The Redeemer is a fast, hard-hitting medium platform designed for decisive strikes against exposed targets. Its rockets and blasters allow it to deliver sudden, concentrated fire before repositioning. Redeemers are deployed where momentum must be seized quickly and held through force.
  • Nagara (NGA-47, 550 tons): Built during an era of holy wars, the Nagara combined heavy firepower with symbolic weight. With its HEC and heavy Vulkan cannon, it was designed to break enemy champions and restore order. To this day, the Imperium frames Nagaras as machines of salvation through fire and steel.

docs.title: Superheavy Assault
docs.text:
• Neomech (NEO-60, 850 tons): Born of experiments in massed rocket fire, the Neomech is a heavy line unit that drowns enemies in salvos while its blasters and lasers cut down survivors. Its name reflects both its modernity and its connection to the Imperium’s constant cycle of rebirth: old forms reforged into new engines of war.
  • Star Slayer (SLY-10, 850 tons): The Star Slayer represents the Imperium’s mythic ambition. With heavy energy cannons and blasters, it was designed as a “god-killer,” capable of standing against the mightiest machines of rival Houses. Its name is both boast and prophecy—declaring that no star, no throne, and no rival shines brighter than the Imperium.

docs.title: Orion Corporate Mechs
docs.text:
→ See also: The Orion Corporate

Corporate mechs showcase Orion’s technical edge: prismatic weapons, advanced shielding, predictive systems, and carefully engineered battlefield efficiency.

The shielding technology used by Corporate mechs is unique to the Corporate and gives their mechs endurance in longer fights that is unmatched across the other major factions.

docs.title: Fire Support
docs.text:
• Deviance (DEV-2N, 550 tons): The Deviance began as an unsanctioned field refit, where pilots overloaded a standard chassis with heavy prismatic arrays and rockets beyond recommended tolerances. Though the Assembly initially condemned the design, its brutal effectiveness in trials forced acceptance. Its name stuck, a reminder that sometimes progress comes from breaking rules.
  • Xerxes (XER-200A, 550 tons): Part of the Archives’ “Kings Series” naming convention, the Xerxes is an expeditionary spearhead with twin heavy prismatics, rockets, and resilient shields. It isn’t subtle—its job is to impose order at the point of contact. Citizen brigades treat it like a mobile verdict: the argument ends where Xerxes stands.

docs.title: Missile Skirmisher
docs.text:
• Darius (DR-300S, 400 tons): Commissioned by the Heavy Systems Bureau to give citizen brigades a dependable lineholder, the Darius blends twin medium prismatics with stabilizing shields and guided missiles. It lacks flourish but excels at steady positional warfare, advancing behind a rational wall of data and fire. Crews respect it for what it is: the dependable center of an orderly advance.
  • Watcher (WAT-002, 500 tons): Designed as a mesh-linked sentry, the Watcher stitches missiles, rockets, and prismatics into the battlespace network, shaping enemy movement rather than chasing it. When a Watcher is present, commanders talk about lanes and closures—the field becomes a diagram, and the diagram favors Orion.

docs.title: Scout
docs.text:
• Photon (PHN-50T, 200 tons): Developed by the Sentinel Program under the Orion Standards Commission, the Photon was the first widely fielded scout with projected shielding. Its medium prismatic array doubles as a sensor-liaison tool, letting it tag targets and survive brushes that would scrap other lights.
  • Tachyon (TCY-2R, 250 tons): An offshoot of the Photon II trials, the Tachyon prioritized velocity and precision fire for counter-raider screens. Twin light and one medium prismatic give it sharp, repeatable bursts, while its avionics tie directly into early-warning buoys. On border nets, “tachyon alert” means a fast mover has already cut the angle.

docs.title: Shielded Assault
docs.text:
• Blaze Bringer (BLZ-360K, 600 tons): A field-illumination and suppression platform, the Blaze Bringer throws a curtain of coherent light—medium and light prismatics that dazzle sensors and scour cover. High sink capacity lets it sustain the show long after others cool off. Night operations doctrine calls it “turning darkness to glass.”
  • Echelon (ECH-4R, 600 tons): Built for charter enforcement and clean disables, the Echelon uses paired light prismatics and mixed missiles to peel weapons without catastrophic kills. Its telemetry suite prioritizes limb and mount solutions over cores. Echelons are routinely assigned to interdiction details, where compliance is required intact rather than destroyed.
  • Reckoner (REK-4M, 650 tons): The Reckoner serves as an anchor and command relay. Heavy and medium prismatics handle threat nodes while a rocket bank shapes approaches. Its hardened comms and fire-control links allow it to coordinate allied units under sustained contact. Once established, a Reckoner position is intended to be held, not redeployed.
  • Eradicator (ERAD-120R, 700 tons): Despite the severe name, the Eradicator is a surgical siege tool. A heavy energy cannon and twin prismatics cut precise breach patterns while rockets clear debris and counter-pushes. City engineers like working behind it; the hole it makes is the hole the plan expected.
  • Zealot (ZEL-20T, 700 tons): Adopted enthusiastically by Citizen Guard auxiliaries, the Zealot layers shields and a full prismatic suite to hold ground with conviction. Rockets punish flanking attempts while heavy and medium beams keep pressure steady. Its “zeal” isn’t mysticism—just the civic habit of not giving an inch.
  • Endermech (END-550M, 750 tons): The Endermech serves as a versatile line-breaker, protected by advanced shielding. Armed with a heavy energy cannon, twin heavy prismatics, and missile support, it can adapt to almost any battlefield role. Pilots often remark on its uncanny precision, as if the machine anticipates threats a moment before they emerge.
  • Markov (MKV-60L, 800 tons): The Markov integrates predictive targeting from the Markov Kernel Project with twin heavy prismatics and deep heat reserves. Pilots joke it only needs to lock once; after that the future is already computed. In analytics drills, its variance charts are a thing of austere beauty.

docs.title: Striker
docs.text:
• Zeno (ZEN-400P, 350 tons): Created for medium-range interdiction, the Zeno pairs a medium prismatic with economical rocket salvos to wear down fleeing targets. Modular hardpoints and easy field service made it a favorite of Citizen Guard patrols. Its name nods to pursuit paradoxes—once a Zeno starts the chase, the gap never seems to close for the prey.
  • Cobalt (CLT-B1, 500 tons): A precision striker from the Alloy Labs Initiative, the Cobalt mates a heavy energy cannon to a medium prismatic under reinforced shields. Its distinctive blue ceramic laminate isn’t for show—it dissipates heat and mitigates spall. Graduation units from several academies award “Cobalt keys” to pilots certified on its demanding fire-control regimen.

docs.title: Superheavy Assault
docs.text:
• Nebuchadnezzar (NEB-Z001, 850 tons): A prestige fortress platform from the Archives’ “City-Builder” lineage, the Nebuchadnezzar layers heavy, medium, and light prismatics with compliant missiles under deep shields. It’s meant to hold capitals and corridors, projecting calm inevitability. The name fits: it builds order by unbuilding resistance.
  • Rift Master (RFM-220C, 850 tons): The Rift Master bends battlefields to its will. A chokepoint dominator, it seals narrow passes with heavy prismatic fire and rocket storms. Its shield lattice is tuned for glancing shots from extreme angles—perfect for canyon walls and megastructure corridors.
  • Aeon (AE-550X, 900 tons): The Aeon is the pinnacle of Corporate technology, a mech designed to embody timeless supremacy. Quad heavy prismatics gave it unrivaled firepower, while advanced shields can make it feel invincible. Corporate propaganda has declared it ‘the machine that will outlast eternity.’

Twin light and one medium prismatic give it sharp, repeatable bursts, while its avionics tie directly into early-warning buoys. On border nets, “tachyon alert” means a fast mover has already cut the angle.

When a Watcher is present, commanders talk about lanes and closures—the field becomes a diagram, and the diagram favors Orion.

It isn’t subtle—its job is to impose order at the point of contact. Citizen brigades treat it like a mobile verdict: the argument ends where Xerxes stands.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate Mechs
docs.text:
→ See also: The Starcrest Protectorate

Protectorate designs are brutal and workmanlike, favoring armor, intimidation, and close-range violence. Many are built to survive ugly fights on industrial worlds and contested borders.

Protectorate mechs are heavier than comparable mechs of other factions, and the Protectorate yields super heavy mechs beyond the weight limits that other factions can produce.

docs.title: Assault
docs.text:
• Neanderthal (NEA-R8, 700 tons): The Neanderthal earned its name during testing, where its sheer, blunt brutality demolished mock fortresses in minutes. It remains the Protectorate’s premier siege breaker, a mech that embodies unrefined but devastating power.
  • Barbarian (BAR-N8, 750 tons): The Barbarian embodies the Protectorate’s doctrine of raw shock power. With its crushing fist, heavy lasers, and layered autoguns, it thrives in brutal close-quarters fighting where finesse is meaningless and only overwhelming force decides the outcome.
  • Overlord (OVR-L1, 800 tons): The Overlord was created as a command mech for Protectorate strike groups, its size and armament meant to dominate attention. Though later fielded in greater numbers, its reputation as a battlefield overseer still clings to it.

docs.title: Brawler
docs.text:
• Harbinger (HAR-G9, 550 tons): Named for its role as a herald of destruction, the Harbinger combines brute force with relentless suppressive fire. Its battle fist and vulkan cannon hammer enemies at close range, while laser support ensures there is no safe angle of approach.
  • Jackal (JCK-L4, 550 tons): Known as the “stormcrow” of the Protectorate, the Harbinger often appears at the outset of offensives, signaling devastation to come. Its combination of fists, vulkan, and lasers makes it a herald of annihilation as much as a front-line bruiser.
  • Beast (BST-R3, 600 tons): The Beast traces back to Protectorate shock-divisions raised from penal battalions. Pilots dubbed it for its savage ultra-vulkan recoil and feral silhouette, and the name stuck. Today it remains a terror weapon, more feared than refined.
  • Instigator (INS-G9, 600 tons): The Instigator is a line-breaker, built to provoke chaos. Its heavy cannon and rocket payload drive enemies from cover, while laser fire harasses stragglers. True to its name, it starts fights the Protectorate intends to finish.
  • Razorback (RZK-7B, 650 tons): With its distinctive armored ridges, the Razorback resembles the boar for which it is named. It first entered service during the Dust Marches, where its heavy autogun and laser battery carved corridors through smoke-choked canyons.

docs.title: Missile Skirmisher
docs.text: • Reaver (RVR-N5, 450 tons): First deployed during the Black Hills Campaign, the Reaver became infamous for tearing through lighter mechs with its crushing fist and rockets. Its brutal reputation lingers; to Protectorate troops, a Reaver on the field is a promise of broken lines.

docs.title: Striker
docs.text: • War Dog (WAR-D1, 400 tons): One of the oldest fielded designs in the Protectorate arsenal, the War Dog earned its name in decades of border wars where packs of them ran down raiders under cover of smoke. Reliable and loyal in function, it remains a cornerstone of Protectorate doctrine.

docs.title: Superheavy Assault
docs.text:
• Berserker (BER-Z3, 850 tons): The Berserker channels raw ferocity into every engagement. Its crushing fist and banks of medium lasers let it drive headlong into enemy lines, while smoke rounds shroud its reckless advance. It is feared less for precision than for the chaos it unleashes up close.
  • Thunder God (TDR-G7, 950 tons): The Thunder God takes its name from its battlefield presence—heavy cannons and vulkans that echo like storms breaking over the horizon. Protectorate lore holds that its arrival signals the final hammerblow of a campaign.
  • Doomhammer (DOOM-H2, 1000 tons): The Doomhammer was first forged to break stone bunkers and hardened enemy positions. With massive vulkans and crushing fists, it still fulfills that grim role, delivering hammerblows of smoke and fire to erase dug-in resistance.
  • Infernal (INF-L9, 1000 tons): The Infernal’s earliest deployments left entire valleys scorched black, and its fire-spewing design cemented its apocalyptic reputation. Protectorate commanders often use it as a psychological weapon as much as a physical one.
  • Brute Machine (BRT-100R, 1050 tons): A product of rushed wartime design, the Brute Machine was derided for its inelegance but proved itself by sheer weight of fire. Its survival and adoption are proof of the Protectorate’s core belief: overwhelming force wins wars.
  • Cromagnon (CRM-N2, 1100 tons): The Cromagnon’s name came from rival houses mocking its primitive simplicity—until Protectorate pilots smashed through their most advanced fortifications. Now it is worn with pride, a blunt instrument that proves sophistication isn’t always required.
  • Juggernaut (JGR-N6, 1100 tons): The second heaviest mech ever fielded in the Core, the Juggernaut was first deployed to break the sieges of the Border Wars, where its unstoppable advance became legend. With layered armor, a crushing fist, and a mix of rockets, lasers, and autoguns, it grinds forward until resistance collapses. Entire fortresses have fallen simply by being in its path.
  • Ragnarok (RGK-5000K, 1200 tons): The largest mech ever fielded, the Ragnarok is the end made metal. A terrifying opponent in close ranges, it is heavily armed with vulkans, cannons, and scorcher systems and turns battlefields into apocalyptic wastelands. Wherever it marches, smoke and ruin follow, sealing the fate of all who oppose the Protectorate.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Infernal’s earliest deployments left entire valleys scorched black, and its fire-spewing design cemented its apocalyptic reputation. Protectorate commanders often use it as a psychological weapon as much as a physical one.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Instigator is a line-breaker, built to provoke chaos. Its heavy cannon and rocket payload drive enemies from cover, while laser fire harasses stragglers. True to its name, it starts fights the Protectorate intends to finish.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Known as the “stormcrow” of the Protectorate, the Harbinger often appears at the outset of offensives, signaling devastation to come. Its combination of fists, vulkan, and lasers makes it a herald of annihilation as much as a front-line bruiser.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The second heaviest mech ever fielded in the Core, the Juggernaut was first deployed to break the sieges of the Border Wars, where its unstoppable advance became legend. With layered armor, a crushing fist, and a mix of rockets, lasers, and autoguns, it grinds forward until resistance collapses. Entire fortresses have fallen simply by being in its path.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Neanderthal earned its name during testing, where its sheer, blunt brutality demolished mock fortresses in minutes. It remains the Protectorate’s premier siege breaker, a mech that embodies unrefined but devastating power.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Overlord was created as a command mech for Protectorate strike groups, its size and armament meant to dominate attention. Though later fielded in greater numbers, its reputation as a battlefield overseer still clings to it.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The largest mech ever fielded, the Ragnarok is the end made metal. A terrifying opponent in close ranges, it is heavily armed with vulkans, cannons, and scorcher systems and turns battlefields into apocalyptic wastelands. Wherever it marches, smoke and ruin follow, sealing the fate of all who oppose the Protectorate.

docs.text: With its distinctive armored ridges, the Razorback resembles the boar for which it is named. It first entered service during the Dust Marches, where its heavy autogun and laser battery carved corridors through smoke-choked canyons.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: First deployed during the Black Hills Campaign, the Reaver became infamous for tearing through lighter mechs with its crushing fist and rockets. Its brutal reputation lingers; to Protectorate troops, a Reaver on the field is a promise of broken lines.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Thunder God takes its name from its battlefield presence—heavy cannons and vulkans that echo like storms breaking over the horizon. Protectorate lore holds that its arrival signals the final hammerblow of a campaign.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: One of the oldest fielded designs in the Protectorate arsenal, the War Dog earned its name in decades of border wars where packs of them ran down raiders under cover of smoke. Reliable and loyal in function, it remains a cornerstone of Protectorate doctrine.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union Mechs
docs.text:
→ See also: The Confederate Vanguard Union

Union designs emphasize durability, direct firepower, disciplined battlefield roles, and rugged logistics. Their mechs tend to favor practical weapons, thick armor, and formations built around coordinated pressure.

Union mechs utilize advanced cooling systems and as a result carry more heatsinks than comparable mechs from other factions. Some Union mechs also have jump capabilities.

docs.title: Assault
docs.text:
• Patriot (PTR-007, 700 tons): The Patriot is perhaps the most symbolic of Union mechs, a walking fortress embodying their doctrine of sacrifice and resilience. With thick armor, broad weapon coverage, and missile support, it became the backbone of ceremonial as well as battlefield formations. Its name was no accident—the Union intended it as a rallying banner, a machine that stood unbroken while others fell.
  • Challenger (CHL-4R, 750 tons): The Challenger was created during the Union’s dueling trials, where mechs were fielded to prove dominance over rival Houses. Mixing rockets, autoguns, and lasers, it was designed for versatility in single combat as well as battlefield challenges. Though less specialized than some designs, its name and legacy are steeped in Union pride: to challenge is to overcome.
  • Hammerhead (HMR-4T, 750 tons): Developed for shipboard and port assaults, the Hammerhead mounts twin heavy energy cannons backed by autoguns and lasers. Its name derives from its brutal frontal assault style, “hammering” through defenses like the predator of the sea. Pilots often describe it as inelegant but unstoppable—exactly the qualities the Union prizes.
  • Brigadier (BRG-88R, 800 tons): Built to lead batteries of missile carriers, the Brigadier became the cornerstone of Union siege doctrine. Capable of drowning entrenched foes in waves of rockets and missiles, it earned a reputation for ending battles before they truly began. The name Brigadier reflects its command role—always at the center of Union formations, directing fire and breaking lines.

docs.title: Brawler
docs.text:
• Crossfire (CRF-0S, 500 tons): Born from Union doctrine that no enemy should leave the field unscathed, the Crossfire bristles with overlapping weapon arcs—Vulkan cannon, rockets, lasers, and autogun. It was intended for siege and suppression, saturating kill zones until nothing remained. Pilots often joked that standing in its line of fire was like being caught between two armies—hence the name Crossfire.
  • Firebrand (FR-3N, 500 tons): The Firebrand’s origin lies in the Union’s scorched-earth campaigns. Armed with an array of Scorchers supported by lasers and autoguns, it was built to terrorize entrenched enemies, burning them from cover and leaving nothing but ash. The Union embraced the mech’s fiery symbolism, fielding it as both weapon and warning: resistance would be answered with flames.
  • Jackman (JCK-77, 650 tons): The Jackman was built during the urban reclamations, where Union forces needed a close-range brawler to clear streets and hold chokepoints. Armed with paired vulkans and heavy lasers, it is devastating in knife-fight ranges. The name reflects the laborers who “jackhammered” the Union’s cities back into shape, embodying relentless industrial grit turned to war.
  • Maverick (MAV-8K, 650 tons): The Maverick was developed for commanders who demanded independence from supply trains. Its rockets and high energy cannon made it a devastating ambush platform, while its jump jets allowed it to strike from unexpected angles. The name Maverick honors both its battlefield role and the defiant Union spirit of breaking from convention to seize victory.

docs.title: Cavalry Striker
docs.text:
• Vagabond (VG-7H, 250 tons): The Vagabond was born of necessity during the Exile, when scattered Union units required a versatile scout and skirmisher. Outfitted with jump jets and a mixed ballistic-energy loadout, it excels in rough terrain where heavier mechs cannot tread. Its name reflects both its wandering origins and its reputation as a survivor, roaming far from supply lines and returning with kills to prove its worth.
  • Slugger (SLG-1A, 350 tons): Designed for brutal city fighting during the Restoration, the Slugger carries a heavy energy cannon backed by close-support lasers. Slow but unyielding, it was built to clear enemy strongpoints building by building. The name Slugger is both a nod to its hammering firepower and the Union’s reputation for winning through attrition, no matter how long the fight drags on.

docs.title: Line Mech
docs.text: • Dragoon (DGN-8S, 600 tons): The Dragoon was designed as a flexible heavy line unit, able to hold or advance with equal confidence. Combining a heavy energy cannon, vulkan, and defensive lasers, it proved especially valuable in prolonged campaigns where reliability outweighed elegance. Its name recalls the mounted infantry of old—soldiers who could fight wherever they were needed, a tradition the Union holds dear.

docs.title: Missile Skirmisher
docs.text: • Alpha Wolf (AWLF-5K, 350 tons): The Alpha Wolf represents the Union’s doctrine of coordinated pack warfare. Armed with paired autoguns, lasers, and missiles, it was designed to anchor wolfpack formations that overwhelmed isolated foes through relentless firepower. Feared for its ability to tear through both armor and morale, the Alpha Wolf earned its name by always leading the kill.

docs.title: Scout
docs.text: • Raptor (RPT-1R, 250 tons): Developed as a fast-response raider during the Union’s early frontier wars, the Raptor sacrifices heavy armor for speed and strike capability. Its rocket pods and light lasers made it ideal for harassing supply lines and crippling softer targets before heavier Union assets arrived. Though lightly protected, pilots trusted its agility and ferocity—earning the name Raptor for its swift, predatory strikes.

docs.title: Superheavy Assault
docs.text:
• Bulwark (BLW-5K, 900 tons): Designed as a defensive anchor, the Bulwark carries massive armor and layered weaponry suited for holding ground against superior numbers. In campaigns where the Union was forced to defend its hard-won territories, the Bulwark became both shield and hammer. Its name speaks for itself: immovable, unshakable, a wall of steel.
  • Sword Breaker (SBRK-7R, 900 tons): The Sword Breaker was engineered specifically to counter the dueling champions of rival Houses. With heavy vulkans and precision medium lasers, it excelled at stripping weapons from enemy mechs and leaving them powerless. Its name is both literal and symbolic—the Union’s message that no foreign blade, however sharp, could pierce their resolve.
  • Punisher (PNR-004, 950 tons): The Punisher was born from campaigns of retribution. With a heavy energy cannon, ultra vulkan, and missile support, it was built to chase down and annihilate those who dared defy Union authority. Slow but merciless, the Punisher lives up to its name, serving as executioner on the battlefield where no mercy is given.
  • Gallimagus (GLM-8G, 1000 tons): The Gallimagus is an ancient design, said to descend from pre-Exile Empire prototypes. Mounting an unprecedented four heavy energy cannons, it was rarely produced due to cost, but every unit became a battlefield legend. Its name, drawn from old Union sagas, evokes a monstrous beast of war—rare, mythical, and terrifying in its wrath.
  • Liberator (LBR-9D, 1000 tons): Developed during the Long Exile by remnants of the fallen Empire, the Liberator was designed to spearhead counteroffensives and break enemy sieges. It played a decisive role in the Great Restoration, leading the charge to reclaim the Core worlds. Adopted as the Union’s premiere assault mech, it endures as both battlefield powerhouse and enduring symbol of liberation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Bulldog earned its name in countless sieges, battering defenses with high energy cannons and rocket salvos. Slow but stubborn, it excelled in drawn-out attrition battles. Even today, the Bulldog remains a favored choice for militias and second-line forces who need a hard-hitting mech that can take punishment and keep advancing.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Once built to serve in ceremonial tournaments as well as war, the Champion balanced missile fire with lasers and a vulkan. Though never as specialized as later duelists, it gained popularity as a reliable all-rounder. Many Houses still field Champions in honor guards or training battalions, where their long heritage and recognizable silhouette command respect.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: A relic of the arena era, the Gladiator was built for spectacle as much as war. With rockets, vulkan, and lasers, it was capable of delivering flashy, punishing blows that made it a crowd favorite. Many Houses retired theirs to ceremonial duty, but in poorer regions of the Core, Gladiators still see combat, proving that even showpieces can draw blood.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: One of the oldest light designs still in circulation, the Hornet was cheap to manufacture and simple to maintain. Armed with twin light lasers, it was never meant to duel, but rather to scout, harass, and sting at vulnerable points before fleeing. Its proliferation across the Core is owed to its reliability and low cost—where there is conflict, Hornets are never far behind.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Kingsguard was once marketed as the ultimate “command mech,” a prestige unit to accompany noble lords into battle. Its missiles and mixed weaponry made it flexible, while its armor kept its pilot secure. Though time has dulled its reputation, the Kingsguard endures as a symbol of authority on the battlefield—still favored by militia leaders and House scions alike.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Lancer was among the first medium-heavies to emphasize precision energy fire over massed rockets. Armed with a heavy laser and layered support beams, it excelled at punching through weakened armor. Once hailed as a duelist’s machine, it is now more often used as a training platform or second-line support, though many veteran pilots still prize its accuracy.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Heavy, slow, and built to endure punishment, the Lineman was conceived as a frontline brawler to hold the line while others maneuvered. Its vulkan cannon and paired autoguns provided steady suppressive fire. Though outclassed by newer heavies, it remains common wherever Houses need durable firepower to anchor defenses.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: An aging missile platform, the Longstrike was originally deployed to provide sustained bombardment from behind the lines. With its paired racks and autogun backups, it could soften enemy positions before an assault. Though slow and vulnerable up close, its simplicity and ease of production have kept it in steady circulation across the Core.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Lynx became popular during the border wars for its balance of mobility and firepower. With paired medium lasers and a small rocket rack, it could serve as both flanker and support unit. Though newer mechs have surpassed it, the Lynx endures thanks to its rugged frame and versatility—qualities that made it a staple in every House’s arsenal.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Marksman was designed as a fire-support chassis, mounting multiple vulkans for sustained barrages. Though criticized for its heat inefficiency and limited armor, it carved a niche as a battlefield “sweeper,” punishing light mechs and infantry that strayed too close. In modern times, it is often relegated to defensive roles, but its name still carries weight.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Built for patrol duty and frontier policing, the Outrider combined endurance with straightforward weaponry. Its heavy and medium lasers gave it enough punch to deter raiders without requiring complex supply lines. While unimpressive by modern standards, the Outrider’s long service life and adaptability have ensured its presence in garrisons across the Core.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Yeoman was designed as a dependable multi-role platform for Houses lacking specialized production. Its mixed loadout—autoguns, lasers, and scorchers—made it a jack-of-all-trades capable of handling infantry, armor, or other mechs in a pinch. Though rarely a commander’s first choice, the Yeoman’s steady performance earned it a reputation as the soldier’s friend.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Designed as a defensive anchor, the Bulwark carries massive armor and layered weaponry suited for holding ground against superior numbers. In campaigns where the Union was forced to defend its hard-won territories, the Bulwark became both shield and hammer. Its name speaks for itself: immovable, unshakable, a wall of steel.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Challenger was created during the Union’s dueling trials, where mechs were fielded to prove dominance over rival Houses. Mixing rockets, autoguns, and lasers, it was designed for versatility in single combat as well as battlefield challenges. Though less specialized than some designs, its name and legacy are steeped in Union pride: to challenge is to overcome.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Born from Union doctrine that no enemy should leave the field unscathed, the Crossfire bristles with overlapping weapon arcs—Vulkan cannon, rockets, lasers, and autogun. It was intended for siege and suppression, saturating kill zones until nothing remained. Pilots often joked that standing in its line of fire was like being caught between two armies—hence the name Crossfire.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Dragoon was designed as a flexible heavy line unit, able to hold or advance with equal confidence. Combining a heavy energy cannon, vulkan, and defensive lasers, it proved especially valuable in prolonged campaigns where reliability outweighed elegance. Its name recalls the mounted infantry of old—soldiers who could fight wherever they were needed, a tradition the Union holds dear.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Firebrand’s origin lies in the Union’s scorched-earth campaigns. Armed with an array of Scorchers supported by lasers and autoguns, it was built to terrorize entrenched enemies, burning them from cover and leaving nothing but ash. The Union embraced the mech’s fiery symbolism, fielding it as both weapon and warning: resistance would be answered with flames.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Gallimagus is an ancient design, said to descend from pre-Exile Empire prototypes. Mounting an unprecedented four heavy energy cannons, it was rarely produced due to cost, but every unit became a battlefield legend. Its name, drawn from old Union sagas, evokes a monstrous beast of war—rare, mythical, and terrifying in its wrath.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Developed for shipboard and port assaults, the Hammerhead mounts twin heavy energy cannons backed by autoguns and lasers. Its name derives from its brutal frontal assault style, “hammering” through defenses like the predator of the sea. Pilots often describe it as inelegant but unstoppable—exactly the qualities the Union prizes.

docs.text: The Jackman was built during the urban reclamations, where Union forces needed a close-range brawler to clear streets and hold chokepoints. Armed with paired vulkans and heavy lasers, it is devastating in knife-fight ranges. The name reflects the laborers who “jackhammered” the Union’s cities back into shape, embodying relentless industrial grit turned to war.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Developed during the Long Exile by remnants of the fallen Empire, the Liberator was designed to spearhead counteroffensives and break enemy sieges. It played a decisive role in the Great Restoration, leading the charge to reclaim the Core worlds. Adopted as the Union’s premiere assault mech, it endures as both battlefield powerhouse and enduring symbol of liberation.

docs.text: The Maverick was developed for commanders who demanded independence from supply trains. Its rockets and high energy cannon made it a devastating ambush platform, while its jump jets allowed it to strike from unexpected angles. The name Maverick honors both its battlefield role and the defiant Union spirit of breaking from convention to seize victory.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Patriot is perhaps the most symbolic of Union mechs, a walking fortress embodying their doctrine of sacrifice and resilience. With thick armor, broad weapon coverage, and missile support, it became the backbone of ceremonial as well as battlefield formations. Its name was no accident—the Union intended it as a rallying banner, a machine that stood unbroken while others fell.

docs.text: The Punisher was born from campaigns of retribution. With a heavy energy cannon, ultra vulkan, and missile support, it was built to chase down and annihilate those who dared defy Union authority. Slow but merciless, the Punisher lives up to its name, serving as executioner on the battlefield where no mercy is given.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Developed as a fast-response raider during the Union’s early frontier wars, the Raptor sacrifices heavy armor for speed and strike capability. Its rocket pods and light lasers made it ideal for harassing supply lines and crippling softer targets before heavier Union assets arrived. Though lightly protected, pilots trusted its agility and ferocity—earning the name Raptor for its swift, predatory strikes.

docs.text: Designed for brutal city fighting during the Restoration, the Slugger carries a heavy energy cannon backed by close-support lasers. Slow but unyielding, it was built to clear enemy strongpoints building by building. The name Slugger is both a nod to its hammering firepower and the Union’s reputation for winning through attrition, no matter how long the fight drags on.

docs.text: The Sword Breaker was engineered specifically to counter the dueling champions of rival Houses. With heavy vulkans and precision medium lasers, it excelled at stripping weapons from enemy mechs and leaving them powerless. Its name is both literal and symbolic—the Union’s message that no foreign blade, however sharp, could pierce their resolve.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Vagabond was born of necessity during the Exile, when scattered Union units required a versatile scout and skirmisher. Outfitted with jump jets and a mixed ballistic-energy loadout, it excels in rough terrain where heavier mechs cannot tread. Its name reflects both its wandering origins and its reputation as a survivor, roaming far from supply lines and returning with kills to prove its worth.

docs.title: Mercenaries
docs.text: “No war is won for free.” — Mercenary saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Common Name Metal Mercs Primary Hub Drake's Landing Primary Authority Mercenary Guild Primary Equipment War Mechs Primary Occupation Private Military Contracting Common Saying “No House Rules The Frontier.”

Mercenaries—commonly known throughout civilized space as Metal Mercs—are private military organizations operating throughout the Core and frontier regions. Built around the deployment of mechs, mercenary companies undertake everything from frontier defense and anti-piracy operations to reconnaissance, convoy escort, industrial security, and large-scale military campaigns.

Although officially independent, mercenary forces have become one of the defining institutions of the modern age. While the Great Houses publicly avoid direct warfare under the authority of the Star Regency, mercenary companies provide a means through which conflicts can be fought, influence projected, and political objectives pursued without openly violating the peace.

Mercenary organizations vary enormously in size. At the top stand the four Greater Companies, military powers whose strength rivals that of the Great Houses themselves. Beneath them are the famous Lesser Companies, followed by hundreds of Minor Mercenary Units operating throughout civilized space. At the foundation of the profession are the countless Free Jacks, independent pilots and small crews seeking contracts wherever work can be found.

Throughout the frontier, mercenary companies are viewed with a mixture of admiration, suspicion, and necessity. To some worlds they are protectors. To others they are opportunists. In many regions they are simply the closest thing available to a standing army.

Whatever their reputation, few institutions have shaped the modern Core more profoundly than the Metal Mercs.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
Mercenaries have existed throughout human history, including during the age of the Old Empire. For much of that era, however, they occupied a relatively minor role compared to the vast military forces maintained by the Empire's authorities.

That changed with the Fall.

The collapse of the Empire shattered the political and military order that had governed human space for centuries. Countless soldiers suddenly found themselves without employers, without nations, and often without any reliable means of support.
