At the same time, thousands of worlds lost the protection of Empire fleets and garrisons. Across known space, governments weakened, trade routes fractured, and entire regions descended into instability.

Into this vacuum stepped pirates, raiders, warlords, private armies, and mercenaries.

Throughout the Dark Age, independent military companies became a common feature of life. Some protected vulnerable settlements and trade routes. Others fought for local rulers, corporate interests, or whoever could afford their services. Many drifted between the roles of soldier, salvager, privateer, and outright bandit as circumstances demanded.

By the time of the Great Restoration, mercenary culture had already become deeply entrenched throughout human space. The Restoration did not create the profession so much as transform it. Organizations such as the Mercenary Guild helped formalize contracts, establish standards, and bring greater legitimacy to an industry that had often operated in the gray space between military service and organized violence.

Today, mercenary activity extends far beyond the boundaries of the Core. From the frontier worlds to the edge of explored space, independent military companies remain one of the most common and influential forces in human civilization.

docs.title: The Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
→ See also: Mercenary Guild

Most recognized mercenary companies operate under the authority of the Mercenary Guild, the neutral institution responsible for regulating private warfare throughout civilized space.

The Guild oversees company registration, contract enforcement, arbitration, reputation tracking, salvage rights, and operational disputes. Through its licensing systems and Open Board exchanges, it provides the framework that allows thousands of independent military organizations to operate across the Core and frontier regions.

For many employers, a Guild seal represents legitimacy. For many mercenaries, it represents opportunity. Companies that maintain strong Guild records often gain access to better contracts, favorable salvage rights, and long-term relationships with major employers. Those that lose Guild standing may find themselves effectively locked out of civilized mercenary work.

Although the Guild commands only modest military forces of its own, its influence extends throughout human space. Few institutions have done more to transform mercenary warfare from a collection of armed companies into a profession.

docs.title: Mercenary Companies
docs.text:
Mercenary organizations vary enormously in size, capability, and influence.

At the top of the profession stand the four Greater Companies, military powers whose strength rivals that of the Great Houses themselves. Beneath them are the famous Lesser Companies, organizations large enough to shape regional conflicts and influence interstellar politics. Together, these elite commands represent the most powerful independent military forces in known space.

The vast majority of professional mercenaries serve within Minor Mercenary Units. Numbering in the hundreds, these companies range from single-company formations to reinforced wards capable of conducting extended military operations. Though smaller than the great commands, many possess long histories, respected commanders, and distinguished combat records.

At the foundation of the profession are the countless Free Jacks, independent pilots and small crews operating without the backing of a major company. Many never rise beyond a handful of mechs. Others eventually earn enough reputation to establish companies of their own.

Regardless of size, a mercenary company's most valuable asset is often its reputation. Reliable organizations gain access to better contracts, stronger employers, and more favorable salvage terms. Poor reputations can rapidly destroy a company's access to financing, recruits, and future work.

Among mercenaries, there is a common belief that machines win battles, but reputation wins careers.

docs.title: Mercenary Life
docs.text:
Life as a mercenary is often romanticized throughout the Core, but the reality is far less glamorous.

Most mercenaries spend far more time negotiating contracts, maintaining equipment, training crews, and managing logistics than they do fighting. A successful company must function as both a military organization and a business. Ammunition must be purchased, reactors maintained, mechs repaired, pilots paid, and transport secured. A company that wins battles but cannot manage its finances rarely survives for long.

The profession attracts a wide variety of people. Former House soldiers, frontier militias, displaced nobles, salvagers, adventurers, and second-generation mercenaries can all be found beneath the banners of independent companies. Some seek wealth. Others seek freedom, glory, revenge, or simply a place to belong.

Despite their reputation for independence, mercenary organizations are often built around loyalty and personal relationships. Crews may spend years or even decades serving alongside one another, creating bonds that frequently become stronger than national or political affiliations.

Reputation occupies a central place in mercenary culture. A company known for honoring contracts, protecting its personnel, and treating employers fairly can secure work almost anywhere in civilized space. Companies that betray employers, abandon allies, or engage in unnecessary brutality often discover that their reputations travel faster than they do.

This emphasis on reputation has produced a distinctive culture throughout the profession. Mercenaries frequently judge one another less by ideology or faction and more by competence, reliability, and battlefield conduct. Among veterans, a respected name is often considered more valuable than a powerful mech.

As one old mercenary saying goes:

"A mech can be replaced. A reputation can't."

docs.title: Drake's Landing
docs.text:
→ See also: Drake's Landing

The heart of mercenary culture lies on Drake's Landing, the neutral world that serves as the profession's unofficial capital.

Home to the Mercenary Guild and the largest concentration of mercenary companies in civilized space, Drake's Landing functions as a meeting place for pilots, recruiters, employers, mechanics, salvagers, and military contractors from every corner of the Core and Frontier. Its contract halls, Open Board exchanges, repair facilities, and training grounds form the center of the modern mercenary economy.

For generations, aspiring mercenaries have traveled to Drake's Landing seeking opportunity. Some arrive as Free Jacks looking for their first contract. Others come representing famous companies whose names are known across the Core. Regardless of status, nearly every mercenary eventually passes through Landing.

Among mercenaries, there is a common saying:

"Every company starts on Landing. Not every company leaves."

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
All five Great Houses make extensive use of mercenary forces.

Officially, mercenary companies are employed for frontier defense, anti-piracy operations, convoy security, colonial expansion, and infrastructure protection. In practice, they frequently serve as instruments of influence in territorial disputes, proxy conflicts, covert operations, and political rivalries.

This relationship benefits both sides. Mercenary companies gain access to lucrative contracts, equipment, and political connections, while the Great Houses acquire military capabilities that can be deployed without fully committing their own armed forces.

The relationship is not without tension. House leaders often view mercenaries as useful but unpredictable, while many mercenaries regard the Houses as employers rather than masters. Most companies maintain their independence carefully, knowing that excessive dependence upon a single patron can quickly transform a mercenary command into a House auxiliary in all but name.

Even the largest companies must navigate these relationships carefully. A contract may provide wealth and influence, but it can also draw a company into conflicts far larger than it intended to fight.

docs.title: Frontier Importance
docs.text:
Nowhere are mercenaries more important than in the Frontier.

Beyond the heavily populated worlds of the Core, many settlements lack the resources to maintain standing militaries capable of defending themselves against pirates, raiders, hostile neighbors, or local warlords. For these worlds, mercenary companies often provide the only practical source of military protection.

Mercenaries escort convoys, defend colonies, suppress pirate activity, train local militias, and secure trade routes across vast regions where government authority is weak or nonexistent. Entire frontier economies depend upon their presence.

As a result, mercenaries are often viewed differently on the Frontier than within the Core. Core citizens may see them as opportunists or soldiers for hire. Frontier populations frequently view them as protectors, partners, and sometimes the only barrier between civilization and chaos.

It is this constant demand for security that ensures the profession continues to thrive far beyond the borders of the Great Houses.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
Mercenary activity remains at some of the highest levels seen since the Great Restoration.

Expanding frontier settlements, growing pirate activity, renewed competition for resources, and rising tensions between the Great Houses have created unprecedented demand for experienced military contractors. Many companies have prospered as a result, while new organizations appear on the Open Board every cycle.

Yet many veterans view the future with caution. The political order established after the Great Restoration has preserved relative stability for centuries, but few believe it will last forever. Proxy conflicts are growing larger, contracts are becoming more dangerous, and some fear the Core is drifting toward a period of instability not seen since the Dark Age.

For now, the Metal Mercs continue to thrive in the space between peace and war.

"When the Houses stop talking, the contracts get bigger."

— Mercenary saying

docs.title: Free Jacks
docs.text:
"No banner. No promises. Just steel."

— Free Jack saying

A Free Jack is any independent mercenary pilot, crew, or military unit operating outside the long-term patronage of a Great House, major employer, or established mercenary organization.

The term encompasses a wide range of individuals and groups. A Free Jack may be a lone mech pilot seeking contracts through the Guild Open Board, a small crew operating a handful of machines, or an upstart company attempting to establish a reputation. What unites them is their independence and lack of institutional backing.

Most Free Jacks survive by accepting short-term contracts, frontier work, security assignments, salvage operations, and other jobs that larger organizations often consider unprofitable or too risky. Their freedom allows them to pursue opportunities wherever they arise, but it also leaves them vulnerable to financial instability, equipment shortages, and sudden losses.

Within mercenary culture, the term carries mixed connotations. Some use it respectfully to describe independent operators unwilling to serve any master. Others use it dismissively to refer to inexperienced companies that have not yet proven themselves.

The majority of Free Jacks never rise beyond a handful of mechs. Many eventually disappear through bankruptcy, combat losses, or absorption into larger organizations. A fortunate few establish successful companies, earn Guild recognition, and grow into respected mercenary units.

For this reason, nearly every mercenary can claim to have been a Free Jack at some point in their career. It is the lowest rung of the profession, but also the place where legends begin.

docs.title: Greater Mercenary Companies
docs.text: “The Great Houses keep the Core in line. The Great Companies keep the Houses in line.” — Old Mercenary Proverb

docs.title: :material-fort: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Large Independent Mercenary Commands Operational Scale Multi-System Economic Role Private Military Powers Territorial Reach Several Worlds Primary Oversight Mercenary Guild Common Reputation “States Without Thrones”

Greater Mercenary Companies are the largest and most powerful mercenary organizations operating throughout the Core and frontier regions.

Unlike ordinary mercenary units, Greater Companies function as massive institutional military organizations possessing: - fleets - logistics networks - recruitment infrastructure - repair facilities - long-term charters - standing military formations

Some rival smaller planetary governments in military capability and economic influence.

Although technically private military organizations, Greater Companies often possess enough political leverage to influence: - frontier stability - trade security - regional conflicts - House relations - Guild policy

Throughout civilized space, they are viewed simultaneously as: - military contractors - strategic assets - political actors - stabilizing forces - potential threats

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
The rise of the Greater Companies began during the centuries following the Great Restoration.

As mercenary warfare became increasingly normalized throughout the Core, certain successful companies accumulated enough: - wealth - salvage - territory - personnel - logistical capability

to expand far beyond the scale of traditional contract forces.

Over generations, some evolved from relatively small mercenary outfits into massive military institutions operating across multiple systems.

Unlike temporary frontier companies or independent Free Jack crews, Greater Companies developed permanent organizational structures capable of surviving beyond the lifespan of individual commanders.

This transformed them from simple mercenary units into enduring military powers.

docs.title: The Three Greater Companies
docs.text:
Although countless mercenary companies operate throughout civilized space, only three are universally recognized as the true Greater Mercenary Companies of the modern era.

These organizations possess such immense military and logistical capability that many political analysts consider them quasi-state powers operating outside direct House authority.

docs.title: The Nomad Company
docs.text:
Supreme Commander Field Lord Combat Doctrine Relentless Assault Warfare Primary Asset Mobile Dreadnought Fortress Frequent Employer Starcrest Protectorate

The Nomad Company is one of the largest and most feared mercenary organizations in the Core.

The company is renowned for deploying overwhelming battlefield force through: - massive mech formations - sustained assaults - aggressive maneuver warfare - attritional campaigns

The Nomads possess a reputation for capturing enemy equipment intact whenever possible, allowing them to expand and maintain their forces through battlefield salvage as much as formal production.

Unlike many large mercenary commands, the Nomad Company maintains no permanent planetary capital.

Instead, its operational headquarters is housed within a heavily modified dreadnought battle cruiser capable of sustaining long-term deep-space operations.

This mobile fortress serves simultaneously as: - command center - fleet anchor - training base - logistics hub - symbolic homeworld

The company officially accepts contracts from all Great Houses, though it maintains especially close operational ties with the Starcrest Protectorate.

Among mercenaries, Nomad forces are often viewed as relentless, disciplined, and extraordinarily difficult to destroy completely.

docs.title: The Warforged Convoy
docs.text:
Supreme Commander War Marshal Combat Doctrine Adaptive Mechanized Warfare Specialization Heavy Mech Modification Frequent Employer Confederate Vanguard Union

The Warforged Convoy is famous throughout the Core for its heavily modified and extensively customized mech forces.

The company’s mechs are maintained and redesigned by highly skilled mechwrights capable of radically altering battlefield configurations between deployments.

Warforged pilots are often trained as both: - mech operators - field engineers - combat technicians

This allows Warforged units to rapidly adapt equipment and weapon systems to changing battlefield conditions.

Enemy commanders frequently struggle to identify Warforged units accurately, as the company’s modified mechs rarely conform to standard military specifications.

The Convoy’s flexible combat doctrine and engineering culture have earned it a formidable reputation across frontier warzones.

The Confederate Vanguard Union is known to employ the Warforged Convoy extensively for: - offensive campaigns - mechanized breakthroughs - industrial warfare - heavy assault operations

Within mercenary circles, the Warforged are respected for their technical expertise and feared for the unpredictability of their steel.

docs.title: Iron Phoenix Regulars
docs.text:
Supreme Commander Red Commander Combat Doctrine Strategic Precision Operations Specialization Elite Raids and Tactical Operations Frequent Employers Helios Sovereignty and Omnisphere Imperium

The Iron Phoenix Regulars are widely regarded as among the most disciplined and strategically sophisticated mercenary forces in civilized space.

Unlike the overwhelming assaults favored by the Nomad Company or the adaptive mechanized warfare of the Warforged Convoy, the Regulars specialize in: - precise operational planning - elite strike missions - strategic raids - extraction operations - targeted battlefield disruption

The Regulars maintain an unusually strict internal code of conduct by mercenary standards.

This professionalism has earned them considerable respect throughout the Core, including among many rival commanders.

Their elite strike teams are particularly famous for conducting high-profile operations capable of influencing broader geopolitical events.

The Helios Sovereignty and Omnisphere Imperium frequently employ the Iron Phoenix Regulars for missions requiring: - discretion - coordination - strategic discipline - political sensitivity

Among mercenary circles, the Regulars are often viewed as the closest thing the profession possesses to a true elite officer corps.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
All five Great Houses employ Greater Mercenary Companies to varying degrees.

These organizations are especially valuable for: - frontier stabilization - anti-piracy campaigns - strategic deniability - expeditionary warfare - border conflicts - politically sensitive operations

Although technically independent, many Greater Companies maintain long-standing historical ties with specific Houses.

Some are widely believed to function as unofficial House auxiliaries despite maintaining nominal neutrality.

The Houses tolerate the existence of Greater Companies because they provide military flexibility without requiring direct inter-house warfare.

At the same time, many political leaders remain deeply uncomfortable with the sheer military power these organizations possess.

docs.title: Relationship with the Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild plays a critical role in regulating Greater Companies.

Because of their scale and strategic importance, Greater Companies are deeply integrated into: - Guild arbitration systems - charter regulation - contract enforcement - salvage oversight - reputation systems

Many Greater Companies possess substantial political influence within Guild institutions themselves.

Smaller mercenary outfits occasionally accuse the Guild of favoring Greater Companies through: - preferential contracts - arbitration bias - salvage rulings - enforcement discretion

Guild officials strongly deny such accusations.

docs.title: Lesser and Minor Companies
docs.text:
Below the Greater Companies exist countless: - Lesser Mercenary Companies - Minor Mercenary Companies - Free Jack crews

The overwhelming majority of mercenary organizations throughout the Core fall into these smaller classifications.

Many survive by accepting: - subcontract work - escort charters - salvage operations - local defense contracts - anti-piracy assignments

from Greater Companies.

This creates a layered mercenary economy in which major military operations frequently involve networks of subordinate or affiliated companies operating beneath larger banners.

docs.title: Mercenary Culture
docs.text:
Greater Companies possess powerful internal cultures and identities.

Veteran mercenaries often identify more strongly with their company banner than with any Great House.

Large companies develop: - traditions - heraldry - rivalries - operational doctrines - command philosophies - internal myths

Among mercenaries, reputation matters enormously.

A respected company banner may attract: - elite pilots - favorable contracts - long charters - House sponsorship - premium Guild access

while a burned banner may struggle to survive.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, the Greater Mercenary Companies are viewed with a mixture of: - admiration - fear - dependence - resentment - respect

To frontier worlds, they may represent the only reliable military protection available.

To the Great Houses, they are useful but potentially dangerous strategic actors.

To independent mercenaries, they are often viewed either as: - aspirational legends - bloated institutions - contract machines - or the ultimate destination of a successful mercenary career

As one old Landing saying goes:

“Minor crews chase contracts. Greater Companies chase sectors.”

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As instability spreads throughout the Core and frontier alike, the influence of the Greater Mercenary Companies continues to grow.

Some StarCom analysts privately warn that several Greater Companies now possess enough military and logistical capability to destabilize entire sectors should they ever fully abandon Guild oversight.

Others argue the Greater Companies have become indispensable to maintaining modern civilization itself.

For now, the balance holds.

The Great Houses still rule the Core.

But increasingly, the wars between them are fought not beneath House banners —

but beneath the steel banners of the Greater Companies.

docs.title: Lesser Mercenary Companies
docs.text: “Greater Companies shape sectors. Lesser Companies win wars.” — Mercenary Guild tactical saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Mid-to-Large Mercenary Commands Operational Scale Regional and Multi-System Military Capability Multiple Divisions Primary Strength Specialized Warfare Doctrine Guild Classification Lesser Mercenary Company Common Reputation “Professional Steel”

In the dynamic and often volatile mercenary landscape of the Core, Lesser Mercenary Companies hold a crucial position.

While they may not possess the overwhelming force and extensive resources of the Greater Mercenary Companies, these units are nonetheless formidable military organizations in their own right.

Most Lesser Companies command: - multiple divisions - dedicated logistics staff - permanent bases - transport fleets - mech repair infrastructure - private starships

Their scale allows them to operate effectively across frontier regions without requiring the enormous economic and logistical structures associated with the Greater Companies.

Unlike many smaller mercenary units, Lesser Companies frequently specialize in highly refined combat doctrines, making them valuable strategic assets for the Great Houses, frontier governments, and Guild-chartered operations.

Though Lesser Mercenary Companies are sufficiently large to have often worked alongside multiple Great Houses throughout their histories, many develop long-standing preferences, loyalties, or operational relationships with particular factions.

docs.title: Role in the Core
docs.text:
Lesser Companies occupy a critical middle ground within mercenary civilization.

They are: - larger and more stable than independent companies - more flexible than the Greater Companies - capable of long-term campaigns - specialized enough for precision operations - small enough to deploy rapidly

Because of this, Lesser Companies are frequently hired for: - regional conflicts - planetary campaigns - strategic raids - siege operations - defensive warfare - urban pacification - deep frontier deployments

Guild analysts often describe Lesser Companies as the backbone of modern mercenary warfare.

docs.title: Military Identity
docs.text:
Unlike the Greater Companies, which frequently function as enormous military institutions, Lesser Companies tend to build reputations around: - battlefield doctrine - tactical specialization - command philosophy - elite operational capability

Their banners are often associated with highly recognizable forms of warfare.

Experienced commanders throughout the Core frequently select Lesser Companies based upon: - terrain - operational style - political sensitivity - mission profile - required strategic flexibility

Among mercenaries, a respected Lesser Company banner carries enormous prestige.

docs.title: Wardens of the Astral Empire
docs.text:
Specialization Defensive Warfare and Siege Defense Preferred Equipment Heavy Assault Mechs Battlefield Role Last-Line Defensive Operations

The Wardens of the Astral Empire are among the most respected defensive specialists in the Core.

Frequently contracted to hold critical defensive positions during: - sieges - planetary invasions - attritional campaigns - large-scale battles

the Wardens are renowned for their ability to endure under overwhelming pressure.

Their formations emphasize: - layered defenses - fortified positions - overlapping fire zones - disciplined heavy mech deployment

The Wardens are widely regarded as one of the few mercenary forces capable of stabilizing collapsing battlefronts after other units have failed.

docs.title: Starfall Exarchs
docs.text:
Specialization Guerrilla Warfare and Deep Recon Commander Commander Lyra Skye Signature Tactic Sky Strike

Led by Commander Lyra Skye, the Starfall Exarchs specialize in: - long-range reconnaissance - deep frontier warfare - surprise assaults - hostile territory operations - prolonged unsupported campaigns

Their pilots are rigorously trained to survive under extreme operational conditions with limited logistical support.

The Exarchs are particularly famous for their Sky Strike doctrine, involving high-altitude combat drops directly into enemy strongholds.

These sudden assaults often disrupt command structures before defenders can properly organize resistance.

Throughout the Core, the Starfall Exarchs are respected for their independence, adaptability, and operational precision.

docs.title: Havoc’s Sandstorm Legion
docs.text:
Specialization Rapid Assault Warfare Combat Style Mobility and Disruption :material-desert: Preferred Terrain Desert and Wasteland Operations

Havoc’s Sandstorm Legion thrives in chaotic battlefield environments.

The Legion specializes in: - rapid mechanized assaults - battlefield disruption - fast-strike operations - mobility warfare - aggressive flanking tactics

Their mech forces emphasize speed and maneuverability, often darting through enemy formations to strike vulnerable assets before disappearing into dust, smoke, and debris.

The Legion is particularly effective in: - desert warfare - wasteland conflicts - low-visibility environments - unstable frontier terrain

Enemy commanders frequently describe Sandstorm assaults as disorienting and psychologically exhausting.

docs.title: Gravewalker’s Horde
docs.text:
:material-cannon: Specialization Heavy Assault and Shock Warfare Founder The Gravewalker Primary Role Fortification Breach Operations

Gravewalker’s Horde is infamous throughout the Core for its relentless offensive doctrine.

The company combines: - heavy artillery - shock troops - armored breakthroughs - concentrated assault formations

to overwhelm even heavily fortified positions.

The company was founded by the legendary mercenary commander known only as The Gravewalker, a figure reputed to have never lost a battle.

Stories surrounding the Gravewalker have become deeply mythologized throughout mercenary culture.

Many accounts describe him personally leading assaults from the front lines during seemingly hopeless battles before turning certain defeat into decisive victory.

Whether all such stories are true remains widely debated.

docs.title: The Shadowland Drifters
docs.text:
Specialization Tactical Deception and Ambush Warfare Combat Style Indirect Engagement Operational Role Disruption Operations

The Shadowland Drifters are masters of stealth, tactical deception, and battlefield disruption.

Rather than engaging in direct confrontation, the Drifters specialize in: - ambush tactics - infiltration - misdirection - indirect engagement - strategic harassment

Their operations frequently focus on weakening enemy formations and creating opportunities for larger allied offensives.

The Drifters are especially effective at dismantling: - supply lines - sensor grids - communications infrastructure - defensive coordination

before enemies fully realize they are under attack.

Their interventions have altered the outcomes of numerous major campaigns despite involving relatively limited direct combat.

docs.title: Abyssal Marauders
docs.text:
Specialization High-Risk Operations Commander Major Jorah Helm :material-rescue: Operational Role Rescue and Asset Recovery

Led by the infamous Major Jorah Helm, the Abyssal Marauders are widely regarded as the daredevils of the mercenary world.

The Marauders routinely accept: - impossible contracts - deep extraction missions - hazardous rescue operations - asset recovery campaigns - high-risk frontier deployments

that many other companies refuse outright.

Their missions frequently involve recovering personnel, technology, or strategic resources capable of altering regional balances of power.

The Marauders possess a reputation for recklessness, but also for succeeding under conditions where conventional military planning has already failed.

docs.title: Ark’s Outcast Guardians
docs.text:
Specialization Urban Warfare and Pacification Combat Style Rapid Deployment Primary Role Settlement and Base Security

Ark’s Outcast Guardians are among the most reliable urban warfare specialists operating within the Core.

The company excels at: - close-quarters combat - rapid deployment - street-level warfare - settlement defense - urban pacification - fortified district security

Their forces are capable of operating independently for extended periods within unstable urban environments.

The Guardians are particularly valued for their ability to: - secure populated regions - stabilize contested settlements - maintain operational control in chaotic environments

Both frontier governments and House administrators frequently rely upon the Guardians during politically unstable occupations or major civil unrest.

docs.title: Relationship with the Great Houses
docs.text:
Lesser Companies frequently develop operational preferences or long-standing relationships with specific Great Houses.

Unlike the Greater Companies, which attempt to preserve broader strategic neutrality, many Lesser Companies maintain: - ideological sympathies - historical alliances - favored contract networks - recurring military partnerships

This often shapes: - recruitment culture - equipment doctrine - battlefield philosophy - political reputation

Some companies are quietly regarded as unofficial extensions of House military strategy despite retaining independent Guild status.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, Lesser Mercenary Companies are generally viewed as: - elite - professional - dangerous - highly specialized - reliable

To frontier populations, a Lesser Company deployment often signals that a conflict is serious enough to require hardened professional steel.

To smaller mercenary crews, the Lesser Companies represent the first truly prestigious tier of mercenary success.

Many Free Jacks dream of someday earning a permanent banner slot within one of these legendary organizations.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As instability spreads throughout the Core and frontier alike, the demand for specialized mercenary warfare continues growing.

Many military observers believe the Lesser Mercenary Companies are becoming increasingly influential due to their combination of: - flexibility - professionalism - strategic specialization - operational independence

Though overshadowed politically by the Greater Companies, the Lesser Companies remain among the most dangerous and respected military forces in the modern era.

As one old Landing saying puts it:

“Anybody can hire steel. Knowing which steel to hire wins wars.”

docs.title: Minor Mercenary Units
docs.text:
"Every Great Company started as a small contract and a handful of steel."

— Mercenary saying

Minor Mercenary Units comprise the overwhelming majority of registered mercenary organizations operating throughout the Core.

While they lack the fame and political influence of the Greater Companies and Lesser Companies, minor units form the backbone of the mercenary profession. Numbering in the hundreds, these organizations undertake everything from frontier security and anti-piracy operations to convoy escort, reconnaissance, garrison duty, and local military contracts.

A typical minor unit ranges in size from a single company to a reinforced ward, though exact strength varies considerably. Some operate only a handful of mechs supported by vehicles and infantry, while others field substantial combined-arms formations capable of conducting independent campaigns.

The term minor should not be interpreted as insignificant. Many minor units possess long histories, respected commanders, and distinguished combat records. Several are among the most recognizable military organizations in the regions where they operate. The distinction is primarily one of scale. Compared to the Greater Companies, whose forces rival the militaries of Great Houses, and the Lesser Companies, which field armies measured in multiple wards, most minor units remain relatively small and specialized.

For many mercenaries, service within a minor unit represents the professional heart of the industry. These organizations are large enough to maintain dedicated logistics, training, and command structures, yet small enough that reputation, leadership, and battlefield performance remain intensely personal.

Many of today's most famous mercenary commanders, company founders, and Greater Companies trace their origins to a minor unit that survived long enough to earn a name.

docs.title: Mercenary Guild
docs.text: “Where words bind war.” — Guild motto

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Organization Type Mercenary Regulatory Authority Headquarters Drake's Landing Primary Function Contract Arbitration and Mercenary Oversight Political Status Neutral Institution Primary Influence Licensing, Contracts, Reputation Systems Guild Motto "Where Words Bind War."

The Mercenary Guild is one of the most influential institutions in the Core and the primary authority governing mercenary activity throughout civilized space.

Officially neutral and recognized by both the Star Regency and the Great Houses, the Guild exists to regulate the vast system of private warfare upon which much of the modern Core depends. It oversees company registration, contract enforcement, arbitration, salvage rights, payment guarantees, reputation records, and operational conduct for thousands of mercenary organizations.

Most legal mercenary work passes through the Guild Open Board, a vast contract exchange network that connects employers with registered companies across known space. Through its licensing systems, arbitration courts, and reputation records, the Guild provides the structure that allows independent military forces to operate on a scale that would otherwise be impossible.

Although it commands only modest military forces of its own, the Guild possesses immense economic and legal influence. Entire frontier economies, military campaigns, and political agreements depend upon the assumption that Guild contracts will be honored and Guild rulings respected.

In practice, the Mercenary Guild serves as one of the primary institutions preventing private warfare from descending into uncontrolled chaos.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild traces its origins to the early mercenary settlements that emerged on Drake's Landing centuries after the world was granted to the legendary commander Drake.

As more companies established permanent operations on the planet, the growing mercenary community faced increasingly familiar problems. Contracts were disputed, employers refused payment, salvage claims overlapped, and rival companies occasionally settled disagreements through violence rather than negotiation.

Initially these matters were handled informally through agreements between senior commanders and respected company leaders. Over time, however, the growing volume of mercenary activity made such arrangements increasingly difficult to maintain.

The largest companies operating from Drake's Landing eventually established common standards for contract enforcement, arbitration, reputation tracking, and operational conduct. These early agreements proved remarkably successful and gradually evolved into the institution now known as the Mercenary Guild.

By the time the Great Houses and the Star Regency formally recognized the Guild, it had already become the accepted authority governing mercenary affairs throughout much of civilized space.

As a result, the Guild occupies a unique position within the Core. Unlike many major institutions, its authority was not granted by governments. It was built by the mercenary community itself and later acknowledged by the powers of the age.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
The Guild's guiding principle is summarized in its motto:

"Where Words Bind War."

To the Mercenary Guild, warfare is not an aberration of civilization. It is a permanent feature of it.

Guild doctrine holds that conflict can never be eliminated. Worlds will continue to compete for resources, governments will continue to pursue power, and soldiers will continue to fight for causes, employers, and survival. The Guild therefore does not seek to prevent war. It seeks to contain it.

Within Guild philosophy, contracts serve a purpose far greater than commerce. A contract defines obligations, limits escalation, establishes accountability, and creates a framework through which violence can occur without collapsing into chaos. Every signed agreement represents a set of boundaries that all parties understand before the first shot is fired.

Guild officials often argue that civilization itself depends upon enforceable agreements. Without trusted contracts, employers would refuse payment, companies would ignore obligations, salvage disputes would become wars, and every battlefield victory would invite a dozen new conflicts.

For this reason, the Guild treats contracts with near-religious seriousness. To Guild administrators, a signed agreement is not merely a business arrangement. It is one of the few things standing between organized warfare and anarchy.

docs.title: Drake's Landing
docs.text:
The Guild maintains its headquarters on Drake's Landing, the mercenary capital of civilized space and the historical birthplace of the Guild itself.

Although located within the borders of the Starcrest Protectorate, Drake's Landing operates as neutral ground for mercenary activity throughout the Core. Its contract halls, recruitment centers, repair facilities, arbitration courts, and Open Board exchanges attract employers and companies from every major power.

The relationship between Drake's Landing and the Guild is inseparable. The earliest foundations of Guild authority emerged from agreements between the mercenary companies that gathered on the world centuries ago. As those agreements evolved into formal systems of arbitration, licensing, and contract enforcement, Drake's Landing gradually became the administrative and cultural center of regulated mercenary activity.

Today, thousands of pilots, mechanics, recruiters, brokers, salvagers, and company representatives pass through Drake's Landing every cycle. Guild influence is strongest there, and many frontier pilots joke that a company is not truly legitimate until it has survived at least one contract season on Landing.

The world remains both sanctuary and proving ground for mercenary organizations. Companies are founded there, fortunes are made there, and reputations are often won or lost there. For many mercenaries, a Guild seal and a successful contract on Drake's Landing mark the beginning of a real career.

docs.title: The Open Board
docs.text:
The Open Board is the Guild's centralized contract exchange network and the primary marketplace for mercenary work throughout the Core.

Governments, corporations, frontier settlements, noble houses, and private citizens may post contracts through the Board. Registered companies review available assignments, negotiate terms, and secure Guild-backed agreements through its systems.

Most mercenary careers are built through the Open Board. A company's reputation, operational history, and Guild standing heavily influence what work becomes available and how much employers are willing to pay.

Although alternatives exist in frontier space, the Open Board remains the dominant mechanism through which private military force is bought and sold throughout civilized space. Many economists consider it one of the most important commercial institutions in the Core, influencing everything from frontier security to interstellar trade.

docs.title: Licensing, Reputation, and Arbitration
docs.text:
Most recognized mercenary companies operate under Guild registration.

Registration grants access to the Open Board, Guild arbitration services, payment guarantees, recognized salvage rights, and official company records. While operating outside Guild authority is possible, particularly in the deep frontier, unregistered organizations are frequently regarded as pirates, raiders, or independent militias rather than legitimate mercenary companies.

The Guild maintains extensive records covering contract fulfillment, battlefield conduct, civilian casualty reports, disciplinary actions, and operational history. These records form the basis of a company's reputation, which many mercenaries consider more valuable than military strength itself.

Reliable companies with strong records often receive better contracts, higher pay, favorable salvage terms, and long-term relationships with major employers. Conversely, companies that engage in piracy, violate contracts, abandon employers, or commit major atrocities may face sanctions, suspensions, or formal blacklisting.

The Guild also serves as the primary arbitration authority for mercenary disputes throughout the Core. Payment disagreements, salvage claims, contract interpretation, and operational conflicts are routinely settled through Guild courts and arbitration panels.

Although the Guild possesses limited military power, its rulings carry significant weight. Most governments, corporations, and mercenary organizations comply not because they are forced to, but because confidence in the entire mercenary system depends upon the belief that contracts will be honored and disputes can be resolved peacefully.

A House governor may ignore a Guild ruling. The Guild cannot stop them. What it can do is ensure that every mercenary company in the Core learns of it.

docs.title: Political Relationships
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild occupies a unique position within the political structure of the Core.

Officially neutral and recognized by both the Star Regency and the Great Houses, the Guild serves as the accepted authority governing mercenary affairs throughout civilized space. All major powers make extensive use of mercenary companies, whether for frontier defense, anti-piracy operations, convoy protection, infrastructure security, or less public activities.

The Guild generally avoids direct involvement in House rivalries. Maintaining neutrality is considered essential to preserving institutional legitimacy. While House sponsorship of mercenary companies is common, Guild officials rarely concern themselves with who employs a company so long as Guild rules and contractual obligations are respected.

Contrary to popular belief, the Guild is not a major military power. It maintains station defense forces, limited patrol fleets, investigative divisions, and security detachments primarily for the protection of Guild infrastructure and personnel.

Its true authority derives from something far more valuable: legitimacy.

The Great Houses tolerate and support the Guild because it provides a framework through which private warfare can remain predictable and manageable. The Star Regency relies upon it for many of the same reasons. Few institutions are better positioned to prevent frontier disputes, mercenary conflicts, and proxy wars from escalating into direct inter-house confrontation.

For this reason, the Guild often exerts influence far beyond what its military strength alone would suggest.

docs.title: Guild Culture
docs.text:
Guild culture is highly procedural, pragmatic, and famously indifferent to ideology.

Guild officials are less concerned with why a conflict occurs than with whether its terms are clearly defined, enforceable, and survivable. Contracts, records, precedents, and arbitration decisions occupy a central place within Guild life, and many officials view predictability as a virtue equal to courage or military skill.

This mindset often places the Guild at odds with both politicians and mercenaries. House leaders frequently become frustrated by Guild insistence on procedure, while mercenary commanders sometimes view Guild administrators as bureaucrats who have forgotten what battlefields look like.

Yet even many critics acknowledge the Guild's importance. For countless companies, the Guild represents the difference between professional military service and outright piracy. A Guild seal opens doors throughout the Core, while the loss of one can end a career.

Among mercenaries, opinions on the Guild vary widely. Some regard it as civilization's necessary referee. Others see it as overly bureaucratic and increasingly favorable toward large companies and House-backed organizations.

Despite these complaints, few companies willingly operate outside Guild systems for long.

As one old Landing saying puts it:

"No steel rides long without a Guild seal."

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions between the Great Houses continue to rise, the Mercenary Guild faces increasing pressure from every direction.

Expanding frontier conflicts, growing pirate activity, House-backed proxy wars, and increasingly aggressive political rivalries have strained many of the systems that have governed mercenary affairs for centuries. Guild arbitrators report growing disputes over salvage rights, contract interpretation, and the use of mercenary forces in conflicts that increasingly resemble undeclared wars.

Many within the Guild fear that its greatest challenge is no longer enforcing individual contracts, but preserving confidence in the system itself. The Guild can function only so long as employers, governments, and mercenary companies continue to accept its authority and honor its rulings.

For now, the Guild remains one of the few institutions trusted across factional lines. Whether it can maintain that position in the years ahead remains an open question.

"Civilizations do not collapse when wars begin. They collapse when nobody agrees on the rules." — Guild arbitration saying

docs.title: Guild Charter
docs.text: The Guild Charter is the legal and customary foundation of mercenary warfare in the Core.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: It defines recognized companies, contract obligations, employer responsibilities, dispute procedures, and the basic protections granted to Guild-certified mercenaries.

docs.title: Notes for Expansion
docs.text: TODO: Add examples, famous disputes, unusual contract clauses, and links to specific mercenary companies.

docs.title: Contracts and Arbitration
docs.text: Guild contracts are designed to prevent limited military work from becoming uncontrolled private war.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Guild records employer obligations, mission terms, payment schedules, salvage clauses, reputation penalties, and arbitration procedures.

docs.title: Notes for Expansion
docs.text: TODO: Add examples, famous disputes, unusual contract clauses, and links to specific mercenary companies.

docs.title: Reputation and Blacklisting
docs.text: Reputation is one of the Guild's most powerful tools.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Companies that abandon contracts, betray employers, or violate Guild law may find themselves blacklisted. Employers who cheat mercenaries can be sanctioned as well.

docs.title: Notes for Expansion
docs.text: TODO: Add examples, famous disputes, unusual contract clauses, and links to specific mercenary companies.

docs.title: Salvage Rights
docs.text: Salvage rights are often as valuable as direct payment.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Mercenary contracts may grant battlefield salvage, partial salvage claims, priority claims, or strict employer ownership. The exact terms can determine whether a contract makes or ruins a company.

docs.title: Notes for Expansion
docs.text: TODO: Add examples, famous disputes, unusual contract clauses, and links to specific mercenary companies.

docs.title: Military Academies
docs.text:
The Great Houses maintain extensive military academy systems responsible for training future officers, pilots, logisticians, and battlefield commanders.

Although all major academies teach common subjects such as mech operation, combined-arms doctrine, battlefield communications, logistics, and leadership, each state emphasizes different philosophies.

The Confederate Vanguard Union is widely regarded as producing the most versatile officers. Union cadets receive extensive training in logistics, strategic planning, and combined-arms warfare, resulting in commanders capable of coordinating complex military operations across multiple domains.

The Omnisphere Imperium emphasizes martial excellence and individual achievement. Imperial academies cultivate intense competition among cadets and place great importance on personal mastery, battlefield initiative, and tactical prowess.

The Starcrest Protectorate is known for rigorous physical and psychological conditioning programs intended to produce resilient leaders capable of operating in harsh environments and prolonged campaigns.

The Helios Sovereignty maintains prestigious academies attended by many members of noble and wealthy families. While these institutions produce many capable officers, critics occasionally argue that social status influences advancement opportunities.

Orion Corporate military education emphasizes technology, systems integration, battlefield analytics, and advanced simulation environments. Orion graduates are often highly proficient in operating sophisticated equipment and coordinating technologically complex formations.

Advanced simulators and dedicated training mechs are widely used throughout the Core. These machines allow cadets to participate in realistic combat exercises with minimal risk, often utilizing specialized training weapons and scoring systems similar to large-scale laser tag.

docs.title: Conventional Forces
docs.text:
"The battlefield belongs to mechs, but world belongs to whoever controls the roads, cities, and supply depots."

— Imperium strategic doctrine

While War Mechs dominate modern warfare throughout the Core, they do not operate entirely alone. Every major power maintains vast conventional military forces consisting of aerospace fighters, gunships, armored vehicles, support units, and infantry.

Mechs are the decisive arm of modern warfare, capable of seizing objectives, breaking defensive lines, and defeating conventional formations with relatively little support. As a result, most military campaigns are planned around mech formations, with conventional forces serving in supporting roles.

Conventional forces remain essential for planetary defense, logistics, reconnaissance, occupation duties, border security, transportation, and long-term control of captured territory. Though they rarely decide major battles on their own, they provide the infrastructure and support necessary for mech forces to operate effectively.

In most campaigns, conventional forces greatly outnumber the mechs they support, even as the outcome of the conflict is often determined by the performance of a comparatively small number of mech units.

docs.title: Aerospace Fighters
docs.text:
Aerospace fighters are among the most versatile military assets in the Core.

Designed for both atmospheric and orbital operations, these craft serve as interceptors, escorts, reconnaissance platforms, and strike aircraft. Aerospace squadrons routinely protect larger warships, escort troop transports, conduct raids against enemy installations, and patrol national borders.

Conflicts between aerospace fighters are relatively common throughout the Core. Skirmishes, interceptions, and border incidents occur regularly between rival powers.

Large-scale fleet engagements, however, are rare. Capital ships are expensive to construct and difficult to replace, and any major fleet action is almost certain to attract the attention of the Star Regent and the Stellar Conclave.

Within planetary atmospheres, aerospace fighters provide close air support, reconnaissance, and rapid-response strike capabilities.

docs.title: Gunships and Dropships
docs.text:
Gunships and dropships form the backbone of military mobility.

Dropships transport troops, vehicles, supplies, and mechs between planets and from orbit to the battlefield. Though less maneuverable than aerospace fighters, they possess significantly greater cargo capacity and endurance.

Gunships are heavily armed atmospheric craft designed to support ground operations. While slower than aerospace fighters, they can remain over a battlefield for extended periods, providing direct fire support against infantry, vehicles, and fortified positions.

Many military operations rely heavily upon gunships to transport troops, reinforce threatened positions, and suppress enemy defenses.

docs.title: VTOL Craft
docs.text:
Vertical Take-Off and Landing (VTOL) craft occupy a niche between aircraft and ground vehicles. Capable of hovering, operating from improvised landing zones, and maneuvering through difficult terrain, VTOLs are commonly employed for reconnaissance, transport, logistics, and close air support.

Military forces throughout the Core make extensive use of VTOL scout craft to monitor enemy movements, identify targets, and coordinate artillery or aerospace strikes. Larger transport variants are frequently used to move infantry, supplies, and special operations teams into areas inaccessible to conventional vehicles.

Armed VTOL gunships provide valuable battlefield support against infantry, light vehicles, and fortified positions. However, their relatively low speed and vulnerability to anti-aircraft weapons make them ill-suited for direct engagement with aerospace fighters or concentrated mech formations.

While rarely as glamorous as aerospace fighters or as powerful as mechs, VTOLs remain among the most versatile and widely used military vehicles in the Core.

docs.title: Armored Vehicles
docs.text: Despite the dominance of mechs, armored vehicles remain common throughout the Core.

docs.title: Main Battle Tanks
docs.text:
Modern tanks range from relatively agile light and medium vehicles to massive heavy tanks designed specifically to engage mechs. While even the largest tanks are smaller than most mechs, they are substantially larger and more heavily armed than contemporary armored vehicles from humanity's past.

Heavy tanks often mount oversized cannons, missile systems, and advanced targeting equipment intended to threaten mechs through concentrated firepower.

docs.title: Fire Support Vehicles
docs.text:
Dedicated support vehicles provide long-range missile bombardment, artillery support, electronic warfare capabilities, and battlefield command functions.

Many armies deploy mobile sensor platforms, radar vehicles, communication centers, and battlefield coordination units alongside frontline forces.

docs.title: Reconnaissance and Transport
docs.text:
Lighter vehicles perform scouting, reconnaissance, courier, and patrol duties. Armored personnel carriers and infantry fighting vehicles transport troops across dangerous terrain while providing limited protection and fire support.

Though vulnerable in direct combat against mechs, these vehicles remain invaluable for screening operations, rapid deployments, and planetary security duties.

docs.title: Artillery
docs.text:
Artillery remains an important component of modern armies, though its role has diminished since the rise of mechs.

Self-propelled guns, rocket artillery, and missile carriers are commonly used to bombard fixed positions, suppress defensive fortifications, and support large-scale military operations. Artillery is particularly effective against infantry concentrations, vehicle formations, and stationary targets.

Against mobile mech formations, however, artillery is far less decisive. Modern mechs possess advanced sensors, electronic countermeasures, active defenses, and exceptional mobility, making them difficult to target and destroy through indirect fire alone. As a result, artillery is typically employed in a supporting role rather than as a primary battlefield asset.

Despite these limitations, artillery remains invaluable for area denial, defensive operations, and the destruction of hardened targets.

docs.title: Infantry
docs.text:
Infantry remains the most numerous military force in the Core.

While infantry rarely decides major battles, soldiers perform countless tasks that mechs cannot efficiently accomplish. They secure facilities, patrol cities, occupy territory, guard supply lines, conduct reconnaissance, and maintain order in conquered regions.

Most infantry falls into one of two broad categories.

docs.title: Basic Infantry
docs.text:
Basic infantry consists of conventional soldiers equipped with rifles, machine guns, anti-vehicle weapons, mines, and support equipment.

Specialized units may carry portable anti-aircraft systems, anti-mech launchers, recoilless rifles, demolition charges, or electronic warfare equipment.

Infantry is rarely used in direct assaults against mech formations. Instead, soldiers typically rely upon ambushes, prepared defenses, minefields, and fortified positions to challenge larger opponents.

docs.title: Enhanced Infantry
docs.text:
Enhanced infantry utilizes powered exoskeletons and advanced combat equipment to improve mobility, endurance, and survivability.

Some exoskeletons provide increased speed, strength, and limited jump capabilities. Others incorporate substantial armor protection, creating heavily armed infantry units capable of threatening vehicles and even light mechs under favorable conditions.

Enhanced infantry is particularly effective in urban environments, dense terrain, and defensive operations.

Despite these advantages, even the most heavily equipped infantry remains vulnerable to direct engagement with mech formations.

docs.title: The Role of Infantry in Modern Warfare
docs.text:
Infantry rarely serves as the primary combat arm of major powers.

In the Core, most military campaigns are designed around mech formations supported by conventional assets. Once mech forces establish control of a battlefield, infantry typically follows to secure objectives, hold territory, police civilian populations, and eliminate remaining resistance.

Defensive forces may use infantry to establish fortified positions supported by turrets, minefields, anti-aircraft batteries, and vehicle formations. Such defenses can slow a mech advance and inflict casualties, but once the defensive line collapses, infantry units often withdraw rather than confront advancing mechs directly.

For this reason, most military theorists view infantry as a supporting arm rather than a decisive battlefield force.

docs.title: Naval Forces
docs.text:
Naval warfare is relatively uncommon throughout the Core.

Most inhabited worlds possess comparatively small populations concentrated around major cities, industrial centers, and transportation hubs. As a result, few governments invest heavily in large naval fleets, preferring instead to project power through aerospace assets capable of operating over both land and sea.

Where naval forces do exist, they are typically limited to coastal patrol vessels, transport ships, search-and-rescue craft, and local security forces. Aircraft and aerospace fighters perform most maritime reconnaissance and strike missions.

A handful of oceanic worlds maintain larger naval formations, but such forces are considered specialized exceptions rather than a major component of modern military doctrine.

docs.title: Modern Warfare
docs.text:
Modern warfare throughout the Core revolves around mechs.

While conventional forces remain important, War Mechs are the primary instrument through which military power is projected. Their combination of mobility, durability, and overwhelming firepower allows relatively small mech formations to defeat far larger conventional forces.

Military commanders commonly deploy mechs alongside infantry, armored vehicles, aerospace support, and logistical elements. Such combined-arms formations offer greater flexibility, improved reconnaissance, and more effective occupation of captured territory.

However, unlike conventional forces, mechs are capable of operating independently. Skilled pilots can perform orbital insertions directly into contested zones, destroy defensive positions, defeat conventional formations, and seize critical objectives without immediate support.

Once resistance has been broken, infantry, vehicles, and support personnel typically move in to secure territory, establish supply lines, and maintain long-term control of the region.

For this reason, most military theorists distinguish between winning battles and holding territory. Conventional forces excel at the latter. Mechs excel at the former.

Across the Core, wars are ultimately decided by the ability of one side's mechs to defeat the other's.

docs.title: Dropships
docs.text:
Dropships serve as the logistical backbone of modern mech warfare. Responsible for transporting military forces between orbital fleets and planetary surfaces, they range from small tactical transports carrying a handful of mechs to enormous mobile bases capable of deploying entire battalions.

Smaller dropships are commonly used by mercenary companies, planetary defense forces, and rapid-response military formations. These vessels typically transport four to twelve mechs alongside maintenance equipment, spare parts, and support personnel.

Larger military dropships may carry dozens of mechs, conventional vehicles, infantry formations, aerospace craft, and extensive repair facilities. The largest command vessels function as mobile headquarters capable of sustaining prolonged planetary campaigns without requiring permanent ground installations.

Many dropships are heavily armed. Defensive weapons, missile batteries, and point-defense systems are common, while dedicated assault dropships may provide fire support during planetary landings. Nevertheless, most commanders regard dropships primarily as strategic assets rather than frontline combat vessels.

Because mechs can be deployed directly from orbit and require comparatively little supporting infrastructure after landing, dropships have become one of the key technologies underpinning modern military doctrine throughout the Core.

docs.title: Fleet Structure
docs.text:
"A mech can be rebuilt. A fleet must be preserved."

— Imperium naval doctrine

Space travel is the foundation of civilization throughout the Core. Every world depends upon fleets to transport people, goods, military forces, and information across interstellar distances.

Despite their importance, military fleets are far less numerous than many citizens imagine. Warships are among the most expensive and technologically demanding assets maintained by the Great Houses, requiring vast industrial resources, specialized shipyards, and years or even decades of construction.

As a result, fleets are used cautiously and losses are taken extremely seriously.

docs.title: The Legacy of the Empire
docs.text:
Modern fleets remain heavily shaped by the collapse of the Golden Empire.

Many of the largest and most powerful vessels in existence today were constructed during the Empire Era. While the Great Houses retain the ability to build modern warships, many ancient technologies have been lost, and certain classes of vessel can no longer be reproduced.

The most famous examples are the Battlestars, immense warships dating back to the Empire itself. These vessels remain among the most powerful military assets in known space.

No Great House possesses the ability to construct new Battlestars.

Every existing vessel is therefore considered irreplaceable.

docs.title: Fleet Composition
docs.text:
Most military fleets consist of a mixture of warships, support vessels, troop transports, and logistical assets.

Common vessel types include:

docs.title: Battlestars
docs.text:
→ See: Battlestars (full article)

The largest and most powerful warships in existence.

Battlestars function as mobile fortresses, fleet headquarters, invasion platforms, and symbols of national power. Possessing enormous aerospace complements, logistical capacity, and command facilities, they often serve as the centerpieces of major military operations.

The Union and Protectorate each possess three Battlestars. The Imperium, Sovereignty, and Corporate each maintain two.

The deployment of a Battlestar is often viewed as a significant political event.

docs.title: Capital Ships
docs.text:
The backbone of most military fleets.

Capital ships carry substantial firepower and frequently serve as flagships for regional commands, patrol fleets, and military expeditions. Though less powerful than Battlestars, they can still dominate conventional naval engagements.

Construction of a single capital ship may require decades of work.

docs.title: Cruisers and Escorts
docs.text:
Cruisers, destroyers, frigates, and escort vessels perform the majority of routine naval operations.

These ships patrol trade routes, escort civilian traffic, protect frontier settlements, suppress piracy, and support military campaigns.

While individually less impressive than capital ships, they represent the majority of military vessels operating throughout the Core.

docs.title: Troop Transports and Logistics Ships
docs.text:
Military power cannot be projected without transportation.

Dedicated transport vessels move mechs, vehicles, infantry, supplies, and personnel between worlds. Logistical support ships sustain military operations far from established bases and often determine how long a campaign can be maintained.

Many military planners consider logistics vessels more strategically important than combat ships.

docs.title: Civilian Shipping
docs.text:
Military fleets represent only a fraction of all spacecraft operating within the Core.

Thousands of civilian vessels transport cargo, passengers, industrial materials, and commercial goods between inhabited worlds. Much of this traffic passes through infrastructure operated or regulated by StarCom.

Because civilian shipping is so vital to economic stability, protecting trade routes remains one of the primary responsibilities of military fleets.

docs.title: StarCom Fleets
docs.text:
Unlike the Great Houses, StarCom maintains extensive civilian transportation and support fleets operating throughout the Core.

These vessels move personnel, supplies, and diplomatic missions between worlds and are often encountered far more frequently than military warships.

StarCom also maintains a substantial exploration fleet operating beyond the borders of known space. The size and activities of this fleet remain largely unknown to the public.

The Orion Corporate is the only major House known to maintain a significant independent exploration program, though its capabilities remain modest compared to those of StarCom.

docs.title: Frontier Patrols
docs.text:
A significant portion of every major fleet is committed to frontier operations.

Pirates, raiders, smugglers, and independent warlords routinely operate beyond the direct control of the Great Houses. Protecting trade routes and monitoring activity along the frontier requires constant naval patrols across vast regions of space.

As a result, many military forces are spread thinly across enormous territories, limiting the ability of governments to respond rapidly to crises.

This reality helps explain the continued importance of mercenary companies and planetary defense forces throughout the Core.

docs.title: Orbital Bombardment
docs.text:
Large-scale orbital bombardment is almost unheard of within the Core.

The devastation caused by the collapse of the Golden Empire left a lasting cultural and political scar upon civilization. Most governments view the deliberate destruction of valuable infrastructure as wasteful, irresponsible, and strategically shortsighted.

Political considerations further discourage such actions. The Stellar Conclave treats large-scale bombardment as an extreme escalation likely to invite severe diplomatic and military consequences. Most leaders also recognize that any precedent they establish may eventually be used against them.

Limited orbital fire support does occur during military operations. Warships may provide precision strikes, artillery support, or targeted attacks against military objectives when requested by forces on the ground.

Planetary devastation, however, remains largely the domain of history rather than modern warfare.

docs.title: Strategic Reality
docs.text:
Despite the immense size of interstellar civilization, military fleets remain surprisingly scarce.

Warships require decades to construct. Battlestars cannot be replaced. Trade routes stretch across hundreds of worlds. Frontier patrols consume enormous resources. Every vessel lost represents years of industrial investment.

As a result, the Great Houses generally employ their fleets to transport, support, and sustain military operations rather than risk them in unnecessary engagements.

The fleets of the Core carry civilization across the stars.

The mechs decide what happens when they arrive.

docs.title: Military Organization
docs.text: “Steel without command is just scrap waiting to happen.” — Union field doctrine

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Primary Military Basis Hierarchical Mech Warfare Core Combat Unit Column Common Command Structure Column → Company → Ward → March Primary Combat Platform War Mechs Operational Philosophy Flexible Combined Command

At the center of nearly every military organization in the Core stands the War Mech. Though supported by conventional forces such as infantry, vehicles, aerospace units, and vast logistical networks, mech formations remain the decisive arm of modern warfare. Across the Core, battles are won and lost by the ability of one side's mechs to defeat the other's.

The military structure of the Core evolved around this reality. For centuries, militaries have organized their forces into scalable formations capable of coordinating everything from small frontier patrols to massive interstellar campaigns. While technology and battlefield doctrine have changed dramatically over time, the underlying organizational principles have remained remarkably consistent.

From mercenary companies and planetary militias to the immense armies of the Great Houses, military formations are built upon a common hierarchical framework designed to provide clear command, operational flexibility, and effective coordination across vast distances. At every level of that hierarchy, the primary purpose remains the same: to deploy, support, and command mech forces in battle.

docs.title: Structure and Hierarchy
docs.text: Formation Typical Size Typical Responsibility Column 4 Mechs Tactical operations Company 12 Mechs Local operations Ward 40-50 Mechs Regional operations March 100-200 Mechs Planetary campaigns Brigade 1,000-2,000 Mechs Multi-world operations Division ~10,000 Mechs Sector command Corps 50,000-75,000 Mechs Major border or theater command Command 200,000-350,000 Mechs National military authority

docs.title: The Column
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 4 Mechs

The fundamental combat unit of modern mech warfare is the Column.

A standard Column consists of four mechs operating under a single commanding officer, supported by technicians, logistics personnel, and other attached specialists. Though small by military standards, a Column possesses enough firepower, mobility, and tactical flexibility to operate independently for extended periods.

The four-mech structure has endured for centuries because it provides an effective balance between command simplicity and battlefield capability. A Column is large enough to perform multiple battlefield roles simultaneously while remaining small enough for a single commander to coordinate effectively under combat conditions.

Throughout the Core, Columns are routinely assigned a wide variety of missions, including reconnaissance, patrol operations, raiding actions, defensive deployments, urban combat, and direct assault operations. Many frontier worlds rely upon a handful of Columns as their primary military force.

Because of their versatility, Columns form the foundation of nearly all military organizations in civilized space. Larger formations are built by combining multiple Columns under increasingly senior commanders.

docs.title: Reinforced Columns
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 6-8 Mechs

In high-risk or specialized operations, commanders may deploy Reinforced Columns consisting of six or, more rarely, eight mechs.

These formations are particularly common among mercenary companies, frontier militias, assault units, and irregular military forces. The additional mechs provide greater battlefield endurance, tactical flexibility, and redundancy, allowing the formation to absorb losses while maintaining operational effectiveness.

While Reinforced Columns possess substantially greater combat power than standard formations, they also require more logistical support and are generally less mobile. As a result, most professional militaries continue to regard the four-mech Column as the ideal balance between flexibility, firepower, and command efficiency.

docs.title: Companies
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 12 Mechs Composition: 3 Columns

Three Columns are typically organized into a Company.

A standard Company fields approximately twelve mechs supported by dedicated maintenance crews, logistical personnel, tactical staff, and command elements. At this level, a formation begins to possess the resources necessary to sustain prolonged military operations without relying heavily upon outside support.

Companies are widely regarded as the smallest formation capable of conducting independent campaigns. A Company may be tasked with defending a frontier settlement, suppressing a local insurgency, conducting anti-piracy operations, or securing strategic objectives on a lightly defended world.

For many smaller powers and frontier governments, a single Company represents a significant military force. Even among the Great Houses, Companies are frequently deployed independently to patrol borders, respond to emergencies, and reinforce threatened regions.

The term mercenary company originates from this organizational level, though actual mercenary formations vary enormously in size. Some independent outfits field only a single Column, while the largest Greater Mercenary Companies command forces rivaling those of entire national militaries.

docs.title: Reinforced Companies
docs.text:
Some militaries and mercenary organizations employ Reinforced Companies consisting of sixteen to twenty mechs. These formations are most commonly used for independent operations, frontier deployments, and specialized assault missions where additional firepower or operational endurance is required.

While Reinforced Companies offer greater battlefield flexibility than standard formations, they also require significantly more logistical support. As a result, most professional militaries continue to regard the twelve-mech Company as the standard organizational model.

docs.title: Wards
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 40-50 Mechs Composition: 3-5 Companies

Several Companies combine to form a Ward.

A typical Ward fields between forty and fifty mechs supported by dedicated command personnel, logistical assets, and increasingly sophisticated coordination systems. At this scale, a formation possesses the resources necessary to sustain prolonged operations across large geographic regions while maintaining multiple independent combat elements.

Wards form the backbone of most military operations throughout the Core. Large enough to conduct offensive campaigns yet small enough to remain highly mobile, they represent the formation most commonly deployed when a government expects serious resistance.

A Ward may be tasked with securing a strategic region, defending a major population center, suppressing a large insurgency, conducting planetary offensives, or serving as the primary military force on a frontier world. Many military planners consider the Ward the smallest formation capable of exerting meaningful strategic influence over an entire campaign.

Because of their balance of mobility, firepower, and operational endurance, Wards are frequently described as the workhorses of modern warfare. Throughout the Core, most major military actions are ultimately fought and won by formations at the Ward level.

docs.title: Marches
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 100-200 Mechs Composition: 3–5 Wards

Several Wards combine to form a March.

A typical March fields between one hundred and two hundred mechs supported by extensive logistical infrastructure, aerospace attachments, command staff, and conventional military assets. At this scale, a formation possesses the resources necessary to conduct independent military campaigns for extended periods without relying heavily upon outside reinforcement.

Marches are among the largest formations commonly encountered in active military operations. They are capable of launching planetary invasions, conducting prolonged offensive campaigns, suppressing large-scale rebellions, and maintaining control over entire regions of contested territory.

Unlike smaller formations, a March functions not merely as a combat force but as a self-contained military organization. Commanders at this level must coordinate logistics, aerospace operations, intelligence gathering, troop movements, and long-term strategic objectives in addition to battlefield engagements.
