The deployment of a full March is often viewed as a sign that a conflict has escalated beyond a localized military action. On frontier worlds, the arrival of a March may represent the largest military force the local population will ever witness.

Military historians frequently describe the March as the smallest formation capable of independently conducting a planetary campaign from beginning to end.

docs.title: Brigades, Divisions, Corps, and Commands
docs.text: Beyond the March, military formations transition from battlefield organizations into strategic instruments of state power. These immense formations coordinate campaigns across multiple worlds, oversee vast logistical networks, and command military resources measured in the tens or hundreds of thousands of mechs.

docs.title: Brigade
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 1,000–2,000 Mechs Composition: 7–10 Marches

A Brigade represents the first truly interplanetary military formation. Possessing enough combat power to conquer, defend, or occupy entire worlds, Brigades are commonly assigned responsibility for strategically important systems, major military campaigns, or clusters of frontier worlds.

Unlike a March, which typically focuses on a single campaign, a Brigade must coordinate multiple simultaneous operations while maintaining reserves, supply networks, and strategic flexibility. For many smaller powers, a Brigade represents the largest military formation they can field.

docs.title: Division
docs.text:
Typical Strength: Approximately 10,000 Mechs Composition: 4–5 Brigades

Divisions oversee military operations across entire sectors of space. In addition to commanding multiple Brigades, divisional headquarters coordinate logistics, transportation, aerospace assets, industrial support, and long-term strategic planning.

Most sector governments maintain close relationships with divisional command staffs, as Divisions often represent the primary military authority within a region. During peacetime, they serve as the foundation of military readiness. During wartime, they become the operational centers from which major campaigns are directed.

docs.title: Corps
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 50,000–75,000 Mechs Composition: 5–7 Divisions

Corps command major theaters of war.

Responsible for defending borders, coordinating large-scale offensives, and managing military operations across multiple sectors, Corps represent some of the most powerful military organizations commonly deployed by the Great Houses.

A single Corps may oversee dozens of active campaigns simultaneously. During periods of heightened tension, entire borders between rival powers may fall under the responsibility of a Corps commander.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
Typical Strength: 200,000–300,000 Mechs Composition: 3–6 Corps

The Command is the highest military formation maintained by most Great Houses.

Commands direct military operations across vast regions of space and frequently coordinate multiple Corps, fleets, industrial centers, and logistical networks. In practice, a Command functions as an entire military establishment rather than a conventional battlefield formation.

The military strength of a Great House is often measured by the number and quality of Commands it can field. Full Command-level mobilizations are exceedingly rare due to their immense economic and logistical requirements, but when they occur they can reshape the balance of power across the Core.

docs.title: Battlestars
docs.text:
Typical Strength: Equivalent to 10,000–30,000 Mechs

Battlestars occupy a unique position within military organization. Though technically warships, their enormous size, aerospace capacity, logistical infrastructure, and command facilities allow them to function as independent strategic formations.

During major campaigns, a Battlestar may serve as a mobile headquarters for divisional or corps-level operations. Possessing the ability to transport, support, and deploy thousands of mechs across multiple star systems, Battlestars act as mobile fortresses capable of projecting military power far beyond the reach of conventional formations.

The deployment of a Battlestar is often regarded as a political statement as much as a military one, signaling that a Great House considers a conflict of exceptional importance.

docs.title: Limited Warfare
docs.text:
Despite the immense military forces maintained by the Great Houses, true Command-level warfare is exceedingly rare in the modern Core.

The cost of transporting, supplying, repairing, and supporting hundreds of thousands of mechs across interstellar distances is staggering. Even the wealthiest powers cannot sustain large-scale campaigns indefinitely without placing enormous strain on their economies and industrial bases.

Political considerations further discourage total war. Major conflicts between Great Houses inevitably attract the attention of the Star Regent and the Stellar Conclave, both of which possess strong incentives to prevent wars from escalating into civilization-threatening disasters.

As a result, most military forces spend far more time defending territory than conquering it. House armies routinely conduct anti-piracy operations, suppress bandit activity, secure trade routes, protect frontier settlements, and respond to raids along the borders of civilized space. While limited conflicts between rival powers do occur, outright wars between Great Houses remain uncommon.

Most inhabited worlds also maintain local defense forces responsible for planetary security. These organizations vary enormously in size and capability, ranging from lightly equipped militias to professional military formations possessing their own mech units, aerospace squadrons, and armored forces. In times of crisis, planetary defense forces are expected to contain threats, protect civilian populations, and delay hostile forces until House militaries or mercenary reinforcements can arrive.

When disputes arise between major powers, direct military intervention is often avoided in favor of mercenary proxies. By employing independent mercenary companies, governments gain plausible deniability while obscuring the true origins of military operations. Raids, sabotage missions, border skirmishes, and territorial disputes can be pursued without openly committing House forces or risking a broader political crisis.

This reliance on mercenary forces has had a profound effect on military culture throughout the Core. While House militaries are generally larger and better supplied, many spend years performing garrison duty, security operations, and defensive assignments. Mercenary companies, by contrast, are frequently deployed to active conflict zones and high-risk operations. As a result, veteran mercenary formations often possess combat experience that rivals—or in some cases exceeds—that of their national counterparts.

This reality has shaped military doctrine throughout the Core. Most formations are organized not for total war, but for rapid deployment, limited campaigns, frontier operations, and the pursuit of strategic objectives without triggering a wider conflict.

docs.title: Faction-Specific Hierarchies
docs.text:
Although military organization remains broadly standardized throughout the Core, command titles vary considerably between factions. These differences reflect centuries of cultural development, political tradition, and military philosophy.

A commander from the Union may immediately recognize the authority of an Imperial Guardian or a Protectorate Commandant, even if the titles themselves differ. While the names change, the underlying hierarchy remains remarkably consistent across civilized space.

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
The Omnisphere Imperium preserves some of the oldest military traditions in the Core. Many Imperial titles are believed to descend from institutions that predate even the Golden Empire itself.

Imperial command ranks emphasize guardianship, continuity, duty, and stewardship. Officers are viewed not merely as military leaders, but as custodians of a legacy stretching back thousands of years.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
The Union employs highly standardized military terminology focused on clarity, efficiency, and operational effectiveness. Its ranks reflect the Union's belief that military command should be practical, merit-based, and easily understood.

Due to the Union's military influence and the large number of former Union officers serving in the mercenary community, Union terminology has become the closest thing to a common military language throughout much of the Core.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
The Sovereignty blends military authority with aristocratic tradition. Many senior commands carry noble or ceremonial connotations, reflecting the faction's dynastic culture and close relationship between military service and social status.

Within the Sovereignty, military rank is often viewed as an extension of noble responsibility and leadership.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
Protectorate ranks emphasize defense, guardianship, and service to the realm. Titles such as Warden, Commandant, and Grand Marshal reflect a military culture focused on protection rather than conquest.

Protectorate officers are traditionally viewed as guardians of the people and stewards of the territory entrusted to their care.

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text:
Corporate command titles reflect the Orion Corporate's administrative and technocratic worldview. Military leaders are often treated as managers of complex systems rather than traditional battlefield aristocrats.

Terms such as Marshal, Flag Marshal, and Prodirector reflect a culture that views warfare as a problem to be coordinated, optimized, and efficiently managed.

docs.title: Mercenary Companies
docs.text:
Most mercenary organizations utilize Union-style rank structures due to their widespread recognition and practicality. However, individual companies frequently adopt unique titles, traditions, and organizational customs based on their history and culture.

Larger mercenary companies often develop military traditions rivaling those of the Great Houses themselves.

docs.title: Comparative Command Titles
docs.text: Unit Mercenary Union Sovereignty Protectorate Imperium Corporate Column Lieutenant Lieutenant Leftenant Armsman Sentinel Lieutenant Company Captain Captain Captain Praetor Vigilant Captain Ward Major Major Major Warden Warden Major March Colonel Colonel Duke Commandant Guardian Marshal Brigade Brigadier General Brigadier General Brigadier General Spartan General General Viceroy Second General Division General General General General General General Corps Varies Field Marshal Lord Marshal Grand Marshal Subcommander Flag Marshal Command - First Commander Lord Commander Oberst Commander High Commander Commodore Supreme Commander - High Minister Archduke / Archon Herzog Imperator Prodirector

docs.title: Mercenary Organization
docs.text:
While most mercenary companies broadly follow the same organizational principles used throughout the Core, few adhere to them rigidly.

Smaller mercenary outfits often maintain informal command structures based on seniority, reputation, battlefield experience, or contractual authority rather than formal military rank. Larger organizations frequently employ reinforced Columns, irregular Companies, temporary task forces, and specialized detachments tailored to the needs of individual contracts.

The greatest mercenary companies rival national militaries in both size and sophistication, while the smallest may consist of little more than a handful of mechs operating under a single commander. This flexibility is often regarded as one of the greatest strengths of the mercenary profession.

docs.title: Conventional Attachments
docs.text:
Although military formations throughout the Core are typically classified by their mech strength, few military organizations consist entirely of mechs.

At smaller scales, such as Columns and Companies, conventional support is usually limited to maintenance crews, logistics personnel, reconnaissance assets, and transport elements. These units exist primarily to keep mech formations supplied, repaired, and combat-ready.

As formations grow larger, substantial conventional forces become increasingly common. Wards and Marches often include attached infantry formations, armored vehicle companies, artillery batteries, aerospace squadrons, engineering units, and logistical support organizations. At the Brigade, Division, and Corps levels, these conventional forces may number in the tens or even hundreds of thousands of personnel.

In most military formations, conventional units are organized around supporting mech operations rather than operating independently. Infantry secure objectives captured by mechs and help maintain control of contested territory, armored vehicles provide fire support and transportation, aerospace units extend reconnaissance, air cover, and strategic mobility, while engineers and logistics personnel sustain military operations across vast distances. Despite their size and importance, conventional forces are generally viewed as supporting elements rather than the centerpiece of military power. Throughout the Core, it is the mech formations that remain the decisive arm of warfare.

As a result, military strength throughout the Core is traditionally measured in mechs rather than total personnel. References to a Company's, Division's, or Corps' size almost always describe its mech complement and do not include the extensive conventional forces attached to it.

This distinction reflects the realities of modern warfare. Conventional forces may support a campaign, occupy territory, and sustain military operations, but it is usually the mech formations that decide the outcome of a battle.

docs.title: Cultural Importance
docs.text:
Military organization is deeply woven into the culture of the Core.

Across civilized space, military terminology influences politics, government, commerce, and everyday life. Many civilian institutions borrow organizational concepts from military structures, while military service remains one of the most respected professions within most major powers.

The influence of military culture is particularly visible among the Great Houses. Generals, marshals, and veteran commanders frequently transition into political leadership, while many rulers maintain military titles alongside their civilian authority. Even in societies that value commerce, culture, or science above warfare, military service is often regarded as evidence of discipline, competence, and leadership.

This influence extends beyond national militaries. Mercenary companies, frontier militias, planetary defense forces, and civilian security organizations all draw heavily from the same traditions. As a result, a commander from one end of the Core can often understand the structure and responsibilities of a military organization from the other.

For many citizens, the military hierarchy represents more than a system of command. It is viewed as one of the institutions that allows hundreds of worlds to remain connected, defended, and stable despite the vast distances separating them.

From the smallest four-mech Column to the immense Commands that coordinate military operations across entire regions of space, the military organization of the Core reflects a civilization built upon duty, discipline, and the belief that order is the foundation of peace.

docs.title: Battlestars
docs.text:
"A hundred Battlestars once guarded the Empire. Twelve now remain."

— StarCom historical archives

docs.title: Current Battlestars
docs.text: Battlestar Faction Heritage Crew Embarked Mechs Odin Starcrest Protectorate Restoration Fleet 88,000 4,000 Warden's Might Starcrest Protectorate Last Warden 72,000 3,200 Valhalla II Starcrest Protectorate Last Warden 68,000 3,000 Radiant Dawn Helios Sovereignty Restoration Fleet 96,000 4,400 Sunfire Helios Sovereignty Restoration Fleet 92,000 4,200 Metaforge Orion Corporate Restoration Fleet 56,000 2,800 Chronex Orion Corporate Restoration Fleet 54,000 2,600 Indomitable Confederate Vanguard Union Restoration Fleet 112,000 5,600 Wrath Bringer Confederate Vanguard Union Restoration Fleet 106,000 4,200 Justice Confederate Vanguard Union Restoration Fleet 102,000 3,800 Eternity's Arcanum Omnisphere Imperium Last Warden 132,000 7,200 Aetherius V Omnisphere Imperium Last Warden 64,000 3,200

docs.title: Heirs of the Empire
docs.text:
"Battlestars are not built. They are inherited."

— Protectorate naval saying

Battlestars are the largest warships operating within known space and among the most valuable assets possessed by the Great Houses.

Historical records suggest that the Old Empire once maintained a fleet of more than one hundred Battlestars. These immense vessels formed the backbone of the Empire's power and were instrumental in maintaining control across thousands of worlds.

During the Fall of the Empire and the subsequent invasion of the Ophidian Supremacy, nearly all of these ships were destroyed. The loss of so many Battlestars remains one of the greatest military disasters in human history, a feat many historians once considered impossible.

Today, only twelve Battlestars are known to survive.

Every surviving vessel was constructed during the Empire Era. Though modern civilizations retain the ability to maintain, repair, and occasionally upgrade these ancient warships, no known power possesses the technological capability to construct new Battlestars.

Possession of a Battlestar carries immense political significance throughout the Core. The Great Houses often view them not merely as military assets, but as symbols of legitimacy and living links to the civilization that preceded them. To command a Battlestar is considered one of the highest honors available within most military traditions.

docs.title: The Great Restoration
docs.text:
Historians generally divide the surviving Battlestars into two traditions. The four vessels that endured the Fall within the Core are collectively known as the Last Wardens, while the eight vessels that accompanied the Empire's exiles and returned during the Great Restoration are remembered as the Restoration Fleet.

When the Old Empire fell before the Ophidian Supremacy, most Battlestars were destroyed alongside the fleets they commanded. Historical records suggest that more than one hundred Battlestars once served the Empire. While eight Battlestars are known to have fled the Core alongside the last remnants of the Empire's authority, the fate of much of the rest of the fleet remains uncertain. Countless warships were destroyed during the Fall, while others simply vanished from the historical record.

What is known is that only four Battlestars survived within the Core itself: Warden's Might, Valhalla II, Eternity's Arcanum, and Aetherius V. These vessels endured the collapse of the Empire and remain in service to this day. The remaining eight known Battlestars disappeared into exile alongside the last Empire fleet. For centuries, they existed only in records, legends, and scattered accounts from beyond the frontier. Many historians believed the exiled Battlestars had been destroyed long ago.

That assumption proved false.

During the Great Restoration, the exiled Battlestars returned.

These ancient warships formed the backbone of the Restoration fleets that reconquered much of the Core and reestablished centralized authority under House Caledon. Throughout the campaign, individual Battlestars fought alongside the emerging powers that would eventually become the modern Great Houses.

When victory was secured and the Star Regent reclaimed the Throne on Citadel, the surviving Battlestars did not return to a single centralized fleet. Instead, their longstanding wartime allegiances were formally recognized. The vessels that had fought alongside the Regent's vanguard armies remained with the newly founded Confederate Vanguard Union. Those that had campaigned beside House Aerin-Payne joined the nascent Helios Sovereignty. Similar arrangements cemented the ties between the Battlestars and the newly formed governments they had helped create.

Official histories portray this distribution as a reward granted by the Star Regent for loyal service during the Restoration. Most historians agree the reality was more complicated. By the end of the war, the Battlestars and the political movements they supported had become inseparable military and political partners. Whatever the legal justification, few believe the Star Regent could realistically have reclaimed them without fracturing the fragile alliance that had restored the Core.

As a result, the Battlestars are not merely relics of the Empire. They are among the founding symbols of the modern Great Houses themselves.

docs.title: Strategic Importance
docs.text:
The loss of a Battlestar would be considered a national catastrophe.

Entire military campaigns are often planned around their presence. In addition to serving as fleet flagships, Battlestars function as mobile command centers, aerospace carriers, logistics hubs, and invasion platforms capable of sustaining major military operations across multiple star systems. A single Battlestar can coordinate fleets, deploy armies, provide strategic mobility, and project military power on a scale unmatched by any other vessel in the modern Core.

Where a Battlestar appears, it inevitably becomes the center of military activity within the region. Few fleets possess the strength necessary to challenge one directly, and fewer commanders are willing to risk the consequences of its destruction.

For this reason, Battlestars are seldom committed to reckless engagements. Their destruction would represent a military, political, economic, and cultural disaster. Most governments therefore employ them cautiously, preferring to use them as command ships, force multipliers, and instruments of deterrence rather than frontline brawlers.

The deployment of a Battlestar is frequently viewed as a political statement as much as a military one. Their arrival signals that a government considers a situation to be of exceptional importance.

docs.title: Legacy
docs.text:
Only twelve Battlestars remain in active service throughout the Core. For the Great Houses, these vessels are more than military assets. They are symbols of legitimacy, continuity, and inheritance. Every surviving Battlestar serves as a tangible link to the Old Empire and a reminder of an age when humanity commanded technologies and fleets beyond anything seen today.

Their names appear in history books, political speeches, military traditions, and popular culture throughout the Core. Entire generations may pass without seeing one in person, yet nearly every citizen can name at least a few of them. For all the power wielded by the modern Great Houses, none can build a replacement.

The Battlestars they command today are not the products of their civilizations, but the inheritance of one that came before.

docs.text:

Its continued operation depends on generations of engineers, naval crews, scavenged Imperial components, and carefully guarded maintenance rites.

docs.title: Strategic Role
docs.text:
Aetherius V serves as a fleet flagship, mobile fortress, and command platform. In wartime it may coordinate planetary invasions, border defense operations, or major punitive campaigns.

When the vessel enters a system, it signals that Omnisphere Imperium considers the crisis a matter of national importance.

docs.title: Arthur Payne
docs.text:
Arthur Payne is the current military ruler of the Helios Sovereignty and one of the most powerful political figures within the modern Core. Though the Sovereignty formally remains under the authority of the Star Regent, many political observers consider Payne to be the true operational power behind the throne during the Regent’s later years.

Born into House Payne during a period of political stagnation within the Sovereignty, Arthur gained prominence through military service rather than inheritance alone. Historical accounts describe his early career as unusually distinguished for a noble of his status, with Payne earning a reputation for discipline, administrative competence, and strategic ruthlessness during a series of frontier security campaigns and internal military reforms.

Arthur’s rise accelerated following the gradual decline in the Regent’s health. Appointed as Voice of the Regent approximately a decade ago, Payne assumed increasing responsibility over military policy, interstellar trade security, diplomatic enforcement, and internal administration throughout the Sovereignty.

Since taking office, Payne has overseen extensive reforms to the Sovereignty’s military readiness, trade infrastructure, and frontier defense systems. Supporters credit him with stabilizing commerce, reducing piracy along major Sovereignty routes, and restoring political cohesion during a period of growing instability across the Core.

Critics however accuse Payne of consolidating excessive authority around himself and ruling through fear, intimidation, and political pressure. Rival factions frequently portray him as authoritarian, expansionist, and dangerously ambitious. His uncompromising style has earned him both admiration and deep resentment among allies and adversaries alike.

Payne is widely regarded as an exceptionally capable administrator. Unlike many aristocrats of the Sovereignty, he is known for maintaining direct involvement in military logistics, economic planning, and political operations. Reports from within the Sovereignty consistently describe him as intensely demanding of both himself and those serving under him.

Publicly, Arthur Payne projects an image of rigid discipline, calculated restraint, and unwavering confidence. He is known for blunt rhetoric, little tolerance for incompetence, and a belief that stability can only be maintained through strength and decisive leadership.

Despite his reputation, Payne’s relationship with the aging Star Regent appears unusually complex. While many citizens view Payne as the dominant figure within the Sovereignty’s government, numerous political analysts have noted that the Regent himself has occasionally acted to moderate or restrain Payne’s harsher policies. This dynamic has fueled persistent speculation regarding the future balance of power within the Sovereignty once the current Regent dies.

Payne is also known to maintain a close relationship with the Regent’s son and heir, serving in many respects as both political mentor and military advisor. Nevertheless rumors of rivalry and tension between the two persist throughout court circles, particularly regarding the question of whether Payne will retain his position and influence following the eventual succession.

Outside the Sovereignty, Arthur Payne is viewed with a mixture of respect and apprehension. Even political rivals generally acknowledge his competence and strategic ability. Across the Core he is widely regarded as one of the few leaders capable of maintaining large-scale political order during an increasingly unstable era.

To supporters he represents discipline, strength, and stability. To critics he embodies the growing concentration of power within the Sovereignty and the dangers posed by increasingly authoritarian rule.

Regardless of perspective, few dispute Arthur Payne’s influence upon modern Core politics.

docs.title: Deckard Noddack
docs.text:
Deckard Noddack is the current executive leader of House Noddack and one of the most influential figures within the Orion Corporate. Known for his reserved demeanor and intense interest in ancient Imperial technology, Noddack has overseen a significant expansion of Corporate research and defensive operations in recent years.

Born into a prominent scientific and political family within the Corporate, Deckard was educated extensively in engineering, history, and systems analysis. From an early age he developed a strong fascination with the remnants of the Golden Age and the Old Empire and the technologies left behind by earlier civilizations.

Prior to assuming leadership of House Noddack, Deckard participated in multiple archaeological and scientific expeditions involving abandoned Imperial facilities and derelict research installations. Several of these expeditions resulted in important technological recoveries that strengthened the Corporate’s research capabilities.

Under Noddack’s leadership the Orion Corporate has increasingly shifted focus toward technological self-sufficiency, advanced research initiatives, and the restoration of ancient infrastructure. This inward focus has generated growing suspicion among rival Houses, many of whom fear the Corporate may be attempting to monopolize lost technologies from the Imperial era.

Political rivals frequently accuse Noddack of prioritizing technological advancement over diplomacy and regional cooperation. Supporters however argue that the Corporate’s scientific leadership remains essential to maintaining the long-term stability and advancement of civilization within the Core.

Deckard himself remains a highly private figure. Unlike many leaders of the Great Houses, he rarely engages in populist rhetoric or large public spectacles. Most of his public appearances are associated with scientific conferences, technological initiatives, or Corporate security announcements.

Within the Core, Noddack has become strongly associated with the growing competition over ancient Imperial ruins and forgotten technologies. Many observers believe the Orion Corporate under his leadership is positioning itself as the leading authority on pre-collapse scientific knowledge and advanced engineering.

Though admired by many within scientific and academic circles, Deckard Noddack remains a controversial figure among rival powers wary of the Corporate’s growing technological influence.

docs.title: Jada Lytherius
docs.text:
Jada Lytherius is the current ruler of the Omnisphere Imperium and one of the most influential political figures in the modern Core. Known widely by titles such as “The Savior” and, more controversially, “The Viper,” Jada rose to power during the devastating civil conflict known as the Free Suns Rebellion.

Jada was the third child born to Empress Seraphina Lytherius, the expansionist ruler often remembered as the “Last Great Noble.” Following Seraphina’s unexpected death, leadership of the Imperium passed not to Jada but to her eldest daughter, Empress Valeria Lytherius.

Valeria attempted to rule as a reformer during a period of growing unrest within the Imperium. Her efforts to negotiate with dissident worlds and reduce political tensions were widely viewed as ineffective, particularly as separatist movements began spreading through the outer Imperial territories. Matters worsened when several worlds formally declared independence under the banner of the Free Suns rebellion.

Rumors long persisted that foreign powers, particularly elements within the Confederate Vanguard Union, covertly supplied or encouraged the rebellion, though no definitive evidence has ever been publicly presented.

As the rebellion escalated, Valeria’s authority rapidly deteriorated. She was eventually assassinated under circumstances that remain officially unresolved. Leadership then passed to her younger brother, Cassian Lytherius, whose rise to power proved deeply controversial within many Imperial circles due to the Imperium’s strong dynastic preference for female succession.

At the time of these events, Jada herself was living away from the Imperial capital. Unlike her siblings, she had not been groomed for political leadership and instead devoted much of her life to the study of philosophy, art, and Imperial history. She was also already the mother of two sons, both still in their youth when the rebellion began.

When summoned back to the capital by her brother following Valeria’s death, Jada instead fled into rebel-held territory alongside her sons. Rebel leaders quickly embraced her presence, believing that support from a legitimate member of House Lytherius would strengthen the political legitimacy of their movement.

Jada’s role within the rebellion remains the subject of intense historical debate. Contemporary accounts suggest she was initially underestimated by both rebel commanders and loyalist officials alike, who viewed her as politically inexperienced and easily manipulated. Over time however she emerged as one of the rebellion’s most influential symbolic figures.

As the war dragged on, public opinion increasingly shifted against Cassian Lytherius. Civilian massacres, failed military operations, and growing unrest within loyalist territories severely damaged his reputation. Supporters of Jada portray this period as proof of her ability to inspire hope amid chaos, while critics argue that she skillfully exploited the conflict to consolidate political power around herself.

Eventually Jada was captured by loyalist forces and returned to the Imperial capital. Shortly afterward, Cassian Lytherius was assassinated by members of his own inner circle under circumstances that remain heavily disputed. Jada was subsequently released and declared the rebellion formally ended.

The final collapse of the Free Suns movement occurred soon afterward during negotiations between remaining rebel leaders and Jada’s supporters. Numerous prominent rebel commanders were killed during the confrontation, effectively ending organized resistance and allowing Jada to assume undisputed control of the Imperium.

Since ascending to the throne, Jada Lytherius has focused heavily on restoring Imperial stability, rebuilding damaged worlds, and strengthening central authority. Her supporters view her as the ruler who preserved the Imperium from collapse during its darkest hour. Critics however accuse her government of fostering a culture of fear, political manipulation, and ruthless centralization beneath its carefully crafted image of unity and renewal.

Jada’s relationships with foreign powers remain notably strained. She has repeatedly criticized both the Confederate Vanguard Union and the Conclave for their alleged roles during the rebellion. Relations with the Star Regent in particular remain politically sensitive due to the Regent’s refusal to directly intervene during the Imperial civil conflict.

Today Jada Lytherius remains one of the most admired and feared individuals in human space: a ruler celebrated as a savior by some and regarded as a dangerously calculating political survivor by others.

docs.title: Victor Hawkins
docs.text:
Victor Hawkins is the current military leader of the Confederate Vanguard Union and one of the most respected strategists within the modern Core. Known widely by the nickname “The Grey Wolf,” Hawkins is recognized for combining military pragmatism with diplomatic skill during several major border crises involving the Union.

Born into House Hawkins, Victor was raised within the heavily militarized culture of the Union during a period of increasing regional instability. Historical records describe his upbringing as highly disciplined, with much of his early education focused on strategy, leadership, and statecraft under the supervision of senior Union military officials.

Victor first gained widespread recognition during his early military career while serving in covert operations along contested frontier regions. Though many details of these operations remain classified, his reputation for careful negotiation and unconventional strategy emerged during this period.

His rise within the Union military hierarchy was rapid, aided by several successful campaigns involving anti-piracy operations, border stabilization efforts, and strategic defense initiatives. Supporters within the Union portray him as a symbol of discipline, competence, and national unity during an increasingly uncertain era.

Unlike many previous Union leaders, Victor is often regarded as more politically flexible and diplomatically capable than purely ideological. Foreign observers have frequently noted his willingness to negotiate with rival powers when doing so serves Union interests.

Public perception of Hawkins throughout the Core is sharply divided. Within the Union he is widely admired as a stabilizing figure capable of preserving security and order. Outside Union territory however, critics accuse him of expanding covert intelligence operations, supporting proxy conflicts, and pursuing increasingly aggressive regional policies under the justification of national defense.

Despite his military reputation, Victor maintains a relatively restrained public image compared to many contemporary political leaders. He rarely appears in entertainment media and is known for avoiding excessive displays of wealth or ceremony.

Political analysts frequently describe Hawkins as representative of the Union itself: disciplined, pragmatic, highly strategic, and willing to make difficult decisions in pursuit of long-term stability.

docs.title: Citadel
docs.text: "Power resides on Citadel." — Common Core saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Citadel is the capital world of the Core and the seat of the Star Regency. Once the throne world of the ancient Empire, it remains the political and symbolic center of human civilization.

Located near the heart of the Core, Citadel is home to the Star Regent, the Stellar Conclave, and many of the oldest institutions in known space. Though numerous worlds surpass Citadel in industrial output, military strength, or resource production, none rival its political influence or historical significance.

To most citizens of the Core, Citadel represents civilization itself.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Citadel served as the throne world of the Empire for centuries and was the seat from which the Star Emperors governed thousands of worlds.

During the Fall, the world suffered catastrophic devastation. Major cities were destroyed, entire regions were abandoned, and much of the planet's ancient infrastructure was reduced to ruins. By the end of the Dark Age, many of Citadel's greatest palaces, archives, and government districts stood empty.

When the Last Emperor died during the Fall, his heirs fled beyond the Core with the surviving refugee fleets. Refusing to claim the title of Emperor, they instead adopted the title of Star Regent and ruled the Empire's remnants in exile for generations.

When these exiled forces eventually returned during the Great Restoration, the recapture of Citadel became one of their primary objectives. Though many worlds were contested during the campaign, Citadel possessed enormous symbolic importance. Whoever held Citadel could credibly claim to be restoring civilization itself.

Following its recapture, House Caledon restored Citadel as the seat of the Star Regency and began centuries of reconstruction. Ancient government districts were rebuilt, ruined monuments restored, and new institutions founded atop the remnants of the old Empire.

Modern Citadel remains a blend of restored Empire-era architecture, Restoration-era construction, and modern development.

docs.title: Government
docs.text:
Citadel serves as the seat of the Star Regency and the meeting place of the Stellar Conclave.

The Star Regent rules from the Palace District, where many of the Core's most important governmental institutions are located. Delegations from every Great House maintain permanent embassies on the world, and representatives from independent worlds, mercenary organizations, and major commercial interests regularly travel to Citadel to petition the Regency.

When matters affect the entire Core, the Great Houses gather on Citadel to convene councils, negotiate treaties, and debate issues of interstellar importance.

The office of Star Regent traces its origins to the years immediately following the Fall. Rather than proclaiming themselves Emperors after the death of the Last Emperor, House Caledon's ancestors declared themselves stewards of a vacant throne. Though the Star Regent exercises significant political authority, the office is officially regarded as the custodian of the Empire's legacy rather than its successor.

Though political disputes are often fierce, Citadel remains neutral territory. Open warfare upon the world is considered unthinkable, and even the bitterest rivals generally respect its neutrality.

No Great House maintains military forces on Citadel beyond those loyal to House Caledon and the Star Regency. This neutrality is considered one of the foundations of modern Core stability.

docs.title: Population and Culture
docs.text:
Citadel is the most populous world in the Core and among the most urbanized.

Vast metropolitan regions stretch across entire continents, connected by ancient transportation networks and modern infrastructure. Despite its immense cities, large portions of the planet remain remarkably beautiful. Rolling green plains, temperate forests, rugged coastlines, and carefully preserved natural reserves are common sights beyond the major population centers.

As the political and cultural heart of the Core, nearly every major tradition, religion, language, and ethnic group can be found somewhere on Citadel.

Many citizens consider the world a microcosm of the Core itself.

docs.title: Military
docs.text:
Citadel maintains no independent Great House military presence beyond forces loyal to House Caledon and the Star Regency.

The Regency Guard is responsible for protecting the Star Regent, securing government facilities, and maintaining order within the capital districts. While smaller than the military forces fielded by the Great Houses, the Guard is highly professional and equipped with some of the finest military technology available.

In accordance with long-standing Conclave agreements, foreign military forces are heavily restricted while operating on Citadel.

The neutrality of the capital is considered essential to the stability of the Core.

docs.title: The Palace District
docs.text: The political heart of the Core and residence of the Star Regent. Much of the district occupies foundations dating back to the Empire itself.

docs.title: The Conclave Hall
docs.text: The meeting place of the Stellar Conclave and site of many of the most important political negotiations in modern history.

docs.title: The Imperial Quarter
docs.text: A vast region containing restored government complexes, monuments, archives, and ruins dating to the Empire.

docs.title: The Restoration Memorial
docs.text: A monument dedicated to those who fought during the Great Restoration and the generations who rebuilt the Core following the Dark Age.

docs.title: The Grand Archives
docs.text: One of the largest repositories of historical records in known space. Though much knowledge was lost during the Fall, the Archives remain the foremost center of historical scholarship in the Core.

docs.title: In Modern Memory
docs.text:
To some, Citadel is proof that civilization survived the Fall.

To others, it is a monument to a past that can never truly be restored.

Regardless of political allegiance, nearly every citizen of the Core recognizes Citadel as humanity's most important world and the symbolic center of modern civilization.

docs.title: Drake's Landing
docs.text: “Every merc gets a shot. No promises on a second one.” — Dockside saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Region Within Starcrest Protectorate borders Primary Role Neutral mercenary hub Major Authority Mercenary Guild Primary Industry Contracts, training, recruitment, and mercenary operations Cultural Identity Independence, strategy, martial prowess Common Reputation “The Mercenary Capital of the Core”

Drake’s Landing is one of the most important mercenary worlds in the Core.

Although located within the borders of the Starcrest Protectorate, the world is recognized as neutral ground for mercenary activity, contract negotiation, recruitment, and inter-faction dealings.

Its neutrality has allowed it to become a central gathering point for:

  • mercenary companies
  • independent soldiers of fortune
  • employers
  • Guild brokers
  • contract negotiators
  • military trainers
  • House intermediaries

Drake’s Landing is celebrated for its culture of independence, strategy, and martial skill. It is both sanctuary and proving ground: a place where mercenary companies are founded, tested, broken, rebuilt, and remembered.

docs.title: Modern Significance
docs.text:
The modern use of Drake’s Landing as a neutral mercenary hub has transformed it into one of the busiest military-commercial centers in civilized space.

Its open ports and inclusive contract culture allow individuals from many backgrounds to gather, exchange skills, recruit talent, and arrange complex operations.

The world supports:

  • contract exchanges
  • military training
  • recruitment halls
  • neutral negotiations
  • Guild arbitration
  • operational planning
  • mercenary networking

Unlike the polished institutions of the Great Houses, Drake’s Landing is practical, crowded, and direct. A commander’s name matters less than their record. A company’s banner matters less than whether its pilots return with their steel intact.

For many Free Jacks and young mercenary crews, Drake’s Landing is the first place where ambition becomes a real contract.

docs.title: Historical Background
docs.text:
Drake’s Landing owes its name and legacy to the legendary mercenary commander Drake.

According to traditional accounts, Drake was hired to defend a strategically insignificant planet against an anticipated invasion. The assignment was initially viewed as a routine defensive contract.

When the invading force arrived, however, it proved vastly larger and more dangerous than expected.

Drake’s forces were badly outnumbered, and by conventional military calculation the planet should have fallen quickly.

Instead, Drake turned the campaign into one of the most famous mercenary actions in Core history.

docs.title: The Legend of Drake
docs.text:
Facing overwhelming odds, Drake used the planet’s rugged terrain to deny the enemy a clean victory.

His unit relied upon:

  • guerrilla tactics
  • ambushes
  • hit-and-run strikes
  • psychological warfare
  • strategic retreats
  • terrain denial

Rather than attempting to defeat the invaders in open battle, Drake forced them into a long and exhausting campaign.

Every advance cost them time, supplies, morale, and lives.

Each victory became pyrrhic.

By delaying the invasion force and disrupting its command structure, Drake bought enough time for his employer to gather and deploy relief forces.

When those reinforcements finally arrived, the invaders were already exhausted, demoralized, and out of position.

The relief force routed them.

In recognition of Drake’s leadership and the decisive role he played in saving the planet, his employer granted him stewardship over the world.

From that point forward, the planet became known as Drake’s Landing.

docs.title: From Stronghold to Mercenary World
docs.text:
With Drake’s mercenary unit now based on the planet, Drake’s Landing gained a reputation as an impregnable stronghold.

Its association with Drake’s victory deterred future invasions and attracted other mercenary forces seeking a secure operational base.

Over time, the world transformed from a defended military outpost into a growing center of mercenary culture.

Mercenary companies came to Drake’s Landing for:

  • shelter
  • repair
  • recruitment
  • contracts
  • training
  • alliance-building

The largest mercenary units operating from the world eventually helped form the Mercenary Guild, further cementing Drake’s Landing as the heart of mercenary activity in the Core.

As its influence grew, Drake’s Landing was eventually granted independence, becoming the neutral mercenary hub it is known as today.

docs.title: The Mercenary Guild
docs.text:
The Mercenary Guild is central to Drake’s Landing’s modern identity.

The Guild regulates contract arbitration, company registration, reputation records, blacklisting, and mercenary law.

On Drake’s Landing, Guild authority is stronger than anywhere else in civilized space.

The planet’s contract halls and Open Board exchanges are among the busiest in the Core, drawing:

  • Great House agents
  • frontier governors
  • corporate employers
  • independent contractors
  • Free Jacks
  • veteran companies
  • new crews seeking their first Guild seal

Although Drake’s Landing remains famously rough, its Guild presence prevents the world from collapsing fully into lawless private warfare.

docs.title: Myth vs. Reality
docs.text:
The precise truth of Drake’s life is uncertain. The names of his employer, the invading force, and many of the campaign’s details have been obscured by generations of retelling.

Some versions describe Drake as a noble commander. Others depict him as a ruthless contract fighter who happened to choose the right battlefield. Some even portray him in a negative light, suggesting he might not have been a legitimate mercenary at all but instead an invader himself, who attacked the planet known as Drake's Landing in an effort to seize it. In this depiction, after a relentless campaign of harrassment he was given Drake's Landing in a capitulation and in order to have him cease his prolonged guerilla attacks on the planet.

Whatever the truth, historians generally agree that the core of the story reflects real events, but many details have become legend. Among the people of Drake’s Landing, this distinction matters little. The legend itself has become part of the world’s identity. Drake’s Landing does not merely preserve the memory of Drake’s victory; it lives inside it.

docs.title: Culture
docs.text:
Drake’s Landing is defined by mercenary culture.

The world values:

  • independence
  • tactical intelligence
  • courage
  • reputation
  • practical skill
  • martial competence

Its society is unusually open by Core standards. Former soldiers, Free Jacks, House veterans, failed nobles, salvagers, pilots, mechanics, and contract brokers all mix in the same districts.

Landing culture is also deeply transactional.

Names matter. Records matter more.

A company that keeps its contracts, protects its crew, and brings back salvage earns respect quickly.

A company that breaks terms, abandons clients, or gets pilots killed for nothing may find itself burned from the Open Board before the next contract cycle.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout the Core, Drake’s Landing is viewed as:

  • independent
  • dangerous
  • strategic
  • mercenary
  • culturally distinct
  • politically useful

To the Great Houses, it is a necessary neutral ground where violence can be negotiated before it becomes open war.

To the Mercenary Guild, it is the institutional center of regulated private warfare.

To Free Jacks, it is a place to become someone.

To frontier worlds, it is often the source of the only protection they can afford.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions between the Great Houses continue to rise, Drake’s Landing grows increasingly important to the political balance of the Core.

Its Open Boards, Guild courts, training grounds, and negotiation halls shape the flow of mercenary force throughout civilized space.

Many StarCom analysts regard Drake’s Landing as one of the essential pressure valves preventing House rivalries from escalating into open war.

For now, Drake’s Landing remains what it has always been:

a world built from a legend, sustained by contracts, and defended by those who choose steel over banners.

docs.title: The Frontier
docs.text:
"The frontier begins where the defenders end."

— Union military saying

Beyond the Fringe Worlds lies the Frontier, a vast and poorly documented region of human space extending far beyond the effective authority of the Great Houses. Unlike the Core and Fringe, the Frontier possesses no unified political structure, stable trade network, or commonly accepted territorial boundaries. Much of the region remains only partially charted, while countless systems are known solely through fragmented reports, outdated navigation data, and rumor.

Knowledge of the Frontier within the Core is limited. Most information originates from StarCom exploration records, long-range traders, salvage expeditions, mercenary companies, and occasional diplomatic contact with isolated worlds. Even this information is often incomplete or decades out of date due to the immense distances involved and the unreliability of interstellar communication beyond established Core routes.

Human settlements within the Frontier vary dramatically in size, technology, and culture. Some worlds maintain relatively stable societies and trade periodically with the Core through StarCom-mediated routes. Others exist as isolated colonies, mining settlements, tribal states, pirate enclaves, or heavily militarized fortress worlds. In many regions technological capabilities are significantly lower than within the Core, with limited industrial infrastructure and poor access to advanced manufacturing.

Travel through the Frontier is dangerous and unpredictable. Navigation routes are poorly maintained, many systems lack reliable jump infrastructure, and emergency support is often nonexistent. Ships operating deep within the Frontier must frequently rely on self-sufficiency and improvised repairs for extended periods.

Pirates, raiders, smugglers, and criminal organizations flourish throughout many Frontier regions. Numerous hidden outposts and clandestine settlements exist beyond the reach of House authority. Bounty hunters, mercenary companies, prospectors, and fortune seekers are also common, drawn by the possibility of abandoned Imperial ruins, untapped resources, or lost technology.

The Frontier occupies an unusual place within Core culture. To many civilians it represents a realm of danger, mystery, and opportunity. Frontier stories are popular subjects within entertainment media, often portraying distant systems as lawless regions populated by pirates, warlords, rogue mercenaries, and forgotten civilizations. While many of these stories are exaggerated, the lack of reliable information allows myth and rumor to flourish.

Despite its lawlessness, the Frontier is not entirely uncivilized. StarCom maintains formal contact with a number of distant worlds and trading partners deep beyond the Fringe. Some remote systems possess stable governments, functioning industries, and long-standing local traditions independent of the Great Houses. These worlds may see Core politics as distant concerns with little relevance to daily life.

The deeper regions of the Frontier remain almost entirely unknown to the Core. Exploration beyond established trade corridors becomes increasingly rare, and many expeditions simply vanish without explanation. Stories persist of lost colonies, ancient Imperial outposts, hidden pirate kingdoms, and isolated civilizations untouched by modern Core politics.

For the Great Houses, the Frontier represents both risk and opportunity. Though difficult to control directly, the region contains valuable resources, potential trade routes, salvage sites, and strategic locations. At the same time its vast distances and fragmented political landscape make large-scale military operations extremely difficult.

To most citizens of the Core however, the Frontier remains less a clearly defined region than an idea: the edge of civilization, where the authority of the Houses fades and humanity disappears into the darkness between the stars.

docs.title: How the Core Sees the Frontier
docs.text:
Popular Core media often depicts the Frontier as a lawless wilderness populated exclusively by pirates, raiders, smugglers, and failed colonies.

While such groups certainly exist, the reality is considerably more complex.

Numerous Frontier societies possess rich histories, functioning governments, established trade networks, and cultural traditions entirely independent of the Great Houses.

Many Frontier citizens view the Core with the same suspicion and condescension that Core citizens direct toward them.

docs.title: Titan Prime
docs.text: “Win the crowds and you win the games.” — Arena broadcast saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Location Within Starcrest Protectorate Borders Political Status Neutral World Primary Function Gladiatorial Mech Combat Major Industry Broadcasting and Arena Sports Economic Driver Betting and Sponsorships Words “Glory awaits the bold.”

Titan Prime is the most famous gladiatorial world in the Core and the undisputed center of professional mech combat sports.

Known throughout civilized space as The Gladiator World, Titan Prime attracts: - mercenary pilots - House champions - Free Jacks - aspiring rookies - corporate sponsors - gamblers - spectators

from across the Core and frontier alike.

The planet’s colossal arenas host high-profile mech battles broadcast to billions across known space.

To many citizens of the Core, Titan Prime represents: - glory - fame - violence - celebrity - spectacle - wealth

The world has become synonymous with mech combat entertainment itself.

docs.title: The Modern Games
docs.text:
Modern arena combat on Titan Prime is treated simultaneously as: - sport - entertainment - military exhibition - cultural spectacle

Pilots compete in a wide variety of formats including: - duel matches - lance battles - team tournaments - endurance campaigns - elimination brackets - live-fire exhibition events

The games attract everyone from: - decorated veteran pilots - House-sponsored champions - mercenary celebrities - desperate Free Jacks hoping for recognition

Victorious pilots can earn: - enormous sponsorship deals - Guild recognition - celebrity status - House recruitment offers - lucrative contract opportunities

For many struggling mercenaries, a successful Titan Prime performance can permanently transform a career.

docs.title: Arena Culture
docs.text:
Titan Prime’s arenas are not merely battlegrounds.

They are stages.

Pilots are treated as: - athletes - celebrities - military icons - entertainment figures

Arena broadcasts dominate large portions of Core media culture.

Famous pilots frequently endorse: - mech manufacturers - reactor brands - weapons systems - Guild services - combat stimulants - luxury products

Some celebrity mech pilots possess fan followings rivaling major political figures.

Entire mech chassis configurations occasionally become fashionable after successful arena seasons.

Throughout the Core, countless young pilots dream of one day hearing their callsign announced before a packed Titan arena.

docs.title: Economy and Infrastructure
docs.text:
The economic impact of the games on Titan Prime is enormous.

The world supports massive industries centered around: - broadcasting - betting - sponsorships - mech repair - mech manufacturing - pilot training - hospitality - entertainment

Titan Prime contains some of the most advanced: - training facilities - mech servicing docks - combat simulators - arena engineering systems

in civilized space.

The planet’s economy is deeply tied to the continued popularity of the games.

Entire sectors of the Core economy are influenced by: - betting markets - sponsor networks - arena licensing - broadcast rights

docs.title: Betting and Gambling
docs.text:
Betting culture is deeply woven into Titan Prime society.

Fortunes are regularly: - won - lost - manipulated - destroyed

on the outcome of major matches.

Professional gamblers, data analysts, Guild brokers, and House-backed financial interests all participate heavily in arena wagering markets.

Some matches generate betting volumes rivaling major industrial trade exchanges.

This gambling culture contributes significantly to Titan Prime’s: - excitement - corruption - celebrity obsession - political influence

Among arena regulars, there is a common saying:

“The house always wins. The pilots just survive long enough to spend it.”

docs.title: Historical Shadows
docs.text:
Despite the glamour of the modern games, Titan Prime’s origins remain deeply controversial.

Many historians note that the original coliseums were not built for entertainment.

They were built for punishment.

Long before the modern era of professional arena combat, Titan Prime’s arenas served as brutal theaters where: - political prisoners - prisoners of war - criminals - slaves

were forced to fight, often to the death.

docs.title: The Dark Beginnings
docs.text:
Historical records indicate that early arena organizers treated combatants as commodities rather than athletes.

Slavers and criminal syndicates reportedly purchased pilots directly and forced them into combat servitude.

Although a small number of successful fighters achieved fame and relative luxury, many remained effectively imprisoned within the arena system.

Public executions and lethal combat spectacles became normalized forms of mass entertainment.

Some historians argue that the modern games continue to romanticize this darker legacy.

Others maintain the current era represents a complete cultural transformation.

The subject remains politically sensitive on Titan Prime itself.

docs.title: The Ophidian Occupation
docs.text:
Ironically, the collapse of the original arena system occurred during the Ophidian Occupation.

Although the Occupation is remembered across the Core as an age of suffering and collapse, many historians view it as the turning point that ended the old gladiatorial slave system on Titan Prime.

When the games eventually returned decades later, Core society had changed dramatically.

Public executions, slave combat, and forced arena servitude had largely fallen out of favor throughout civilized space.

The reborn games were restructured as professional competitive sport rather than state-sanctioned execution.

This transformation permanently reshaped Titan Prime’s identity.

docs.title: The Modern Debate
docs.text:
Despite reforms, debate surrounding Titan Prime remains intense.

Critics argue the games still exploit: - impoverished mercenaries - desperate Free Jacks - debt-ridden pilots - psychologically damaged veterans

Supporters counter that modern arena pilots: - compete voluntarily - earn enormous rewards - achieve social mobility - inspire billions

Many pilots themselves openly embrace the risks.

Within mercenary culture, surviving Titan Prime is often viewed as proof of elite skill.

docs.title: The Great Houses and Titan Prime
docs.text:
All five Great Houses maintain a strong interest in Titan Prime.

The arenas serve as: - recruitment grounds - propaganda platforms - technology showcases - cultural influence centers

House-sponsored pilots frequently become major celebrities.

Political rivalries occasionally spill indirectly into arena competition, though official regulations prohibit overt inter-house warfare within Titan Prime events.

Unofficially, many observers suspect the arenas are used for: - covert negotiations - influence operations - sponsorship wars - intelligence gathering

beneath the spectacle of public entertainment.

docs.title: Mech Combat as Entertainment
docs.text:
Titan Prime helped transform mech warfare into mass entertainment across the Core.

Arena broadcasts have heavily influenced: - popular culture - mech aesthetics - military propaganda - sports culture - civilian perceptions of warfare

Many critics within StarCom and the Regency quietly worry that generations of citizens raised on arena broadcasts increasingly view warfare itself as spectacle rather than tragedy.

Others argue the games provide a safe outlet for humanity’s fascination with violence and competition.

docs.title: Public Reputation
docs.text:
Throughout civilized space, Titan Prime is viewed as: - glamorous - dangerous - legendary - excessive - corrupt - culturally influential

To mercenaries, it represents opportunity.

To the Great Houses, it is a useful political and cultural instrument.

To critics, it is proof that the Core remains obsessed with violence beneath its veneer of civilization.

To billions of ordinary citizens, however, Titan Prime remains simple:

the greatest show in the galaxy.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
Titan Prime’s influence continues to grow with every passing decade.

As mech combat culture expands across the Core, the arenas increasingly shape: - public opinion - military celebrity - mech design trends - sponsorship economies - mercenary recruitment

Some analysts believe Titan Prime now exerts more cultural influence than entire planetary governments.

Whether viewed as noble sport, exploitative spectacle, or civilized gladiatorial warfare, one truth remains undeniable:

when the arena lights ignite on Titan Prime, the entire Core watches.

docs.title: Cities of the Core
docs.text:
The cities of the Core are among the largest and most densely populated human settlements in known space. Built atop layers of older Imperial infrastructure accumulated across centuries, many Core cities stretch for hundreds of kilometers and contain populations numbering in the tens or even hundreds of millions.

Modern Core cities are typically composed of multiple historical layers. At their centers stand the active urban districts of the modern age: dense commercial sectors, government centers, industrial zones, residential arcologies, and transportation hubs maintained by the Great Houses and local planetary authorities. Beneath and beyond these active districts however lie older regions dating back to the Empire, many of which now stand abandoned or only partially inhabited.

Towering arcologies dominate the skylines of many wealthy Core worlds. These immense self-contained structures house residential sectors, commercial districts, industrial facilities, transit systems, and public services within single interconnected megastructures. Some arcologies contain populations equivalent to entire cities on their own.

Infrastructure within the Core is heavily dependent upon ancient Imperial systems still maintained centuries after their construction. Automated transit networks, subterranean maglev systems, orbital elevators, atmospheric processors, and industrial fabrication complexes remain essential to daily life. Though modern engineers can maintain and repair much of this infrastructure, much of its original design and construction methods are only partially understood.

Social inequality is common within many cities of the Core. Wealthy districts are often clean, heavily secured, and technologically sophisticated, populated by political elites, corporate executives, military officers, and influential merchant families. Lower industrial sectors and labor districts are typically more crowded and economically unstable, particularly on manufacturing and refinery worlds.

Beneath many major cities lie vast underlevels and abandoned sectors collectively referred to by numerous local names such as the Undercity, Lower Zones, Dead Levels, or Old Districts. These areas often contain forgotten transit tunnels, collapsed industrial facilities, abandoned residential sectors, and sealed military infrastructure left behind from earlier periods of Imperial expansion.

Some abandoned districts have become home to criminal organizations, smugglers, salvage crews, undocumented populations, and black-market industries operating beyond effective government oversight. Local authorities frequently tolerate such activity so long as it remains contained within lower sectors and does not threaten the stability of the upper city.

Security within major Core cities is extensive. Surveillance systems, automated checkpoints, local security forces, and House military patrols are common in politically important urban centers. Despite this, organized crime, political corruption, sabotage, and espionage remain widespread beneath the surface of civilized society.

Daily life within the cities of the Core is shaped by both modern civilization and the lingering presence of the Empire. Citizens may commute through transit tunnels older than their governments, live within structures constructed centuries earlier, or work alongside machinery whose original manufacturers no longer exist.

For many citizens the cities of the Core represent the height of human civilization: safe, prosperous, technologically advanced, and culturally vibrant. At the same time they also serve as reminders of humanity’s gradual decline. No modern government could fully recreate the immense urban systems inherited from the Empire, and much of the ancient cityscape now exists as silent ruins surrounding the surviving centers of civilization.

docs.title: CoreNet
docs.text:
CoreNet is the primary interstellar communications and media network used throughout civilized human space. Maintained through a vast system of relay stations, orbital transmitters, planetary hubs, and StarCom communication infrastructure, CoreNet allows the rapid distribution of news, propaganda, entertainment, military reports, financial data, and civilian communications across much of the Core.

Although often described as a single unified network, CoreNet is in reality a fragmented collection of regional systems maintained by governments, corporations, StarCom installations, and private communication providers. The central Core worlds possess the most advanced and reliable access, while communication quality and transmission speed degrade significantly farther into the Fringe and Frontier.

For most civilians CoreNet serves as the primary source of information about events beyond their homeworlds. News broadcasts, political commentary, military updates, entertainment programming, and House propaganda are consumed daily across the Core. Most major governments maintain extensive media divisions dedicated to shaping public perception through carefully managed reporting and patriotic broadcasts.

Political propaganda is a major feature of CoreNet culture. The Great Houses regularly accuse one another of corruption, instability, aggression, or economic exploitation through official broadcasts and media campaigns. Frontier incidents, pirate attacks, border disputes, and military operations are frequently framed in ways favorable to each House’s political interests.

Mercenary companies also maintain a substantial presence across the network. Popular mercenary commanders, mech pilots, and military units often become celebrities through interviews, combat footage, sponsorship deals, and public recruitment campaigns. Arena combat broadcasts and military exhibitions are among the most widely viewed entertainment programs within many regions of the Core.

Despite its importance, CoreNet is not fully centralized or universally accessible. Transmission delays become increasingly severe farther from the Core due to the limitations of relay infrastructure and the dangers of maintaining communication routes through unstable frontier regions. Some distant systems may receive updates weeks or months after events occur within the central Core.

Censorship and information control vary considerably between regions and political powers. Certain governments heavily regulate local media and restrict outside broadcasts, while others permit relatively open information exchange. During periods of political crisis or military conflict, emergency restrictions and communication blackouts are common.

The Frontier remains only loosely connected to CoreNet. Many distant settlements possess limited or outdated communication infrastructure, while some isolated worlds operate entirely independent local networks. Information arriving from deep frontier space is often fragmented, unreliable, or heavily distorted by rumor and myth.

StarCom plays a critical role in maintaining long-distance communication infrastructure throughout human space. Many remote relay stations and frontier communication hubs depend entirely on StarCom maintenance and logistical support. As a result, StarCom possesses enormous influence over the flow of information between the Core, Fringe, and Frontier.

Beyond simple communication, CoreNet functions as one of the primary cultural bonds holding the Core together. Citizens separated by vast interstellar distances still consume many of the same news broadcasts, propaganda campaigns, sporting events, and entertainment programs. In this way CoreNet helps sustain a shared sense of civilization across a fragmented and declining human empire.

docs.title: Currency
docs.text:
"Trade requires trust. Trust requires a measure all sides accept."

— CoreNet Economic Primer

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The standard currency of the modern Core is the K, commonly referred to as the modern credit.

The K is used throughout the territories of the Great Houses, the Stellar Conclave, StarCom ports, major trade stations, and most recognized mercenary markets. It is also widely accepted in the Frontier wherever formal currency remains in use.

Although local currencies, barter systems, corporate scrip, and trade goods remain common in remote regions, the K is the closest thing humanity possesses to a universal currency.

docs.title: Use in the Core
docs.text:
Within the Core, the K is used for nearly all major commercial transactions, including:

  • military contracts
  • mech purchases
  • repairs and refits
  • mercenary payments
  • cargo exchange
  • interstellar finance
  • industrial trade

Because the Great Houses maintain extensive banking, communication, and trade networks, K-denominated accounts can usually be verified and transferred within a matter of days.

docs.title: Use in the Frontier
docs.text:
In the Frontier, the K remains valuable but less universally reliable.

Many larger settlements, trade hubs, StarCom stations, and mercenary ports accept K as standard currency. Smaller or more isolated communities may prefer barter, local notes, fuel, ammunition, spare parts, or direct service contracts.

As a result, mercenary commanders operating beyond the Core often carry both K reserves and practical trade goods.

docs.title: Military and Mercenary Accounting
docs.text:
Mech prices, contract payments, salvage values, and repair costs are commonly listed in K.

For example, a mech listed at 2,300 K costs two thousand three hundred K.

Because military hardware is extremely expensive, even small differences in listed price can represent major financial decisions for independent mercenary companies.

docs.title: Trust and Enforcement
docs.text:
The stability of the K depends upon the combined economic weight of the Great Houses, the Mercenary Guild, major banking institutions, and StarCom communication networks.

Guild-certified contracts are almost always denominated in K, allowing mercenary companies to compare offers across faction borders.

This shared accounting system is one of the reasons mercenary work remains possible across the Core.

docs.title: Local Alternatives
docs.text:
Despite the dominance of the K, alternate systems remain common in less developed regions.

These may include:

  • local planetary currencies
  • noble house bonds
  • corporate credit vouchers
  • military requisition notes
  • fuel and ammunition barter
  • salvage shares
  • direct labor exchange

Such systems are especially common in the Frontier, where access to banks and communication relays may be unreliable.

docs.title: Economic Significance
docs.text:
The K is more than a unit of money.

It is part of the infrastructure that allows the Core to function as a connected civilization.

Where the K is accepted, contracts can be enforced, debts can be tracked, and mercenaries can be paid.

Where it is not, commerce often becomes slower, more personal, and more dangerous.

docs.title: The Deep Fringe
docs.text:
Beyond the settled Core and frontier systems lies the Deep Fringe — isolated regions of space with weak governance, poor communications, and countless dangers.

The Deep Fringe is home to raiders, isolated colonies, mercenary enclaves, and forgotten relics of earlier ages.

docs.title: The Fringe Worlds
docs.text:
The Fringe Worlds are independent planetary states located along the outer edge of the Core. Though culturally and economically connected to the Core in varying degrees, these worlds are not formally governed by any of the Great Houses. Fringe governments range from stable republics and military protectorates to authoritarian regimes, mining syndicates, and merchant coalitions.

Unlike the Great Houses, Fringe Worlds generally lack the industrial capacity and logistical infrastructure required to maintain large interstellar fleets. Most possess only small defensive spacecraft intended for local patrol, customs enforcement, or orbital defense. Their military power is instead concentrated on the defense of individual planets through planetary militias, mech forces, fixed defenses, and localized aerospace units.

While no single Fringe World could rival the military power of a Great House, many are nevertheless formidable within their own systems. Invasion of a major Fringe World would often prove costly and politically difficult, especially given the limited strategic value of many frontier regions. As a result the Great Houses typically prefer diplomacy, trade agreements, economic pressure, or indirect influence rather than direct military conquest.

Life within the Fringe is generally harsher and less predictable than within the central Core. Infrastructure is often older and less reliable, while economic conditions vary dramatically from world to world. Frontier farming colonies, mining settlements, refinery towns, and isolated industrial outposts are common. Civilian populations frequently rely on local militias and mercenary contracts for protection against raiders and pirates.

Piracy is significantly more common within Fringe space. Numerous pirate clans, raider groups, smuggling networks, and criminal organizations operate from hidden bases on remote moons, asteroid fields, and isolated systems. Trade convoys traveling through the Fringe often require armed escorts, while many smaller settlements maintain extensive underground shelters and defensive fortifications.

Mercenary companies play an important role throughout the Fringe. Many independent worlds lack the manpower to maintain large standing armies and instead rely on mercenary units to supplement local defense forces. Successful mercenary commanders can become highly influential figures on Fringe Worlds, occasionally acting as military advisors, governors, or even political leaders.

The Fringe also serves as a gateway to the Frontier beyond. Trade routes, exploration expeditions, salvage operations, and StarCom relay stations extend outward from Fringe space into increasingly isolated and poorly documented regions. Information beyond the Fringe becomes fragmented and unreliable, with many systems remaining only partially charted.

Culturally, Fringe populations often view themselves as more independent and self-reliant than the populations of the central Core. While many worlds maintain strong economic and cultural ties to the Great Houses, distrust of centralized authority is common. Frontier traditions, local militias, survivalism, and planetary identity tend to play a larger role in public life than within the highly urbanized Core worlds.

To many citizens of the Core, the Fringe represents both danger and opportunity.
