It is a region associated with fortune seekers, mercenary companies, smugglers, explorers, bounty hunters, and pioneers. Though less stable than the central Core, the Fringe remains one of the few regions of human space where individuals and organizations can still rapidly rise to power and influence.

docs.title: Life in the Core
docs.text:
The Core is the heart of civilized human space and the center of political, economic, and cultural life. It occupies the territory once ruled by the ancient Empire, a civilization far larger and more populous than the modern Core. Though the great cities of the Core rival those of the Imperial age in safety and prosperity, much of the old Empire remains abandoned. Across even the wealthiest worlds, forgotten districts, sealed arcologies, and ruined industrial sectors stand as reminders of a greater age.

For most civilians, life in the Core is stable and relatively secure. The average citizen is far removed from the battlefields and frontier raids often depicted on the CoreNet. Millions work ordinary jobs as laborers, mechanics, engineers, administrators, merchants, medical personnel, and technicians. While standards of living vary between worlds and social classes, the central Core worlds are generally prosperous and orderly.

Daily life differs dramatically depending on location. Citizens of wealthy central worlds often live in towering urban centers supported by advanced infrastructure inherited from the Empire. High-speed transit systems, automated manufacturing, and vast commercial districts dominate these worlds. Outside the largest population centers however, signs of decline are common. Entire districts may stand abandoned for centuries, while old transit tunnels and industrial sectors remain sealed or forgotten beneath modern cities.

In the outer regions of the Core, life becomes harsher. Agricultural colonies, mining settlements, and frontier industrial worlds often require difficult manual labor and constant maintenance simply to survive. Equipment failures, supply shortages, and pirate activity are more common farther from the central worlds. In many regions mercenary companies supplement local House forces in defending civilian populations from raiders and criminal organizations.

Mercenaries occupy a unique place within Core culture. To many civilians they are viewed as larger-than-life figures, somewhere between soldiers, celebrities, and gladiators. Famous mercenary commanders and mech pilots are often featured in entertainment broadcasts, recruitment advertisements, and news coverage. Arena combat, military tournaments, and mech exhibitions attract enormous audiences across the Core. While many admire mercenaries for their skill and independence, others view them as dangerous opportunists motivated solely by profit.

Despite the relative safety of the Core, political tensions between the Great Houses are ever present. News broadcasts regularly feature reports of border incidents, trade disputes, sabotage accusations, pirate raids, and military exercises. Most citizens however experience these conflicts indirectly through propaganda, entertainment, and economic fluctuations rather than open warfare.

Beyond the authority of the Great Houses lie the Fringe Worlds and the distant Frontier. To many Core citizens these regions are viewed with a mixture of fascination and suspicion. Stories of pirates, frontier warlords, ancient ruins, and unexplored systems remain popular subjects throughout Core media and entertainment.

Though the modern Core remains powerful and civilized, it exists in the shadow of the Empire that came before. Humanity survives not through expansion, but through preservation. Much of daily life within the Core revolves around maintaining the immense infrastructure, technology, and institutions inherited from earlier ages.

docs.title: The Core Dream
docs.text:
Most citizens believe they live in the greatest civilization humanity has ever known.

Children are taught that the Great Houses restored order following the collapse of the Empire and safeguarded civilization from centuries of chaos.

This narrative contains elements of truth.

The Core is safer, wealthier, and more stable than most regions of known space.

Yet historians continue to debate whether modern society represents a restoration of Imperial civilization or merely its most successful surviving fragment.

docs.title: Mercenary Culture
docs.text:
Mercenaries occupy a unique and highly visible role within Core society. Though technically private military contractors, many mercenary companies possess enormous public recognition and cultural influence across civilized space. To much of the civilian population, mercenary pilots and commanders are viewed less as ordinary soldiers and more as legendary warriors, celebrities, and symbols of independence.

The public fascination with mercenaries stems partly from the rarity of open warfare within the Core. Large-scale conflicts between the Great Houses are uncommon due to the immense political and economic risks involved. As a result, most civilians experience warfare indirectly through news broadcasts, propaganda reports, arena competitions, recruitment campaigns, and heavily publicized mercenary operations.

Successful mercenary companies often cultivate highly recognizable public identities. Company insignias, mech paint schemes, famous pilots, and battlefield reputations are widely known throughout the Core. Popular mercenary commanders frequently appear in interviews, military documentaries, advertisements, and entertainment broadcasts. Some companies maintain dedicated media divisions responsible for public relations and propaganda.

Arena combat and military exhibitions are a major source of public interest across many worlds. While true combat remains dangerous and politically sensitive, organized mech duels, training demonstrations, and sponsored competitions attract enormous audiences. These events serve both as entertainment and as recruitment tools for House militaries and mercenary organizations alike.

Public attitudes toward mercenaries are mixed. Many civilians admire mercenary pilots as heroic and independent figures willing to operate in dangerous frontier regions beyond the safety of the central Core. Others view mercenary companies as opportunistic and morally questionable organizations motivated primarily by profit rather than loyalty or duty.

In the Fringe Worlds mercenaries are often viewed far more pragmatically. Frontier settlements frequently rely on mercenary protection against pirates, raiders, and hostile militias. In many isolated systems mercenary companies may provide security, disaster relief, convoy protection, and even local governance where formal state authority is weak or absent.

The relationship between mercenary companies and the Great Houses is complex. While the Houses publicly emphasize the importance of their own military forces, nearly all rely heavily on mercenary units for deniable operations, frontier defense, anti-piracy campaigns, and proxy conflicts. Competition for elite mercenary contracts can become a significant element of inter-House politics.

Mercenary life itself is highly romanticized within Core media. Recruitment advertisements and entertainment programs frequently portray mercenary pilots as symbols of freedom, adventure, and personal ambition. Young civilians throughout the Core often dream of joining famous companies and piloting mechs on distant frontier worlds.

The reality of mercenary service however is often far harsher. Casualties remain common, frontier deployments can last years, and many smaller companies survive only through dangerous salvage operations and low-paying security contracts. Numerous mercenary groups disappear entirely during pirate raids, failed campaigns, or frontier expeditions far from the Core.

Despite these dangers, mercenaries remain deeply embedded within the culture and mythology of human space. Across the Core and Fringe alike, the image of the independent mech pilot continues to symbolize humanity’s ability to survive amid the instability and decline of the modern age.

docs.title: Pirates and Raiders
docs.text:
Piracy remains one of the most persistent threats throughout the Fringe Worlds and Frontier regions. While large-scale naval warfare between the Great Houses is relatively rare, smaller conflicts involving pirate fleets, raider bands, smugglers, and criminal syndicates occur constantly beyond the heavily patrolled Core systems.

Pirate groups vary enormously in size and organization. Some consist of little more than a handful of lightly armed ships targeting isolated cargo convoys and frontier settlements. Others operate as highly organized criminal enterprises controlling hidden bases, smuggling networks, salvage operations, and black-market trade routes across multiple systems.

Most pirate activity occurs within the Fringe, where the authority of the Great Houses weakens and civilian shipping routes become more difficult to protect. Merchant convoys traveling through frontier systems frequently hire mercenary escorts, while many independent settlements maintain extensive local defenses against raids.

Pirate bases are commonly hidden on remote moons, asteroid belts, abandoned mining colonies, or forgotten Imperial facilities. Many such outposts are intentionally located far from major jump routes to avoid detection by House patrols or StarCom monitoring stations. Some larger pirate enclaves function as full criminal settlements complete with repair docks, marketplaces, refineries, gambling halls, and black-market trade hubs.

Raiders differ somewhat from traditional pirates in both organization and objectives. Whereas pirates generally focus on smuggling, theft, and profit, raider groups are often more militarized and destructive. Raider attacks may target civilian settlements directly, seizing supplies, fuel, industrial equipment, or prisoners before withdrawing into poorly charted frontier regions.

Numerous criminal organizations operate within pirate-controlled territories. Smugglers traffic in illegal weapons, military technology, narcotics, forged identification systems, and stolen industrial goods. Salvage gangs frequently strip abandoned Imperial ruins or battlefields for valuable components and weapons systems.

Relations between pirate groups and the Great Houses are often more complicated than officially acknowledged. Though all major governments publicly condemn piracy, House intelligence agencies have at times been accused of covertly supporting certain pirate organizations for political or strategic purposes. Pirates may also serve as intermediaries for smuggling operations, deniable attacks, or black-market trade networks connected to larger political interests.

Mercenary companies frequently conduct anti-piracy operations throughout the Fringe. Escort missions, bounty contracts, base assaults, and convoy defense operations represent a major source of employment for smaller mercenary units. Successful pirate hunters can become highly respected figures within frontier regions, while notorious pirate commanders may achieve infamy throughout the Core.

For ordinary civilians living near the Fringe, piracy is often viewed as an unavoidable reality of frontier life. Many settlements maintain emergency bunkers, planetary militia forces, and warning systems specifically designed to respond to pirate attacks. Convoy delays, supply shortages, and security alerts are common occurrences on remote worlds.

Within Core media and popular culture, pirates occupy a contradictory role. Official propaganda portrays them as violent criminals threatening civilized space, while entertainment programs often romanticize pirate life as rebellious and adventurous. Frontier folklore similarly contains countless stories of hidden treasure fleets, rogue admirals, lost pirate kingdoms, and legendary raider captains operating beyond the reach of House authority.

Despite ongoing military campaigns and security operations, piracy persists because of the immense scale of human space itself. The Great Houses simply cannot patrol every trade route, moon, asteroid field, and isolated colony scattered across the Fringe and Frontier.

docs.title: Ruins of the Empire
docs.text:
Scattered across the Core are the remnants of the ancient Empire, vast abandoned regions left behind following centuries of decline, depopulation, economic collapse, and political fragmentation. Though the modern Core remains highly civilized and technologically advanced, much of humanity now occupies only a fraction of the infrastructure once maintained during the height of Imperial rule.

On many worlds, modern cities stand beside or atop far older urban centers dating back to the Empire. Massive abandoned districts, sealed transit systems, forgotten industrial sectors, and derelict arcologies are common even on wealthy Core planets. Entire metropolitan regions may stand empty for hundreds of kilometers beyond the populated zones of modern cities.

The Empire possessed a far larger population than the modern Core and maintained extensive interstellar infrastructure across thousands of systems. During its height, planetary populations reached levels that many modern worlds could no longer sustain following the collapse of centralized authority and the decline of interstellar trade networks. Over time, populations consolidated around defensible and economically viable urban centers while countless peripheral settlements were abandoned.

Many ruins remain dangerous and poorly understood. Automated defense systems, unstable infrastructure, toxic industrial contamination, and unexplored subterranean sectors continue to pose hazards centuries after abandonment. Some ancient transit systems and utility networks still function autonomously despite no longer being fully understood by modern engineers.

Imperial ruins are especially common beneath the largest cities of the Core. Vast underground levels, abandoned maglev tunnels, forgotten military bunkers, and sealed industrial complexes often lie beneath modern population centers. In some cities entire lower districts have been declared permanently off-limits due to structural instability, criminal occupation, or unexplained system failures.

Salvage operations within Imperial ruins represent a major industry throughout the Core and Fringe alike. Lost technology, industrial components, military hardware, and archival data recovered from ancient facilities can command enormous value. Many mercenary companies supplement their income through salvage contracts, escort duties, and ruin security operations.

The Great Houses frequently conduct classified expeditions into major Imperial sites, particularly former research installations, shipyards, and military facilities. Rumors persist of hidden Imperial weapons caches, sealed artificial intelligences, lost colonies, and experimental technologies buried within forgotten sectors of the old Empire.

For ordinary citizens the ruins of the Empire are both mundane and mythic. Most Core civilians live among Imperial infrastructure in some form, using transportation systems, reactors, or industrial machinery whose origins long predate modern civilization. At the same time the deeper ruins remain objects of fascination, superstition, and fear. Stories of hidden vaults, lost riches, rogue defense systems, and vanished expeditions are common throughout Core media and frontier folklore.

The existence of the ruins serves as a constant reminder that modern civilization survives in the shadow of a greater age. Humanity did not inherit an expanding empire, but the remnants of one already fallen.

docs.title: The Core Dream
docs.text:
"The Empire gave humanity the stars. The Great Houses gave them back."

— Common saying taught in Core schools

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Core Dream is the widely held belief that the modern Core represents the restoration of civilization following the collapse of the Golden Empire.

Although rarely formalized as a political doctrine, the Core Dream influences nearly every aspect of Core society. It shapes education, culture, politics, foreign policy, and the collective identity of billions of citizens.

To most inhabitants of the Core, the Great Houses are not merely governments. They are the guardians of civilization itself.

docs.title: Foundations of the Dream
docs.text:
Following the fall of the Golden Empire, humanity endured centuries of fragmentation, isolation, piracy, famine, and warfare.

Entire worlds vanished from contact.

Trade routes collapsed.

Colonies were abandoned.

Technological knowledge was lost.

The eventual rise of the Great Houses and the formation of the Stellar Conclave brought an end to much of this chaos. Trade resumed. Borders stabilized. Infrastructure was rebuilt. Populations recovered.

For many citizens, this period represents the rebirth of civilization.

The Core Dream emerged naturally from this success.

According to the Dream:

  • The Great Houses restored order.
  • The Core represents the pinnacle of human achievement.
  • Humanity has largely recovered from the Collapse.
  • The Frontier remains backward, unstable, and dangerous.
  • Continued House leadership is necessary to preserve civilization.

Most citizens never question these assumptions.

docs.title: Why the Dream Persists
docs.text:
Unlike many political myths, the Core Dream contains substantial truth.

The Core is genuinely prosperous.

Major trade routes are secure.

Education is widespread.

Life expectancy is high.

Large-scale wars are rare.

For the average citizen living on Khorhall, Lumina, Horizon's Reach, Starhold, or Celestia Prime, civilization appears healthy and stable.

Many therefore conclude that humanity has recovered.

The evidence of daily life seems to support this belief.

docs.title: The View from the Frontier
docs.text:
The Frontier occupies a peculiar place within the Core imagination.

Popular media often depicts Frontier worlds as places of:

  • pirates
  • smugglers
  • warlords
  • criminals
  • failed colonies
  • desperate settlers

While such places certainly exist, the reality is considerably more complex.

Many Frontier societies possess long histories, stable governments, thriving economies, and cultures that developed independently of the Great Houses.

Frontier citizens often view Core residents as arrogant, sheltered, and overly dependent upon ancient institutions.

A common Frontier saying states:

"The Core believes it built civilization. The Frontier remembers who survived without it."

docs.title: Historians and Critics
docs.text:
Not everyone accepts the Core Dream without question.

Many historians point out that the Golden Empire governed territory vastly larger than that controlled by the modern Houses.

The Empire maintained infrastructure, communication networks, and military capabilities beyond anything seen in the present era.

From this perspective, the Core is not the restoration of civilization.

It is merely its largest surviving fragment.

Critics argue that the Core has mistaken stability for recovery.

Others counter that civilization is defined by what exists today, not by comparisons to a distant golden age.

The debate remains ongoing.

docs.title: StarCom's Perspective
docs.text:
StarCom occupies an unusual position within the discussion.

Unlike the Great Houses, StarCom routinely operates beyond established borders and maintains exploration programs in poorly charted regions.

As a result, StarCom personnel are frequently exposed to evidence that humanity's understanding of itself remains incomplete.

Forgotten colonies continue to be rediscovered.

Ancient installations continue to be uncovered.

Previously unknown settlements continue to appear.

Many StarCom officers privately view the Core Dream as an understandable but incomplete picture of reality.

Publicly, however, StarCom rarely comments on political or cultural matters.

docs.title: The Great Irony
docs.text:
The Core Dream is neither wholly true nor wholly false.

The Great Houses genuinely ended centuries of chaos.

They genuinely created peace and prosperity for billions.

They genuinely represent the strongest military and economic powers known to humanity.

Yet they also govern only a small portion of the territory once claimed by the Empire.

The Core views itself as the center of civilization.

Whether it is civilization's rebirth or merely its most successful remnant remains one of the great unanswered questions of the age.

docs.title: In Popular Culture
docs.text:
The Core Dream appears frequently in literature, education, and entertainment.

Common themes include:

  • noble House leaders defending civilization
  • heroic explorers bringing order to the Frontier
  • pirates threatening civilized society
  • the glory of the Empire reborn
  • humanity's inevitable march toward unity

These stories remain among the most popular forms of entertainment throughout the Core.

For many citizens, they are not merely fiction.

They are reflections of how the universe is believed to work.

docs.title: Technology
docs.text:
From the jump drives that connect distant stars to the communications networks that carry information between worlds, modern society depends upon an immense web of machinery, infrastructure, and scientific knowledge accumulated over thousands of years.

Although humanity remains technologically advanced by almost any standard, much of its most important technology originates from earlier eras. Many systems are maintained, refined, and reproduced without being fully understood. Engineers can repair them, operators can use them, and manufacturers can build replacements, yet the deeper scientific principles behind some technologies remain the subject of ongoing research and debate.

This reliance upon inherited knowledge has shaped every aspect of modern civilization. The Great Houses compete not only through military strength and political influence, but through control of industrial worlds, research institutions, shipyards, communications networks, and manufacturing centers.

docs.title: Major Technology in the Modern Core
docs.text:
Several technological systems form the backbone of interstellar civilization:

  • Jump Drives allow faster-than-light travel between star systems.
  • Communication Networks transmit information across vast distances through relay infrastructure.
  • Industrial Infrastructure produces the ships, vehicles, weapons, and equipment required to sustain civilization.
  • Space Warfare Systems project military power between worlds and protect vital trade routes.
  • Weapons Technology provides the firepower used by infantry, vehicles, mechs, aerospace forces, and warships throughout the Core.
  • Mechs and Military Technologies dominate planetary battlefields throughout known space.

Together, these systems bind hundreds of worlds into a single interconnected civilization.

docs.title: Lost Knowledge and Technological Stagnation
docs.text:
One of the defining characteristics of the modern era is the gradual loss of knowledge.

While humanity remains capable of producing advanced spacecraft, weapons, vehicles, and industrial equipment, certain technologies from the Golden Empire remain only partially understood. Some ancient systems continue to operate despite centuries of repairs and modifications, while others can no longer be reproduced at all.

This technological stagnation is particularly visible in areas such as capital ship construction, ancient industrial infrastructure, and certain branches of Hypercomplex Mathematics used in jump navigation.

As a result, many governments treat scientific archives, engineering records, and technical expertise as strategic assets every bit as valuable as military forces.

docs.title: Innovation and Competition
docs.text:
The Great Houses invest heavily in research programs, industrial expansion, military modernization, and scientific exploration. Competition between states and corporations drives continual improvements in weapons, electronics, aerospace systems, communications infrastructure, and manufacturing techniques.

The Orion Corporate is particularly renowned for technological innovation, while other powers often emphasize reliability, scale, craftsmanship, or military practicality depending upon their cultural priorities.

docs.title: Technology and Civilization
docs.text:
Most citizens rarely think about the immense technological systems that support their daily lives.

Every shipment of food, every interstellar message, every military deployment, and every voyage between the stars depends upon networks of engineers, scientists, navigators, manufacturers, technicians, and infrastructure operators working behind the scenes.

Without technology, the Core would fragment into isolated worlds almost immediately. It is not merely a collection of machines. It is the foundation that allows civilization itself to exist.

docs.title: Communications
docs.text:
"Information travels faster than light. Civilization travels at the speed of infrastructure."

— StarCom Communications Manual

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Interstellar communication throughout the Core relies upon vast networks of communication stations, relay buoys, orbital facilities, and starships that continuously rebroadcast data between systems.

Using the same exotic physics that make jump travel possible, communication systems can transmit data faster than light across interstellar distances. However, these signals degrade rapidly over distance and cannot simply be broadcast across the galaxy from a single transmitter.

As a result, communication speed depends heavily upon the availability and reliability of relay networks.

docs.title: Relay Networks
docs.text:
A transmission sent from one world rarely reaches its destination directly.

Instead, communications are transmitted from one relay station to another, with each station receiving, amplifying, verifying, and rebroadcasting the signal onward.

A typical message traveling between major Core worlds may pass through dozens of relay stations before reaching its final destination.

These relay networks form the backbone of modern civilization.

Without them, interstellar communication would become impractically slow or impossible.

docs.title: Faster-Than-Light Transmission
docs.text:
Unlike ancient electromagnetic communications, modern interstellar communications utilize FTL transmission technology derived from the same mathematical breakthroughs that enable jump drives.

However, FTL communication is not unlimited.

Signals gradually weaken and accumulate distortion as they travel. Beyond a certain distance, a transmission becomes indistinguishable from background noise and can no longer be reliably decoded.

To overcome this limitation, relay stations continuously receive, reconstruct, and rebroadcast messages throughout known space.

The result is a vast communication web spanning much of human civilization.

docs.title: The Great House Networks
docs.text:
Each of the Great Houses maintains extensive communication infrastructure throughout its territory.

Relay stations are commonly found on:

  • inhabited worlds
  • orbital facilities
  • military outposts
  • navigation buoys
  • strategic jump points

Military fleets can also function as temporary communication nodes.

During major military operations, warships frequently establish relay chains that allow commanders to coordinate forces across multiple systems.

The destruction or disruption of these networks is considered a serious military threat.

docs.title: StarCom Communications
docs.text:
No organization possesses a larger communication network than StarCom.

Over centuries, StarCom has constructed and maintained thousands of relay stations, navigation beacons, communication buoys, survey outposts, and communication vessels throughout known space.

This infrastructure allows information to move between the Frontier and the Core with remarkable speed.

Under normal circumstances:

  • Messages within the Core are delivered within a few days.
  • Messages from most Frontier settlements reach the Core within several days.
  • Remote Frontier regions may require up to a week.
  • Exceptional circumstances can result in longer delays.

The StarCom network is widely regarded as one of the most important pieces of infrastructure in human space.

Many historians argue that StarCom's communications network is nearly as important as its navigation services.

docs.title: Communication Delays
docs.text:
Despite traveling faster than light, communication is not instantaneous.

Every relay introduces delays.

Stations require time to:

  • receive transmissions
  • verify signal integrity
  • correct transmission errors
  • route traffic
  • rebroadcast messages

As a result, even routine communication may require days to reach its destination.

Citizens of the Core generally view such delays as normal.

A message sent across the Core often arrives in a timeframe comparable to physical mail services of earlier eras.

docs.title: Frontier Communication
docs.text:
Communication becomes increasingly difficult beyond established relay networks.

A vessel operating in remote space without access to relay stations may be effectively isolated from the wider galaxy.

Although a ship may possess powerful communication equipment, it cannot simply broadcast a message all the way back to the Core.

Without intermediate stations to receive and rebroadcast the signal, transmission quality eventually degrades beyond recovery.

This reality creates significant challenges for:

  • explorers
  • survey vessels
  • mercenary companies
  • Frontier colonies
  • military patrols

Many remote settlements depend entirely upon periodic StarCom visits to remain connected to the wider human community.

docs.title: Courier Ships
docs.text:
For highly sensitive information, physical delivery remains common.

Encrypted data storage devices can be transported aboard ships, avoiding many of the interception risks associated with conventional communication networks.

Government agencies, military organizations, intelligence services, and major corporations frequently employ courier vessels for critical communications.

Such couriers may carry:

  • diplomatic correspondence
  • military orders
  • intelligence reports
  • financial records
  • classified research

In some situations, a trusted courier is considered more secure than any communication network.

docs.title: Military Communications
docs.text:
Communication infrastructure is considered a strategic asset.

Military campaigns often revolve around securing or disrupting relay networks.

A commander who controls communication infrastructure can coordinate forces across multiple systems, while an isolated force may find itself effectively blind.

Many military planners view communications as equally important as fuel, ammunition, and logistics.

Entire offensives have succeeded or failed based upon the reliability of relay chains.

docs.title: The Information Age
docs.text:
Despite the immense distances separating human worlds, the relay networks maintained by the Great Houses and StarCom ensure that most citizens remain connected to the wider galaxy.

News, commerce, diplomacy, military orders, and personal correspondence flow continuously across known space.

Modern communication technology allows information to travel faster than light, but only where infrastructure exists to support it.

Beyond the reach of relay networks, even the most advanced civilizations can find themselves isolated.

In the modern era, communication is not limited by technology.

It is limited by the reach of civilization itself.

docs.title: Industry
docs.text:
"Every mech, every starship, and every city begins as ore in the ground and steel in a factory."

— Core Infrastructure Authority

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Industry forms the foundation of modern civilization throughout the Core.

From the construction of mechs and starships to the production of food, consumer goods, and advanced technologies, every aspect of life depends upon vast industrial networks spanning hundreds of worlds.

The Great Houses compete not only through military strength and political influence, but through their ability to manufacture, transport, and maintain the infrastructure upon which civilization depends.

docs.title: Industrial Power and the Great Houses
docs.text: Each of the Great Houses possesses a unique industrial identity shaped by its history, culture, and priorities.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
The Protectorate specializes in heavy manufacturing, military production, mining, and resource extraction.

Its factories are renowned for producing rugged equipment capable of operating in harsh conditions. Protectorate industries prioritize durability and reliability over elegance or efficiency.

Major industrial worlds include Khorhall, Smokefall, and Dunerift.

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
The Sovereignty focuses on high-value manufacturing, luxury goods, finance, and commercial infrastructure.

While it maintains substantial industrial capacity, many Sovereign industries emphasize quality, prestige, and profitability rather than raw production volume.

Major industrial worlds include Lumina, Haven, and Helix III.

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text:
The Orion Corporate is widely regarded as the technological leader of the Core.

Corporate industries focus on advanced manufacturing, electronics, aerospace systems, scientific equipment, and cutting-edge research.

Its products are often among the most sophisticated available in human space.

Major industrial worlds include Horizon's Reach, Veridian, and Aether VII.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
The Union maintains one of the largest industrial bases in known space.

Its factories produce enormous quantities of military hardware, vehicles, weapons, ammunition, and infrastructure.

The Union's industrial philosophy emphasizes scale, standardization, and efficiency.

Major industrial worlds include Fortuna, Hammerfall, and Galvin's Forge.

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
The Imperium balances industrial production with centuries of tradition and craftsmanship.

Many Imperial manufacturers combine modern production techniques with historical design philosophies, producing equipment known for quality and longevity.

Major industrial worlds include Nythoros, Kethos, and Morventhar.

docs.title: Resource Extraction
docs.text:
Modern industry depends upon a constant flow of raw materials.

Throughout the Core, countless worlds specialize in:

  • mining
  • refining
  • gas extraction
  • agriculture
  • heavy industry

Many frontier colonies were originally established specifically to exploit valuable natural resources.

Control of these resources remains a major source of political and economic power.

docs.title: Manufacturing
docs.text:
Manufacturing facilities range from small workshops to massive industrial arcologies spanning entire regions.

Products commonly manufactured throughout the Core include:

  • mechs
  • vehicles
  • spacecraft
  • weapons
  • communications equipment
  • consumer goods
  • industrial machinery

The largest facilities employ millions of workers and produce goods on a scale difficult for most citizens to comprehend.

docs.title: Shipbuilding
docs.text:
Shipbuilding represents one of the most advanced industrial activities in human space.

Constructing modern starships requires:

  • advanced materials
  • precision manufacturing
  • sophisticated electronics
  • highly skilled labor

Only a limited number of worlds possess the infrastructure necessary to construct major warships.

docs.title: Battlestars
docs.text:
Although modern civilizations continue to build starships, no known power possesses the industrial capability required to construct new Battlestars.

These immense vessels remain relics of the Golden Empire.

Many historians consider the inability to reproduce Battlestars one of the clearest examples of technological decline since the Imperial era.

docs.title: Industrial Corporations
docs.text:
Much of the Core's manufacturing capacity is controlled by large corporations, industrial combines, and state-owned enterprises.

Many of these organizations possess histories spanning centuries.

Notable industrial organizations include:

  • Konnen Heavy Industries
  • Payne Dynamics
  • Orion Advanced Systems
  • Union Forgeworks
  • Imperial Arms Consortium

Additional information pending archival review.

docs.title: Industry and Warfare
docs.text:
Modern warfare depends upon industrial strength.

Military forces consume staggering quantities of:

  • fuel
  • ammunition
  • replacement parts
  • food
  • vehicles
  • communications equipment

As a result, industrial capacity is often considered just as important as military strength itself.

Many wars have ultimately been decided not on the battlefield, but in the factories that supplied it.

docs.title: Legacy of the Golden Empire
docs.text:
Much of the Core's industrial infrastructure traces its origins to the Golden Empire.

Ancient shipyards, manufacturing complexes, transportation systems, and resource extraction facilities continue to serve as the foundation of modern civilization.

Although the Great Houses have maintained and expanded much of this infrastructure, many industrial systems remain only partially understood.

Some facilities continue operating centuries after their original construction.

Others have become the subject of ongoing archaeological and engineering study.

docs.title: The Industrial Foundation
docs.text:
Citizens often celebrate soldiers, explorers, political leaders, and heroes.

Yet beneath every achievement lies a vast industrial network of workers, engineers, technicians, miners, farmers, and manufacturers.

Without them, modern civilization would simply cease to function.

The factories of the Core may not inspire the same admiration as its armies or Battlestars, but they remain the foundation upon which all other achievements rest.

docs.title: Jump Drive Technology
docs.text:
Jump drives are the foundation of interstellar civilization within the Core. Without them, communication, trade, military operations, and political authority across human space would collapse almost immediately. Despite their importance however, very few individuals fully understand how jump drives actually function.

Modern civilization relies upon mathematical and technological principles inherited from the long ago, whose original discoveries remain only partially understood. While contemporary scientists and navigators can operate and maintain jump systems reliably, the deeper theoretical foundations behind faster-than-light travel remain among the greatest mysteries in human history.

docs.title: Hypercomplex Mathematics
docs.text:
At the center of all jump technology lies Hypercomplex Mathematics, a field so strange that many scientists consider it less a branch of mathematics and more a discovered property of reality itself.

Hypercomplex equations routinely produce valid solutions by passing through mathematical states that appear impossible within conventional mathematics. Certain values loop back upon themselves. Others generate answers that seem to depend upon events that have not yet occurred. Some solutions can only be reached by accepting contradictions that should not be mathematically valid.

Despite this apparent absurdity, the mathematics works.

Its most important application is the solution of highly chaotic gravitational systems. Problems involving multiple stars, planets, moons, and other gravity wells rapidly become too complex for conventional mathematical methods. Yet Hypercomplex calculations consistently produce stable navigational solutions, even in situations where no reliable solution should be possible.

Modern navigators rely upon these calculations every day to predict gravitational interactions, stabilize jump corridors, and traverse distances that would otherwise be impossible. Few, however, can adequately explain why the equations function as reliably as they do.

Most mathematicians therefore treat Hypercomplex mathematics less as an invention and more as a discovery. The equations are known to work. The deeper reason remains one of the greatest unanswered questions in human history.

docs.title: Chaotic Numbers
docs.text:
One of the most important branches of Hypercomplex Mathematics is the study of Chaotic Numbers.

Chaotic Numbers allow navigators to model gravitational systems that would otherwise be effectively impossible to predict. They are particularly important in solving variations of the Three-Body Problem, where the interactions of multiple stars, planets, moons, and gravity wells produce outcomes that rapidly exceed the capabilities of conventional mathematics.

What makes Chaotic Numbers remarkable is not merely their accuracy, but their consistency. Even in situations where conventional models diverge into countless possibilities, Chaotic calculations repeatedly converge upon stable navigational solutions.

No modern scientist fully understands why this occurs.

Many navigators describe Chaotic calculations as less a method of predicting the future and more a process of eliminating futures that cannot occur. The mathematics does not identify a single outcome. Instead, it progressively narrows possibilities until only stable solutions remain.

Without Chaotic Numbers, reliable jump navigation would be impossible. The vast network of trade routes, military corridors, and interstellar travel lanes that connect the Core could not exist.

docs.title: Paradoxical Numbers
docs.text:
Paradoxical Numbers form the second major branch of Hypercomplex Mathematics.

Where Chaotic Numbers address complexity, Paradoxical Numbers address contradiction.

Faster-than-light travel appears to violate fundamental assumptions about causality. Under conventional physics, information cannot travel faster than light without producing paradoxes involving time, sequence, and observation. Yet jump drives function reliably despite these apparent impossibilities.

Paradoxical calculations provide the mathematical framework that makes such travel possible.

The equations routinely generate solutions that appear internally contradictory. Certain values depend upon future states. Others seem to require information that should not yet exist. Despite this, the resulting navigational solutions consistently produce stable jumps.

Even among professional navigators, Paradoxical mathematics has a reputation for being less intuitive than Chaotic calculations. Many describe it as a branch of mathematics that functions not by resolving contradictions, but by containing them.

Without Paradoxical Numbers, faster-than-light travel would remain theoretically impossible. With them, it merely remains unexplained.

docs.title: The Feyn-Rachis Equation
docs.text:
The best-known ancient jump equation is the Feyn-Rachis Equation, a mathematical framework that unifies Chaotic and Paradoxical calculations into a single navigational model.

Every modern jump drive ultimately relies upon some derivative of the Feyn-Rachis framework. The equation governs the initiation of jumps, the formation and stabilization of jump corridors, the synchronization of temporal effects, and the resolution of a vessel's eventual exit point.

Despite its importance, no modern civilization fully understands the equation's original derivation. Navigational academies teach its application extensively, yet the deeper mathematical principles behind it remain largely inaccessible.

Among mathematicians, the Feyn-Rachis Equation is often regarded less as a human invention and more as a discovered feature of reality itself. Like gravity or geometry, it appears to describe something fundamental about the structure of the universe rather than something humanity created.

docs.title: Jump Corridors and Navigation
docs.text:
Jump travel does not function equally well throughout all regions of space.

Stable jumps require carefully mapped gravitational conditions and heavily refined navigational solutions. Most travel within the Core occurs along established jump corridors maintained through centuries of observation, refinement, and accumulated navigational data.

Leaving known corridors significantly increases risk. Poorly charted systems, unstable stellar regions, black holes, and dense gravitational interactions can rapidly overwhelm conventional navigation systems.

For this reason, long-range exploration into the Frontier remains dangerous despite the existence of mature jump technology.

docs.title: Computational Limitations
docs.text:
Modern civilization's greatest limitation is not the jump drive itself, but the mathematics that supports it.

While navigators can operate existing systems and engineers can maintain them, very few institutions possess the knowledge necessary to develop entirely new jump frameworks. Most navigation relies upon inherited equation libraries, centuries of accumulated route data, and ancient computational methods whose underlying principles are only partially understood.

As a result, navigational archives have become strategic assets comparable to fleets, industrial worlds, and Battlestars. Governments devote enormous resources to protecting jump records, refining corridor maps, and preserving mathematical knowledge accumulated over generations.

This dependence on inherited knowledge contributes heavily to the technological stagnation of the modern Core. Humanity can maintain the systems it inherited. Improving upon them is far more difficult.

docs.title: Cultural Impact
docs.text:
Jump technology profoundly shapes every aspect of civilization within the Core.

To most civilians, jump travel appears almost mystical. Ships vanish into folded spacetime and emerge light-years away without visible acceleration or relativistic delay. Even educated citizens often possess only a superficial understanding of the mathematics involved.

As a result, jump navigation occupies an unusual place between science and mythology within Core culture. Navigators, mathematicians, and jump engineers are often viewed with a mixture of admiration, suspicion, and superstition.

Many religious traditions interpret Hypercomplex mathematics as evidence of deeper cosmic truths or divine structures hidden beneath reality itself.

Despite centuries of use, humanity still does not fully understand the forces that allow it to cross the stars.

docs.title: Space Warfare
docs.text: “Ground wars can be rebuilt. Fleet wars are remembered forever.” — Core naval doctrine saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Primary Theater Orbital and Interstellar Space Major Fleet Types Destroyers, Cruisers, Battleships, Battlestars Manufacturing Status Largely Lost Technology Strategic Function Deterrence and Force Projection Conflict Frequency Extremely Rare Common Strategic Reality “Every Ship Is Precious”

All major powers within the Core maintain formidable starship fleets.

These fleets include: - destroyers - cruisers - battleships - carriers - the dreaded battlestars

Yet despite their immense military power, large-scale fleet warfare is exceedingly rare in the modern era.

Unlike most conventional military equipment, major warships are extraordinarily difficult — and in many cases effectively impossible — to replace.

Much of the advanced knowledge required for constructing large military vessels has been lost over centuries of decline, fragmentation, and technological stagnation.

As a result, many of the largest ships in service today are ancient relics maintained through generations of painstaking repair and preservation.

Throughout the Core, fleets are viewed less as expendable military assets and more as irreplaceable pillars of civilization itself.

docs.title: The Ancient Fleets
docs.text:
Most major fleets operating in the modern Core trace their origins back centuries, with some vessels believed to predate even the Great Restoration.

Many battleships and battlestars currently in service are: - ancient war relics - heavily reconstructed hulls - inherited fleet assets - restored pre-collapse vessels

In numerous cases, modern engineers no longer fully understand all systems operating within the ships they maintain.

Entire generations of naval engineers dedicate their lives to preserving fleet functionality through: - salvage - repair - reverse engineering - component cannibalization - ritualized maintenance traditions

Because complete reconstruction of major warships is often impossible, even damaged vessels may remain in drydock for decades awaiting restoration.

docs.title: Fleet Manufacturing
docs.text:
Although smaller spacecraft can still be produced throughout the Core, true capital ship manufacturing has become extraordinarily rare.

Modern shipyards primarily exist to: - maintain existing fleets - repair damaged vessels - retrofit aging systems - recover battle damage - preserve ancient ship infrastructure

Constructing entirely new capital-class warships requires: - industrial capability - material infrastructure - reactor engineering - fabrication systems - lost technical knowledge

that few factions fully possess.

This technological decline has fundamentally shaped modern naval doctrine.

Unlike earlier eras, fleets cannot simply replace losses through industrial expansion.

A destroyed battleship may represent a permanent strategic loss lasting generations.

docs.title: Destroyers
docs.text:
Destroyers are relatively fast and maneuverable warships used for: - escort duty - screening operations - patrol missions - anti-fighter defense - rapid response operations

Most modern destroyers are among the few larger warships still produced in limited numbers.

docs.title: Cruisers
docs.text:
Cruisers form the backbone of most fleet formations.

They balance: - firepower - durability - operational range - troop transport capability

Cruisers frequently serve as: - command vessels - fleet anchors - expeditionary platforms

during military deployments.

docs.title: Battleships
docs.text:
Battleships are among the oldest and most heavily armed vessels still operating within the Core.

These immense warships are designed for: - fleet engagements - orbital dominance - strategic deterrence

Many battleships possess immense symbolic importance beyond their military value.

The destruction of a major battleship can become a civilization-defining political event.

docs.title: Battlestars
docs.text:
→ See also: Battlestars

Battlestars are the largest and most feared military vessels in known space.

These colossal warships combine: - fleet command capability - heavy weapons systems - aerospace deployment - troop transport - long-term operational endurance

A single battlestar may project enough military power to dominate entire sectors.

Fortunately, true battlestars are extraordinarily rare.

Many analysts believe no modern power fully understands how to construct new ones.

docs.title: Why Fleet Warfare Is Rare
docs.text:
Large-scale naval conflict is uncommon throughout the Core for a simple reason:

fleet losses are often irreplaceable.

Even victorious fleet engagements can cripple a faction strategically for decades.

As a result, modern fleets are used primarily for: - deterrence - intimidation - force projection - transport - blockade operations - strategic presence

rather than direct annihilation.

Fleet commanders are generally highly cautious regarding escalation.

Among naval officers, there is a common saying:

“A reckless admiral burns centuries in a single hour.”

docs.title: Orbital Bombardment
docs.text:
Planetary bombardment is extremely rare throughout civilized space.

Several factors contribute to this restraint.

docs.title: Cultural Stigma
docs.text:
The Core possesses a deeply ingrained cultural hostility toward weapons of mass destruction.

Many citizens regard indiscriminate orbital bombardment as: - barbaric - dishonorable - existentially dangerous

This stigma dates back centuries and remains deeply embedded in military doctrine.

docs.title: Political Consequences
docs.text:
Open planetary bombardment is generally viewed as an outright declaration of unrestricted war.

The Star Regency would almost certainly condemn any faction engaging in unauthorized mass bombardment.

Such actions could potentially result in: - expulsion from the Conclave - political isolation - coordinated retaliation - rogue state designation

Few factions are willing to risk such consequences.

docs.title: Strategic Inefficiency
docs.text:
Contrary to popular belief, orbital bombardment is often militarily inefficient.

Although devastating against: - cities - infrastructure - fixed installations

bombardment is far less effective against mobile mech formations capable of dispersal and maneuver.

Precision targeting from orbit also remains difficult under many battlefield conditions.

Most powers prefer to capture valuable infrastructure rather than destroy it outright.

In an age where advanced technology itself is increasingly precious, annihilation is often viewed as wasteful.

docs.title: Mechs and Naval Warfare
docs.text:
Although fleets dominate space itself, mechs remain central to planetary warfare.

Modern military doctrine generally views fleets as tools for: - transport - deployment - orbital control - strategic movement

while mechs determine the outcome of ground campaigns.

This distinction has shaped warfare throughout the modern era.

A fleet may seize orbit.

But holding a world still requires steel on the ground.

docs.title: The Great Houses and Fleet Doctrine
docs.text: Each major faction maintains distinct naval traditions.

docs.title: Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text: The Union favors: - large coordinated fleet formations - aggressive strategic positioning - heavy logistical mobilization

docs.title: Helios Sovereignty
docs.text: The Sovereignty emphasizes: - prestige fleets - ceremonial formations - political projection - highly visible naval presence

docs.title: Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
The Imperium maintains some of the oldest surviving fleets in the Core.

Imperium naval doctrine values: - endurance - discipline - fleet preservation - overwhelming retaliation

Fire symbolism remains deeply integrated into many Imperium naval traditions.

docs.title: Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text: The Protectorate focuses heavily upon: - defensive fleet posture - territorial security - fortified orbital networks - attritional fleet doctrine

docs.title: Orion Corporate
docs.text: Orion naval strategy emphasizes: - advanced systems integration - precision targeting - electronic warfare - sensor superiority - fleet coordination

docs.title: Civilian Perception
docs.text:
To ordinary citizens throughout the Core, fleets possess an almost mythical status.

Most people will never directly encounter: - battleships - battlestars - major fleet formations

outside of propaganda broadcasts or ceremonial displays.

Many ancient warships are treated with near-religious reverence by their crews.

Some vessels possess: - centuries of recorded combat history - famous commanders - legendary reputations - political symbolism

far beyond their battlefield roles.

docs.title: Modern Outlook
docs.text:
As tensions rise throughout the Core, concerns regarding fleet escalation continue to grow.

Many analysts fear that if a major inter-house naval war ever truly erupted, the resulting losses could permanently cripple civilization itself.

Others argue this very fear is what continues preventing such wars from occurring.

For now, the fleets endure.

Ancient steel drifting between the stars — maintained by generations who no longer fully understand the machines they preserve, yet still trust them to hold civilization together.

docs.title: Weapons
docs.text:
"Show me a mech's weapons and I'll tell you how its pilot intends to fight."

— Mercenary Guild instructor

The war machines of the Core field an enormous variety of weapon systems, ranging from simple ballistic cannons to advanced energy weapons dating back to the age of the Great Restoration.

Although thousands of individual weapon models exist, most military systems can be grouped into a handful of major weapon families. Each family reflects a different approach to battlefield combat, with strengths, weaknesses, and tactical roles that have remained largely unchanged for centuries.

Modern weapons are commonly categorized by their method of attack: energy weapons, ballistic weapons, missile systems, support weapons, and melee systems.

docs.title: Technological Eras
docs.text:
Weapons throughout the Core are often classified according to their technological lineage.

Tech Level Era Availability I Modern Core Production Common II Advanced Core Production Uncommon III Post-Restoration Era Rare IV Restoration Era Epic

Most modern military forces rely almost entirely on Tech I and Tech II equipment. Tech III and IV weapons originate from an era when manufacturing standards, materials science, and engineering techniques exceeded those commonly available today. As a result, older weapons are often smaller, lighter, more efficient, or more powerful than their modern descendants. Many are treated as heirlooms, strategic assets, or collector's items.

docs.title: Major Weapon Families
docs.text: Weapon Family Type Typical Role Lasers Energy General-purpose combat Blasters Energy Alpha strike and assault Prismatic Lasers Energy Multi-target engagement High Energy Cannons Energy Heavy assault weapon Autoguns Ballistic Sustained direct fire Vulkan Cannons Ballistic Long-range anti-mech combat Magnetic Accelerator (MAC) Cannons Ballistic Precision penetration Impulse Cannons Advanced Energy High-tech precision assault Rockets Rockets Short-range saturation fire Missiles Missile Long-range support fire Scorchers Support Heat warfare Smoke Shot Support Obscuration and screening Battle Fists Melee Close-quarters combat

docs.title: Weapon Statistics
docs.text:
For a comparison of common military weapon systems, see:

Weapon Reference Table

docs.title: Energy Weapons
docs.text:
Energy weapons remain among the most common armaments throughout the Core. Their primary advantage is logistical simplicity. Because they draw power directly from a mech's fusion reactor, they require no ammunition and can remain operational for extended campaigns.

This advantage comes at a cost. Most energy weapons generate substantial heat, forcing pilots to carefully balance firepower against reactor efficiency and thermal management.

The major energy weapon families of the Core are:

  • Lasers — the most widespread energy weapon in known space.
  • Blasters — produced exclusively by the Omnisphere Imperium.
  • Prismatic Lasers — produced exclusively by the Orion Corporate.
  • High Energy Cannons — powerful assault weapons used throughout the Core.
  • Impulse Cannons — advanced energy weapons developed exclusively by the Orion Corporate.

Although all four families share a common technological ancestry, centuries of military development have resulted in distinct doctrines and regional preferences.

docs.title: Ballistic Weapons
docs.text:
Ballistic weapons rely upon physical projectiles rather than directed energy. Although they require ammunition, they generally produce less heat and are often easier to maintain in harsh battlefield conditions.

Many military commanders value ballistic weapons for their reliability, sustained fire capability, and ability to continue operating even when energy systems are damaged.

The major ballistic weapon families of the Core are:

  • Autoguns — common among the Union and Protectorate.
  • Vulkan Cannons — widely used throughout the Core except by the Corporate.
  • Magnetic Accelerator (MAC) Cannons — produced exclusively by the Helios Sovereignty.

Ballistic weapons generally sacrifice logistical simplicity in exchange for lower heat generation, strong sustained firepower, and exceptional battlefield reliability.

docs.title: Rocket Pods
docs.text:
Rockets are short-range saturation weapons employed by nearly every major military.

Unlike guided missile systems, rocket pods rely on large salvos of unguided projectiles designed to overwhelm enemy defenses through sheer volume of fire. Their simplicity, low cost, and devastating close-range firepower have made them a staple of mech warfare for centuries.

Rocket-equipped mechs are particularly common among assault units, urban combat specialists, and frontline formations expected to engage at close range.

docs.title: Missile Systems
docs.text:
Missiles are long-range guided weapons favored by most factions, though notably uncommon within the Starcrest Protectorate.

Unlike rocket pods, missile systems emphasize range, accuracy, and battlefield flexibility. Many designs are capable of engaging targets beyond direct line of sight, allowing missile carriers to support allied forces from considerable distances.

Missile-equipped mechs frequently serve in reconnaissance, fire-support, and command formations where information and positioning are as important as firepower.

docs.title: Specialized Weapons
docs.text:
Not all weapon systems are designed to destroy enemy mechs through direct firepower alone.

Specialized weapons focus on exploiting battlefield vulnerabilities, disrupting enemy operations, or attacking targets in unconventional ways. While often less common than standard energy, ballistic, or missile weapons, they can be devastating when employed by experienced pilots.

The most common specialized weapon family in widespread service is:

  • Scorchers — heat-based weapons most commonly associated with the Omnisphere Imperium.

Rather than relying on raw damage, Scorchers overwhelm a target's thermal management systems, gradually reducing combat effectiveness and forcing enemy pilots to contend with rising heat levels. Though uncommon compared to conventional armaments, specialized weapons often provide tactical advantages unavailable through standard weapon systems.

docs.title: Support Systems
docs.text:
Not every battlefield system is designed to destroy enemy mechs directly.

Support systems provide concealment, disruption, battlefield control, and other tactical advantages that can dramatically influence the outcome of an engagement.

The most common support system in widespread military service is:

  • Smoke Shot — specialized obscuration systems fielded solely by the Protectorate.

While rarely decisive on their own, support systems often create the opportunities that allow conventional weapons to succeed.

docs.title: Melee Combat
docs.text:
While most modern militaries emphasize ranged combat, close-quarters fighting remains an important part of mech warfare. Urban environments, reactor shortages, ammunition depletion, and battlefield damage can all force combatants into direct physical confrontation.

The most common dedicated melee system is the Battle Fist, a weapon strongly associated with the Starcrest Protectorate and its tradition of aggressive close-range combat.

Though uncommon among conventional military formations, melee-focused mechs remain popular among Protectorate forces, mercenaries, arena fighters, and assault specialists.

docs.title: Legacy of the Old Empire
docs.text:
Many of the most coveted weapons in the Core originate from the Great Restoration. Tech III and Tech IV weapons are prized not simply because they are more powerful, but because they are often more efficient, compact, and finely engineered than their modern counterparts.

For collectors, nobles, and military commanders, possessing a Restoration-era weapon is a symbol of prestige.

For mercenaries, it is often the difference between life and death.

docs.title: Autoguns
docs.text:
"Lasers win duels. Autoguns win wars."

— Union military proverb

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Autoguns are among the most common ballistic weapons fielded throughout the Core.

Essentially oversized machine guns designed for mech combat, Autoguns fire a continuous stream of high-velocity projectiles capable of damaging vehicles, infantry, and enemy mechs alike. Their simple construction, low cost, and dependable performance have made them a mainstay of military forces for centuries.

Though often overshadowed by larger cannons and advanced energy weapons, Autoguns remain one of the most versatile and widely deployed weapons in known space.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Autoguns excel at delivering a continuous stream of fire against lightly armored targets.

Unlike most mech weapons, which rely on powerful individual shots, Autoguns achieve their effectiveness through volume of fire. Their rapid firing rates allow pilots to steadily wear down enemy armor while generating virtually no heat. As long as ammunition remains available, an Autogun-equipped mech can maintain pressure on a target almost indefinitely.

Autoguns are particularly effective against lighter mechs, vehicles, and infantry formations. Their high rate of fire also makes them dangerous once enemy armor has been breached. A steady stream of rounds striking exposed internal systems dramatically increases the likelihood of critical damage, making Autoguns a favorite weapon among pilots who specialize in finishing crippled opponents.

The weapon's primary weakness is its relatively modest damage per shot. Heavy armor can often withstand Autogun fire for extended periods, forcing pilots to rely on sustained engagements or coordinate with heavier weapon systems.

For this reason, Autoguns are frequently paired with larger weapons that strip armor away while the Autogun exploits any vulnerabilities that appear.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Autogun variants include:

  • Autogun
  • Heavy Autogun

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Autoguns trace their lineage to some of humanity's earliest projectile weapons.

Although countless improvements have been made over the centuries, the underlying concept remains largely unchanged: launch a solid projectile at extremely high velocity and allow kinetic energy to do the rest.

The rise of fusion-powered mechs and advanced energy weapons led many military theorists to predict the eventual disappearance of ballistic weapons. Instead, Autoguns persisted. Their low cost, minimal maintenance requirements, and immunity to many forms of energy-system failure ensured they remained useful even as more advanced technologies emerged.

Following the Great Restoration, many governments reduced military spending and sought weapons that could be manufactured cheaply and deployed in large numbers. Autoguns proved perfectly suited to this environment and became particularly popular within the growing militaries of the Union and Protectorate.

Today, Autoguns are often viewed as unglamorous weapons. Yet few veterans underestimate them. History is filled with examples of expensive machines being destroyed by pilots carrying simple weapons that refused to stop firing.

docs.title: Battle Fists
docs.text:
"The shortest distance between victory and defeat is often a single punch."

— Protectorate military saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Battle Fists are specialized close-assault weapons employed exclusively by the Starcrest Protectorate.

Unlike conventional manipulators, Battle Fists incorporate reinforced striking surfaces, shock-absorption systems, and integrated maneuvering thrusters designed specifically for mech-to-mech combat. When combined with a mech's jump jets, these systems allow a pilot to convert the momentum of an accelerating assault directly into a devastating impact.

Though often described as melee weapons, Battle Fists function more like short-range kinetic weapons, delivering destructive force comparable to a Vulkan Cannon at point-blank range.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Battle Fists are designed to deliver overwhelming damage during close-range assaults.

As a Protectorate mech closes on its target, integrated jump jets and maneuvering thrusters are used to rapidly accelerate the machine across the battlefield. While these systems lack the power necessary for true flight, they allow the mech to skim across terrain at high speed before striking with its reinforced fist.

The resulting impact can inflict damage comparable to a direct hit from a Vulkan Cannon, often shattering armor and crippling lighter opponents in a single blow.

Unlike ranged weapons, Battle Fists require no ammunition and cannot be intercepted by defensive systems. Their effectiveness, however, depends entirely upon a pilot's ability to close the distance. Battle Fist-equipped mechs frequently rely upon terrain, smoke screens, aggressive maneuvering, and coordinated assaults to survive long enough to engage their targets.

For this reason, Battle Fists are strongly associated with Protectorate military doctrine. While other powers generally favor stand-off combat, Protectorate commanders often view close-range engagements as opportunities rather than risks. A successful charge can rapidly collapse an enemy formation and create openings that conventional firepower might struggle to achieve.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Battle Fist variants include:

  • Battle Fist

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Battle Fists originated within the Starcrest Protectorate during the centuries following the Great Restoration.

Protectorate military planners recognized that advances in armor technology were steadily increasing the survivability of front-line mechs. Rather than competing directly in long-range weapons development, engineers pursued an alternative solution: converting a mech's own mass and momentum into a weapon.

The resulting Battle Fist systems combined reinforced impact structures with maneuvering thrusters capable of dramatically increasing striking force during an attack. Though dangerous and difficult to employ effectively, the weapon proved devastating in the hands of skilled pilots.

Over time, Battle Fists became one of the defining symbols of Protectorate warfare. Their continued use reflects the Protectorate's longstanding belief that aggression, courage, and decisive action can overcome technological or numerical disadvantages.

Today, Battle Fists remain exclusive to the Starcrest Protectorate and are regarded as one of the most iconic weapons in the Core.

docs.title: Blasters
docs.text:
"A laser wounds. A blaster finishes the argument."

— Imperium military saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Blasters are advanced energy weapons most closely associated with the Omnisphere Imperium.

Unlike conventional lasers, which project a continuous beam, blasters fire discrete packets of concentrated energy. These luminous bolts travel across the battlefield as visible bursts of destructive force, giving blasters their distinctive appearance and reputation.

What makes blasters unique is their ability to store energy before combat. A blaster can accumulate up to three charges in advance, allowing pilots to unleash a devastating opening volley the moment battle begins. This capability gives blaster-equipped mechs exceptional alpha-strike potential, enabling them to inflict disproportionate damage during the first moments of an engagement.

Once these stored charges are expended, however, blasters recharge at a rate comparable to conventional lasers while generally delivering less sustained damage over time. As a result, blasters excel at sudden violence rather than prolonged exchanges of fire.

Although blasters are technically available outside Imperium space, their manufacture remains closely controlled, and relatively few examples are found beyond Imperial borders.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Blasters are designed for decisive engagements rather than prolonged firefights.

The ability to begin combat with multiple stored charges makes them exceptionally effective during ambushes, flanking attacks, and rapid assaults. A formation equipped with blasters can deliver an overwhelming burst of firepower before an opponent has an opportunity to respond, often crippling key targets within seconds.

This characteristic aligns closely with traditional Imperium doctrine. Imperial forces frequently emphasize mobility, initiative, and aggressive maneuver warfare. Fast mechs armed with blasters can strike suddenly, inflict heavy damage, and disengage before an enemy can effectively retaliate.

The weapon's weaknesses become apparent during longer engagements. Once its stored charges have been exhausted, a blaster's sustained damage output generally falls below that of comparable laser systems. Commanders who fail to achieve decisive results early may find themselves at a disadvantage as a battle drags on.

For this reason, blasters are often associated with attack-and-feint tactics, breakthrough operations, and rapid strikes against vulnerable targets. In the hands of skilled pilots, a well-coordinated blaster assault can decide a battle before it truly begins.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common blaster variants include:

  • Light Blaster
  • Medium Blaster
  • Heavy Blaster

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of the modern blaster remain a source of pride within the Omnisphere Imperium.

Imperial historians trace the weapon's development to early efforts to improve upon conventional laser technology, creating a weapon capable of maintaining battlefield effectiveness while reducing thermal strain on pilots and machines alike. Over centuries of refinement, these designs evolved into the modern blaster systems fielded today.

As the Imperium gradually withdrew from many areas of technological competition following the Great Restoration, blaster technology became one of the military traditions it continued to cultivate and protect. Modern Imperial blasters are therefore viewed not merely as weapons, but as symbols of the Imperium's enduring commitment to craftsmanship, discipline, and martial excellence.

Today, blasters remain one of the defining weapons of the Omnisphere Imperium and a common sight among its elite military formations.

docs.title: High Energy Cannons
docs.text:
"There are elegant weapons. Then there are High Energy Cannons."

— Mech pilot saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
High Energy Cannons, commonly abbreviated as HECs, are among the oldest and most destructive energy weapons still in widespread military service.

Unlike lasers and blasters, which prioritize efficiency and control, High Energy Cannons focus extraordinary amounts of energy through a narrow discharge channel and release it in a single devastating burst. The resulting beam is only partially stable, producing tremendous destructive power at the cost of heat generation, reactor strain, and efficiency.

Though crude by comparison to many modern energy weapons, HECs remain feared throughout the Core for one simple reason: few weapons can match their raw stopping power.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
High Energy Cannons are designed to destroy heavily armored targets.

A successful HEC strike can cripple lighter mechs and inflict catastrophic damage against assault-class opponents. Their immense firepower makes them popular among breakthrough units, assault formations, and heavy combat mechs expected to engage enemy front-line forces directly.

The weapon's primary drawback is heat. Every discharge places enormous strain upon a mech's cooling systems, forcing pilots to carefully manage firing cycles during prolonged engagements.

Several specialized variants have emerged over the centuries. The Long-Range HEC, fielded exclusively by the Helios Sovereignty, sacrifices efficiency for extended engagement ranges and reflects the Sovereignty's preference for precision long-range warfare. The Heavy HEC, employed primarily by the Starcrest Protectorate, further increases the weapon's destructive power at the cost of additional heat, weight, and instability. These oversized cannons are most commonly found on the Protectorate's heaviest assault mechs.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common High Energy Cannon variants include:

  • HEC
  • Long-Range HEC
  • Heavy HEC

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Most historians believe High Energy Cannons date back to the earliest generations of mech warfare.

While modern energy weapons have undergone countless refinements over the centuries, the fundamental principles behind the HEC have changed remarkably little. At its core, the weapon remains an elegant solution to a simple military problem: how to deliver the maximum possible amount of energy to a target in the shortest possible time.

Because of this simplicity, HECs survived technological collapses, political upheavals, and the Fall of the Empire largely unchanged. Even today, engineers often describe them as one of the purest expressions of energy weapon design ever created.

Every major military power fields standard HECs, though several factions have developed their own specialized variants. The Sovereignty's Long-Range HEC and the Protectorate's Heavy HEC are the most famous examples, each reflecting the battlefield philosophies of the nations that employ them.

For thousands of years, countless weapons have risen and fallen from favor. The High Energy Cannon endures because destruction, in its simplest form, rarely goes out of style.

docs.title: Impulse Cannons
docs.text:
"The projectile does not exist. The impact does."

— Orion Corporate weapons researcher

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Impulse Cannons are advanced energy weapons developed exclusively by the Orion Corporate.

Unlike conventional energy weapons, which project beams or plasma discharges, Impulse Cannons fire a precisely timed energy impulse that forces field-aligned energy into a temporary solid-state configuration. For a brief period, this artificial construct behaves much like a physical projectile, allowing it to strike targets with tremendous force before rapidly losing coherence.

The field alignment responsible for creating the projectile also produces a secondary effect: the temporary suppression of local entropy. As particle motion slows dramatically within the immediate vicinity of the slug, surrounding matter experiences rapid cooling. Air moisture crystallizes, surfaces frost over, and targets struck by the weapon are often coated in ice-like residue.

This phenomenon has led to the widespread belief that Impulse Cannons fire "ice slugs." In reality, the weapon fires no physical ammunition at all. The apparent freezing effect is merely a byproduct of the entropy-suppressing field that gives the projectile its temporary mass-like properties.

Though expensive and technologically demanding to manufacture, Impulse Cannons are widely regarded as one of the most sophisticated weapon systems currently fielded in the Core.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Impulse Cannons are precision assault weapons optimized for medium- to long-range combat.
