At short and moderate ranges, the projectile's field alignment remains highly coherent, allowing it to deliver exceptional damage with remarkable efficiency. However, as the slug travels, the artificial structure gradually degrades. The longer it remains in flight, the more rapidly its destructive potential diminishes.

This behavior creates a unique engagement profile. Unlike most long-range weapons, which maintain relatively consistent performance throughout their effective range, Impulse Cannons become progressively less destructive at extreme distances. Skilled pilots therefore seek to engage targets at ranges where the projectile remains largely intact while still exploiting the weapon's superior reach.

Because the projectile suppresses local entropy, its passage is frequently accompanied by visible freezing effects. Air surrounding the slug can momentarily crystallize, leaving a brief trail of frost and condensation in its wake. Targets struck by an Impulse Cannon often exhibit flash-freezing around the impact site, with armor plates becoming coated in ice before rapidly thawing.

The effect is highly localized. The entropy-suppressing field falls off exponentially with distance from the projectile and exists only for a fraction of a second. Nevertheless, the visual spectacle has contributed to the enduring myth that Impulse Cannons fire cryogenic ammunition or frozen projectiles.

Corporate physicists are quick to point out that neither explanation is correct. The weapon does not create cold so much as briefly remove motion itself.

The weapon's combination of efficiency, precision, and advanced engineering has made it a favorite among Orion Corporate forces, particularly elite units that emphasize coordinated engagements and superior battlefield information.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Impulse Cannon variants include:

  • Impulse Cannon

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Impulse Cannons emerged from Orion Corporate research into advanced energy containment, entropy manipulation, and field-alignment technologies.

Originally conceived as an attempt to improve the efficiency of conventional energy weapons, the project produced unexpected results when researchers discovered methods of temporarily forcing energy into a coherent, mass-like state. Though extraordinarily difficult to achieve and maintain, the resulting weapon demonstrated capabilities unlike any other system in widespread military service.

To this day, the precise mechanisms involved remain poorly understood outside Orion Corporate research divisions. Numerous attempts by rival powers to duplicate the technology have met with limited success.

As a result, the Impulse Cannon remains one of the defining symbols of Orion military engineering and a testament to the Corporate's reputation for pursuing unconventional technological solutions to battlefield problems.

docs.title: Lasers
docs.text:
"When in doubt, bring a laser."

— Mercenary proverb

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Lasers are the most common energy weapons fielded throughout the Core.

Reliable, relatively inexpensive, and requiring no ammunition, laser systems have served as the backbone of mech warfare for centuries. Nearly every military force, mercenary company, and militia organization maintains large numbers of laser-equipped mechs.

Though rarely the most specialized weapon available, lasers are often regarded as the standard against which all other weapon systems are measured.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Lasers occupy a versatile middle ground between firepower, range, weight, and reliability.

Unlike ballistic or missile weapons, lasers draw power directly from a mech's fusion reactor and can continue operating indefinitely so long as sufficient reactor output remains available.

Their primary limitation is heat generation. Sustained laser fire can quickly overwhelm a mech's cooling systems, forcing pilots to carefully manage their firing cycles during prolonged engagements.

Because of their flexibility, lasers are commonly found on reconnaissance mechs, line units, assault machines, and virtually every class of military vehicle. Every major power fields laser-equipped formations, and many commanders consider them the most dependable weapon system available.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common laser variants include:

  • Light Laser
  • Medium Laser
  • Heavy Laser

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Laser technology predates the modern Great Houses by thousands of years.

Though countless refinements have been introduced over the centuries, the fundamental principles behind laser weaponry have changed remarkably little. Their simplicity, durability, and ease of manufacture allowed lasers to survive the collapse of governments, the rise and fall of dynasties, and the technological disruptions that accompanied the Fall of the Empire.

Today, lasers remain one of the few weapon systems produced by every major military power in the Core. While more specialized weapons have emerged over time, none have succeeded in replacing the humble laser as the foundation of modern mech warfare.

docs.title: Magnetic Accelerator Cannons
docs.text:
"Why throw a shell when you can throw certainty?"

— Helios Sovereignty artillery saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Magnetic Accelerator Cannons, commonly known as MACs or MAC Cannons, are advanced ballistic weapons that use powerful electromagnetic fields to accelerate projectiles to extreme velocities.

Originally developed as an evolution of the traditional Vulkan Cannon, MACs sacrifice simplicity and affordability in exchange for superior range, accuracy, and armor penetration. Though expensive to manufacture and maintain, they have become one of the defining weapons of the Helios Sovereignty.

Few weapons in the Core are as feared at long range as a well-placed MAC round.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
MACs are precision anti-armor weapons designed to engage enemy mechs at extreme distances.

Unlike Vulkan Cannons, which rely upon large explosive shells and overwhelming impact force, MACs launch dense projectiles at extraordinary velocities. The resulting strikes deliver tremendous kinetic energy while maintaining exceptional accuracy over long distances.

This makes MAC-equipped mechs particularly effective in open terrain, defensive operations, and long-range duels. Sovereignty doctrine frequently emphasizes battlefield positioning, reconnaissance, and coordinated fire support, allowing MAC units to destroy opponents before they can effectively respond.

The weapon's primary drawback is cost. MAC systems require advanced manufacturing techniques, specialized ammunition, and sophisticated maintenance procedures. Few military organizations can afford to field them in large numbers.

As a result, MACs are strongly associated with the Helios Sovereignty, whose wealth and industrial capabilities allow it to deploy the weapon on a scale unmatched elsewhere in the Core.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common MAC variants include:

  • Light MAC
  • Medium MAC
  • Heavy MAC

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of the Magnetic Accelerator Cannon lie within centuries of Sovereignty military research.

As mech armor improved, military engineers sought methods of increasing projectile velocity beyond the limits imposed by conventional chemical propellants. Their solution was to replace explosive acceleration entirely with electromagnetic launch systems capable of propelling projectiles at unprecedented speeds.

The resulting weapons proved exceptionally effective. MAC rounds possessed greater range, superior accuracy, and significantly improved armor penetration compared to traditional Vulkan Cannons. Over time, these advantages led the Sovereignty to gradually replace most of its Vulkan-equipped formations with MAC-equipped units.

While other factions occasionally field MAC systems acquired through trade, salvage, or limited domestic production, none employ them as extensively as the Sovereignty.

Today, the MAC Cannon is regarded as one of the clearest expressions of Sovereignty military philosophy: precision, discipline, and the belief that a battle should be won before the enemy ever reaches firing range.

docs.title: Missiles
docs.text:
"A rocket hits where you fire it. A missile hits where you meant to fire it."

— Sovereignty artillery officer

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Missile Launchers are long-range guided weapon systems designed to engage targets beyond the reach of most direct-fire weapons.

Unlike Rocket Pods, which rely upon large salvos of semi-guided projectiles, missiles employ sophisticated guidance systems capable of tracking and adjusting course throughout flight. This allows missile-equipped units to engage moving targets, strike from beyond visual range, and coordinate attacks using information provided by scouts, sensors, and allied forces.

Their exceptional reach has made missiles one of the most important fire-support weapons in modern mech warfare.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Missiles specialize in long-range engagement and battlefield support.

A missile launcher can threaten enemy units at distances where most weapons cannot effectively respond. Combined with modern sensor networks, this allows missile-equipped formations to engage targets that are hidden, maneuvering, or operating beyond direct line of sight. Many missile systems are capable of indirect fire, allowing them to strike enemies concealed behind terrain or other obstacles when targeting information is available.

Unlike rockets, missiles concentrate their damage into fewer but more powerful impacts. This makes them particularly effective against exposed internal systems and vulnerable components once armor has been breached. Missile strikes frequently cause critical damage disproportionate to their apparent size.

Missile-equipped units are commonly positioned behind the front line where they can provide continuous fire support while remaining protected from direct attack. Their ability to influence engagements across vast distances makes them valuable force multipliers in both offensive and defensive operations.

The weapon's primary disadvantages are cost, ammunition requirements, and slow reload cycles. Missile launchers typically fire less frequently than most other weapon systems, making every salvo important. They also perform poorly at extremely close range, where guidance systems have insufficient time to properly acquire and maneuver toward their targets.

For this reason, missile warfare is often closely tied to reconnaissance assets, sensor platforms, aerospace support, and electronic warfare systems.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common missile launcher variants include:

  • 2/Missiles
  • 4/Missiles
  • 6/Missiles

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Modern missile systems emerged from centuries of advances in guidance technology, sensor integration, and battlefield communications.

While early missile weapons were little more than self-propelled explosives, modern launchers employ sophisticated targeting packages capable of tracking targets across vast distances. As communications networks improved throughout the Core, missile warfare became increasingly integrated with reconnaissance, command, and sensor systems.

This evolution transformed missiles from simple weapons into components of larger battlefield networks. A missile launched by one mech may ultimately strike a target identified by a scout several kilometers away.

Most major powers field missile-equipped formations, though the Starcrest Protectorate remains a notable exception. Protectorate military doctrine traditionally emphasizes direct engagement, aggressive maneuver, and close-range combat, leaving little room for the stand-off warfare that missile systems encourage.

Today, missiles remain among the most feared weapons on the battlefield. A pilot may never see the mech responsible for launching the salvo that destroys them.

docs.title: Prismatic Lasers
docs.text:
"One beam is a weapon. A hundred beams are a solution."

— Orion Corporate engineering maxim

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Prismatic Lasers are advanced energy weapons developed and manufactured exclusively by the Orion Corporate.

Unlike conventional lasers, which focus their energy into a single coherent beam, Prismatic Lasers divide their output into multiple synchronized beams capable of striking several nearby targets simultaneously. This unique capability allows a single weapon to engage formations, exploit battlefield congestion, and punish clustered opponents in ways conventional energy weapons cannot.

Though expensive and technically demanding to manufacture, Prismatic Lasers have become one of the defining symbols of Orion military technology.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Prismatic Lasers excel in situations where multiple enemy units operate in close proximity.

A successful hit can cause the weapon's energy discharge to arc or split into nearby targets, allowing Orion forces to inflict damage across entire formations rather than concentrating exclusively on a single opponent. This makes Prismatic-equipped mechs particularly effective against infantry concentrations, vehicle formations, aerospace swarms, and tightly coordinated mech units.

The weapon's primary drawback is efficiency. While devastating against grouped enemies, Prismatic Lasers often provide little advantage when facing isolated targets. Their complex focusing arrays also make them more expensive and difficult to maintain than conventional laser systems.

Corporate doctrine frequently emphasizes information superiority and battlefield control. Within this framework, Prismatic Lasers serve as force multipliers capable of exploiting opportunities identified through advanced sensors, communications networks, and tactical coordination systems.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Prismatic Laser variants include:

  • Light Prismatic Laser
  • Medium Prismatic Laser
  • Heavy Prismatic Laser

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Prismatic Lasers emerged from Orion Corporate research into advanced optical systems and battlefield energy distribution technologies.

Originally developed for industrial and scientific applications, early prototypes demonstrated an unexpected ability to distribute energy across multiple impact points without a proportional increase in reactor output. Corporate military researchers quickly recognized the potential battlefield applications of the technology and adapted the design for combat use.

The resulting weapons proved difficult for other powers to replicate. Manufacturing requires highly specialized optical components, precision alignment systems, and advanced materials that remain closely guarded Orion trade secrets.

Over time, Prismatic Lasers became one of the most recognizable products of Orion military engineering. Their distinctive multi-beam discharge patterns are instantly recognizable across the Core and are often viewed as symbols of the Corporate's technological sophistication.

Today, Prismatic Lasers remain among the most advanced energy weapons in widespread military service and are closely associated with Orion's reputation for innovation, research, and technical excellence.

docs.title: Rockets
docs.text:
"Armor first. Problems later."

— Mercenary saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Rocket Pods are medium-range missile systems designed to overwhelm targets through volume of fire.

Unlike long-range missile launchers, rockets rely on compact semi-guided munitions optimized for direct battlefield engagements. Large salvos can saturate an area with explosive warheads, making Rocket Pods one of the most effective weapons in the Core for rapidly stripping armor from enemy mechs.

Their combination of flexibility, reliability, and destructive potential has made them a common sight among military forces throughout known space.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Rocket Pods specialize in armor destruction.

A typical salvo launches multiple semi-guided projectiles toward a target, allowing the weapon to achieve consistent hits even against moving opponents. The resulting explosions spread damage across a wide area of armor, making rockets exceptionally effective at wearing down heavily protected targets.

This strength comes with an important limitation. Because rocket impacts are dispersed across a target's exterior, they are less effective at penetrating deeply into internal systems. As a result, Rocket Pods generally inflict fewer critical hits than weapons such as Autoguns, Vulkan Cannons, or Magnetic Accelerator Cannons.

For many commanders, this is not a disadvantage. Rocket-equipped units frequently operate alongside heavier weapon systems, stripping away armor and exposing vulnerable components for other weapons to exploit.

The weapon's balance of firepower, range, and ease of use has made it popular among nearly every major military power in the Core.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Rocket Pod variants include:

  • 4/Rockets
  • 8/Rockets
  • 12/Rockets
  • 24/Rockets

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Rocket technology predates modern mech warfare by thousands of years and remains one of humanity's oldest military technologies.

Though propulsion systems and guidance packages have improved considerably over the centuries, the underlying concept has remained remarkably consistent: launch multiple explosive warheads toward a target and allow quantity to compensate for imperfect accuracy.

Modern Rocket Pods evolved from earlier unguided systems through the addition of compact guidance packages capable of making limited course corrections during flight. These advances significantly improved hit probabilities without sacrificing the affordability and simplicity that made rockets attractive in the first place.

Today, Rocket Pods remain one of the most widely fielded weapon systems in the Core. While more sophisticated missile technologies exist, few weapons can match the ability of a rocket barrage to rapidly peel armor from an enemy mech and prepare it for destruction.

docs.title: Scorchers
docs.text:
"Armor can stop a shell. It cannot stop a fire."

— Imperium weapons instructor

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Scorchers are specialized incendiary weapons designed to attack a target's thermal management systems rather than its armor.

Unlike most battlefield weapons, Scorchers generate intense heat within enemy mechs while inflicting only minor direct damage. Their effects can gradually damage internal systems regardless of armor protection, making them one of the few weapons capable of threatening a mech without first stripping away its armor.

Widely employed by the Omnisphere Imperium and occasionally fielded by the Union and Protectorate, Scorchers occupy a unique niche within modern mech warfare.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Scorchers are heat warfare weapons.

A successful hit rapidly increases the target's heat levels while causing minor but persistent internal damage. Unlike conventional weapons, which must first penetrate armor before threatening critical systems, Scorchers can slowly degrade internal components regardless of how much armor remains intact.

This makes them particularly dangerous against lighter mechs, whose limited internal durability leaves them vulnerable to cumulative damage. Against heavier machines, the effects are often less dramatic, serving more as a persistent annoyance and heating issue than an immediate threat. Even so, sustained Scorcher fire can gradually reduce the combat effectiveness of larger targets over time.

Scorchers possess another important advantage: they are exceptionally easy to hit with. Their large incendiary payloads and broad area of effect make accurate fire relatively straightforward, leading many military academies to use Scorchers as training weapons for inexperienced pilots.

They are also among the most effective anti-infantry weapons in the Core. Infantry formations, light vehicles, fortifications, and other lightly protected targets can be devastated by incendiary attacks that would barely inconvenience a heavy mech.

The weapon's primary limitation is ammunition dependency. Unlike most energy-based heat weapons, Scorchers rely upon specialized incendiary munitions and must be regularly resupplied during extended campaigns.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Scorcher variants include:

  • Scorcher

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of the Scorcher are closely tied to Imperium military doctrine.

Imperium strategists long recognized that destroying an enemy mech outright was not always necessary to remove it from a battle. Weapons capable of degrading performance, stressing cooling systems, and disrupting battlefield operations could often achieve strategic objectives more efficiently than pure firepower alone.

The first Scorchers were developed to exploit this philosophy. Rather than maximizing damage, engineers focused on maximizing thermal transfer and persistent battlefield effects. The resulting weapon proved highly effective against infantry, light vehicles, and lightly armored mechs while simultaneously providing Imperium forces with a means of harassing larger opponents.

Over time, Scorchers became a common sight within Imperium formations. Though they never replaced conventional weapons, they earned a reputation as valuable support systems capable of influencing battles in ways their modest damage values often failed to suggest.

Today, Scorchers remain strongly associated with the Imperium, though limited numbers can also be found within Union and Protectorate forces. Many commanders continue to underestimate them.

Veteran pilots rarely make that mistake twice.

docs.title: Smoke Shot
docs.text:
"If they cannot see you, they cannot stop you."

— Protectorate infantry saying

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Smoke Shot is a specialized battlefield support weapon employed exclusively by the Starcrest Protectorate.

Rather than inflicting direct damage, Smoke Shot launches canisters that rapidly deploy dense clouds of obscuring smoke across the battlefield. These screens disrupt visual targeting, limit reconnaissance, and create temporary zones of concealment that can be exploited by advancing forces.

Though often overlooked by inexperienced commanders, Smoke Shot remains one of the most effective battlefield control systems in modern warfare.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Smoke Shot is designed to shape the battlefield rather than destroy enemy units.

A well-placed smoke screen can block lines of sight, conceal troop movements, protect vulnerable units, and complicate enemy targeting solutions. This makes Smoke Shot particularly valuable during assaults, withdrawals, and urban operations where battlefield visibility often determines the outcome of an engagement.

The weapon is strongly associated with Protectorate doctrine. Protectorate commanders frequently employ smoke to cover advances, conceal maneuver elements, and close the distance against opponents who possess superior long-range firepower. Many melee-focused Protectorate formations rely upon smoke screens to survive long enough to engage the enemy at close range.

Smoke can also interfere with reconnaissance operations, forcing opponents to rely more heavily upon sensors, scouts, and indirect intelligence. While modern targeting systems can partially compensate for obscured vision, few military technologies completely negate the tactical advantages provided by concealment.

The weapon's primary limitation is that it inflicts no direct damage and requires careful coordination to employ effectively. Poorly positioned smoke screens can hinder friendly forces as easily as enemy units.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Smoke Shot variants include:

  • Smoke Shot

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
The origins of Smoke Shot can be traced to the Protectorate's long tradition of defensive warfare and close-range combat.

Throughout much of its history, the Protectorate faced opponents possessing superior numbers, longer-ranged weapons, or greater industrial resources. To offset these disadvantages, Protectorate commanders developed doctrines emphasizing maneuver, terrain exploitation, and battlefield deception.

Modern Smoke Shot systems evolved from these traditions. While smoke deployment technologies have improved considerably over the centuries, the underlying concept remains unchanged: deny the enemy information while creating opportunities for friendly forces.

Today, Smoke Shot remains a defining feature of Protectorate military doctrine. Though other powers occasionally employ similar systems, none have integrated battlefield obscuration as deeply into their tactical philosophy as the Starcrest Protectorate.

docs.title: Vulkan Cannons
docs.text:
"When a Vulkan speaks, armor listens."

— Protectorate tank commander

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Vulkan Cannons are heavy ballistic weapons designed specifically for engaging armored targets at range.

Unlike Autoguns, which rely on sustained fire, Vulkan Cannons launch large-caliber shells capable of inflicting devastating damage with a single hit. Their combination of firepower, reliability, and relatively low heat generation has made them one of the most widely used anti-mech weapons in the Core.

Though often overshadowed by more advanced technologies, few commanders underestimate the destructive potential of a well-placed Vulkan round.

docs.title: Battlefield Role
docs.text:
Vulkan Cannons serve as the primary long-range ballistic weapon for many military forces.

The weapon's greatest strength is its ability to deliver substantial damage while generating comparatively little heat. This allows Vulkan-equipped mechs to maintain consistent fire over extended engagements without suffering the thermal limitations common to many energy weapons.

Their high-impact shells are particularly effective against heavily armored targets. Even when a round fails to penetrate armor completely, the kinetic force of the impact can degrade protective plating, strip armor, and create opportunities for follow-up attacks.

The primary drawback of the Vulkan Cannon is ammunition dependency. Unlike energy weapons, Vulkan-equipped units must maintain a steady supply of shells during prolonged operations. Ammunition shortages can quickly reduce even the most powerful cannon-equipped mech to a liability.

Vulkan Cannons are especially common within the Confederate Vanguard Union, the Starcrest Protectorate, and the Omnisphere Imperium. Their reputation for reliability and straightforward battlefield effectiveness aligns closely with the military doctrines of all three powers.

docs.title: Common Variants
docs.text:
Common Vulkan Cannon variants include:

  • Light Vulkan Cannon
  • Medium Vulkan Cannon
  • Heavy Vulkan Cannon
  • Ultra Vulkan Cannon

See the Weapon Reference for detailed specifications.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Vulkan Cannons trace their lineage back thousands of years and are among the oldest mech-scale weapon systems still in widespread military service.

While individual designs have evolved considerably over the centuries, the underlying concept has remained remarkably consistent: launch a large projectile at high velocity and allow mass, momentum, and kinetic energy to do the rest.

The weapon's longevity stems from its simplicity. Unlike many advanced energy weapons, Vulkan Cannons require no exotic focusing arrays, specialized reactor technologies, or proprietary manufacturing techniques. They are straightforward, dependable machines capable of delivering tremendous firepower under almost any battlefield conditions.

Over time, many military powers experimented with more sophisticated ballistic systems. The most successful of these efforts produced the Magnetic Accelerator Cannon (MAC), a weapon that eventually replaced the Vulkan Cannon within the Helios Sovereignty. While more expensive and technologically demanding, MACs offered superior range, accuracy, and armor penetration, making them a natural fit for Sovereignty doctrine.

Elsewhere, however, Vulkan Cannons remained dominant. The Union, Protectorate, and Imperium continue to field large numbers of Vulkan-equipped mechs, valuing their reliability, ease of maintenance, and proven battlefield effectiveness.

Today, Vulkan Cannons remain one of the defining weapons of modern mech warfare. Though newer technologies continue to emerge, few weapons can match the combination of simplicity, affordability, and raw stopping power that has kept the Vulkan relevant for millennia.

docs.title: Battlefield Use
docs.text: Strengths: dependable damage, good against armor, simple maintenance. Weaknesses: ammunition dependence, recoil, weight.

docs.text: Prismatic lasers are advanced Orion Corporate energy weapons that split, tune, or modulate beams for specialized battlefield effects.

docs.title: Battlefield Use
docs.text: They are expensive, technically demanding, and difficult for non-Corporate forces to maintain.

docs.title: Rockets
docs.text: Rockets are simpler than guided missiles and usually deliver short-range burst damage.

docs.title: Battlefield Use
docs.text: They are popular among poorer forces, pirates, raiders, and units that value overwhelming opening strikes.

docs.title: Major Worlds of the Core
docs.text:
The Core contains hundreds of inhabited worlds ranging from industrial powerhouses and agricultural centers to military fortresses and frontier colonies.

While each Great House governs dozens of systems, several worlds stand above the rest in terms of population, economic importance, political influence, or military significance.

docs.title: Great House Worlds
docs.text:
• Starcrest Protectorate Worlds
  • Helios Sovereignty Worlds
  • Orion Corporate Worlds
  • Confederate Vanguard Union Worlds
  • Omnisphere Imperium Worlds

docs.title: Neutral Worlds
docs.text: • Neutral Worlds

docs.title: Major Worlds of the Orion Corporate
docs.text:
"For Futures Yet to Come."

— Motto of House Noddack

The Orion Corporate is the technological powerhouse of the Core. Governed by House Noddack from Horizon's Reach, the Corporate values innovation, education, efficiency, and scientific advancement above tradition or ideology.

Its worlds are among the most technologically advanced in human space. Universities, research facilities, orbital shipyards, and industrial arcologies dominate much of Corporate territory.

While other Houses often define themselves through military power, wealth, or ancient heritage, the Corporate believes humanity's future will be determined by knowledge.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
World Population Primary Role Horizon's Reach 2.1 Billion Capital / Administration Virixia 1.2 Billion Research Prometheus 0.5 Billion Experimental Technology Aether VII 0.7 Billion Aerospace Veridian 2.3 Billion Industry Astraeus 1.6 Billion Education Novaxar 0.6 Billion Resource Extraction Zenith IX 1.8 Billion Commerce Kryllix 0.6 Billion Frontier Research

Total Population: 11.4 Billion

Military Strength: 189,000 Mechs

Battlestars

  • Metaforge
  • Chronex

docs.title: Horizon's Reach
docs.text:
Population: 2.1 Billion**

Capital of the Orion Corporate and seat of House Noddack.

Horizon’s Reach serves as the administrative and technological heart of the Corporate. Vast research complexes, corporate headquarters, and advanced manufacturing districts dominate its skyline.

The planet is often described as a city of engineers, scientists, and entrepreneurs.

Many of the technologies that define modern life throughout the Core were first developed on Horizon's Reach.

docs.title: Virixia
docs.text:
Population: 1.2 Billion

One of the premier scientific centers in human space.

Virixia hosts hundreds of research institutes focused on everything from materials science and medicine to advanced computing and propulsion systems.

Its universities attract students from every major House.

The world has earned a reputation for producing ideas that eventually transform entire industries.

docs.title: Prometheus
docs.text:
Population: 500 Million

The Corporate's most ambitious experimental world.

Prometheus is home to countless prototype facilities, advanced laboratories, and classified research programs.

New technologies are often tested here before being deployed elsewhere throughout Corporate territory.

The world has become synonymous with both innovation and risk.

docs.title: Aether VII
docs.text:
Population: 700 Million

A major aerospace and orbital manufacturing center.

Shipyards, aerospace facilities, and orbital construction platforms surround the planet, producing civilian spacecraft, military vessels, and commercial infrastructure.

Aether VII plays a critical role in maintaining the Corporate's transportation and logistics networks.

docs.title: Veridian
docs.text:
Population: 2.3 Billion

The largest industrial world in Corporate space.

Massive arcologies, automated factories, and industrial megacomplexes stretch across entire continents. Veridian supplies much of the machinery, electronics, and advanced components used throughout Orion territory.

Despite its industrial nature, strict environmental regulations have preserved much of the planet's natural beauty.

docs.title: Astraeus
docs.text:
Population: 1.6 Billion

The educational heart of the Orion Corporate.

Astraeus hosts some of the most prestigious universities in the Core and maintains a culture centered around learning, scholarship, and discovery.

Many of the Corporate's future leaders begin their careers on Astraeus.

Academic achievement carries enormous social prestige on the planet.

docs.title: Novaxar
docs.text:
Population: 600 Million

A resource-rich world supporting the Corporate's industrial machine.

Mining operations, processing facilities, and transportation networks dominate much of the planet.

Although less glamorous than many Corporate worlds, Novaxar supplies critical materials used throughout Corporate territory.

docs.title: Zenith IX
docs.text:
Population: 1.8 Billion

The commercial center of Orion space.

Financial firms, technology companies, trade organizations, and corporate exchanges make Zenith IX one of the most economically influential worlds in the Core.

Many visitors describe it as a world where business never sleeps.

docs.title: Kryllix
docs.text:
Population: 600 Million

One of the outermost major worlds of the Orion Corporate.

Kryllix serves as both a frontier colony and a research outpost. Exploration teams, survey organizations, and scientific expeditions frequently operate from the planet.

Its location has made it an important gateway between the Core and the wider unknown regions beyond.

docs.title: The Corporate Character
docs.text:
The worlds of the Orion Corporate are united by a belief in progress.

Where the Protectorate sees strength in endurance and the Sovereignty sees strength in wealth, the Corporate sees strength in knowledge.

Education is highly valued. Scientific achievement brings prestige. Innovation is rewarded.

This philosophy has produced some of the most advanced technologies in human space, allowing the Corporate to maintain its influence despite fielding one of the smallest militaries among the Great Houses.

Its citizens often believe that tomorrow can be better than today.

The Corporate exists to ensure that it is.

docs.title: Major Worlds of the Omnisphere Imperium
docs.text:
"Fierce Beauty."

— Motto of House Lytherius

The Omnisphere Imperium is the oldest of the Great Houses and the civilization that most openly claims the legacy of the Golden Empire. Ruled by House Lytherius from Celestia Prime, the Imperium places great importance on tradition, culture, lineage, and the preservation of humanity's historical achievements.

To outsiders, the Imperium can appear aristocratic, ceremonial, and obsessed with the past. To its citizens, these traditions represent continuity with thousands of years of civilization.

The Imperium fields a formidable military, but its greatest strength lies in the prestige of its institutions and the loyalty of its people.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
World Population Primary Role Celestia Prime 1.9 Billion Capital / Culture Galathor 0.3 Billion Fortress World Kyndros 0.7 Billion Agriculture Lumithar 0.6 Billion Education Nythoros 1.2 Billion Industry Morventhar 1.8 Billion Population Center Seraphsis 1.6 Billion Religion / Culture Aeloria 0.9 Billion Research Kethos 1.3 Billion Mining & Industry

Total Population: 10.3 Billion

Military Strength: 220,000 Mechs

Battlestars

  • Eternity's Arcanum
  • Aetherius V

docs.title: Celestia Prime
docs.text:
Population: 1.9 Billion

Capital of the Omnisphere Imperium and seat of House Lytherius.

Celestia Prime is one of the most beautiful worlds in known space. Magnificent cities, ancient monuments, imperial palaces, and carefully preserved historical districts cover much of its surface.

Many of the Imperium's most important cultural institutions are located here, including archives, museums, and academies whose histories stretch back centuries.

To many Imperials, Celestia Prime represents the soul of their civilization.

docs.title: Galathor
docs.text:
Population: 300 Million

A rugged fortress world located along important strategic approaches to Imperium territory.

Massive defensive installations, ancient fortresses, and military academies dominate much of the landscape.

Despite its relatively small population, Galathor has played a critical role in numerous wars throughout Imperium history.

docs.title: Kyndros
docs.text:
Population: 700 Million

One of the Imperium's primary agricultural worlds.

Rolling plains, fertile valleys, and carefully maintained farming regions support large populations and provide food exports throughout Imperial territory.

Many noble houses maintain ancestral estates on Kyndros.

docs.title: Lumithar
docs.text:
Population: 600 Million

A world dedicated to scholarship, philosophy, and higher learning.

Its universities are among the oldest in the Core and attract students from every major House.

Lumithar's scholars often serve as advisors, historians, diplomats, and administrators throughout the Imperium.

docs.title: Nythoros
docs.text:
Population: 1.2 Billion

One of the Imperium's most important industrial worlds.

Shipyards, manufacturing districts, and heavy industry support both civilian and military production.

Though less glamorous than Celestia Prime, Nythoros remains essential to the Imperium's continued strength.

docs.title: Morventhar
docs.text:
Population: 1.8 Billion

A densely populated world known for its sprawling cities and vibrant urban culture.

Morventhar serves as one of the primary economic and population centers of the Imperium.

Its markets, universities, and cultural institutions draw visitors from across human space.

docs.title: Seraphsis
docs.text:
Population: 1.6 Billion

The spiritual heart of the Imperium.

Ancient temples, monumental architecture, and countless cultural landmarks cover the planet.

Many of the traditions and ceremonies that define Imperial identity originated on Seraphsis.

Pilgrimages to its historic sites remain common among Imperial citizens.

docs.title: Aeloria
docs.text:
Population: 900 Million

A center of scientific and artistic achievement.

Aeloria combines research institutions with cultural academies, producing both technological innovation and artistic excellence.

The world reflects the Imperium's belief that knowledge and beauty should advance together.

docs.title: Kethos
docs.text:
Population: 1.3 Billion

A resource-rich industrial world that supplies much of the raw material required by the Imperium.

Mining complexes, refineries, and manufacturing centers dominate many regions of the planet.

Kethos provides the industrial foundation upon which much of Imperial prosperity is built.

docs.title: The Imperial Character
docs.text:
The worlds of the Omnisphere Imperium are united by a profound respect for history.

Imperial citizens see themselves not merely as members of a modern state, but as participants in a civilization stretching back through countless generations.

This perspective shapes everything from government and education to architecture and military tradition.

Critics sometimes accuse the Imperium of living in the past.

Imperials often respond that civilizations survive because they remember where they came from.

To them, history is not a burden.

It is the foundation upon which the future is built.

docs.title: The Imperial Legacy
docs.text:
Among all the Great Houses, none identifies more strongly with the legacy of the Golden Empire than House Lytherius.

Imperial historians, artists, and statesmen have spent centuries preserving records, traditions, and institutions that might otherwise have been lost.

Whether this represents genuine stewardship or simple nostalgia remains a matter of debate throughout the Core.

Yet even the Imperium's critics acknowledge one truth:

No House remembers the past more clearly than the Imperium.

docs.title: Major Worlds of the Starcrest Protectorate
docs.text:
"Smoke and Steel."

— Motto of House Konnen

The Starcrest Protectorate is one of the oldest and most militarized nations in the Core. Though smaller in population than many of its rivals, it maintains one of the largest standing armies in human space. Its worlds are known for harsh climates, rugged populations, and a culture that prizes resilience, sacrifice, and self-reliance.

The Protectorate is ruled by House Konnen from the icy capital world of Khorhall.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
World Population Primary Role Khorhall 1.4 Billion Capital / Industry Skyreach 0.9 Billion Agriculture / Aerospace Smokefall 1.2 Billion Manufacturing Nordra 0.5 Billion Military Recruitment Dunerift 0.4 Billion Mining Cygnus Major 1.1 Billion Agriculture Tyndrall 0.6 Billion Fortress World Astridia 0.4 Billion Gas Extraction Vonhelm 0.7 Billion Frontier Colony

Total Population: 7.2 Billion

Military Strength: 288,000 Mechs

Battlestars

  • Odin
  • Warden's Might
  • Valhalla II

docs.title: Khorhall
docs.text:
Population: 1.4 Billion

Capital of the Starcrest Protectorate and seat of House Konnen.

Khorhall is a frozen world of towering mountain ranges, vast glaciers, and sprawling industrial cities. Despite its unforgiving climate, it serves as the political, economic, and military heart of the Protectorate.

The world is famous for its heavy industries, foundries, and military production complexes. Many of the Protectorate's most durable weapons, armor systems, and military vehicles originate from Khorhall's factories.

The harsh environment has shaped the Protectorate's culture for centuries, producing a population known throughout the Core for its toughness and determination.

docs.title: Skyreach
docs.text:
Population: 900 Million

A spectacular low-gravity world known for its towering peaks, thin atmosphere, and breathtaking views of nearby nebulae.

Skyreach is one of the Protectorate's largest agricultural producers, supplying specialized crops adapted to its unique environment. The world is also famous for its aerospace industries and extreme sports culture.

Many of the Protectorate's pilots receive their earliest flight training on Skyreach.

docs.title: Smokefall
docs.text:
Population: 1.2 Billion

One of the industrial centers of the Protectorate.

Centuries of manufacturing have left much of the planet beneath a permanent layer of smoke and haze. Massive foundries, refineries, and factories dominate the landscape, producing everything from civilian machinery to military hardware.

Life on Smokefall is difficult, but its people take pride in their contribution to the Protectorate's strength.

Many citizens view the smoke-filled skies as a symbol of prosperity rather than pollution.

docs.title: Nordra
docs.text:
Population: 500 Million

A rugged frontier world renowned for its warrior culture.

Massive fortresses dot the mountains and frozen valleys of Nordra, many tracing their origins back centuries. Military service is considered both an honor and a civic duty, and many of the Protectorate's finest soldiers and mech pilots hail from this world.

Nordra's inhabitants place great value on strength, endurance, and personal honor.

docs.title: Dunerift
docs.text:
Population: 400 Million

A harsh desert world battered by frequent sandstorms and extreme temperatures.

Despite its hostile environment, Dunerift possesses rich mineral deposits that make it one of the Protectorate's most valuable resource worlds. Vast mining operations stretch across the deserts, while isolated settlements cluster around underground water reserves.

Life on Dunerift is difficult, but its mineral wealth helps fuel much of the Protectorate's industrial economy.

docs.title: Cygnus Major
docs.text:
Population: 1.1 Billion

Often referred to as the Breadbasket of the Protectorate.

Cygnus Major is a lush world of forests, rivers, rolling plains, and fertile farmland. It produces enormous quantities of food and agricultural exports that sustain both civilian populations and military forces throughout Protectorate territory.

Compared to many Protectorate worlds, Cygnus Major enjoys a relatively mild climate and high standard of living.

docs.title: Tyndrall
docs.text:
Population: 600 Million

A barren and heavily fortified military world.

Although poor in natural resources, Tyndrall occupies a strategically important location along several major transit routes. Over generations it has been transformed into one of the most heavily defended worlds in the Protectorate.

Massive military installations, orbital defense systems, and fortified cities dominate the landscape.

Many military planners consider Tyndrall the shield of the Protectorate.

docs.title: Astridia
docs.text:
Population: 400 Million

A gas giant surrounded by dozens of inhabited moons and orbital habitats.

The Astridia system contains vast reserves of rare gases and industrial resources critical to the Protectorate's economy. Most inhabitants live on orbital platforms, refinery stations, or the system's colonized moons.

The system serves as one of the Protectorate's most important resource extraction centers.

docs.title: Vonhelm
docs.text:
Population: 700 Million

One of the outermost major worlds of the Protectorate.

Vonhelm combines frontier independence with fierce loyalty to House Konnen. Its settlements are spread across vast wilderness regions where isolated communities, mining operations, and agricultural colonies coexist.

The world remains less developed than many core Protectorate worlds, giving it a reputation as a land of opportunity for pioneers, prospectors, and adventurers.

Many citizens consider Vonhelm the embodiment of the Protectorate spirit: proud, resilient, and self-reliant.

docs.title: The Protectorate Character
docs.text:
The worlds of the Starcrest Protectorate share few environmental similarities. Frozen tundra, fertile plains, desert mining worlds, industrial centers, and frontier colonies all exist within House Konnen's domain.

What unites them is a common culture built upon resilience, duty, and sacrifice.

While other powers may boast greater wealth or technological sophistication, the Protectorate takes pride in its people, its military traditions, and its willingness to endure hardships that would break lesser nations.

docs.title: Major Worlds of the Helios Sovereignty
docs.text:
"Wealth is Power."

— Motto of House Aerin-Payne

The Helios Sovereignty is the wealthiest of the Great Houses and the undisputed financial center of the Core. Ruled by House Aerin-Payne from the radiant world of Lumina, the Sovereignty commands vast commercial networks, powerful banking institutions, and some of the most luxurious cities in human space.

Its worlds are known for prosperity, refinement, commerce, and influence. While critics often accuse the Sovereignty of excess and decadence, its citizens point to generations of stability, wealth, and cultural achievement as proof of their success.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
World Population Primary Role Lumina 2.7 Billion Capital / Finance New Tarsis 0.8 Billion Trade Hub Eridanius IV 0.4 Billion Luxury Agriculture Arcadia 1.8 Billion Culture / Tourism Haven 1.4 Billion Banking Lyra II 0.5 Billion Research Helix III 0.7 Billion Manufacturing Luxoria 0.9 Billion Noble Estates Greenworld VII 0.5 Billion Agriculture

Total Population: 9.7 Billion

Military Strength: 273,000 Mechs

Battlestars

  • Radiant Dawn
  • Sunfire

docs.title: Lumina
docs.text:
Population: 2.7 Billion

Capital of the Helios Sovereignty and home of House Aerin-Payne.

Lumina is one of the most prosperous worlds in known space. Towering arcologies, glittering financial districts, and luxurious residential complexes dominate its continents. The world serves as the commercial heart of the Sovereignty and hosts many of the Core's largest financial institutions.

To its citizens, Lumina represents the pinnacle of civilization. To its critics, it symbolizes the excesses of the wealthy elite.

Regardless of opinion, few can deny its importance.

docs.title: New Tarsis
docs.text:
Population: 800 Million

One of the Sovereignty's most important trade worlds.

Located along several major commercial routes, New Tarsis serves as a crossroads for merchants, shipping companies, and travelers from across the Core.

Its orbital facilities handle staggering volumes of cargo each year, making it one of the busiest commercial systems under Sovereignty control.

docs.title: Eridanius IV
docs.text:
Population: 400 Million

A world renowned for luxury agriculture and fine food production.

Its temperate climate and fertile soils support vineyards, orchards, ranches, and agricultural estates that supply many of the Core's wealthiest consumers.

Products bearing the Eridanius name command premium prices throughout known space.

docs.title: Arcadia
docs.text:
Population: 1.8 Billion

The cultural jewel of the Sovereignty.

Arcadia is famous for its beautiful cities, universities, museums, concert halls, and artistic traditions. Tourism forms a major component of the planetary economy, drawing visitors from every major House.

Many of humanity's most celebrated artists, writers, and musicians trace their origins to Arcadia.

docs.title: Haven
docs.text:
Population: 1.4 Billion

The financial engine of the Sovereignty.

While Lumina serves as the political capital, Haven is home to many of the banking institutions, investment houses, and commercial exchanges that underpin the House's immense wealth.

Economic decisions made on Haven can influence markets throughout the Core.

docs.title: Lyra II
docs.text:
Population: 500 Million

A world dedicated to higher learning and scientific advancement.

Its universities and research institutes attract students and scholars from across human space. Although smaller than many Sovereignty worlds, Lyra II exerts an influence far greater than its population would suggest.

Many important technological and economic innovations originate here.

docs.title: Helix III
docs.text:
Population: 700 Million

The industrial backbone of the Sovereignty.

Unlike the luxurious worlds for which the House is famous, Helix III is dominated by factories, shipyards, and manufacturing complexes. It produces much of the equipment necessary to sustain the Sovereignty's economy.

Its workers often joke that they build the wealth enjoyed elsewhere.

docs.title: Luxoria
docs.text:
Population: 900 Million

Home to some of the wealthiest families in the Core.

Luxoria is famous for its estates, private enclaves, luxury resorts, and exclusive communities. Political influence often flows through its halls as readily as wealth.

Many members of the Sovereignty's aristocracy maintain residences on Luxoria.

docs.title: Greenworld VII
docs.text:
Population: 500 Million

A fertile agricultural world supplying food to much of the Sovereignty.

Vast farming regions cover large portions of the planet, while small cities and agricultural cooperatives form the backbone of local society.

Although less glamorous than many Sovereignty worlds, Greenworld VII remains essential to the stability of the House.

docs.title: The Sovereignty Character
docs.text:
The worlds of the Helios Sovereignty embody prosperity, commerce, and influence.

Where the Protectorate values resilience and military strength, the Sovereignty values wealth, culture, and economic power. Its leaders believe prosperity creates stability and that influence can often accomplish what armies cannot.

Though frequently criticized for its extravagance, the Sovereignty remains one of the most successful and powerful states in human space.

To its citizens, wealth is not merely a measure of success.

It is the foundation upon which civilization itself is built.

docs.title: Neutral Worlds
docs.text:
Several important worlds exist outside the direct control of the Great Houses.

These worlds often serve as diplomatic, commercial, or strategic hubs.

docs.title: Citadel
docs.text:
Population: 4.2 Billion

Seat of the Stellar Conclave and home of the Star Regent.

Citadel serves as the political heart of the Core and hosts representatives from every major House.

docs.title: Drake's Landing
docs.text:
Population: 1.8 Billion

A major commercial crossroads located along several important trade routes.

Known for its free ports, massive orbital markets, and strong mercantile traditions.

docs.title: Titan Prime
docs.text:
Population: 2.6 Billion

One of the largest independent industrial centers in known space.

Titan Prime's shipyards, refineries, and manufacturing complexes make it a vital economic partner for every House.

docs.title: Major Worlds of the Confederate Vanguard Union
docs.text:
"A map may mark our territory but our soldiers define it."

— Union military saying

The Confederate Vanguard Union is the largest military power in the Core. Ruled by House Hawkins from Starhold, the Union values discipline, service, duty, and collective strength above individual ambition.

Its citizens are taught that civilization survives only because someone is willing to defend it. Military service is respected throughout society, and many of the Union's institutions are organized around principles of merit, discipline, and public duty.

The Union fields the largest military force among the Great Houses and is widely regarded as the most dangerous conventional military power in human space.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
World Population Primary Role Starhold 2.9 Billion Capital / Military Command Fortuna 3.6 Billion Industry Hammerfall 0.8 Billion Weapons Manufacturing Galvin's Forge 0.5 Billion Heavy Industry New Ramos 1.9 Billion Agriculture Daxton's Dominion 1.8 Billion Recruitment & Training Freedom 0.3 Billion Penal Colony Mercer's Gate 0.6 Billion Border Fortress World Liberty's End 2.4 Billion Autonomous Technology Hub

Total Population: 14.8 Billion

Military Strength: 346,000 Mechs

Battlestars

  • Indomitable
  • Wrath Bringer
  • Justice

docs.title: Starhold
docs.text:
Population: 2.9 Billion

Capital of the Confederate Vanguard Union and seat of House Hawkins.

Starhold is the political, military, and administrative center of the Union. Vast government complexes, military headquarters, strategic command centers, and research facilities are spread across the planet, coordinating operations that reach every corner of Union space.

The world is dominated by immense arid deserts that stretch across much of its surface. Over the centuries, the Union has transformed these harsh landscapes into some of the most heavily fortified territory in the Core. Massive military installations, hardened command bunkers, orbital defense networks, and sprawling training grounds make Starhold one of the most secure worlds in human space.

Virtually every major military campaign undertaken by the Union is planned, coordinated, or supported from Starhold. The planet also serves as a center for military research and strategic development, ensuring the Union remains prepared for threats both within and beyond its borders.

To its citizens, Starhold represents the ideals upon which the Union was built: duty, discipline, service, and strength. Throughout the Core, it stands as the enduring symbol of Union power.

docs.title: Fortuna
docs.text:
Population: 3.6 Billion

The wealthiest and most prosperous world in the Union, Fortuna is often regarded as the crown jewel of Union space.

Its great cities are sprawling centers of commerce, industry, and culture, defined by towering arcologies, modern infrastructure, and a standard of living unmatched on most Union worlds. Prosperity is visible throughout the planet, from its thriving commercial districts to its extensive public works and industrial centers.

Much of Fortuna's wealth originates from its abundant natural resources and powerful industrial base. Vast mining operations extract strategic minerals from across the planet, while advanced manufacturing complexes transform those resources into everything from consumer goods and industrial equipment to military hardware. Together, these industries form one of the largest economic engines in the Union.

Despite its reputation for wealth and luxury, Fortuna remains firmly aligned with Union values. Its citizens often view prosperity not as a reward for privilege, but as the result of discipline, productivity, and service to society.

Throughout the Union, Fortuna stands as both a symbol of what the nation has achieved and a reminder of what its leaders believe can be accomplished through unity, hard work, and collective purpose.

docs.title: Hammerfall
docs.text:
Population: 800 Million

One of the Union's most renowned military worlds and home to one of its two elite training academies.

Hammerfall is a harsh and unforgiving planet where scorching deserts, jagged mountain ranges, violent storms, and barren wastelands test the limits of human endurance. Life on the world is difficult even for civilians, a fact the Union has long viewed as an advantage rather than a hardship.

The planet's military academies are among the most demanding in Union space. Recruits from across the Union travel to Hammerfall to undergo rigorous physical, mental, and tactical training designed to identify those capable of thriving under extreme pressure. Attrition rates are high, and many candidates fail to complete the program.

Graduates of Hammerfall earn considerable prestige within the Union military. The world has developed a reputation for producing disciplined soldiers, capable officers, and battlefield leaders who remain effective even in the most adverse conditions.

While Galvin's Forge is known for teaching survival and self-reliance, Hammerfall is respected for forging discipline, determination, and unwavering commitment to duty. Among Union soldiers, assignment to Hammerfall is often regarded as both an honor and an ordeal.

docs.title: Galvin's Forge
docs.text:
Population: 500 Million

A remote and unforgiving world that serves as one of the Union's most prestigious military training centers.

The planet is renowned for its harsh terrain, extreme weather, and inhospitable wilderness. Mountain ranges, frozen wastelands, rocky deserts, and violent seasonal storms create an environment where survival is never guaranteed and mistakes are often fatal.

The Union has long viewed these conditions as ideal for preparing soldiers for the realities of warfare. Vast training complexes, combat academies, and field exercises are spread across the planet, attracting recruits, officers, and specialists from throughout Union space.

Galvin's Forge is particularly respected for its emphasis on endurance, discipline, and self-reliance. Those who complete its most demanding training programs earn a reputation for resilience and determination, qualities highly valued throughout the Union military.

Among soldiers, it is often said that Galvin's Forge does not teach people how to fight—it teaches them how to survive.

docs.title: New Ramos
docs.text:
Population: 1.9 Billion

One of the most prosperous and economically diverse worlds in the Confederate Vanguard Union.

Unlike many Union worlds that specialize in a single industry, New Ramos supports a broad range of economic activity. Advanced manufacturing centers, technology firms, agricultural regions, mining operations, and commercial hubs all contribute to the planet's thriving economy. This diversity has made New Ramos one of the most stable and self-sufficient worlds in Union space.

The planet is particularly renowned for its exports. Luxury consumer goods, advanced technologies, precision-manufactured products, and exotic agricultural specialties from New Ramos can be found throughout the Core. Many of the Union's wealthiest merchants and industrialists maintain significant interests on the world.

New Ramos is also known for its vibrant cultural landscape. Trade, migration, and economic opportunity have attracted people from across Union territory, creating a society that is often viewed as more cosmopolitan than many of the Union's traditionally military-focused worlds.

Though prosperity defines modern New Ramos, its citizens remain fiercely loyal to the Union, viewing economic strength and industrial achievement as vital contributions to the Union's continued success.

docs.title: Daxton's Dominion
docs.text:
Population: 1.8 Billion

Daxton's Dominion was once ruled by the legendary and charismatic ruler named Daxton before coming under the thumb of the Union. Today, Daxton's Dominion serves as one of the Union's most important military-industrial worlds. Vast factories, orbital shipyards, and weapons production facilities operate around the clock, supplying the Union's armed forces with everything from ammunition and armored vehicles to warships and military infrastructure.

The planet's industrial output and strategic location have made it a cornerstone of Union military power. While much of its former independence has faded, the Dominion's citizens continue to take pride in their reputation for hard work, discipline, and unwavering support of the Union war effort.

Throughout the Union, Daxton's Dominion is regarded as a world where history, industry, and military service remain closely intertwined.

docs.title: Freedom
docs.text:
→ See: Freedom (full article)

Population: 300 Million

A harsh penal world where the Union sends its most dangerous criminals, insurgents, deserters, failed officials, and political dissidents.

Sentencing to Freedom is permanent. Those exiled there forfeit their citizenship, titles, inheritance, and legal ties to their former lives. Once transferred to Freedom, an individual belongs to the world for life and serves the Union through one of two paths: labor or military service.

Life on Freedom is deliberately unforgiving. Vast penal settlements, labor camps, military compounds, and fortified detention zones dominate much of the planet's habitable regions. Beyond the walls lies a hostile wilderness filled with dangerous native fauna and environmental hazards that make escape nearly impossible.

Freedom is also one of the Union's most valuable resource worlds. Its mines produce vast quantities of strategic minerals and industrial materials under brutal conditions that claim countless lives each year. Those not assigned to the mines serve within the Freedom Corps, a harsh military organization recruited from the planet's exile population.

Though often dismissed by regular military personnel as little more than criminals in uniform, the Freedom Corps has earned a reputation for fanatic discipline, extreme casualty tolerance, and unwavering loyalty. Deployed primarily along the frontier, its soldiers are expected to endure hardships and losses that would break most conventional units. Throughout Union history, a handful of Freedom Corps commanders have risen from exile to become celebrated heroes of the Union.

The Union maintains Freedom not merely as a prison, but as a warning. Throughout Union space, the threat of exile to Freedom serves as a powerful deterrent against rebellion, sedition, desertion, and serious crime.

docs.title: Mercer's Gate
docs.text:
Population: 600 Million

One of the most heavily fortified worlds in Union territory.

Strategically positioned along critical border regions, Mercer's Gate serves as a defensive bulwark against external threats and internal unrest. Massive fortress complexes, military bases, orbital defense platforms, and hardened command centers cover much of the planet.

The world also houses major weapons factories, military training facilities, and logistics infrastructure that support Union military operations across nearby sectors.

Mercer's Gate is often described by Union strategists as the shield of the Union—a world built for war and prepared to withstand siege.

docs.title: Liberty's End
docs.text:
Population: 2.4 Billion

One of the most prosperous and independent worlds within the Confederate Vanguard Union.

Known for its sprawling metropolitan centers, advanced infrastructure, and thriving technological sector, Liberty's End enjoys a level of local autonomy uncommon within the highly centralized Union. Its citizens take pride in their traditions of self-governance and civic independence while remaining loyal members of the Union.

The planet's universities, research institutions, and technology firms contribute significantly to Union economic and military development. Many advanced systems used throughout Union space originated on Liberty's End.

Though culturally distinct from many of the Union's more militarized worlds, Liberty's End remains a valuable and influential member of the Union, providing both innovation and resources to support Union strength.

docs.title: The Union Character
docs.text:
The worlds of the Confederate Vanguard Union are united by a belief that strength creates stability.

Its citizens view military service as a responsibility rather than a burden. Public institutions emphasize merit, discipline, and collective achievement.

To critics, the Union can appear rigid and militaristic.

To its supporters, it represents humanity's best defense against chaos.

The Union does not apologize for its military power.

Its leaders believe peace is maintained not through hope or diplomacy, but through the certainty that aggression will be met with overwhelming force.

docs.title: Freedom
docs.text:
"On Freedom, no exile is burdened by the concerns of their former life."

— Union Penal Authority Doctrine

Freedom is a penal world of the Confederate Vanguard Union and one of the largest sources of strategic minerals in Union territory. Feared throughout the Core, the planet serves as both a place of permanent exile and a critical source of labor and military manpower.

Those sentenced to Freedom lose their citizenship, titles, inheritance rights, political privileges, and legal ties to their former lives. Once transferred to the planet, they belong to Freedom for the remainder of their lives.

To critics, Freedom is little more than a prison world.

To its defenders, it is a system through which those who have failed the Union may repay their debt through service. Union officials often describe Freedom not as a punishment, but as a form of liberation—a place where individuals are stripped of the distractions, ambitions, and obligations of their former lives and given a single purpose: service.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Attribute Value Population 300 Million Affiliation Confederate Vanguard Union Primary Roles Penal Colony, Mining World, Military Recruitment Military Force Freedom Corps Government Union Penal Authority

docs.title: History
docs.text:
Freedom was founded during the Great Restoration as a remote detention world used to hold prisoners of war captured during the conflict. Rich deposits of strategic minerals quickly made the planet economically valuable, and the Union soon expanded the original camps into large-scale mining operations worked primarily by prisoner labor.

As the war continued, military authorities began drawing manpower directly from the prison population. What began as ad hoc penal battalions gradually evolved into the Freedom Corps, a permanent military organization tasked with frontier security, hazardous operations, and other assignments deemed unsuitable for conventional forces.

Early Freedom Corps units suffered catastrophic casualty rates. Equipment was often outdated, missions were dangerous, and commanders viewed the exiles as expendable. Over time, however, the harsh conditions produced a distinct military culture that emphasized obedience, endurance, sacrifice, and loyalty above all else. Those unable or unwilling to adapt rarely survived.

Following the Great Restoration, the Union retained the Freedom system rather than dismantling it. Criminals, deserters, insurgents, failed officials, debtors, and political dissidents gradually replaced the original prisoners of war. Mining operations expanded, military installations multiplied, and Freedom developed into a permanent institution within Union society.

Over the centuries, several Freedom Corps officers rose from exile to achieve fame through extraordinary acts of service. Their stories remain a common feature of Union propaganda, presented as proof that even those who have lost everything may still find purpose through service to the Union.

Today, Freedom stands as both a warning and an ideal. To some, it is the harshest symbol of Union authority. To others, it represents one of the Union's oldest beliefs: that a person's worth is measured not by what they possess, but by what they are willing to sacrifice.

docs.title: Sentencing to Freedom
docs.text:
Exile to Freedom is among the harshest punishments available within the Confederate Vanguard Union.

Individuals sentenced to Freedom permanently lose their status as Union citizens. Noble titles are stripped away. Property and inheritance claims are voided. Political offices are forfeited. Existing military commissions are revoked.

Upon arrival, all exiles become servants of Freedom.

Former identities carry little weight on the planet. Criminals, failed nobles, disgraced officers, political dissidents, debtors, and deserters often find themselves living side-by-side within the same settlements.

Union doctrine teaches that exile frees an individual from the burdens of status, wealth, family expectations, and personal ambition. Critics view this as little more than political rhetoric. Supporters argue that only on Freedom can a person be judged solely by their willingness to serve.

Among Freedom's inhabitants, it is often said:

"Freedom receives no nobles, no officers, no criminals. Only exiles."

docs.title: The Two Paths
docs.text: Every inhabitant of Freedom ultimately serves through one of two institutions: the Mines or the Freedom Corps.

docs.title: The Mines
docs.text:
The majority of exiles spend their lives in the mines.

Freedom contains vast deposits of strategic minerals that are essential to Union industry and military production. Mining operations stretch across entire continents and are notorious for dangerous working conditions, industrial accidents, violent labor disputes, and brutal environmental hazards.

Life expectancy within many mining districts is short. The work is exhausting, dangerous, and often lethal. Yet despite their reputation, the mines remain one of the most important economic assets in the Union.

docs.title: The Freedom Corps
docs.text:
The Freedom Corps serves as the military arm of Freedom.

Recruitment is drawn almost entirely from the world's exile population. The Corps is not designed to produce elite soldiers. Instead, it produces obedient, resilient, and expendable troops capable of operating in conditions that would break conventional military formations.

The training process is notoriously brutal. Attrition rates are high, discipline is severe, and failure is rarely tolerated. Those who survive develop a reputation for extraordinary endurance and unwavering loyalty.

Freedom Corps units are rarely deployed to the Union's major worlds and are almost never entrusted with critical military operations against rival Great Houses. Instead, they are typically assigned to remote frontier settlements, isolated border garrisons, pirate suppression campaigns, anti-bandit operations, and other dangerous postings where casualties are expected and reinforcements are often far away.

Among the regular military, the Corps is frequently viewed as a force of last resort—useful, reliable, and ultimately expendable. Yet despite this reputation, Freedom Corps soldiers have defended countless frontier communities and held some of the Union's most isolated outposts for generations.

docs.title: Culture of Service
docs.text:
Life on Freedom revolves around service.

Unlike the rest of the Union, where military service is viewed as a civic duty, the inhabitants of Freedom possess no path back to ordinary citizenship. Their former lives are gone.

For many, service becomes the only remaining source of purpose, status, or identity.

This culture has produced a reputation for fanaticism among both miners and soldiers. Loyalty is valued above talent, and commitment is valued above ambition.

Within the Freedom Corps, individuals who attempt to exploit the system for personal gain are viewed with particular contempt. Veterans often place greater value on reliability and sacrifice than on battlefield skill.

Many Freedom Corps soldiers openly embrace the belief that service itself is the highest form of redemption available to them. Among the lower classes of the Union, a small number of volunteers even choose to travel to Freedom willingly, believing that a life spent in service on the world is preferable to a life of failure elsewhere. Such volunteers are rare, but their existence reflects the deep influence of Union ideals regarding duty, sacrifice, and purpose.

docs.title: Reputation
docs.text:
Freedom occupies a strange place within Union society.

Most citizens fear it. Many military officers mock it. Politicians invoke it. Parents threaten their children with it. Yet despite its reputation, few deny its importance.

The mines fuel Union industry. The Freedom Corps guards some of the Union's most dangerous frontiers. Generations of exiles have shed blood in service to a nation that no longer considers them citizens.

For some, the planet's name is a bitter irony. For others, it reflects a distinctly Union belief: that true freedom is found not through personal choice, but through absolute dedication to a purpose greater than oneself.

As a result, Freedom is simultaneously despised, feared, respected, and relied upon.

docs.title: Legacy
docs.text:
Throughout Union history, a small number of Freedom Corps officers have risen from exile to become legendary military figures. Their stories are frequently celebrated in Union propaganda as examples of redemption through service.

Critics note that for every celebrated hero, millions have died anonymously in the mines or on distant frontier worlds.

Supporters argue that Freedom represents one of the Union's most fundamental beliefs:

A citizen may lose their rights, their wealth, and their status.

They never lose the opportunity to serve.
